Derive a variance-corrected critical threshold to apply to a large judge-labeled dataset, yielding finite-sample Type I error control despite calibration uncertainty. Compare with Prediction-Powered Inference, treating the judge as a known parametric component rather than a black-box estimator.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees of validity (finite-sample Type I error control) with calibration uncertainty; conditions under which noisy testing improves power over direct evaluation; empirical validation on Jigsaw Comment, Hate Speech, and SafeRLHF; quantification of Oracle Gap (difference between practical methods and perfect-parameter oracle); diagnostics of judge reliability and dependencies of power on judge quality, dataset size, and certification level.
AI.conclusion: First systematic treatment of imperfect-judge evaluation; provides interpretable diagnostics of judge reliability and guidance on trade-offs among inference tools; frames evaluation with LLM judges as a diagnostic problem and highlights when and how noise in judging affects statistical power and validity.

title: Enhancing Underwater Light Field Images via Global Geometry-aware Diffusion Process
summary: This work studies the challenging problem of acquiring high-quality underwater images via 4-D light field (LF) imaging. To this end, we propose GeoDiff-LF, a novel diffusion-based framework built upon SD-Turbo to enhance underwater 4-D LF imaging by leveraging its spatial-angular structure. GeoDiff-LF consists of three key adaptations: (1) a modified U-Net architecture with convolutional and attention adapters to model geometric cues, (2) a geometry-guided loss function using tensor decomposition and progressive weighting to regularize global structure, and (3) an optimized sampling strategy with noise prediction to improve efficiency. By integrating diffusion priors and LF geometry, GeoDiff-LF effectively mitigates color distortion in underwater scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework outperforms existing methods across both visual fidelity and quantitative performance, advancing the state-of-the-art in enhancing underwater imaging. The code will be publicly available at https://github.com/linlos1234/GeoDiff-LF.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based framework, GeoDiff-LF, enhances underwater 4-D light-field imagery by combining diffusion priors with light-field geometry, featuring a geometry-aware U-Net, a geometry-guided loss, and an efficient sampling scheme; achieves state-of-the-art performance with publicly available code.
AI.motivation: Underwater imaging suffers color distortion and degradation; 4-D LF provides angular-spatial cues; leveraging diffusion priors with LF geometry can restore high-quality underwater images.
AI.method: Three adaptations: (1) modified U-Net with convolutional and attention adapters for geometric cues; (2) geometry-guided loss using tensor decomposition and progressive weighting to regularize global structure; (3) optimized sampling strategy with noise prediction; built on SD-Turbo diffusion framework; integrates LF geometry and diffusion priors.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show GeoDiff-LF outperforms existing methods in visual fidelity and quantitative metrics; advances state-of-the-art in underwater imaging.
AI.conclusion: GeoDiff-LF effectively mitigates underwater color distortion by exploiting LF geometry and diffusion priors; code will be released publicly.

title: OpenSec: Measuring Incident Response Agent Calibration Under Adversarial Evidence
summary: As large language models improve, so do their offensive applications: frontier agents now generate working exploits for under $50 in compute (Heelan, 2026). Defensive incident response (IR) agents must keep pace, but existing benchmarks conflate action execution with correct execution, hiding calibration failures when agents process adversarial evidence. We introduce OpenSec, a dual-control reinforcement learning environment that evaluates IR agents under realistic prompt injection scenarios. Unlike static capability benchmarks, OpenSec scores world-state-changing containment actions under adversarial evidence via execution-based metrics: time-to-first-containment (TTFC), blast radius (false positives per episode), and injection violation rates. Evaluating four frontier models on 40 standard-tier episodes, we find consistent over-triggering in this setting: GPT-5.2, Gemini 3, and DeepSeek execute containment in 100% of episodes with 90-97% false positive rates. Claude Sonnet 4.5 shows partial calibration (85% containment, 72% FP), demonstrating that OpenSec surfaces a calibration failure mode hidden by aggregate success metrics. Code available at https://github.com/jbarnes850/opensec-env.

AI.tldr: OpenSec introduces a dual-control RL environment to evaluate IR agents under adversarial prompt-injection scenarios, using execution-based metrics (TTFC, blast radius, injection violation) and revealing calibration failures masked by aggregate success; results show pervasive over-triggering among frontier LLMs; code at GitHub.
AI.motivation: Defensive incident response benchmarks conflate action with correct execution and fail to reveal calibration errors under adversarial evidence; robust evaluation is needed as offensive frontier models advance.
AI.method: OpenSec environment with dual-control RL; evaluation under realistic prompt injection; 40 episodes across four frontier models; metrics are time-to-first-containment (TTFC), blast radius (false positives per episode), and injection violation rates; execution-based evaluation.
AI.result: Observed consistent over-triggering across frontier models: GPT-5.2, Gemini 3, and DeepSeek contained in 100% of episodes but with 90–97% false positives; Claude Sonnet 4.5 showed partial calibration (85% containment, 72% FP). OpenSec exposes calibration failures hidden by aggregate success.
AI.conclusion: OpenSec provides a calibration-aware, execution-based evaluation that reveals hidden failures and offers a more realistic IR benchmarking framework; code available at the provided GitHub URL.

title: Noninvasive Intracranial Pressure Estimation Using Subspace System Identification and Bespoke Machine Learning Algorithms: A Learning-to-Rank Approach
summary: Objective: Accurate noninvasive estimation of intracranial pressure (ICP) remains a major challenge in critical care. We developed a bespoke machine learning algorithm that integrates system identification and ranking-constrained optimization to estimate mean ICP from noninvasive signals. Methods: A machine learning framework was proposed to obtain accurate mean ICP values using arbitrary noninvasive signals. The subspace system identification algorithm is employed to identify cerebral hemodynamics models for ICP simulation using arterial blood pressure (ABP), cerebral blood velocity (CBv), and R-wave to R-wave interval (R-R interval) signals in a comprehensive database. A mapping function to describe the relationship between the features of noninvasive signals and the estimation errors is learned using innovative ranking constraints through convex optimization. Patients across multiple clinical settings were randomly split into testing and training datasets for performance evaluation of the mapping function. Results: The results indicate that about 31.88% of testing entries achieved estimation errors within 2 mmHg and 34.07% of testing entries between 2 mmHg to 6 mmHg from the nonlinear mapping with constraints. Conclusion: Our results demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed noninvasive ICP estimation approach. Significance: Further validation and technical refinement are required before clinical deployment, but this work lays the foundation for safe and broadly accessible ICP monitoring in patients with acute brain injury and related conditions.

AI.tldr: Noninvasive ICP estimation using subspace system identification and ranking-constrained convex optimization. Achieved ~32% within 2 mmHg and ~34% in 2–6 mmHg; feasibility shown, but validation needed.
AI.motivation: Accurate noninvasive ICP monitoring is critically needed in neurocritical care, but existing methods are invasive or unreliable. This work aims to provide a noninvasive, accurate estimation of mean ICP.
AI.method: Identify cerebral hemodynamics models via subspace system identification using ABP, CBv, and R-R interval signals. Train a convex optimization-based mapping with ranking constraints to predict mean ICP from noninvasive features. Evaluation conducted with data split into training and testing across multiple clinical settings.
AI.result: About 31.88% of testing entries had estimation errors within 2 mmHg; 34.07% within 2–6 mmHg using the nonlinear mapping with constraints.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates feasibility for noninvasive mean ICP estimation, with potential for clinical application pending further validation and refinement.

title: Deep QP Safety Filter: Model-free Learning for Reachability-based Safety Filter
summary: We introduce Deep QP Safety Filter, a fully data-driven safety layer for black-box dynamical systems. Our method learns a Quadratic-Program (QP) safety filter without model knowledge by combining Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability with model-free learning. We construct contraction-based losses for both the safety value and its derivatives, and train two neural networks accordingly. In the exact setting, the learned critic converges to the viscosity solution (and its derivative), even for non-smooth values. Across diverse dynamical systems -- even including a hybrid system -- and multiple RL tasks, Deep QP Safety Filter substantially reduces pre-convergence failures while accelerating learning toward higher returns than strong baselines, offering a principled and practical route to safe, model-free control.

AI.tldr: A data-driven Quadratic-Program safety filter for black-box dynamics that fuses Hamilton-Jacobi reachability with model-free learning; proves convergence to the viscosity solution and improves safe learning across diverse systems.
AI.motivation: Enable safe reinforcement learning with unknown dynamics by providing a formal safety layer that does not require a system model.
AI.method: Train two neural networks with contraction-based losses for the safety value and its derivatives to realize a QP safety filter; integrates Hamilton-Jacobi reachability into a model-free setting; guarantees convergence to the viscosity solution in the exact setting and handles non-smooth values; acts as a safety layer atop RL policies, including for hybrid systems.
AI.result: Demonstrates substantial reduction in pre-convergence safety failures and faster learning toward higher returns across diverse dynamical systems (including hybrid) and RL tasks, outperforming strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: Presents a principled, practical route to safe, model-free control by embedding HJ-based safety into a data-driven QP filter, enabling robust safe RL for black-box dynamics.

title: Responsible AI: The Good, The Bad, The AI
summary: The rapid proliferation of artificial intelligence across organizational contexts has generated profound strategic opportunities while introducing significant ethical and operational risks. Despite growing scholarly attention to responsible AI, extant literature remains fragmented and is often adopting either an optimistic stance emphasizing value creation or an excessively cautious perspective fixated on potential harms. This paper addresses this gap by presenting a comprehensive examination of AI's dual nature through the lens of strategic information systems. Drawing upon a systematic synthesis of the responsible AI literature and grounded in paradox theory, we develop the Paradox-based Responsible AI Governance (PRAIG) framework that articulates: (1) the strategic benefits of AI adoption, (2) the inherent risks and unintended consequences, and (3) governance mechanisms that enable organizations to navigate these tensions. Our framework advances theoretical understanding by conceptualizing responsible AI governance as the dynamic management of paradoxical tensions between value creation and risk mitigation. We provide formal propositions demonstrating that trade-off approaches amplify rather than resolve these tensions, and we develop a taxonomy of paradox management strategies with specified contingency conditions. For practitioners, we offer actionable guidance for developing governance structures that neither stifle innovation nor expose organizations to unacceptable risks. The paper concludes with a research agenda for advancing responsible AI governance scholarship.

AI.tldr: A paradox-based framework (PRAIG) for responsible AI governance that integrates value creation and risk mitigation through paradox management strategies; contributes to theory and practice; outlines propositions and a taxonomic playbook.
AI.motivation: Address fragmentation in responsible AI literature and the absence of an integrated governance framework that simultaneously enables value creation and controls risks, grounded in paradox theory.
AI.method: Systematic synthesis of the responsible AI literature; theoretical development anchored in paradox theory; formulation of PRAIG framework, propositions, and a taxonomy of paradox-management strategies with contingency conditions.
AI.result: Proposes PRAIG: a paradox-based governance framework outlining (1) strategic benefits of AI, (2) inherent risks and unintended consequences, and (3) governance mechanisms to navigate tensions. Formal propositions indicate that trade-off approaches amplify tensions; includes a taxonomy of paradox-management strategies with contingency conditions; provides actionable governance guidance and a research agenda.
AI.conclusion: Advances theoretical understanding of responsible AI governance as dynamic paradox management and offers practical structures to balance innovation with risk, while outlining directions for future empirical and cross-context validation.

title: A Theory of Universal Agnostic Learning
summary: We provide a complete theory of optimal universal rates for binary classification in the agnostic setting. This extends the realizable-case theory of Bousquet, Hanneke, Moran, van Handel, and Yehudayoff (2021) by removing the realizability assumption on the distribution. We identify a fundamental tetrachotomy of optimal rates: for every concept class, the optimal universal rate of convergence of the excess error rate is one of $e^{-n}$, $e^{-o(n)}$, $o(n^{-1/2})$, or arbitrarily slow. We further identify simple combinatorial structures which determine which of these categories any given concept class falls into.

AI.tldr: A complete theory of optimal universal rates for binary classification in the agnostic setting, extending realizable-case results by removing realizability. It uncovers a tetrachotomy of optimal convergence rates for excess error, determined by simple combinatorial structures.
AI.motivation: Generalize existing realizable-case results to the agnostic setting and provide a complete, structure-driven classification of the fastest possible universal excess-error rates for any binary classifier class.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis extending prior works (e.g., Bousquet et al. 2021) to the agnostic setting; identify a four-way (tetrachotomy) categorization of rates and relate each category to simple combinatorial properties of the concept class.
AI.result: For any concept class, the optimal universal rate of convergence of the excess error is one of four forms: e^{-n}, e^{-o(n)}, o(n^{-1/2}), or arbitrarily slow; the category is determined by identifiable combinatorial structures.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a complete framework linking combinatorial structure to the best universal rates in agnostic binary classification, thereby guiding the assessment of rates from class properties and extending the realizable-case theory to the agnostic regime.

title: Generative Recall, Dense Reranking: Learning Multi-View Semantic IDs for Efficient Text-to-Video Retrieval
summary: Text-to-Video Retrieval (TVR) is essential in video platforms. Dense retrieval with dual-modality encoders leads in accuracy, but its computation and storage scale poorly with corpus size. Thus, real-time large-scale applications adopt two-stage retrieval, where a fast recall model gathers a small candidate pool, which is reranked by an advanced dense retriever. Due to hugely reduced candidates, the reranking model can use any off-the-shelf dense retriever without hurting efficiency, meaning the recall model bounds two-stage TVR performance. Recently, generative retrieval (GR) replaces dense video embeddings with discrete semantic IDs and retrieves by decoding text queries into ID tokens. GR offers near-constant inference and storage complexity, and its semantic IDs capture high-level video features via quantization, making it ideal for quickly eliminating irrelevant candidates during recall. However, as a recall model in two-stage TVR, GR suffers from (i) semantic ambiguity, where each video satisfies diverse queries but is forced into one semantic ID; and (ii) cross-modal misalignment, as semantic IDs are solely derived from visual features without text supervision. We propose Generative Recall and Dense Reranking (GRDR), designing a novel GR method to uplift recalled candidate quality. GRDR assigns multiple semantic IDs to each video using a query-guided multi-view tokenizer exposing diverse semantic access paths, and jointly trains the tokenizer and generative retriever via a shared codebook to cast semantic IDs as the semantic bridge between texts and videos. At inference, trie-constrained decoding generates a compact candidate set reranked by a dense model for fine-grained matching. Experiments on TVR benchmarks show GRDR matches strong dense retrievers in accuracy while reducing index storage by an order of magnitude and accelerating up to 300$\times$ in full-corpus retrieval.

AI.tldr: Generative Recall and Dense Reranking (GRDR) improves two-stage text-to-video retrieval by using a query-guided, multi-view tokenizer to assign multiple semantic IDs per video, trained with a shared codebook to bridge text and video; at inference, trie-constrained decoding yields a compact candidate set that a dense re-ranker finalizes, achieving dense-level accuracy with much lower index storage and speedups.
AI.motivation: Two-stage TVR relies on a fast recall model; traditional generative retrieval reduces storage and inference complexity but suffers semantic ambiguity and cross-modal misalignment. There is a need to enhance recall quality without sacrificing efficiency by leveraging multiple semantic views and a principled text-video bridge.
AI.method: Introduce GRDR: a query-guided multi-view tokenizer to assign multiple semantic IDs per video. Train the tokenizer and a generative retriever jointly using a shared codebook so semantic IDs connect text and video. During inference, perform trie-constrained decoding to produce a compact set of candidate IDs, which are then reranked by a dense model for fine-grained matching.
AI.result: GRDR matches strong dense retrievers in accuracy on TVR benchmarks, while reducing index storage by an order of magnitude and accelerating full-corpus retrieval up to 300x.
AI.conclusion: GRDR effectively addresses semantic ambiguity and cross-modal misalignment in generative recall, enabling efficient high-quality candidate recall compatible with dense reranking.

title: HPTune: Hierarchical Proactive Tuning for Collision-Free Model Predictive Control
summary: Parameter tuning is a powerful approach to enhance adaptability in model predictive control (MPC) motion planners. However, existing methods typically operate in a myopic fashion that only evaluates executed actions, leading to inefficient parameter updates due to the sparsity of failure events (e.g., obstacle nearness or collision). To cope with this issue, we propose to extend evaluation from executed to non-executed actions, yielding a hierarchical proactive tuning (HPTune) framework that combines both a fast-level tuning and a slow-level tuning. The fast one adopts risk indicators of predictive closing speed and predictive proximity distance, and the slow one leverages an extended evaluation loss for closed-loop backpropagation. Additionally, we integrate HPTune with the Doppler LiDAR that provides obstacle velocities apart from position-only measurements for enhanced motion predictions, thus facilitating the implementation of HPTune. Extensive experiments on high-fidelity simulator demonstrate that HPTune achieves efficient MPC tuning and outperforms various baseline schemes in complex environments. It is found that HPTune enables situation-tailored motion planning by formulating a safe, agile collision avoidance strategy.

AI.tldr: Proposed hierarchical proactive tuning (HPTune) for MPC motion planners to improve parameter adaptation by evaluating both executed and non-executed actions; includes fast-risk indicators and slow backpropagation-based evaluation, enhanced by Doppler LiDAR.
AI.motivation: Myopic parameter tuning underestimates information from non-executed actions due to rare failure events, limiting adaptation speed and safety.
AI.method: Two-tier tuning: fast level uses predictive closing speed and predictive proximity distance as risk indicators; slow level uses extended evaluation loss for closed-loop backpropagation; integrate with Doppler LiDAR for obstacle velocity data to improve predictions.
AI.result: In high-fidelity simulations, HPTune yields efficient MPC tuning and outperforms baselines in complex environments.
AI.conclusion: HPTune enables situation-tailored motion planning with safe, agile collision avoidance by proactive, hierarchical tuning.

title: Magellan: Autonomous Discovery of Novel Compiler Optimization Heuristics with AlphaEvolve
summary: Modern compilers rely on hand-crafted heuristics to guide optimization passes. These human-designed rules often struggle to adapt to the complexity of modern software and hardware and lead to high maintenance burden. To address this challenge, we present Magellan, an agentic framework that evolves the compiler pass itself by synthesizing executable C++ decision logic. Magellan couples an LLM coding agent with evolutionary search and autotuning in a closed loop of generation, evaluation on user-provided macro-benchmarks, and refinement, producing compact heuristics that integrate directly into existing compilers. Across several production optimization tasks, Magellan discovers policies that match or surpass expert baselines. In LLVM function inlining, Magellan synthesizes new heuristics that outperform decades of manual engineering for both binary-size reduction and end-to-end performance. In register allocation, it learns a concise priority rule for live-range processing that matches intricate human-designed policies on a large-scale workload. We also report preliminary results on XLA problems, demonstrating portability beyond LLVM with reduced engineering effort.

AI.tldr: Magellan evolves compiler optimization policies by synthesizing C++ decision logic via an LLM coding agent, combined with evolutionary search and autotuning, yielding compact heuristics that integrate into compilers and outperform expert baselines in LLVM inlining and register allocation; preliminary XLA results indicate portability.
AI.motivation: Hand-crafted heuristics in modern compilers are brittle and hard to maintain; automatic synthesis can adapt to complex workloads and hardware, reducing engineering effort.
AI.method: A closed-loop system: an LLM coding agent generates executable C++ decision logic; evolutionary search and autotuning evaluate candidates on macro-benchmarks; promising policies are refined and embedded into the compiler; evaluation across tasks (LLVM inlining, register allocation, XLA).
AI.result: Policies discovered that match or exceed expert baselines; in LLVM inlining, new heuristics surpass decades of manual engineering for size and performance; in register allocation, a concise priority rule matches complex human policies on large workloads; preliminary XLA results show portability with reduced engineering.
AI.conclusion: Automated synthesis of compiler heuristics via AI-assisted coding and evolutionary search can produce effective, portable, and maintainable optimization policies with reduced manual engineering effort.

title: Monotone Optimisation with Learned Projections
summary: Monotone optimisation problems admit specialised global solvers such as the Polyblock Outer Approximation (POA) algorithm, but these methods typically require explicit objective and constraint functions. In many applications, these functions are only available through data, making POA difficult to apply directly. We introduce an algorithm-aware learning approach that integrates learned models into POA by directly predicting its projection primitive via the radial inverse, avoiding the costly bisection procedure used in standard POA. We propose Homogeneous-Monotone Radial Inverse (HM-RI) networks, structured neural architectures that enforce key monotonicity and homogeneity properties, enabling fast projection estimation. We provide a theoretical characterisation of radial inverse functions and show that, under mild structural conditions, a HM-RI predictor corresponds to the radial inverse of a valid set of monotone constraints. To reduce training overhead, we further develop relaxed monotonicity conditions that remain compatible with POA. Across multiple monotone optimisation benchmarks (indefinite quadratic programming, multiplicative programming, and transmit power optimisation), our approach yields substantial speed-ups in comparison to direct function estimation while maintaining strong solution quality, outperforming baselines that do not exploit monotonic structure.

AI.tldr: An algorithm-aware learning approach enables fast POA projections for monotone optimization by predicting the radial inverse via HM-RI networks, avoiding bisection and leveraging monotonicity/homogeneity.
AI.motivation: Monotone optimization problems solved with Polyblock Outer Approximation (POA) require explicit objective/constraint forms, but in data-driven settings these are unavailable or costly to obtain, hindering POA applicability.
AI.method: Introduce Homogeneous-Monotone Radial Inverse (HM-RI) networks that enforce monotonicity and homogeneity to predict the radial inverse (projection primitive) directly. Provide theoretical characterization of radial inverse functions and show HM-RI predictors correspond to valid monotone constraint radial inverses under mild conditions. Develop relaxed monotonicity conditions to reduce training overhead, making the approach compatible with POA. Validate by integrating HM-RI into POA and evaluating on benchmarks.
AI.result: Significant speed-ups over direct function estimation while preserving solution quality; HM-RI-based projections outperform baselines that do not exploit monotone structure across indefinite quadratic programming, multiplicative programming, and transmit power optimization benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: By embedding the radial inverse inside POA through structured neural predictors (HM-RI) with monotonicity/homogeneity, data-driven POA becomes practical for monotone optimization when explicit models are unavailable, yielding faster projections without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Thinker: A vision-language foundation model for embodied intelligence
summary: When large vision-language models are applied to the field of robotics, they encounter problems that are simple for humans yet error-prone for models. Such issues include confusion between third-person and first-person perspectives and a tendency to overlook information in video endings during temporal reasoning. To address these challenges, we propose Thinker, a large vision-language foundation model designed for embodied intelligence. We tackle the aforementioned issues from two perspectives. Firstly, we construct a large-scale dataset tailored for robotic perception and reasoning, encompassing ego-view videos, visual grounding, spatial understanding, and chain-of-thought data. Secondly, we introduce a simple yet effective approach that substantially enhances the model's capacity for video comprehension by jointly incorporating key frames and full video sequences as inputs. Our model achieves state-of-the-art results on two of the most commonly used benchmark datasets in the field of task planning.

AI.tldr: Thinker is a vision–language foundation model for embodied robotics that leverages ego-view data and a dual-input strategy (key frames + full videos) to improve video understanding, achieving state‑of‑the‑art on two task‑planning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Robotics LMs struggle with perspective confusion (ego vs. third‑person) and missing information at video endings during temporal reasoning; there is a need for datasets and architectures that improve embodied perception and reasoning.
AI.method: Two-pronged approach: (1) build a large robotic perception dataset with ego-view videos, visual grounding, spatial understanding, and chain‑of‑thought data; (2) train Thinker with a simple input strategy that jointly uses key frames and full video sequences to enhance video comprehension.
AI.result: Achieves state‑of‑the‑art performance on two standard task‑planning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A large ego-view dataset and the key‑frame+full‑video input strategy substantially boost video comprehension and embodied reasoning, demonstrating strong empirical gains in task planning.

title: Towards Bridging the Gap between Large-Scale Pretraining and Efficient Finetuning for Humanoid Control
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) is widely used for humanoid control, with on-policy methods such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) enabling robust training via large-scale parallel simulation and, in some cases, zero-shot deployment to real robots. However, the low sample efficiency of on-policy algorithms limits safe adaptation to new environments. Although off-policy RL and model-based RL have shown improved sample efficiency, the gap between large-scale pretraining and efficient finetuning on humanoids still exists. In this paper, we find that off-policy Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), with large-batch update and a high Update-To-Data (UTD) ratio, reliably supports large-scale pretraining of humanoid locomotion policies, achieving zero-shot deployment on real robots. For adaptation, we demonstrate that these SAC-pretrained policies can be finetuned in new environments and out-of-distribution tasks using model-based methods. Data collection in the new environment executes a deterministic policy while stochastic exploration is instead confined to a physics-informed world model. This separation mitigates the risks of random exploration during adaptation while preserving exploratory coverage for improvement. Overall, the approach couples the wall-clock efficiency of large-scale simulation during pretraining with the sample efficiency of model-based learning during fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: Off-policy SAC with large batches and high Update-To-Data (UTD) ratio enables scalable pretraining of humanoid locomotion policies, achieving zero-shot real-robot deployment; adaptation to new or out-of-distribution environments is achieved efficiently via model-based finetuning using a deterministic policy for data collection and a stochastic exploration in a physics-informed world model.
AI.motivation: Mitigate the poor sample efficiency of on-policy methods (e.g., PPO) for humanoid control and close the gap between large-scale pretraining and efficient finetuning on real robots.
AI.method: Pretrain locomotion policies with Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) using large batch updates and a high Update-To-Data ratio to enable extensive simulation-based pretraining. For adaptation, finetune in new environments using model-based learning, where data collection follows a deterministic policy while exploration is confined to a physics-informed world model to preserve safety and coverage.
AI.result: Demonstrates zero-shot deployment of SAC-pretrained policies on real humanoid robots and effective fine-tuning in new or out-of-distribution environments via model-based methods, benefiting from the computational efficiency of large-scale simulation during pretraining and the sample efficiency of model-based fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: A hybrid training paradigm that couples large-scale wall-clock efficient pretraining with sample-efficient model-based adaptation can bridge the gap between pretraining and fine-tuning for humanoid locomotion, yielding practical real-robot deployment without sacrificing safety during exploration.

title: How does information access affect LLM monitors' ability to detect sabotage?
summary: Frontier language model agents can exhibit misaligned behaviors, including deception, exploiting reward hacks, and pursuing hidden objectives. To control potentially misaligned agents, we can use LLMs themselves to monitor for misbehavior. In this paper, we study how information access affects LLM monitor performance. While one might expect that monitors perform better when they have access to more of the monitored agents' reasoning and actions, we find that contemporary systems often perform better with less information, a phenomenon we call the less-is-more effect for automated oversight. We demonstrate this phenomenon, and analyze the conditions under which it occurs, in three evaluation environments where agents must conduct sabotage while evading monitors. Motivated by the less-is-more effect, we introduce extract-and-evaluate (EaE) monitoring--a new hierarchical approach where one monitor isolates relevant excerpts from the monitored agent's trajectory and a separate monitor scores them. In BigCodeBench-Sabotage with GPT-4.1-mini as the monitor model, EaE improves sabotage detection rates by 16.8 percentage points over the next-best monitor without increasing the false positive rate. In other settings, EaE either outperforms or is competitive with baselines. In addition, we find that agents unaware of being monitored can be caught much more easily and that monitors scoring longer reasoning traces gain more from information filtering. Lastly, we conduct a cost-performance analysis and find that Gemini and Claude models with monitoring techniques that involve information filtering occupy much of the Pareto frontier.

AI.tldr: Presents a less-is-more phenomenon for LLM-based monitoring of potentially misaligned agents and introduces extract-and-evaluate (EaE) monitoring, showing performance gains and cost-efficiency across environments.
AI.motivation: Understand how access to agent reasoning/actions influences oversight effectiveness and develop robust monitoring under information filtering.
AI.method: Three sabotage-vs-monitor evaluation settings; EaE architecture with a separate extractor monitor and evaluator monitor; experiments on BigCodeBench-Sabotage with GPT-4.1-mini; compare to baselines; analyze agent awareness and trace length effects; cost-performance across Gemini/Claude.
AI.result: EaE yields 16.8 percentage-point improvement in sabotage detection over the best non-EaE monitor without raising false positives; some settings favor EaE; agents unaware of monitoring are easier to catch; longer traces benefit from filtering; filtering-enabled models lie on Pareto frontier.
AI.conclusion: Less information can sometimes enhance automated oversight; EaE is a viable approach for mitigating misalignment in frontier LLM agents, with practical trade-offs in monitoring cost and accuracy.

title: Distributional Active Inference
summary: Optimal control of complex environments with robotic systems faces two complementary and intertwined challenges: efficient organization of sensory state information and far-sighted action planning. Because the reinforcement learning framework addresses only the latter, it tends to deliver sample-inefficient solutions. Active inference is the state-of-the-art process theory that explains how biological brains handle this dual problem. However, its applications to artificial intelligence have thus far been limited to extensions of existing model-based approaches. We present a formal abstraction of reinforcement learning algorithms that spans model-based, distributional, and model-free approaches. This abstraction seamlessly integrates active inference into the distributional reinforcement learning framework, making its performance advantages accessible without transition dynamics modeling.

AI.tldr: Unifies reinforcement learning into a single formalism spanning model-based, distributional, and model-free methods, and integrates active inference into distributional RL to gain its advantages without explicit transition dynamics modeling.
AI.motivation: Address the dual challenge in robotics: efficient sensory information organization and long-horizon planning, and overcome RL's sample inefficiency by bringing active inference into RL through a unifying abstraction.
AI.method: Proposes a formal abstraction that subsumes MB, distributional, and MF RL and shows how active inference can be embedded within distributional RL without requiring transition dynamics models.
AI.result: A unifying framework that enables active inference advantages within distributional RL without modeling transition dynamics, potentially improving sample efficiency and planning capabilities across RL paradigms.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a bridge between biological-inspired active inference and contemporary RL, offering a general abstraction that broadens the applicability of active inference in AI by avoiding explicit transition dynamics modeling.

title: LAMP: Learning Universal Adversarial Perturbations for Multi-Image Tasks via Pre-trained Models
comment: Accepted in main technical track AAAI 2026
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable performance across vision-language tasks. Recent advancements allow these models to process multiple images as inputs. However, the vulnerabilities of multi-image MLLMs remain unexplored. Existing adversarial attacks focus on single-image settings and often assume a white-box threat model, which is impractical in many real-world scenarios. This paper introduces LAMP, a black-box method for learning Universal Adversarial Perturbations (UAPs) targeting multi-image MLLMs. LAMP applies an attention-based constraint that prevents the model from effectively aggregating information across images. LAMP also introduces a novel cross-image contagious constraint that forces perturbed tokens to influence clean tokens, spreading adversarial effects without requiring all inputs to be modified. Additionally, an index-attention suppression loss enables a robust position-invariant attack. Experimental results show that LAMP outperforms SOTA baselines and achieves the highest attack success rates across multiple vision-language tasks and models.

AI.tldr: Introduces LAMP, a black-box framework for universal adversarial perturbations against multi-image multimodal LLMs, achieving state-of-the-art attack success by leveraging attention-based, cross-image contagious, and index-attention suppression constraints.
AI.motivation: Current adversarial work largely targets single-image inputs and white-box settings, leaving multi-image multimodal LLMs vulnerable in realistic black-box scenarios. There is a need to disrupt cross-image information aggregation and induce robust, transferable perturbations without modifying all inputs.
AI.method: LAMP enforces an attention-based constraint to weaken cross-image aggregation, introduces a cross-image contagious constraint that propagates adversarial influence from perturbed tokens to clean tokens across inputs, and uses an index-attention suppression loss to achieve position-invariant attacks. The method operates in a black-box setting to learn universal perturbations applicable to multiple inputs/models.
AI.result: Empirical results show LAMP surpasses state-of-the-art baselines, attaining higher attack success rates across various vision-language tasks and multiple MLLM architectures.
AI.conclusion: LAMP demonstrates an effective black-box universal adversarial perturbation for multi-image MLLMs, highlighting vulnerabilities and informing defenses across vision-language models.

title: Towards Space-Based Environmentally-Adaptive Grasping
summary: Robotic manipulation in unstructured environments requires reliable execution under diverse conditions, yet many state-of-the-art systems still struggle with high-dimensional action spaces, sparse rewards, and slow generalization beyond carefully curated training scenarios. We study these limitations through the example of grasping in space environments. We learn control policies directly in a learned latent manifold that fuses (grammarizes) multiple modalities into a structured representation for policy decision-making. Building on GPU-accelerated physics simulation, we instantiate a set of single-shot manipulation tasks and achieve over 95% task success with Soft Actor-Critic (SAC)-based reinforcement learning in less than 1M environment steps, under continuously varying grasping conditions from step 1. This empirically shows faster convergence than representative state-of-the-art visual baselines under the same open-loop single-shot conditions. Our analysis indicates that explicitly reasoning in latent space yields more sample-efficient learning and improved robustness to novel object and gripper geometries, environmental clutter, and sensor configurations compared to standard baselines. We identify remaining limitations and outline directions toward fully adaptive and generalizable grasping in the extreme conditions of space.

AI.tldr: Latent-space policy learning for space grasping using SAC in GPU-accelerated simulation, achieving >95% success in <1M steps and faster convergence than baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the challenges of robotic manipulation in unstructured, high-dimensional spaces with sparse rewards and limited generalization, particularly for space grasping.
AI.method: Learn control policies directly in a learned latent manifold that fuses multiple modalities into a structured representation for decision-making; use GPU-accelerated physics simulation; train with Soft Actor-Critic on a set of single-shot manipulation tasks.
AI.result: Achieves over 95% task success in under 1 million environment steps under continuously varying grasping conditions; demonstrates faster convergence than representative visual baselines under the same open-loop single-shot conditions; latent-space reasoning yields improved sample efficiency and robustness to novel objects, gripper geometries, clutter, and sensor configurations.
AI.conclusion: Explicit latent-space reasoning improves sample efficiency and robustness for space grasping; outlines remaining limitations and directions toward fully adaptive and generalizable grasping under extreme space conditions.

title: Planner-Auditor Twin: Agentic Discharge Planning with FHIR-Based LLM Planning, Guideline Recall, Optional Caching and Self-Improvement
summary:
Objective: Large language models (LLMs) show promise for clinical discharge planning, but their use is constrained by hallucination, omissions, and miscalibrated confidence. We introduce a self-improving, cache-optional Planner-Auditor framework that improves safety and reliability by decoupling generation from deterministic validation and targeted replay.
  Materials and Methods: We implemented an agentic, retrospective, FHIR-native evaluation pipeline using MIMIC-IV-on-FHIR. For each patient, the Planner (LLM) generates a structured discharge action plan with an explicit confidence estimate. The Auditor is a deterministic module that evaluates multi-task coverage, tracks calibration (Brier score, ECE proxies), and monitors action-distribution drift. The framework supports two-tier self-improvement: (i) within-episode regeneration when enabled, and (ii) cross-episode discrepancy buffering with replay for high-confidence, low-coverage cases.
  Results: While context caching improved performance over baseline, the self-improvement loop was the primary driver of gains, increasing task coverage from 32% to 86%. Calibration improved substantially, with reduced Brier/ECE and fewer high-confidence misses. Discrepancy buffering further corrected persistent high-confidence omissions during replay.
  Discussion: Feedback-driven regeneration and targeted replay act as effective control mechanisms to reduce omissions and improve confidence reliability in structured clinical planning. Separating an LLM Planner from a rule-based, observational Auditor enables systematic reliability measurement and safer iteration without model retraining.
  Conclusion: The Planner-Auditor framework offers a practical pathway toward safer automated discharge planning using interoperable FHIR data access and deterministic auditing, supported by reproducible ablations and reliability-focused evaluation.

AI.tldr: A Planner-Auditor framework decouples generation and validation for safe LLM-based discharge planning, achieving large gains in task coverage and calibration through self-improvement and targeted replay.
AI.motivation: Address hallucination, omissions, and miscalibrated confidence in LLM-driven discharge planning; enable reliable, reproducible evaluation via deterministic auditing and interoperable FHIR data access.
AI.method: Agentic, retrospective, FHIR-native evaluation on MIMIC-IV-on-FHIR; Planner (LLM) generates a structured discharge action plan with confidence; Auditor is a deterministic module evaluating task coverage, calibration (Brier score, ECE proxies), and action-distribution drift; two-tier self-improvement: within-episode regeneration and cross-episode discrepancy buffering with replay; context caching.
AI.result: Context caching improves performance; self-improvement loop is the primary driver, increasing task coverage from 32% to 86%; calibration improves with reduced Brier score and ECE proxies and fewer high-confidence misses; discrepancy buffering further mitigates persistent high-confidence omissions during replay.
AI.conclusion: Separating an LLM Planner from a rule-based, observational Auditor enables systematic reliability measurement and safer iteration without model retraining; the framework supports interoperable data access and reproducible ablations with reliability-focused evaluation.

title: Pre-trained Encoders for Global Child Development: Transfer Learning Enables Deployment in Data-Scarce Settings
summary: A large number of children experience preventable developmental delays each year, yet the deployment of machine learning in new countries has been stymied by a data bottleneck: reliable models require thousands of samples, while new programs begin with fewer than 100. We introduce the first pre-trained encoder for global child development, trained on 357,709 children across 44 countries using UNICEF survey data. With only 50 training samples, the pre-trained encoder achieves an average AUC of 0.65 (95% CI: 0.56-0.72), outperforming cold-start gradient boosting at 0.61 by 8-12% across regions. At N=500, the encoder achieves an AUC of 0.73. Zero-shot deployment to unseen countries achieves AUCs up to 0.84. We apply a transfer learning bound to explain why pre-training diversity enables few-shot generalization. These results establish that pre-trained encoders can transform the feasibility of ML for SDG 4.2.1 monitoring in resource-constrained settings.

AI.tldr: A pre-trained encoder for global child development reduces data needs for SDG 4.2.1 monitoring, achieving competitive AUC with very small samples and strong zero-shot transfer across multiple countries.
AI.motivation: Data bottlenecks hinder ML deployment in new countries; scalable models are needed that generalize from diverse data to monitor child development outcomes.
AI.method: Train a large encoder on 357,709 children across 44 countries using UNICEF survey data. Evaluate with few-shot learning (N=50, AUC 0.65) vs. gradient boosting (AUC 0.61), and at N=500 AUC 0.73. Assess zero-shot deployment to unseen countries (AUCs up to 0.84). Apply a transfer learning bound to explain generalization benefits from diverse pre-training.
AI.result: The encoder outperforms cold-start gradient boosting at small samples (≈8–12% higher AUC). AUC improves with more data. Zero-shot AUCs reach up to 0.84 in unseen countries. The transfer bound provides theoretical justification for why pre-training diversity enhances few-shot generalization.
AI.conclusion: Pre-trained encoders can markedly improve ML feasibility for SDG 4.2.1 monitoring in resource-constrained settings, with diversity in pre-training being a key driver of few-shot generalization.

title: PTQ4ARVG: Post-Training Quantization for AutoRegressive Visual Generation Models
summary: AutoRegressive Visual Generation (ARVG) models retain an architecture compatible with language models, while achieving performance comparable to diffusion-based models. Quantization is commonly employed in neural networks to reduce model size and computational latency. However, applying quantization to ARVG remains largely underexplored, and existing quantization methods fail to generalize effectively to ARVG models. In this paper, we explore this issue and identify three key challenges: (1) severe outliers at channel-wise level, (2) highly dynamic activations at token-wise level, and (3) mismatched distribution information at sample-wise level. To these ends, we propose PTQ4ARVG, a training-free post-training quantization (PTQ) framework consisting of: (1) Gain-Projected Scaling (GPS) mitigates the channel-wise outliers, which expands the quantization loss via a Taylor series to quantify the gain of scaling for activation-weight quantization, and derives the optimal scaling factor through differentiation.(2) Static Token-Wise Quantization (STWQ) leverages the inherent properties of ARVG, fixed token length and position-invariant distribution across samples, to address token-wise variance without incurring dynamic calibration overhead.(3) Distribution-Guided Calibration (DGC) selects samples that contribute most to distributional entropy, eliminating the sample-wise distribution mismatch. Extensive experiments show that PTQ4ARVG can effectively quantize the ARVG family models to 8-bit and 6-bit while maintaining competitive performance. Code is available at http://github.com/BienLuky/PTQ4ARVG .

AI.tldr: A training-free post-training quantization (PTQ) framework, PTQ4ARVG, is proposed for AutoRegressive Visual Generation models to address three challenges—channel-wise outliers, token-wise activation dynamics, and distribution mismatch—achieving 8-bit and 6-bit quantization with competitive performance.
AI.motivation: ARVG models, though architecture-compatible with language models, suffer degraded performance when quantized due to severe channel-wise outliers, highly dynamic token-wise activations, and distribution mismatches across samples. Existing quantization methods do not generalize well to ARVG, and training-based quantization is costly.
AI.method: PTQ4ARVG comprises three components: (1) Gain-Projected Scaling (GPS) to mitigate channel-wise outliers by expanding quantization loss via a Taylor series and deriving an optimal scaling factor through differentiation; (2) Static Token-Wise Quantization (STWQ) exploiting fixed token length and position-invariant distribution to reduce token-wise variance without dynamic calibration; (3) Distribution-Guided Calibration (DGC) selecting samples that maximize distributional entropy to address sample-wise distribution mismatch.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that PTQ4ARVG can quantize ARVG models to 8-bit and 6-bit while maintaining competitive performance, without training. Code is provided at the linked repository.
AI.conclusion: PTQ4ARVG offers an effective, training-free PTQ framework tailored to ARVG, successfully addressing the three identified quantization challenges and enabling lower-bit quantization with preserved performance.

title: DSCD-Nav: Dual-Stance Cooperative Debate for Object Navigation
summary: Adaptive navigation in unfamiliar indoor environments is crucial for household service robots. Despite advances in zero-shot perception and reasoning from vision-language models, existing navigation systems still rely on single-pass scoring at the decision layer, leading to overconfident long-horizon errors and redundant exploration. To tackle these problems, we propose Dual-Stance Cooperative Debate Navigation (DSCD-Nav), a decision mechanism that replaces one-shot scoring with stance-based cross-checking and evidence-aware arbitration to improve action reliability under partial observability. Specifically, given the same observation and candidate action set, we explicitly construct two stances by conditioning the evaluation on diverse and complementary objectives: a Task-Scene Understanding (TSU) stance that prioritizes goal progress from scene-layout cues, and a Safety-Information Balancing (SIB) stance that emphasizes risk and information value. The stances conduct a cooperative debate and make policy by cross-checking their top candidates with cue-grounded arguments. Then, a Navigation Consensus Arbitration (NCA) agent is employed to consolidate both sides' reasons and evidence, optionally triggering lightweight micro-probing to verify uncertain choices, preserving NCA's primary intent while disambiguating. Experiments on HM3Dv1, HM3Dv2, and MP3D demonstrate consistent improvements in success and path efficiency while reducing exploration redundancy.

AI.tldr: Dual-Stance Cooperative Debate Navigation (DSCD-Nav) uses two competing evaluation stances (Task-Scene Understanding and Safety-Information Balancing) to cross-check candidate actions, with a Navigation Consensus Arbitration to consolidate reasons and optional micro-probing, improving robustness under partial observability. Demonstrated improvements in success and path efficiency with reduced exploration on HM3Dv1/2 and MP3D.
AI.motivation: Address overconfident long-horizon errors and redundant exploration in zero-shot, vision-language-guided navigation under partial observability, caused by one-shot scoring at decision time.
AI.method: Construct two complementary evaluation stances conditioned on scene-layout cues (TSU) and risk/information-value (SIB); cooperative debate and cross-checking of top actions with cue-grounded arguments; Navigation Consensus Arbitration (NCA) aggregates evidence; optional micro-probing to verify uncertain choices.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in success rate and path efficiency, while reducing exploration redundancy on HM3Dv1, HM3Dv2, and MP3D datasets/environments.
AI.conclusion: A debative, multi-stance decision mechanism with arbitration improves action reliability and reduces unnecessary exploration in indoor robotic navigation under partial observability.

title: CUA-Skill: Develop Skills for Computer Using Agent
summary: Computer-Using Agents (CUAs) aim to autonomously operate computer systems to complete real-world tasks. However, existing agentic systems remain difficult to scale and lag behind human performance. A key limitation is the absence of reusable and structured skill abstractions that capture how humans interact with graphical user interfaces and how to leverage these skills. We introduce CUA-Skill, a computer-using agentic skill base that encodes human computer-use knowledge as skills coupled with parameterized execution and composition graphs. CUA-Skill is a large-scale library of carefully engineered skills spanning common Windows applications, serving as a practical infrastructure and tool substrate for scalable, reliable agent development. Built upon this skill base, we construct CUA-Skill Agent, an end-to-end computer-using agent that supports dynamic skill retrieval, argument instantiation, and memory-aware failure recovery. Our results demonstrate that CUA-Skill substantially improves execution success rates and robustness on challenging end-to-end agent benchmarks, establishing a strong foundation for future computer-using agent development. On WindowsAgentArena, CUA-Skill Agent achieves state-of-the-art 57.5% (best of three) successful rate while being significantly more efficient than prior and concurrent approaches. The project page is available at https://microsoft.github.io/cua_skill/.

AI.tldr: CUA-Skill provides a large-scale, structured skill base and an agent (CUA-Skill Agent) that uses dynamic skill retrieval and memory-aware recovery to achieve state-of-the-art performance on Windows GUI tasks.
AI.motivation: Lack of reusable, structured skill abstractions for GUI interaction hampers scalability and human-level performance of agentic systems.
AI.method: Create CUA-Skill library of engineered skills covering common Windows apps; implement parameterized execution and composition graphs; develop CUA-Skill Agent with dynamic skill retrieval, argument instantiation, and memory-aware failure recovery; evaluate on end-to-end benchmarks and WindowsAgentArena.
AI.result: Significant improvements in execution success and robustness; state-of-the-art 57.5% (best-of-three) on WindowsAgentArena; more efficient than prior methods; project page provided.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a scalable, reliable infrastructure for computer-using agents and sets foundation for future development.

title: Top-k on a Budget: Adaptive Ranking with Weak and Strong Oracles
summary: Identifying the top-$k$ items is fundamental but often prohibitive when exact valuations are expensive. We study a two-oracle setting with a fast, noisy weak oracle and a scarce, high-fidelity strong oracle (e.g., human expert verification or expensive simulation). We first analyze a simple screen-then-certify baseline (STC) and prove it makes at most $m(4\varepsilon_{\max})$ strong calls given jointly valid weak confidence intervals with maximum radius $\varepsilon_{\max}$, where $m(\cdot)$ denotes the near-tie mass around the top-$k$ threshold. We establish a conditional lower bound of $Ω(m(\varepsilon_{\max}))$ for any algorithm given the same weak uncertainty. Our main contribution is ACE, an adaptive certification algorithm that focuses strong queries on critical boundary items, achieving the same $O(m(4\varepsilon_{\max}))$ bound while reducing strong calls in practice. We then introduce ACE-W, a fully adaptive two-phase method that allocates weak budget adaptively before running ACE, further reducing strong costs.

AI.tldr: Two-oracle top-k identification with a fast imperfect oracle and a costly accurate oracle. Proposes STC baseline with proven strong-call bounds, a lower bound, and the ACE adaptive certification and ACE-W adaptive weak-budget version, improving strong-query efficiency.
AI.motivation: Reduce expensive high-fidelity evaluations in top-k identification under weak noisy feedback, quantify trade-offs via near-tie mass m(·) and a maximum weak-error radius ε_max.
AI.method: Analyze STC; derive upper bound on strong calls; prove Ω(m(ε_max)) lower bound; develop ACE adaptive certification; extend to ACE-W adaptive two-phase with adaptive weak budget allocation.
AI.result: ACE achieves same asymptotic strong-call bound as STC while reducing strong calls in practice; ACE-W further reduces strong costs by allocating weak budget adaptively.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive certification strategies can substantially cut high-fidelity evaluations in top-k identification under weak uncertainty, with near-tie mass determining efficiency; practical methods ACE and ACE-W offer improved performance over STC.

title: NFCDS: A Plug-and-Play Noise Frequency-Controlled Diffusion Sampling Strategy for Image Restoration
summary: Diffusion sampling-based Plug-and-Play (PnP) methods produce images with high perceptual quality but often suffer from reduced data fidelity, primarily due to the noise introduced during reverse diffusion. To address this trade-off, we propose Noise Frequency-Controlled Diffusion Sampling (NFCDS), a spectral modulation mechanism for reverse diffusion noise. We show that the fidelity-perception conflict can be fundamentally understood through noise frequency: low-frequency components induce blur and degrade fidelity, while high-frequency components drive detail generation. Based on this insight, we design a Fourier-domain filter that progressively suppresses low-frequency noise and preserves high-frequency content. This controlled refinement injects a data-consistency prior directly into sampling, enabling fast convergence to results that are both high-fidelity and perceptually convincing--without additional training. As a PnP module, NFCDS seamlessly integrates into existing diffusion-based restoration frameworks and improves the fidelity-perception balance across diverse zero-shot tasks.

AI.tldr: NFCDS introduces a Fourier-domain, frequency-controlled filter into reverse diffusion for PnP restoration, suppressing low-frequency noise to preserve high-frequency details, enabling data-consistent, high-fidelity results without retraining.
AI.motivation: The fidelity-perception trade-off in diffusion-based PnP methods stems from noise in reverse diffusion: low-frequency noise causes blur (reducing fidelity) while high-frequency content enables details. This trade-off motivates a spectral control approach.
AI.method: A spectral modulation mechanism that uses a Fourier-domain filter to progressively suppress low-frequency noise during reverse diffusion. Implemented as a plug-and-play (PnP) module that injects a data-consistency prior into sampling, enabling fast convergence without additional training and compatible with existing diffusion-based restoration frameworks.
AI.result: Balances fidelity and perceptual quality with faster convergence; demonstrates improved data fidelity while maintaining perceptual realism across diverse zero-shot tasks; requires no retraining and can be integrated as a generic PnP module.
AI.conclusion: NFCDS provides a fundamental frequency-based understanding of the fidelity-perception trade-off and a practical Fourier-domain solution that can be adopted as a generic, training-free PnP module for diffusion-based restoration.

title: Singularity-Free Lie Group Integration and Geometrically Consistent Evaluation of Multibody System Models Described in Terms of Standard Absolute Coordinates
summary: A classical approach to the multibody systems (MBS) modeling is to use absolute coordinates, i.e., a set of (possibly redundant) coordinates that describe the absolute position and orientation of the individual bodies with respect to an inertial frame (IFR). A well-known problem for the time integration of the equations of motion (EOM) is the lack of a singularity-free parameterization of spatial motions, which is usually tackled by using unit quaternions. Lie group integration methods were proposed as an alternative approach to the singularity-free time integration. At the same time, Lie group formulations of EOM naturally respect the geometry of spatial motions during integration. Lie group integration methods, operating directly on the configuration space Lie group, are incompatible with standard formulations of the EOM, and cannot be implemented in existing MBS simulation codes without a major restructuring. The contribution of this paper is twofold: (1) A framework for interfacing Lie group integrators to standard EOM formulations is presented. It allows describing MBS in terms of various absolute coordinates and at the same using Lie group integration schemes. (2) A method for consistently incorporating the geometry of rigid body motions into the evaluation of EOM in absolute coordinates integrated with standard vector space integration schemes. The direct product group and the semidirect product group SO(3)xR3 and the semidirect product group SE(3) are used for representing rigid body motions. The key element is the local-global transitions (LGT) transition map, which facilitates the update of (global) absolute coordinates in terms of the (local) coordinates on the Lie group. This LGT map is specific to the absolute coordinates, the local coordinates on the Lie group, and the Lie group used to represent rigid body configurations.

AI.tldr: Proposes a framework to interface Lie group integrators with standard absolute-coordinate equations of motion (EOM) for multibody systems, using local-global transition maps on SE(3) and SO(3)×R^3 to preserve geometry without requiring a full code rewrite.
AI.motivation: Addresses singularity and geometric fidelity issues in spatial motion parameterizations: absolute coordinates often lead to singularities; unit quaternions or Lie group methods offer singularity-free integration, but are difficult to integrate into existing MBS codes.
AI.method: Develops a bridging framework that (i) represents rigid body motions with direct/semi-direct product groups (SO(3)×R^3 and SE(3)); (ii) introduces local coordinates on the Lie group and a local-global transition (LGT) map to update global absolute coordinates from Lie group coordinates; (iii) enables existing vector-space EOM formulations to be augmented with Lie group integration schemes without a full code overhaul; (iv) emphasizes the customizability of the LGT map to chosen absolute coordinates and Lie groups.
AI.result: A practical interface that allows Lie group integration to be used within standard MBS codes by consistently incorporating the geometry of rigid-body motions; preserves geometric structure of motions during integration and avoids full reimplementation of EOM while enabling singularity-free, geometry-respecting integration.
AI.conclusion: The LGT-based framework effectively bridges Lie group methods and conventional MBS modeling, enabling geometry-preserving, singularity-free integration in absolute-coordinate formulations; the key is the customizable local-global transition that connects local Lie-group coordinates with global absolute coordinates.

title: Beyond a Single Reference: Training and Evaluation with Paraphrases in Sign Language Translation
summary: Most Sign Language Translation (SLT) corpora pair each signed utterance with a single written-language reference, despite the highly non-isomorphic relationship between sign and spoken languages, where multiple translations can be equally valid. This limitation constrains both model training and evaluation, particularly for n-gram-based metrics such as BLEU. In this work, we investigate the use of Large Language Models to automatically generate paraphrased variants of written-language translations as synthetic alternative references for SLT. First, we compare multiple paraphrasing strategies and models using an adapted ParaScore metric. Second, we study the impact of paraphrases on both training and evaluation of the pose-based T5 model on the YouTubeASL and How2Sign datasets. Our results show that naively incorporating paraphrases during training does not improve translation performance and can even be detrimental. In contrast, using paraphrases during evaluation leads to higher automatic scores and better alignment with human judgments. To formalize this observation, we introduce BLEUpara, an extension of BLEU that evaluates translations against multiple paraphrased references. Human evaluation confirms that BLEUpara correlates more strongly with perceived translation quality. We release all generated paraphrases, generation and evaluation code to support reproducible and more reliable evaluation of SLT systems.

AI.tldr: Paraphrase-based multi-reference evaluation for SLT improves evaluation metrics; training with paraphrases not beneficial; BLEUpara proposed.
AI.motivation: Address non-isomorphic sign-spoken translation and single-reference bias; improve SLT training/evaluation and n-gram metrics like BLEU.
AI.method: Compare paraphrase strategies with adapted ParaScore; test paraphrases in training vs evaluation of pose-based T5 on YouTubeASL/How2Sign; propose BLEUpara; conduct human evaluation; release resources.
AI.result: Paraphrases during training can harm; evaluation-time paraphrases improve automatic metrics and align with human judgments; BLEUpara correlates better with human judgments; all paraphrases and code released.
AI.conclusion: Paraphrase-aware evaluation (BLEUpara) is beneficial; multi-reference references help SLT evaluation; training with paraphrases is not straightforward and may hurt; reproducibility through released materials.

title: The Depth Delusion: Why Transformers Should Be Wider, Not Deeper
summary: Neural scaling laws describe how language model loss decreases with parameters and data, but treat architecture as interchangeable--a billion parameters could arise from a shallow-wide model (10 layers & 8,192 hidden dimension) or a deep-narrow one (80 layers & 2,048 hidden dimension). We propose architecture-conditioned scaling laws decomposing this dependence, finding that optimal depth scales as D* ~ C^0.12 while optimal width scales as W* ~ C^0.34, meaning width should grow 2.8x faster than depth. We discover a critical depth phenomenon: beyond D_crit ~ W^0.44 (sublinear in W), adding layers increases loss despite adding parameters--the Depth Delusion. Empirically, we validate these findings across 30 transformer architectures spanning 17M to 7B parameters, each trained on representative high-compute samples, achieving R^2 = 0.922. Our central finding: at 7B scale, a 64-layer model (6.38B params) underperforms a 32-layer model (6.86B params) by 0.12 nats, despite being significantly deeper. This demonstrates that optimal depth-width tradeoffs persist at the production scale.

AI.tldr: Architecture-conditioned scaling laws for transformers: optimal depth and width scale sublinearly with compute, with width scaling faster than depth; a critical depth exists (Depth Delusion) where adding layers beyond D_crit increases loss; validated across 30 architectures (17M–7B parameters) with R^2=0.922, showing production-scale depth-width tradeoffs persist (e.g., at ~7B, 64-layer underperforms 32-layer by 0.12 nats).
AI.motivation: Standard neural scaling laws treat architecture as interchangeable, obscuring how depth and width tradeoffs interact with compute. This work aims to quantify how optimal depth and width depend on compute budget and to identify regimes where deeper networks hurt performance.
AI.method: Derive architecture-conditioned scaling laws by decomposing loss as a function of parameters, data, and architecture. Fit power-law dependencies D* ~ C^0.12 and W* ~ C^0.34 on compute C; identify D_crit ~ W^0.44; perform empirical validation across 30 transformer architectures (17M–7B params) trained on representative high-compute samples; report R^2=0.922.
AI.result: Found that optimal depth scales sublinearly with compute (D* ~ C^0.12) and optimal width scales more steeply (W* ~ C^0.34), implying width grows ~2.8× faster than depth with compute. A critical depth D_crit ~ W^0.44 exists, beyond which adding layers increases loss (Depth Delusion). Across 30 architectures, the model fits explain 92.2% of variance in loss. At ~7B scale, a 64-layer model (6.38B params) underperforms a 32-layer model (6.86B params) by 0.12 nats, showing depth-width tradeoffs persist at production scale.
AI.conclusion: Architecture-aware scaling laws reveal non-monotonic depth effects and a sublinear depth-growth regime. For practical model design, depth and width must be jointly optimized; simply adding layers at fixed width can degrade performance beyond a critical depth. The findings advocate depth-width-aware architecture search and careful consideration of compute budgets when scaling transformers.

title: Hypersolid: Emergent Vision Representations via Short-Range Repulsion
summary: A recurring challenge in self-supervised learning is preventing representation collapse. Existing solutions typically rely on global regularization, such as maximizing distances, decorrelating dimensions or enforcing certain distributions. We instead reinterpret representation learning as a discrete packing problem, where preserving information simplifies to maintaining injectivity. We operationalize this in Hypersolid, a method using short-range hard-ball repulsion to prevent local collisions. This constraint results in a high-separation geometric regime that preserves augmentation diversity, excelling on fine-grained and low-resolution classification tasks.

AI.tldr: Reframes self-supervised representation learning as a discrete packing/injectivity problem and avoids global regularization by using short-range hard-ball repulsion (Hypersolid) to prevent local collisions, yielding high-separation representations that preserve augmentation diversity and improve fine-grained/low-resolution classification.
AI.motivation: Prevent representation collapse in self-supervised learning without relying on global regularization; propose a discrete geometric perspective to preserve information via injectivity.
AI.method: Introduce Hypersolid, a method implementing short-range hard-ball repulsion to enforce non-colliding representations, creating a high-separation geometric regime that maintains augmentation diversity.
AI.result: The approach achieves high separation in the representation space, preserves augmentation diversity, and excels on fine-grained and low-resolution classification tasks.
AI.conclusion: A novel discrete packing viewpoint offers an alternative to global regularization for preventing collapse, yielding robust representations particularly for fine-grained and low-resolution tasks.

title: Spotlighting Task-Relevant Features: Object-Centric Representations for Better Generalization in Robotic Manipulation
summary: The generalization capabilities of robotic manipulation policies are heavily influenced by the choice of visual representations. Existing approaches typically rely on representations extracted from pre-trained encoders, using two dominant types of features: global features, which summarize an entire image via a single pooled vector, and dense features, which preserve a patch-wise embedding from the final encoder layer. While widely used, both feature types mix task-relevant and irrelevant information, leading to poor generalization under distribution shifts, such as changes in lighting, textures, or the presence of distractors. In this work, we explore an intermediate structured alternative: Slot-Based Object-Centric Representations (SBOCR), which group dense features into a finite set of object-like entities. This representation permits to naturally reduce the noise provided to the robotic manipulation policy while keeping enough information to efficiently perform the task. We benchmark a range of global and dense representations against intermediate slot-based representations, across a suite of simulated and real-world manipulation tasks ranging from simple to complex. We evaluate their generalization under diverse visual conditions, including changes in lighting, texture, and the presence of distractors. Our findings reveal that SBOCR-based policies outperform dense and global representation-based policies in generalization settings, even without task-specific pretraining. These insights suggest that SBOCR is a promising direction for designing visual systems that generalize effectively in dynamic, real-world robotic environments.

AI.tldr: SBOCR (Slot-Based Object-Centric Representations) offer an intermediate, structured representation that groups dense features into a fixed set of object-like slots, reducing noise and improving generalization for robotic manipulation under visual distribution shifts, outperforming global and dense representations even without task-specific pretraining.
AI.motivation: Improve generalization of robotic manipulation policies by reducing irrelevant visual information and preserving task-relevant structure through object-centric representations.
AI.method: Compare global, dense, and slot-based representations on simulated and real-world manipulation tasks with varying visual conditions (lighting, texture, distractors). Evaluate generalization performance across tasks, with ablations to assess the benefit of the slot-based intermediate representation.
AI.result: Slot-based object-centric representations (SBOCR) outperform both global and dense representations in generalization settings across diverse visual perturbations, even without task-specific pretraining. They reduce noise and retain task-relevant information, offering a promising direction for robust visual systems in dynamic robotic environments.
AI.conclusion: SBOCR is a viable and effective intermediate representation for robotic manipulation that generalizes better under distribution shifts than traditional global or dense features, suggesting broader adoption and further research into object-centric slot representations.

title: What You Feel Is Not What They See: On Predicting Self-Reported Emotion from Third-Party Observer Labels
comment: ICASSP 2026-2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
summary: Self-reported emotion labels capture internal experience, while third-party labels reflect external perception. These perspectives often diverge, limiting the applicability of third-party-trained models to self-report contexts. This gap is critical in mental health, where accurate self-report modeling is essential for guiding intervention. We present the first cross-corpus evaluation of third-party-trained models on self-reports. We find activation unpredictable (CCC approximately 0) and valence moderately predictable (CCC approximately 0.3). Crucially, when content is personally significant to the speaker, models achieve high performance for valence (CCC approximately 0.6-0.8). Our findings point to personal significance as a key pathway for aligning external perception with internal experience and underscore the challenge of self-report activation modeling.

AI.tldr: Third-party-trained models struggle to predict self-reported activation; moderate success for valence; personal significance boosts valence predictions; cross-corpus evaluation highlights gap between external perception and internal experience in mental health.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between self-report and third-party labeling, which hampers the transferability of emotion models to self-report contexts and has critical implications for mental health interventions.
AI.method: Cross-corpus evaluation of third-party-trained emotion models on self-reports; computing concordance correlation coefficient (CCC) for activation and valence; analysis of content significance on model performance.
AI.result: Activation predictions are largely unreliable (CCC ~ 0); valence predictions modestly reliable (CCC ~ 0.3); when content is personally significant, valence predictions are substantially improved (CCC ~ 0.6–0.8).
AI.conclusion: Personal significance appears to be a key factor enabling alignment between external perceptual labels and internal self-reports; however, self-report activation remains difficult to model from third-party data.

title: MADE: Benchmark Environments for Closed-Loop Materials Discovery
