summary: Existing benchmarks for computational materials discovery primarily evaluate static predictive tasks or isolated computational sub-tasks. While valuable, these evaluations neglect the inherently iterative and adaptive nature of scientific discovery. We introduce MAterials Discovery Environments (MADE), a novel framework for benchmarking end-to-end autonomous materials discovery pipelines. MADE simulates closed-loop discovery campaigns in which an agent or algorithm proposes, evaluates, and refines candidate materials under a constrained oracle budget, capturing the sequential and resource-limited nature of real discovery workflows. We formalize discovery as a search for thermodynamically stable compounds relative to a given convex hull, and evaluate efficacy and efficiency via comparison to baseline algorithms. The framework is flexible; users can compose discovery agents from interchangeable components such as generative models, filters, and planners, enabling the study of arbitrary workflows ranging from fixed pipelines to fully agentic systems with tool use and adaptive decision making. We demonstrate this by conducting systematic experiments across a family of systems, enabling ablation of components in discovery pipelines, and comparison of how methods scale with system complexity.

AI.tldr: End-to-end benchmarking framework for autonomous materials discovery; MADE simulates closed-loop campaigns with budget-constrained agents and modular components.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on static tasks and isolated sub-tasks, failing to capture iterative, resource-limited discovery processes.
AI.method: Formalizes discovery as thermodynamic stability search relative to a convex hull; simulates agent proposals, evaluations, and refinements within an oracle budget; supports modular workflows with generative models, filters, and planners; provides ablation and scaling experiments across system families.
AI.result: Demonstrates flexibility and utility of MADE across multiple systems, enabling ablation studies and analysis of how methods scale with system complexity.
AI.conclusion: MADE offers flexible, end-to-end benchmarking for autonomous materials discovery pipelines, enabling systematic study of efficiency, scalability, and component contributions from fixed pipelines to fully agentic, tool-using workflows.

title: Lightweight High-Fidelity Low-Bitrate Talking Face Compression for 3D Video Conference
summary: The demand for immersive and interactive communication has driven advancements in 3D video conferencing, yet achieving high-fidelity 3D talking face representation at low bitrates remains a challenge. Traditional 2D video compression techniques fail to preserve fine-grained geometric and appearance details, while implicit neural rendering methods like NeRF suffer from prohibitive computational costs. To address these challenges, we propose a lightweight, high-fidelity, low-bitrate 3D talking face compression framework that integrates FLAME-based parametric modeling with 3DGS neural rendering. Our approach transmits only essential facial metadata in real time, enabling efficient reconstruction with a Gaussian-based head model. Additionally, we introduce a compact representation and compression scheme, including Gaussian attribute compression and MLP optimization, to enhance transmission efficiency. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves superior rate-distortion performance, delivering high-quality facial rendering at extremely low bitrates, making it well-suited for real-time 3D video conferencing applications.

AI.tldr: Lightweight, high-fidelity 3D talking-face compression at very low bitrates using FLAME-based parametric modeling and 3DGS rendering with Gaussian attribute compression and MLP optimization.
AI.motivation: Need for real-time, high-quality 3D talking faces at low bitrate; 2D compression loses geometry; NeRF-like methods are too computationally heavy; a compact, parametric approach with neural rendering aims to reduce bitrate while preserving geometry and appearance.
AI.method: Integrates FLAME-based parametric facial modeling with 3DGS neural rendering; transmits only essential facial metadata in real time; uses a Gaussian-based head model; introduces compact representation including Gaussian attribute compression and MLP optimization to improve transmission efficiency.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate superior rate-distortion performance, delivering high-quality facial rendering at extremely low bitrates suitable for real-time 3D video conferencing.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a practical, efficient framework for low-bitrate, high-fidelity 3D talking faces; demonstrates potential deployment in real-time communication; future work could explore robustness, generalization, and optimization of the compression scheme.

title: Nimbus: A Unified Embodied Synthetic Data Generation Framework
summary: Scaling data volume and diversity is critical for generalizing embodied intelligence. While synthetic data generation offers a scalable alternative to expensive physical data acquisition, existing pipelines remain fragmented and task-specific. This isolation leads to significant engineering inefficiency and system instability, failing to support the sustained, high-throughput data generation required for foundation model training. To address these challenges, we present Nimbus, a unified synthetic data generation framework designed to integrate heterogeneous navigation and manipulation pipelines. Nimbus introduces a modular four-layer architecture featuring a decoupled execution model that separates trajectory planning, rendering, and storage into asynchronous stages. By implementing dynamic pipeline scheduling, global load balancing, distributed fault tolerance, and backend-specific rendering optimizations, the system maximizes resource utilization across CPU, GPU, and I/O resources. Our evaluation demonstrates that Nimbus achieves a 2-3X improvement in end-to-end throughput compared to unoptimized baselines and ensuring robust, long-term operation in large-scale distributed environments. This framework serves as the production backbone for the InternData suite, enabling seamless cross-domain data synthesis.

AI.tldr: Nimbus is a unified, four-layer synthetic data pipeline that decouples trajectory planning, rendering, and storage into asynchronous stages, enabling dynamic scheduling, load balancing, and fault-tolerant distributed execution to accelerate large-scale data synthesis for embodied intelligence.
AI.motivation: Scaling data for embodied intelligence requires high-throughput, diverse synthetic data. Current pipelines are fragmented and task-specific, causing inefficiency and instability, hindering foundation-model training.
AI.method: Nimbus introduces a modular four-layer architecture with a decoupled execution model. It separates trajectory planning, rendering, and storage into asynchronous stages, uses dynamic pipeline scheduling, global load balancing, distributed fault tolerance, and backend-specific rendering optimizations to maximize CPU/GPU/I/O resource utilization across heterogeneous pipelines.
AI.result: Nimbus achieves a 2–3× improvement in end-to-end throughput over unoptimized baselines and supports robust, long-term operation in large-scale distributed environments; it underpins the production backbone for the InternData suite enabling cross-domain data synthesis.
AI.conclusion: Nimbus provides a scalable, robust framework for unified synthetic data generation, enabling high-throughput, cross-domain data synthesis and serving as a production backbone for large-scale embodied AI data pipelines.

title: BrainStack: Neuro-MoE with Functionally Guided Expert Routing for EEG-Based Language Decoding
summary: Decoding linguistic information from electroencephalography (EEG) remains challenging due to the brain's distributed and nonlinear organization. We present BrainStack, a functionally guided neuro-mixture-of-experts (Neuro-MoE) framework that models the brain's modular functional architecture through anatomically partitioned expert networks. Each functional region is represented by a specialized expert that learns localized neural dynamics, while a transformer-based global expert captures cross-regional dependencies. A learnable routing gate adaptively aggregates these heterogeneous experts, enabling context-dependent expert coordination and selective fusion. To promote coherent representation across the hierarchy, we introduce cross-regional distillation, where the global expert provides top-down regularization to the regional experts. We further release SilentSpeech-EEG (SS-EEG), a large-scale benchmark comprising over 120 hours of EEG recordings from 12 subjects performing 24 silent words, the largest dataset of its kind. Experiments demonstrate that BrainStack consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models, achieving superior accuracy and generalization across subjects. Our results establish BrainStack as a functionally modular, neuro-inspired MoE paradigm that unifies neuroscientific priors with adaptive expert routing, paving the way for scalable and interpretable brain-language decoding.

AI.tldr: BrainStack is a functionally guided Neuro-MoE model that uses anatomically partitioned experts per brain region plus a transformer global expert, with learnable routing and cross-regional distillation; paired with SilentSpeech-EEG as a large EEG benchmark for silent speech decoding, achieving superior cross-subject performance vs state-of-the-art.
AI.motivation: EEG-based language decoding is difficult due to distributed, nonlinear brain organization and inter-subject variability; a modular, neuro-inspired modeling approach could better capture region-specific dynamics and cross-region interactions while improving generalization.
AI.method: Propose BrainStack: functionally guided neuro-MoE with region-specific experts representing anatomical partitions, a transformer-based global expert for cross-regional dependencies, and a learnable routing gate for adaptive integration. Introduce cross-regional distillation where the global expert regularizes regional experts. Release SilentSpeech-EEG (SS-EEG): 120+ hours of EEG from 12 subjects performing 24 silent words. Evaluate against state-of-the-art models, focusing on accuracy and generalization across subjects.
AI.result: BrainStack outperforms state-of-the-art models, achieving higher accuracy and better cross-subject generalization on EEG-based silent word decoding; demonstrates improved coherence across hierarchical representations via cross-regional distillation and adaptive routing.
AI.conclusion: A functionally modular, neuro-inspired MoE framework effectively unifies neuroscientific priors with adaptive routing for scalable, interpretable brain-language decoding; the SS-EEG benchmark provides a valuable resource for future research.

title: Solver-in-the-Loop: MDP-Based Benchmarks for Self-Correction and Behavioral Rationality in Operations Research
summary: Operations Research practitioners routinely debug infeasible models through an iterative process: analyzing Irreducible Infeasible Subsystems (\IIS{}), identifying constraint conflicts, and systematically repairing formulations until feasibility is achieved. Yet existing LLM benchmarks evaluate OR as one-shot translation -- given a problem description, generate solver code -- ignoring this diagnostic loop entirely. We introduce two benchmarks that place the \textbf{solver in the evaluation loop}. \textbf{\ORDebug{}} evaluates iterative self-correction through 5,000+ problems spanning 9 error types; each repair action triggers solver re-execution and \IIS{} recomputation, providing deterministic, verifiable feedback. \textbf{\ORBias{}} evaluates behavioral rationality through 2,000 newsvendor instances (1,000 ID + 1,000 OOD), measuring systematic deviations from closed-form optimal policies. Across 26 models and 12,000+ samples, we find that domain-specific RLVR training enables an 8B model to surpass frontier APIs: 95.3\% vs 86.2\% recovery rate (+9.1\%), 62.4\% vs 47.8\% diagnostic accuracy (+14.6\%), and 2.25 vs 3.78 steps to resolution (1.7$\times$ faster). On \ORBias{}, curriculum training achieves the only negative ID$\rightarrow$OOD bias drift among models evaluated (-9.6\%), reducing systematic bias by 48\% (from 20.0\% to 10.4\%). These results demonstrate that process-level evaluation with verifiable oracles enables targeted training that outperforms scale.

AI.tldr: Process-level evaluation benchmarks for OR with verifiable feedback; ORD Debug emphasizes iterative self-correction with IIS and solver re-execution; ORBias assesses behavioral rationality; results show domain-specific RLVR training improves performance and reduces bias, outperforming larger frontier APIs.
AI.motivation: Current LLM benchmarks for Operations Research largely rely on one-shot problem-to-code translation and do not capture the diagnostic loop used by practitioners to debug infeasible models. There is a need for verifiable, looped evaluation with oracle feedback to train models that can diagnose and repair formulations.
AI.method: ORDebug: 5,000+ problems spanning 9 error types; iterative repairs trigger solver re-execution and IIS recomputation for deterministic feedback. ORBias: 2,000 newsvendor instances (1,000 in-distribution, 1,000 out-of-distribution); 26 models evaluated over 12,000+ samples. Training uses domain-specific RLVR with curriculum strategies. Metrics include recovery rate, diagnostic accuracy, steps to resolution, and ID→OOD bias drift.
AI.result: For ORD Debug, an 8B model surpasses frontier APIs: 95.3% recovery vs 86.2% ( +9.1 points ), 62.4% diagnostic accuracy vs 47.8% ( +14.6 points ), and 2.25 vs 3.78 steps to resolution (1.7× faster). For ORBias, curriculum training achieves the only negative ID→OOD bias drift among evaluated models (−9.6%), reducing systematic bias from 20.0% to 10.4% (48% reduction). Overall, process-level evaluation with verifiable oracles enables targeted training that outperforms scaling.
AI.conclusion: Process-level evaluation with verifiable oracles can guide targeted training that yields accuracy and efficiency gains beyond scaling up model size, and can simultaneously reduce distributional biases in decision policies.

title: GeoRC: A Benchmark for Geolocation Reasoning Chains
summary: Vision Language Models (VLMs) are good at recognizing the global location of a photograph -- their geolocation prediction accuracy rivals the best human experts. But many VLMs are startlingly bad at explaining which image evidence led to their prediction, even when their location prediction is correct. The reasoning chains produced by VLMs frequently hallucinate scene attributes to support their location prediction (e.g. phantom writing, imagined infrastructure, misidentified flora). In this paper, we introduce the first benchmark for geolocation reasoning chains. We focus on the global location prediction task in the popular GeoGuessr game which draws from Google Street View spanning more than 100 countries. We collaborate with expert GeoGuessr players, including the reigning world champion, to produce 800 ground truth reasoning chains for 500 query scenes. These expert reasoning chains address hundreds of different discriminative visual attributes such as license plate shape, architecture, and soil properties to name just a few. We evaluate LLM-as-a-judge and VLM-as-a-judge strategies for scoring VLM-generated reasoning chains against our expert reasoning chains and find that Qwen 3 LLM-as-a-judge correlates best with human scoring. Our benchmark reveals that while large, closed-source VLMs such as Gemini and GPT 5 rival human experts at prediction locations, they still lag behind human experts when it comes to producing auditable reasoning chains. Open weights VLMs such as Llama and Qwen catastrophically fail on our benchmark -- they perform only slightly better than a baseline in which an LLM hallucinates a reasoning chain with oracle knowledge of the photo location but no visual information at all. We believe the gap between human experts and VLMs on this task points to VLM limitations at extracting fine-grained visual attributes from high resolution images.

AI.tldr: Large geolocation-capable VLMs still struggle to produce auditable, fine-grained reasoning chains; a GeoGuessr-based benchmark reveals gaps in reasoning transparency even when location prediction is strong.
AI.motivation: Evaluate and quantify the quality and audibility of reasoning chains accompanying geolocation predictions, addressing hallucinations and interpretability in VLMs.
AI.method: Build a ground-truth benchmark with 800 expert-annotated reasoning chains for 500 GeoGuessr scenes; collaborate with expert players; assess VLM-generated chains using LLM-as-a-judge and VLM-as-a-judge setups; compare against expert chains and human scores.
AI.result: Qwen 3 LLM-as-a-judge best correlates with human scoring; large closed-source VLMs (Gemini, GPT-5) rival humans in location accuracy but lag in auditable reasoning chains; open-weight VLMs (Llama, Qwen) perform only marginally better than a chain hallucination baseline with oracle location but no visual input; reveals limitations in extracting fine-grained visual attributes from high-res images.
AI.conclusion: There is a persistent gap between geolocation accuracy and the ability to generate trustworthy, auditable reasoning chains; findings point to limitations in VLMs' capability to extract and articulate fine-grained visual evidence, motivating future work on explainable vision-language models.

title: 4D-CAAL: 4D Radar-Camera Calibration and Auto-Labeling for Autonomous Driving
summary: 4D radar has emerged as a critical sensor for autonomous driving, primarily due to its enhanced capabilities in elevation measurement and higher resolution compared to traditional 3D radar. Effective integration of 4D radar with cameras requires accurate extrinsic calibration, and the development of radar-based perception algorithms demands large-scale annotated datasets. However, existing calibration methods often employ separate targets optimized for either visual or radar modalities, complicating correspondence establishment. Furthermore, manually labeling sparse radar data is labor-intensive and unreliable. To address these challenges, we propose 4D-CAAL, a unified framework for 4D radar-camera calibration and auto-labeling. Our approach introduces a novel dual-purpose calibration target design, integrating a checkerboard pattern on the front surface for camera detection and a corner reflector at the center of the back surface for radar detection. We develop a robust correspondence matching algorithm that aligns the checkerboard center with the strongest radar reflection point, enabling accurate extrinsic calibration. Subsequently, we present an auto-labeling pipeline that leverages the calibrated sensor relationship to transfer annotations from camera-based segmentations to radar point clouds through geometric projection and multi-feature optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves high calibration accuracy while significantly reducing manual annotation effort, thereby accelerating the development of robust multi-modal perception systems for autonomous driving.

AI.tldr: A unified 4D radar–camera calibration and auto-labeling framework (4D-CAAL) uses a dual‑purpose target (front checkerboard for camera, back corner reflector for radar) to establish correspondences by aligning the checkerboard center with the strongest radar reflection, enabling accurate extrinsic calibration. It then auto-labels radar point clouds by transferring camera-based segmentations through geometric projection and multi‑feature optimization, significantly reducing manual labeling while maintaining calibration accuracy.
AI.motivation: Calibrating 4D radar with cameras is challenging due to modality-specific targets and labor-intensive labeling of radar data. A unified framework can improve calibration accuracy and greatly reduce annotation effort, accelerating multi-modal perception development for autonomous driving.
AI.method: Introduce a dual-purpose calibration target: a checkerboard pattern on the front for camera detection and a corner reflector at the center of the back surface for radar detection. Develop a robust correspondence matching algorithm that pairs the checkerboard center with the strongest radar reflection point, enabling precise extrinsic calibration. Build an auto-labeling pipeline that, using the calibrated sensor geometry, transfers camera-based segmentations to radar point clouds via geometric projection and multi-feature optimization to refine alignment.
AI.result: Empirical results show high calibration accuracy and substantial reduction in manual labeling effort, demonstrating the practicality and scalability of the proposed framework for multi-modal perception in autonomous driving.
AI.conclusion: 4D-CAAL provides a practical, unified solution for simultaneous extrinsic calibration and auto-labeling of 4D radar–camera systems, enhancing data quality and accelerating development of robust multi-modal perception pipelines.

title: Bridging the Arithmetic Gap: The Cognitive Complexity Benchmark and Financial-PoT for Robust Financial Reasoning
summary: While Large Language Models excel at semantic tasks, they face a critical bottleneck in financial quantitative reasoning, frequently suffering from "Arithmetic Hallucinations" and a systemic failure mode we term "Cognitive Collapse". To strictly quantify this phenomenon, we introduce the Cognitive Complexity Benchmark (CCB), a robust evaluation framework grounded in a dataset constructed from 95 real-world Chinese A-share annual reports. Unlike traditional datasets, the CCB stratifies financial queries into a three-dimensional taxonomy, Data Source, Mapping Difficulty, and Result Unit, enabling the precise diagnosis of reasoning degradation in high-cognitive-load scenarios. To address these failures, we propose the Iterative Dual-Phase Financial-PoT framework. This neuro-symbolic architecture enforces a strict architectural decoupling: it first isolates semantic variable extraction and logic formulation, then offloads computation to an iterative, self-correcting Python sandbox to ensure deterministic execution. Evaluation on the CCB demonstrates that while standard Chain-of-Thought falters on complex tasks, our approach offers superior robustness, elevating the Qwen3-235B model's average accuracy from 59.7\% to 67.3\% and achieving gains of up to 10-fold in high-complexity reasoning tasks. These findings suggest that architectural decoupling is a critical enabling factor for improving reliability in financial reasoning tasks, providing a transferable architectural insight for precision-critical domains that require tight alignment between semantic understanding and quantitative computation.

AI.tldr: Proposes Cognitive Complexity Benchmark and a neuro-symbolic Iterative Dual-Phase Financial-PoT to improve financial reasoning robustness in LLMs; demonstrates performance gains on Qwen3-235B.
AI.motivation: Addresses arithmetic hallucinations and cognitive collapse in financial quantitative reasoning; need robust evaluation and reliable computation.
AI.method: Constructs CCB dataset from 95 real-world Chinese A-share reports, with three-dimensional taxonomy; introduces dual-phase architecture with semantic extraction/logic formulation separated from computation in an iterative sandbox.
AI.result: CCB reveals CoT fails on high-complexity tasks; using the proposed framework improves model accuracy from 59.7% to 67.3% and up to 10x in high complexity tasks.
AI.conclusion: Architectural decoupling between understanding and computation is key for reliability in precision-critical domains and is transferable beyond finance.

title: Order-Aware Test-Time Adaptation: Leveraging Temporal Dynamics for Robust Streaming Inference
summary: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) enables pre-trained models to adjust to distribution shift by learning from unlabeled test-time streams. However, existing methods typically treat these streams as independent samples, overlooking the supervisory signal inherent in temporal dynamics. To address this, we introduce Order-Aware Test-Time Adaptation (OATTA). We formulate test-time adaptation as a gradient-free recursive Bayesian estimation task, using a learned dynamic transition matrix as a temporal prior to refine the base model's predictions. To ensure safety in weakly structured streams, we introduce a likelihood-ratio gate (LLR) that reverts to the base predictor when temporal evidence is absent. OATTA is a lightweight, model-agnostic module that incurs negligible computational overhead. Extensive experiments across image classification, wearable and physiological signal analysis, and language sentiment analysis demonstrate its universality; OATTA consistently boosts established baselines, improving accuracy by up to 6.35%. Our findings establish that modeling temporal dynamics provides a critical, orthogonal signal beyond standard order-agnostic TTA approaches.

AI.tldr: OATTA introduces an order-aware test-time adaptation framework using gradient-free recursive Bayesian estimation with a learned temporal prior (transition matrix) and a likelihood-ratio gate to safely incorporate temporal dynamics into predictions, yielding up to 6.35% accuracy gains across diverse domains.
AI.motivation: To exploit temporal information in test streams, addressing the limitation of treating samples as independent in standard TTA.
AI.method: Gradient-free recursive Bayesian estimation; learned dynamic transition matrix as temporal prior; likelihood-ratio gate to revert to base predictor when temporal evidence is absent; lightweight, model-agnostic.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over baselines across image, wearable/physiological signals, and language sentiment; up to 6.35% accuracy gains; demonstrates universality.
AI.conclusion: Temporal dynamics provide complementary signal to order-agnostic TTA; OATTA offers universal, lightweight adaptation with safety mechanism; encourages integrating temporal priors in TTA.

title: Token Entropy Regularization for Multi-modal Antenna Affiliation Identification
summary: Accurate antenna affiliation identification is crucial for optimizing and maintaining communication networks. Current practice, however, relies on the cumbersome and error-prone process of manual tower inspections. We propose a novel paradigm shift that fuses video footage of base stations, antenna geometric features, and Physical Cell Identity (PCI) signals, transforming antenna affiliation identification into multi-modal classification and matching tasks. Publicly available pretrained transformers struggle with this unique task due to a lack of analogous data in the communications domain, which hampers cross-modal alignment. To address this, we introduce a dedicated training framework that aligns antenna images with corresponding PCI signals. To tackle the representation alignment challenge, we propose a novel Token Entropy Regularization module in the pretraining stage. Our experiments demonstrate that TER accelerates convergence and yields significant performance gains. Further analysis reveals that the entropy of the first token is modality-dependent. Code will be made available upon publication.

AI.tldr: Multi-modal framework fusing base-station video, antenna geometry, and PCI signals for automatic antenna affiliation; introduces Token Entropy Regularization (TER) to align representations and improve pretraining.
AI.motivation: Manual tower inspections are costly and error-prone. Domain-specific data for cross-modal transformers is scarce, hindering effective alignment across video, geometry, and PCI signals.
AI.method: Fuse video footage, antenna geometric features, and PCI signals into a cross-modal classification/matching framework. Introduce a dedicated training regime that aligns antenna images with PCI signals, and a Token Entropy Regularization (TER) module during pretraining to promote representation alignment across modalities.
AI.result: TER accelerates convergence and yields meaningful performance gains. Analysis indicates the first token's entropy is modality-dependent. Code will be released after publication.
AI.conclusion: The TER-based cross-modal pretraining coupled with an antenna–PCI alignment framework effectively improves automatic antenna affiliation identification and demonstrates the viability of modality-aware entropy regularization for cross-modal tasks.

title: DexTac: Learning Contact-aware Visuotactile Policies via Hand-by-hand Teaching
summary: For contact-intensive tasks, the ability to generate policies that produce comprehensive tactile-aware motions is essential. However, existing data collection and skill learning systems for dexterous manipulation often suffer from low-dimensional tactile information. To address this limitation, we propose DexTac, a visuo-tactile manipulation learning framework based on kinesthetic teaching. DexTac captures multi-dimensional tactile data-including contact force distributions and spatial contact regions-directly from human demonstrations. By integrating these rich tactile modalities into a policy network, the resulting contact-aware agent enables a dexterous hand to autonomously select and maintain optimal contact regions during complex interactions. We evaluate our framework on a challenging unimanual injection task. Experimental results demonstrate that DexTac achieves a 91.67% success rate. Notably, in high-precision scenarios involving small-scale syringes, our approach outperforms force-only baselines by 31.67%. These results underscore that learning multi-dimensional tactile priors from human demonstrations is critical for achieving robust, human-like dexterous manipulation in contact-rich environments.

AI.tldr: DexTac presents a kinesthetically taught visuo-tactile manipulation framework that collects rich tactile data (contact force distributions and spatial contact regions) to train a policy for dexterous, contact-aware manipulation. Evaluated on a unimanual injection task, it achieves 91.67% success and outperforms force-only baselines by 31.67% in high-precision syringe tasks, highlighting the value of multi-dimensional tactile priors.
AI.motivation: Current data collection and skill-learning systems often rely on low-dimensional tactile signals, limiting the ability to learn robust, contact-rich manipulation policies. There is a need to leverage richer tactile information to enable dexterous hands to select and maintain optimal contact during interactions.
AI.method: DexTac integrates kinesthetic demonstrations with visuo-tactile data collection, capturing multi-dimensional tactile information (contact force distributions and spatial contact regions) and incorporating it into a policy network. The agent learns to autonomously select and maintain optimal contact regions during complex contact-rich tasks, demonstrated on a challenging unimanual injection task.
AI.result: Achieves 91.67% success on the injection task; in high-precision scenarios with small-scale syringes, outperforms force-only baselines by 31.67%.
AI.conclusion: Learning multi-dimensional tactile priors from human demonstrations is critical for robust, human-like dexterous manipulation in contact-rich environments.

title: Concise Geometric Description as a Bridge: Unleashing the Potential of LLM for Plane Geometry Problem Solving
summary: Plane Geometry Problem Solving (PGPS) is a multimodal reasoning task that aims to solve a plane geometric problem based on a geometric diagram and problem textual descriptions. Although Large Language Models (LLMs) possess strong reasoning skills, their direct application to PGPS is hindered by their inability to process visual diagrams. Existing works typically fine-tune Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) end-to-end on large-scale PGPS data to enhance visual understanding and reasoning simultaneously. However, such joint optimization may compromise base LLMs' inherent reasoning capability. In this work, we observe that LLM itself is potentially a powerful PGPS solver when appropriately formulating visual information as textual descriptions. We propose to train a MLLM Interpreter to generate geometric descriptions for the visual diagram, and an off-the-shelf LLM is utilized to perform reasoning. Specifically, we choose Conditional Declaration Language (CDL) as the geometric description as its conciseness eases the MLLM Interpreter training. The MLLM Interpreter is fine-tuned via CoT (Chain-of-Thought)-augmented SFT followed by GRPO to generate CDL. Instead of using a conventional solution-based reward that compares the reasoning result with the ground-truth answer, we design CDL matching rewards to facilitate more effective GRPO training, which provides more direct and denser guidance for CDL generation. To support training, we construct a new dataset, Formalgeo7k-Rec-CoT, by manually reviewing Formalgeo7k v2 and incorporating CoT annotations. Extensive experiments on Formalgeo7k-Rec-CoT, Unigeo, and MathVista show our method (finetuned on only 5.5k data) performs favorably against leading open-source and closed-source MLLMs.

AI.tldr: CDL-based textual diagram descriptions are generated by a fine-tuned MLLM Interpreter to enable off-the-shelf LLMs to solve plane geometry problems, achieving strong performance with ~5.5k data via CoT-SFT and GRPO, validated on multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: PGPS requires visual understanding; pure LLMs cannot parse diagrams. End-to-end MLLM fine-tuning can degrade base LLM reasoning. If visual info is reformulated as text, LLMs can leverage their reasoning capacity.
AI.method: Use Conditional Declaration Language (CDL) as concise geometric descriptions. Train an MLLM Interpreter with Chain-of-Thought augmented supervised fine-tuning (CoT-SFT) followed by GRPO to produce CDL. Employ CDL-matching rewards rather than solution-based rewards for GRPO. Build Formalgeo7k-Rec-CoT by augmenting Formalgeo7k v2 with CoT annotations. Evaluate on Formalgeo7k-Rec-CoT, Unigeo, and MathVista.
AI.result: The approach achieves favorable performance against leading open-source and closed-source MLLMs, despite fine-tuning on only 5.5k data.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling perception (MLLM Interpreter) from reasoning (LLM) via textual CDL representations effectively leverages LLM reasoning for PGPS, enabling data-efficient training and strong cross-dataset transfer.

title: Conditional Denoising Model as a Physical Surrogate Model
summary: Surrogate modeling for complex physical systems typically faces a trade-off between data-fitting accuracy and physical consistency. Physics-consistent approaches typically treat physical laws as soft constraints within the loss function, a strategy that frequently fails to guarantee strict adherence to the governing equations, or rely on post-processing corrections that do not intrinsically learn the underlying solution geometry. To address these limitations, we introduce the {Conditional Denoising Model (CDM)}, a generative model designed to learn the geometry of the physical manifold itself. By training the network to restore clean states from noisy ones, the model learns a vector field that points continuously towards the valid solution subspace. We introduce a time-independent formulation that transforms inference into a deterministic fixed-point iteration, effectively projecting noisy approximations onto the equilibrium manifold. Validated on a low-temperature plasma physics and chemistry benchmark, the CDM achieves higher parameter and data efficiency than physics-consistent baselines. Crucially, we demonstrate that the denoising objective acts as a powerful implicit regularizer: despite never seeing the governing equations during training, the model adheres to physical constraints more strictly than baselines trained with explicit physics losses.

AI.tldr: A denoising-based conditional model (CDM) learns the geometry of the physical solution manifold and projects noisy approximations to the equilibrium subspace with a deterministic fixed-point inference, yielding data- and parameter-efficiency and stronger physical adherence than soft-constraint baselines.
AI.motivation: Surrogate models often trade data-fitting accuracy against physical consistency; physics-based losses may not guarantee strict adherence to governing equations, and post-hoc corrections fail to learn the underlying solution geometry.
AI.method: Introduce the Conditional Denoising Model (CDM) that denoises states to learn a vector field toward the valid solution subspace. Use a time-independent formulation that yields a deterministic fixed-point iteration to project noisy approximations onto the equilibrium manifold; training does not require explicit governing equations.
AI.result: CDM demonstrates higher parameter and data efficiency compared with physics-consistent baselines on a low-temperature plasma physics and chemistry benchmark, and the denoising objective acts as a strong implicit regularizer, yielding stricter adherence to physical constraints than baselines trained with explicit physics losses.
AI.conclusion: Learning the geometry of the physical manifold via denoising provides a principled, data-efficient route to physics-consistent surrogate modeling, with a deterministic projection mechanism and broad applicability beyond equation-constrained losses.

title: WorldBench: Disambiguating Physics for Diagnostic Evaluation of World Models
summary: Recent advances in generative foundational models, often termed "world models," have propelled interest in applying them to critical tasks like robotic planning and autonomous system training. For reliable deployment, these models must exhibit high physical fidelity, accurately simulating real-world dynamics. Existing physics-based video benchmarks, however, suffer from entanglement, where a single test simultaneously evaluates multiple physical laws and concepts, fundamentally limiting their diagnostic capability. We introduce WorldBench, a novel video-based benchmark specifically designed for concept-specific, disentangled evaluation, allowing us to rigorously isolate and assess understanding of a single physical concept or law at a time. To make WorldBench comprehensive, we design benchmarks at two different levels: 1) an evaluation of intuitive physical understanding with concepts such as object permanence or scale/perspective, and 2) an evaluation of low-level physical constants and material properties such as friction coefficients or fluid viscosity. When SOTA video-based world models are evaluated on WorldBench, we find specific patterns of failure in particular physics concepts, with all tested models lacking the physical consistency required to generate reliable real-world interactions. Through its concept-specific evaluation, WorldBench offers a more nuanced and scalable framework for rigorously evaluating the physical reasoning capabilities of video generation and world models, paving the way for more robust and generalizable world-model-driven learning.

AI.tldr: WorldBench is a two-level, concept-disentangled video benchmark to evaluate physics understanding in world/models; it isolates single physical concepts (intuitive and material properties); results show SOTA video-based world models struggle with physical consistency; provides a diagnostic framework for robust world-model-driven learning.
AI.motivation: Need reliable deployment of generative world models in robotics and autonomous systems. Existing physics video benchmarks entangle multiple physical laws, hindering diagnostic assessment. A concept-disentangled benchmark enables precise identification of specific physics understanding gaps.
AI.method: Design WorldBench as a video-based benchmark with two levels: (1) intuitive physical understanding (e.g., object permanence, scale/perspective); (2) low-level physical constants/material properties (e.g., friction coefficients, fluid viscosity). Each test isolates a single physical concept to diagnose model capabilities. Evaluate state-of-the-art video-based world models on these tests to reveal failure patterns and lack of physical consistency.
AI.result: Evaluation reveals specific failure patterns across physics concepts; SOTA models lack the physical consistency required for reliable real-world interactions.
AI.conclusion: WorldBench provides a nuanced, scalable framework for rigorously evaluating physical reasoning in video generation and world models, guiding development of more robust, generalizable world-model-based learning.

title: Don't double it: Efficient Agent Prediction in Occlusions
summary: Occluded traffic agents pose a significant challenge for autonomous vehicles, as hidden pedestrians or vehicles can appear unexpectedly, yet this problem remains understudied. Existing learning-based methods, while capable of inferring the presence of hidden agents, often produce redundant occupancy predictions where a single agent is identified multiple times. This issue complicates downstream planning and increases computational load. To address this, we introduce MatchInformer, a novel transformer-based approach that builds on the state-of-the-art SceneInformer architecture. Our method improves upon prior work by integrating Hungarian Matching, a state-of-the-art object matching algorithm from object detection, into the training process to enforce a one-to-one correspondence between predictions and ground truth, thereby reducing redundancy. We further refine trajectory forecasts by decoupling an agent's heading from its motion, a strategy that improves the accuracy and interpretability of predicted paths. To better handle class imbalances, we propose using the Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) to evaluate occupancy predictions. By considering all entries in the confusion matrix, MCC provides a robust measure even in sparse or imbalanced scenarios. Experiments on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset demonstrate that our approach improves reasoning about occluded regions and produces more accurate trajectory forecasts than prior methods.

AI.tldr: MatchInformer; a transformer-based occluded-traffic predictor that uses Hungarian matching to enforce one-to-one predictions and decouples heading from motion; evaluated with MCC on Waymo Open Motion dataset, yielding improved occlusion reasoning and trajectory accuracy.
AI.motivation: Occluded agents create hidden or partially observed targets; existing methods produce redundant predictions and burden planning. A one-to-one assignment and robust evaluation can improve reliability and efficiency.
AI.method: Extends SceneInformer by integrating Hungarian matching into training for one-to-one correspondence; decouples agent heading from motion in trajectory forecasting; uses Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) to evaluate occupancy predictions; evaluated on Waymo Open Motion Dataset.
AI.result: Outperforms prior methods in occluded-region reasoning and trajectory accuracy; demonstrates reduced redundancy and improved interpretability; MCC provides robust performance metrics under class imbalance.
AI.conclusion: Hungarian matching reduces duplicate predictions; heading-motion decoupling enhances trajectory interpretability; MCC offers robust evaluation for occupancy; results on Waymo dataset validate effectiveness and potential planning benefits.

title: FrontierScience: Evaluating AI's Ability to Perform Expert-Level Scientific Tasks
summary:
We introduce FrontierScience, a benchmark evaluating expert-level scientific reasoning in frontier language models. Recent model progress has nearly saturated existing science benchmarks, which often rely on multiple-choice knowledge questions or already published information. FrontierScience addresses this gap through two complementary tracks: (1) Olympiad, consisting of international olympiad problems at the level of IPhO, IChO, and IBO, and (2) Research, consisting of PhD-level, open-ended problems representative of sub-tasks in scientific research.
  FrontierScience contains several hundred questions (including 160 in the open-sourced gold set) covering subfields across physics, chemistry, and biology, from quantum electrodynamics to synthetic organic chemistry. All Olympiad problems are originally produced by international Olympiad medalists and national team coaches to ensure standards of difficulty, originality, and factuality. All Research problems are research sub-tasks written and verified by PhD scientists (doctoral candidates, postdoctoral researchers, or professors). For Research, we introduce a granular rubric-based evaluation framework to assess model capabilities throughout the process of solving a research task, rather than judging only a standalone final answer.

AI.tldr: FrontierScience is a benchmark to evaluate expert-level scientific reasoning in frontier language models via two tracks (Olympiad and Research) with hundreds of questions and a rubric-based open-ended evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing science benchmarks largely rely on multiple-choice or retrieval of published information; there is a need to assess genuine, frontier-level scientific reasoning and problem-solving that mirrors authentic research tasks.
AI.method: Two complementary tracks: Olympiad (IPhO, IChO, IBO) with problems authored by Olympiad medalists/coaches; Research with PhD-level, open-ended problems; content spanning physics, chemistry, biology; include 160 open-sourced gold questions; a granular rubric-based evaluation framework to assess the model's reasoning process throughout a task, not just final answers.
AI.result: Introduction of FrontierScience as a new benchmark, comprising several hundred questions (including 160 gold-set items) and a rubric-driven assessment method for evaluating step-by-step reasoning across scientific subfields, with open access to a portion of the dataset.
AI.conclusion: FrontierScience fills an evaluation gap for frontier-level scientific reasoning, enabling robust measurement of model capabilities beyond traditional benchmarks and potentially guiding future model development in authentic research-like tasks.

title: SIGMA-PPG: Statistical-prior Informed Generative Masking Architecture for PPG Foundation Model
summary: Current foundation model for photoplethysmography (PPG) signals is challenged by the intrinsic redundancy and noise of the signal. Standard masked modeling often yields trivial solutions while contrastive methods lack morphological precision. To address these limitations, we propose a Statistical-prior Informed Generative Masking Architecture (SIGMA-PPG), a generative foundation model featuring a Prior-Guided Adversarial Masking mechanism, where a reinforcement learning-driven teacher leverages statistical priors to create challenging learning paths that prevent overfitting to noise. We also incorporate a semantic consistency constraint via vector quantization to ensure that physiologically identical waveforms (even those altered by recording artifacts or minor perturbations) map to shared indices. This enhances codebook semantic density and eliminates redundant feature structures. Pre-trained on over 120,000 hours of data, SIGMA-PPG achieves superior average performance compared to five state-of-the-art baselines across 12 diverse downstream tasks. The code is available at https://github.com/ZonghengGuo/SigmaPPG.

AI.tldr: SIGMA-PPG: a generative foundation model for PPG signals using prior-guided adversarial masking and vector-quantized semantic consistency; pre-trained on 120k+ hours; outperforms 5 baselines across 12 tasks; code available.
AI.motivation: Address redundancy and noise in PPG signals where standard masked modeling yields trivial solutions and contrastive methods lack morphological precision; need a generative framework that leverages statistical priors and semantic consistency to improve downstream performance.
AI.method: Introduce Prior-Guided Adversarial Masking via a reinforcement-learning–driven teacher that uses statistical priors to construct challenging learning paths, preventing overfitting to noise. Add a semantic consistency constraint through vector quantization to ensure physiologically identical waveforms map to the same codebook indices, increasing semantic density. Pre-train on 120k+ hours of data.
AI.result: SIGMA-PPG achieves superior average performance compared with five state-of-the-art baselines across 12 diverse downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: The approach mitigates overfitting to noise and improves morphological fidelity in PPG representations by combining prior-guided masking with vector-quantized semantic consistency, enabling robust downstream performance and a semantically dense codebook. The work demonstrates the value of large-scale pretraining for PPG with a generative, priors-informed framework, and provides open-source code.

title: Gaussian Belief Propagation Network for Depth Completion
summary: Depth completion aims to predict a dense depth map from a color image with sparse depth measurements. Although deep learning methods have achieved state-of-the-art (SOTA), effectively handling the sparse and irregular nature of input depth data in deep networks remains a significant challenge, often limiting performance, especially under high sparsity. To overcome this limitation, we introduce the Gaussian Belief Propagation Network (GBPN), a novel hybrid framework synergistically integrating deep learning with probabilistic graphical models for end-to-end depth completion. Specifically, a scene-specific Markov Random Field (MRF) is dynamically constructed by the Graphical Model Construction Network (GMCN), and then inferred via Gaussian Belief Propagation (GBP) to yield the dense depth distribution. Crucially, the GMCN learns to construct not only the data-dependent potentials of MRF but also its structure by predicting adaptive non-local edges, enabling the capture of complex, long-range spatial dependencies. Furthermore, we enhance GBP with a serial \& parallel message passing scheme, designed for effective information propagation, particularly from sparse measurements. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GBPN achieves SOTA performance on the NYUv2 and KITTI benchmarks. Evaluations across varying sparsity levels, sparsity patterns, and datasets highlight GBPN's superior performance, notable robustness, and generalizable capability.

AI.tldr: Proposes Gaussian Belief Propagation Network (GBPN) that combines deep learning with probabilistic graphical models for depth completion. It builds a scene-specific MRF via GMCN, learns data terms and adaptive non-local edges, and infers dense depth with an enhanced Gaussian Belief Propagation (GBP) using serial/parallel message passing. Demonstrates SOTA performance on NYUv2 and KITTI, with robustness across sparsity levels and patterns.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of sparse, irregular depth data in completion tasks by leveraging probabilistic graphical models alongside deep networks. The aim is to capture complex, long-range dependencies and improve robustness to high sparsity.
AI.method: Construct a scene-specific MRF using a Graphical Model Construction Network (GMCN) that predicts data-dependent potentials and adaptive non-local edges. Infer the dense depth distribution with Gaussian Belief Propagation (GBP), augmented with a serial & parallel message passing scheme to enhance information propagation from sparse measurements. The framework is end-to-end trainable.
AI.result: GBPN achieves state-of-the-art performance on NYUv2 and KITTI. It shows robustness across varying sparsity levels and patterns, and demonstrates strong generalization across datasets.
AI.conclusion: GBPN effectively fuses deep learning with probabilistic graphical models for depth completion. Adaptive structure learning and enhanced GBP enable improved performance and robustness, suggesting potential applicability to other sparse data inference problems.

title: IROS: A Dual-Process Architecture for Real-Time VLM-Based Indoor Navigation
summary: Indoor mobile robot navigation requires fast responsiveness and robust semantic understanding, yet existing methods struggle to provide both. Classical geometric approaches such as SLAM offer reliable localization but depend on detailed maps and cannot interpret human-targeted cues (e.g., signs, room numbers) essential for indoor reasoning. Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models introduce semantic grounding but remain strictly reactive, basing decisions only on visible frames and failing to anticipate unseen intersections or reason about distant textual cues. Vision-Language Models (VLMs) provide richer contextual inference but suffer from high computational latency, making them unsuitable for real-time operation on embedded platforms. In this work, we present IROS, a real-time navigation framework that combines VLM-level contextual reasoning with the efficiency of lightweight perceptual modules on low-cost, on-device hardware. Inspired by Dual Process Theory, IROS separates fast reflexive decisions (System One) from slow deliberative reasoning (System Two), invoking the VLM only when necessary. Furthermore, by augmenting compact VLMs with spatial and textual cues, IROS delivers robust, human-like navigation with minimal latency. Across five real-world buildings, IROS improves decision accuracy and reduces latency by 66% compared to continuous VLM-based navigation.

AI.tldr: IROS is a real-time indoor navigation framework that fuses lightweight perceptual modules with compact VLMs, guided by Dual Process Theory to achieve low latency with robust semantic reasoning; evaluated in five buildings with 66% latency reduction and improved decision accuracy versus continuous VLM navigation.
AI.motivation: Indoor robotic navigation demands both fast responsiveness and semantic understanding. Traditional SLAM provides localization but lacks interpretive cues; pure VLM/VLA approaches are either too reactive or too slow; the goal is to integrate semantic grounding with efficient on-device inference.
AI.method: IROS architecture employs Dual Process Theory: System One (fast reflexive decisions via lightweight perceptual modules) and System Two (slower, deliberative reasoning invoking compact VLMs). VLMs are augmented with spatial/textual cues to improve reasoning; inference is on low-cost hardware with VLM invoked only as needed, enabling real-time operation and anticipation of unseen intersections or distant cues.
AI.result: Empirical validation across five real-world buildings shows a 66% reduction in navigation latency and improved decision accuracy compared to continuous VLM-based navigation, demonstrating robust and responsive, human-like navigation.
AI.conclusion: IROS demonstrates that real-time, human-like navigation is feasible on on-device hardware by combining efficient perception with targeted VLM reasoning, achieving low latency without sacrificing semantic understanding; the approach holds promise for scalable deployment and further enhancements in cue integration and gating strategies.

title: MAD: Modality-Adaptive Decoding for Mitigating Cross-Modal Hallucinations in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) suffer from cross-modal hallucinations, where one modality inappropriately influences generation about another, leading to fabricated output. This exposes a more fundamental deficiency in modality-interaction control. To address this, we propose Modality-Adaptive Decoding (MAD), a training-free method that adaptively weights modality-specific decoding branches based on task requirements. MAD leverages the model's inherent ability to self-assess modality relevance by querying which modalities are needed for each task. The extracted modality probabilities are then used to adaptively weight contrastive decoding branches, enabling the model to focus on relevant information while suppressing cross-modal interference. Extensive experiments on CMM and AVHBench demonstrate that MAD significantly reduces cross-modal hallucinations across multiple audio-visual language models (7.8\% and 2.0\% improvements for VideoLLaMA2-AV, 8.7\% and 4.7\% improvements for Qwen2.5-Omni). Our approach demonstrates that explicit modality awareness through self-assessment is crucial for robust multimodal reasoning, offering a principled extension to existing contrastive decoding methods. Our code is available at \href{https://github.com/top-yun/MAD}{https://github.com/top-yun/MAD}

AI.tldr: MAD is a training-free method that adaptively weights modality-specific decoding branches in multimodal LLMs to reduce cross-modal hallucinations by self-assessing modality relevance and applying those probabilities to contrastive decoding.
AI.motivation: Cross-modal hallucinations reveal weak modality-interaction control in MLLMs; there is a need for explicit modality-awareness to gate information flow across modalities.
AI.method: Query modality relevance for each task to obtain modality probabilities; use these to weight contrastive decoding branches (modality-specific decoding paths), enabling the model to emphasize relevant modalities and suppress interference.
AI.result: On CMM and AVHBench benchmarks, MAD reduces cross-modal hallucinations across several audio-visual language models, with improvements of 7.8% and 2.0% for VideoLLaMA2-AV, and 8.7% and 4.7% for Qwen2.5-Omni.
AI.conclusion: Explicit modality-awareness via self-assessment is crucial for robust multimodal reasoning; MAD offers a principled, training-free extension to existing contrastive decoding methods and demonstrates practical effectiveness; code is publicly available.

title: Predict-Project-Renoise: Sampling Diffusion Models under Hard Constraints
summary: Neural emulators based on diffusion models show promise for scientific applications, but vanilla models cannot guarantee physical accuracy or constraint satisfaction. We address this by introducing a constrained sampling framework that enforces hard constraints, such as physical laws or observational consistency, at generation time. Our approach defines a constrained forward process that diffuses only over the feasible set of constraint-satisfying samples, inducing constrained marginal distributions. To reverse this, we propose Predict-Project-Renoise (PPR), an iterative algorithm that samples from the constrained marginals by alternating between denoising predictions, projecting onto the feasible set, and renoising. Experiments on 2D distributions, PDEs, and global weather forecasting demonstrate that PPR reduces constraint violations by over an order of magnitude while improving sample consistency and better matching the true constrained distribution compared to baselines.

AI.tldr: Constrained diffusion sampling with PPR enforces hard physical constraints by restricting the forward process to the feasible set and iteratively denoising, projecting, and renoising to sample from constrained marginals.
AI.motivation: Vanilla diffusion models for neural emulators may violate physical laws or observational constraints; ensuring constraint satisfaction is critical for scientific reliability and realism.
AI.method: Introduce a constrained forward process that diffuses only over the constraint-satisfying feasible set. Develop Predict-Project-Renoise (PPR), an iterative sampler that alternates between denoising predictions, projecting onto the feasible set, and renoising to draw samples from constrained marginals.
AI.result: PPR reduces constraint violations by more than an order of magnitude, improves sample consistency, and better matches the true constrained distribution compared to baselines. Demonstrated on 2D distributions, PDEs, and global weather forecasting.
AI.conclusion: The constrained sampling framework enables hard constraint satisfaction in diffusion-based neural emulators, improving physical fidelity and reliability across diverse scientific applications.

title: Mam-App: A Novel Parameter-Efficient Mamba Model for Apple Leaf Disease Classification
summary: The rapid growth of the global population, alongside exponential technological advancement, has intensified the demand for food production. Meeting this demand depends not only on increasing agricultural yield but also on minimizing food loss caused by crop diseases. Diseases account for a substantial portion of apple production losses, despite apples being among the most widely produced and nutritionally valuable fruits worldwide. Previous studies have employed machine learning techniques for feature extraction and early diagnosis of apple leaf diseases, and more recently, deep learning-based models have shown remarkable performance in disease recognition. However, most state-of-the-art deep learning models are highly parameter-intensive, resulting in increased training and inference time. Although lightweight models are more suitable for user-friendly and resource-constrained applications, they often suffer from performance degradation. To address the trade-off between efficiency and performance, we propose Mam-App, a parameter-efficient Mamba-based model for feature extraction and leaf disease classification. The proposed approach achieves competitive state-of-the-art performance on the PlantVillage Apple Leaf Disease dataset, attaining 99.58% accuracy, 99.30% precision, 99.14% recall, and a 99.22% F1-score, while using only 0.051M parameters. This extremely low parameter count makes the model suitable for deployment on drones, mobile devices, and other low-resource platforms. To demonstrate the robustness and generalizability of the proposed model, we further evaluate it on the PlantVillage Corn Leaf Disease and Potato Leaf Disease datasets. The model achieves 99.48%, 99.20%, 99.34%, and 99.27% accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on the corn dataset and 98.46%, 98.91%, 95.39%, and 97.01% on the potato dataset, respectively.

AI.tldr: A highly parameter-efficient, Mamba-based model (Mam-App) delivers competitive disease classification on PlantVillage datasets with only 0.051M parameters, suitable for low-resource devices, and demonstrates cross-dataset generalization across apple, corn, and potato leaf diseases.
AI.motivation: Mitigate food loss from crop diseases by enabling accurate leaf disease diagnosis on resource-constrained devices; address the trade-off between model accuracy and parameter count in deep learning models.
AI.method: Introduce Mam-App, a parameter-efficient Mamba-based feature extractor and classifier; evaluate on PlantVillage Apple Leaf Disease, Corn Leaf Disease, Potato Leaf Disease datasets; compare with state-of-the-art; metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, F1; model uses 0.051M parameters.
AI.result: Apple: 99.58% accuracy, 99.30% precision, 99.14% recall, 99.22% F1. Corn: 99.48% accuracy, 99.20% precision, 99.34% recall, 99.27% F1. Potato: 98.46% accuracy, 98.91% precision, 95.39% recall, 97.01% F1. Parameter count: 0.051M.
AI.conclusion: Mam-App achieves competitive state-of-the-art performance with an extremely low parameter count, enabling deployment on drones and mobile devices; demonstrates robustness and generalization across multiple crops.

title: Training slow silicon neurons to control extremely fast robots with spiking reinforcement learning
summary: Air hockey demands split-second decisions at high puck velocities, a challenge we address with a compact network of spiking neurons running on a mixed-signal analog/digital neuromorphic processor. By co-designing hardware and learning algorithms, we train the system to achieve successful puck interactions through reinforcement learning in a remarkably small number of trials. The network leverages fixed random connectivity to capture the task's temporal structure and adopts a local e-prop learning rule in the readout layer to exploit event-driven activity for fast and efficient learning. The result is real-time learning with a setup comprising a computer and the neuromorphic chip in-the-loop, enabling practical training of spiking neural networks for robotic autonomous systems. This work bridges neuroscience-inspired hardware with real-world robotic control, showing that brain-inspired approaches can tackle fast-paced interaction tasks while supporting always-on learning in intelligent machines.

AI.tldr: A compact neuromorphic spiking network on a mixed-signal processor learns real-time air-hockey control via reinforcement learning with fixed random connectivity and local e-prop, enabling in-the-loop training for fast, energy-efficient robotic interaction.
AI.motivation: Bridge neuroscience-inspired hardware with real-world robotic control and enable real-time, always-on learning in fast-paced tasks using neuromorphic systems.
AI.method: Hardware–software co-design of a spiking neural network implemented on a mixed-signal neuromorphic chip, using fixed random connectivity to capture temporal structure; local e-prop learning in the readout layer; reinforcement learning with the computer–chip loop for real-time training.
AI.result: Demonstrates real-time learning with a minimal number of trials; successful puck interactions in air hockey; shows practicality of training SNNs for robotic autonomous systems in an in-the-loop setup.
AI.conclusion: Brain-inspired neuromorphic hardware can tackle fast-paced interaction tasks and support continuous learning in intelligent machines, validating the applicability of neuromorphic approaches to real-world robotic control.

title: Sycophantic Anchors: Localizing and Quantifying User Agreement in Reasoning Models
summary: Reasoning models frequently agree with incorrect user suggestions -- a behavior known as sycophancy. However, it is unclear where in the reasoning trace this agreement originates and how strong the commitment is. To localize and quantify this behavior, we introduce \emph{sycophantic anchors} -- sentences that causally lock models into user agreement. Analyzing over 10,000 counterfactual rollouts on a distilled reasoning model, we show that anchors can be reliably detected and quantified mid-inference. Linear probes distinguish sycophantic anchors with 84.6\% balanced accuracy, while activation-based regressors predict the magnitude of the commitment ($R^2 = 0.74$). We further observe asymmetry where sycophantic anchors are significantly more distinguishable than correct reasoning anchors, and find that sycophancy builds gradually during reasoning, revealing a potential window for intervention. These results offer sentence-level mechanisms for localizing model misalignment mid-inference.

AI.tldr: Introduces sycophantic anchors as sentence-level cues that causally lock a reasoning model into agreement with user suggestions, enabling mid-inference detection and quantification of alignment failures.
AI.motivation: To understand and localize where and how models commit to incorrect user suggestions during reasoning, addressing the phenomenon of sycophancy and proposing a localizable mechanism for misalignment.
AI.method: Analyze over 10,000 counterfactual rollouts on a distilled reasoning model. Identify sycophantic anchors as causal sentence-level factors. Use linear probes to distinguish anchors (84.6% balanced accuracy) and activation-based regressors to predict commitment magnitude (R^2 = 0.74). Examine asymmetry vs correct anchors and track gradual buildup during reasoning.
AI.result: Sycophantic anchors can be reliably detected mid-inference with high accuracy; the magnitude of commitment can be predicted from activations; anchors are more distinguishable than correct anchors; the effect accumulates gradually, offering a window for intervention.
AI.conclusion: Sentence-level mechanisms enable localizing model misalignment mid-inference and provide practical handles for intervention to mitigate sycophancy during reasoning.

title: Thinking in Frames: How Visual Context and Test-Time Scaling Empower Video Reasoning
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures, 3 tables (26 pages, 13 figures, 6 tables including references and appendices)
summary: Vision-Language Models have excelled at textual reasoning, but they often struggle with fine-grained spatial understanding and continuous action planning, failing to simulate the dynamics required for complex visual reasoning. In this work, we formulate visual reasoning by means of video generation models, positing that generated frames can act as intermediate reasoning steps between initial states and solutions. We evaluate their capacity in two distinct regimes: Maze Navigation for sequential discrete planning with low visual change and Tangram Puzzle for continuous manipulation with high visual change. Our experiments reveal three critical insights: (1) Robust Zero-Shot Generalization: In both tasks, the model demonstrates strong performance on unseen data distributions without specific finetuning. (2) Visual Context: The model effectively uses visual context as explicit control, such as agent icons and tangram shapes, enabling it to maintain high visual consistency and adapt its planning capability robustly to unseen patterns. (3) Visual Test-Time Scaling: We observe a test-time scaling law in sequential planning; increasing the generated video length (visual inference budget) empowers better zero-shot generalization to spatially and temporally complex paths. These findings suggest that video generation is not merely a media tool, but a scalable, generalizable paradigm for visual reasoning.

AI.tldr: Frame-wise video generation as intermediate reasoning steps between states and solutions enables zero-shot generalization and scalable planning in spatial-temporal tasks.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language Models excel at textual reasoning but struggle with fine-grained spatial understanding and continuous action planning; video-based reasoning could bridge this gap.
AI.method: Use video generation models to produce frames that serve as intermediate reasoning steps connecting the initial state to the solution. Evaluate on two tasks: Maze Navigation (sequential discrete planning with low visual change) and Tangram Puzzle (continuous manipulation with high visual change).
AI.result: Three insights: (1) Robust zero-shot generalization to unseen data distributions without finetuning; (2) Visual context acts as explicit control, preserving visual consistency and enabling robust planning across unseen patterns; (3) A test-time scaling law: longer generated videos at inference improve zero-shot generalization to spatially and temporally complex paths.
AI.conclusion: Video generation constitutes a scalable, generalizable paradigm for visual reasoning, extending beyond mere media generation to guiding planning in visual tasks.

title: HiFi-Mesh: High-Fidelity Efficient 3D Mesh Generation via Compact Autoregressive Dependence
summary: High-fidelity 3D meshes can be tokenized into one-dimension (1D) sequences and directly modeled using autoregressive approaches for faces and vertices. However, existing methods suffer from insufficient resource utilization, resulting in slow inference and the ability to handle only small-scale sequences, which severely constrains the expressible structural details. We introduce the Latent Autoregressive Network (LANE), which incorporates compact autoregressive dependencies in the generation process, achieving a $6\times$ improvement in maximum generatable sequence length compared to existing methods. To further accelerate inference, we propose the Adaptive Computation Graph Reconfiguration (AdaGraph) strategy, which effectively overcomes the efficiency bottleneck of traditional serial inference through spatiotemporal decoupling in the generation process. Experimental validation demonstrates that LANE achieves superior performance across generation speed, structural detail, and geometric consistency, providing an effective solution for high-quality 3D mesh generation.

AI.tldr: LANE introduces a latent autoregressive approach for 1D-tokenized high-fidelity 3D meshes, paired with AdaGraph for adaptive computation, achieving a 6× increase in maximum generatable sequence length and faster, more detailed, geometrically consistent mesh generation.
AI.motivation: Existing autoregressive mesh models underutilize resources, suffering slow inference and inability to handle long sequences, which constrains expressiveness and detail. There is a need for more efficient, scalable generation of large-scale mesh sequences.
AI.method: LANE uses compact autoregressive dependencies within a latent autoregressive framework to model 1D mesh tokens more efficiently, enabling longer sequences. AdaGraph (Adaptive Computation Graph Reconfiguration) dynamically reconfigures computation graphs to decouple spatiotemporal aspects during generation, mitigating the bottlenecks of serial inference and speeding up generation.
AI.result: The approach yields superior performance in generation speed, structural detail, and geometric consistency, with a reported 6× expansion in the maximum generatable sequence length relative to prior methods.
AI.conclusion: LANE, together with AdaGraph, provides an effective solution for high-quality 3D mesh generation, enabling longer, more detailed, and faster generation.

title: AIR-VLA: Vision-Language-Action Systems for Aerial Manipulation
summary: While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have achieved remarkable success in ground-based embodied intelligence, their application to Aerial Manipulation Systems (AMS) remains a largely unexplored frontier. The inherent characteristics of AMS, including floating-base dynamics, strong coupling between the UAV and the manipulator, and the multi-step, long-horizon nature of operational tasks, pose severe challenges to existing VLA paradigms designed for static or 2D mobile bases. To bridge this gap, we propose AIR-VLA, the first VLA benchmark specifically tailored for aerial manipulation. We construct a physics-based simulation environment and release a high-quality multimodal dataset comprising 3000 manually teleoperated demonstrations, covering base manipulation, object & spatial understanding, semantic reasoning, and long-horizon planning. Leveraging this platform, we systematically evaluate mainstream VLA models and state-of-the-art VLM models. Our experiments not only validate the feasibility of transferring VLA paradigms to aerial systems but also, through multi-dimensional metrics tailored to aerial tasks, reveal the capabilities and boundaries of current models regarding UAV mobility, manipulator control, and high-level planning. AIR-VLA establishes a standardized testbed and data foundation for future research in general-purpose aerial robotics. The resource of AIR-VLA will be available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/AIR-VLA-dataset-B5CC/.

AI.tldr: Proposes AIR-VLA, the first VLA benchmark for aerial manipulation, with a physics-based simulator and a 3000-demo multimodal dataset to evaluate VLA models in UAV manipulation tasks; establishes a standardized testbed for aerial robotics research.
AI.motivation: VLA models excel in ground-based embodied tasks but struggle with aerial robots due to floating-base dynamics, strong UAV–manipulator coupling, and long-horizon planning. A dedicated aerial VLA benchmark is needed to study perception, control, and planning in this domain.
AI.method: Develop a physics-based simulation environment and release AIR-VLA, a high-quality multimodal dataset of 3000 manually teleoperated demonstrations covering base manipulation, object/spatial understanding, semantic reasoning, and long-horizon planning. Systematically evaluate mainstream VLA models and state-of-the-art VLMs on this platform using metrics tailored to aerial tasks.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of transferring VLA paradigms to aerial systems. Reveals capabilities and limits of current models in UAV mobility, manipulator control, and high-level planning. Establishes AIR-VLA as a standardized testbed and data foundation for future aerial robotics research.
AI.conclusion: AIR-VLA provides a standardized, publicly available data-and-simulation resource to advance research in general-purpose aerial robotics and to benchmark how VLA approaches perform in complex, long-horizon aerial tasks.

title: Do Reasoning Models Enhance Embedding Models?
comment: 10 main pages, 18 appendix pages, 13 figures, 11 tables, 4 prompts
summary: State-of-the-art embedding models are increasingly derived from decoder-only Large Language Model (LLM) backbones adapted via contrastive learning. Given the emergence of reasoning models trained via Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), a natural question arises: do enhanced reasoning translate to superior semantic representations when these models serve as embedding initializations? Contrary to expectation, our evaluation on MTEB and BRIGHT reveals a **null effect**: embedding models initialized from RLVR-tuned backbones yield no consistent performance advantage over their base counterparts when subjected to identical training recipes. To unpack this paradox, we introduce **H**ierarchical **R**epresentation **S**imilarity **A**nalysis (HRSA), a framework that decomposes similarity across representation, geometry, and function levels. HRSA reveals that while RLVR induces irreversible latent manifold's local geometry reorganization and reversible coordinate basis drift, it preserves the global manifold geometry and linear readout. Consequently, subsequent contrastive learning drives strong alignment between base- and reasoning-initialized models, a phenomenon we term **Manifold Realignment**. Empirically, our findings suggest that unlike Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), RLVR optimizes trajectories within an existing semantic landscape rather than fundamentally restructuring the landscape itself.

AI.tldr: RLVR-tuned backbones used for embedding initialization show no consistent advantage over base backbones under identical training recipes, due to manifold realignment during subsequent contrastive learning.
AI.motivation: Explain why enhanced reasoning from RLVR does not translate into superior semantic representations when used as embeddings, as observed on MTEB and BRIGHT.
AI.method: Propose and apply Hierarchical Representation Similarity Analysis (HRSA) to decompose representation similarity into three levels (representation, geometry, function). Compare RLVR-initialized vs base backbones across RLVR and after subsequent contrastive learning using standard embedding pipelines evaluated on MTEB and BRIGHT.
AI.result: RLVR induces irreversible local geometry reorganization and reversible coordinate basis drift, but preserves global manifold geometry and linear readout. Subsequent contrastive learning aligns representations, causing a Manifold Realignment and yielding no persistent advantage over base initializations in embeddings.
AI.conclusion: RLVR moves trajectories within an existingSemantic landscape rather than reshaping the landscape itself; unlike Supervised Fine-Tuning, RLVR does not create lasting representation-level gains for embeddings when followed by standard contrastive training.

title: Test-Time Adaptation for Unsupervised Combinatorial Optimization
summary: Unsupervised neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) enables learning powerful solvers without access to ground-truth solutions.
