Existing approaches fall into two disjoint paradigms: models trained for generalization across instances, and instance-specific models optimized independently at test time. While the former are efficient during inference, they lack effective instance-wise adaptability; the latter are flexible but fail to exploit learned inductive structure and are prone to poor local optima. This motivates the central question of our work: how can we leverage the inductive bias learned through generalization while unlocking the flexibility required for effective instance-wise adaptation? We first identify a challenge in bridging these two paradigms: generalization-focused models often constitute poor warm starts for instance-wise optimization, potentially underperforming even randomly initialized models when fine-tuned at test time. To resolve this incompatibility, we propose TACO, a model-agnostic test-time adaptation framework that unifies and extends the two existing paradigms for unsupervised NCO. TACO applies strategic warm-starting to partially relax trained parameters while preserving inductive bias, enabling rapid and effective unsupervised adaptation. Crucially, compared to naively fine-tuning a trained generalizable model or optimizing an instance-specific model from scratch, TACO achieves better solution quality while incurring negligible additional computational cost. Experiments on canonical CO problems, Minimum Vertex Cover and Maximum Clique, demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of TACO across static, distribution-shifted, and dynamic combinatorial optimization problems, establishing it as a practical bridge between generalizable and instance-specific unsupervised NCO.

AI.tldr: TACO enables test-time adaptation for unsupervised NCO by warm-starting partially relaxed parameters to preserve inductive bias while enabling rapid instance-wise optimization; it bridges generalization and instance-specific paradigms.
AI.motivation: Generalization-based NCO models are efficient but less adaptable; instance-specific models adapt well but ignore learned inductive structure and risk poor optima. A unified approach is needed to combine the strengths.
AI.method: A model-agnostic test-time adaptation framework that partially relaxes trained parameters (warm-start) to strike a balance between retaining inductive bias and allowing adaptation; it unifies the two paradigms and outperforms naive fine-tuning or scratch optimization.
AI.result: Empirical results on Minimum Vertex Cover and Maximum Clique show TACO improves solution quality with negligible extra cost and robustness across static, distribution-shifted, and dynamic CO settings.
AI.conclusion: TACO provides a practical bridge between generalizable and instance-specific unsupervised NCO, enabling efficient, effective adaptation without sacrificing inductive bias.

title: Optimal Transport-Induced Samples against Out-of-Distribution Overconfidence
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) often produce overconfident predictions on out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs, undermining their reliability in open-world environments. Singularities in semi-discrete optimal transport (OT) mark regions of semantic ambiguity, where classifiers are particularly prone to unwarranted high-confidence predictions. Motivated by this observation, we propose a principled framework to mitigate OOD overconfidence by leveraging the geometry of OT-induced singular boundaries. Specifically, we formulate an OT problem between a continuous base distribution and the latent embeddings of training data, and identify the resulting singular boundaries. By sampling near these boundaries, we construct a class of OOD inputs, termed optimal transport-induced OOD samples (OTIS), which are geometrically grounded and inherently semantically ambiguous. During training, a confidence suppression loss is applied to OTIS to guide the model toward more calibrated predictions in structurally uncertain regions. Extensive experiments show that our method significantly alleviates OOD overconfidence and outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: An OT-based framework identifies singular boundaries between a base distribution and training embeddings to generate OTIS samples near these boundaries, applying a confidence suppression loss to improve OOD calibration and outperform current methods.
AI.motivation: DNNs tend to be overconfident on out-of-distribution inputs; singularities in semi-discrete OT mark semantic ambiguities where high-confidence mispredictions occur; a geometry-informed approach is needed to calibrate predictions in uncertain regions.
AI.method: Formulate an OT problem between a continuous base distribution and the latent embeddings of training data to locate singular boundaries. Sample near these boundaries to create OTIS (optimal transport-induced OOD samples). Train with a confidence suppression loss on OTIS to push predictions toward calibrated outputs in structurally uncertain regions.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments demonstrate substantial reduction of OOD overconfidence and performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging the geometry of OT-induced singular boundaries yields a principled and effective strategy for calibrating DNN predictions in OOD regions, with OTIS providing a geometrically grounded, semantically ambiguous OOD support.

title: From Instruction to Event: Sound-Triggered Mobile Manipulation
summary: Current mobile manipulation research predominantly follows an instruction-driven paradigm, where agents rely on predefined textual commands to execute tasks. However, this setting confines agents to a passive role, limiting their autonomy and ability to react to dynamic environmental events. To address these limitations, we introduce sound-triggered mobile manipulation, where agents must actively perceive and interact with sound-emitting objects without explicit action instructions. To support these tasks, we develop Habitat-Echo, a data platform that integrates acoustic rendering with physical interaction. We further propose a baseline comprising a high-level task planner and low-level policy models to complete these tasks. Extensive experiments show that the proposed baseline empowers agents to actively detect and respond to auditory events, eliminating the need for case-by-case instructions. Notably, in the challenging dual-source scenario, the agent successfully isolates the primary source from overlapping acoustic interference to execute the first interaction, and subsequently proceeds to manipulate the secondary object, verifying the robustness of the baseline.

AI.tldr: Presents a sound-triggered mobile manipulation framework with Habitat-Echo data platform and a baseline (high-level planner + low-level policies) enabling autonomous detection and interaction with sound-emitting objects, demonstrated to handle overlapping sound sources and perform sequential manipulations without explicit instructions.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of instruction-driven agents that rely on predefined commands; enable autonomy by allowing agents to perceive and react to dynamic auditory events in the environment.
AI.method: Introduce Habitat-Echo, a data platform that fuses acoustic rendering with physical interaction; develop a baseline consisting of a high-level task planner and low-level policy models to perform sound-triggered manipulation tasks; conduct extensive experiments, including a dual-source interference scenario, to assess robustness.
AI.result: The baseline enables agents to actively detect and respond to auditory events without case-by-case instructions; in a challenging dual-source setup, the agent isolates the primary sound source from interference to perform the first interaction and then manipulates the secondary object, indicating robustness of the approach.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of autonomous audio-driven mobile manipulation and provides a solid baseline and data platform to study sound-aware interactions; highlights robustness to auditory interference and anticipates further exploration of complex acoustic environments and generalization to real-world settings.

title: When should I search more: Adaptive Complex Query Optimization with Reinforcement Learning
summary: Query optimization is a crucial component for the efficacy of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. While reinforcement learning (RL)-based agentic and reasoning methods have recently emerged as a promising direction on query optimization, most existing approaches focus on the expansion and abstraction of a single query. However, complex user queries are prevalent in real-world scenarios, often requiring multiple parallel and sequential search strategies to handle disambiguation and decomposition. Directly applying RL to these complex cases introduces significant hurdles. Determining the optimal number of sub-queries and effectively re-ranking and merging retrieved documents vastly expands the search space and complicates reward design, frequently leading to training instability. To address these challenges, we propose a novel RL framework called Adaptive Complex Query Optimization (ACQO). Our framework is designed to adaptively determine when and how to expand the search process. It features two core components: an Adaptive Query Reformulation (AQR) module that dynamically decides when to decompose a query into multiple sub-queries, and a Rank-Score Fusion (RSF) module that ensures robust result aggregation and provides stable reward signals for the learning agent. To mitigate training instabilities, we adopt a Curriculum Reinforcement Learning (CRL) approach, which stabilizes the training process by progressively introducing more challenging queries through a two-stage strategy. Our comprehensive experiments demonstrate that ACQO achieves state-of-the-art performance on three complex query benchmarks, significantly outperforming established baselines. The framework also showcases improved computational efficiency and broad compatibility with different retrieval architectures, establishing it as a powerful and generalizable solution for next-generation RAG systems.

AI.tldr: Adaptive RL framework for complex query optimization in RAG (ACQO) with adaptive query reformulation (AQR) and rank-score fusion (RSF), trained via curriculum reinforcement learning; achieves state-of-the-art on three benchmarks and improves efficiency across architectures.
AI.motivation: Real-world user queries are often complex, requiring multiple sub-queries and robust result aggregation. Existing RL approaches focusing on a single query face large search spaces and unstable reward signals, hindering training and performance.
AI.method: ACQO introduces (i) Adaptive Query Reformulation (AQR) to decide when to decompose a query into sub-queries, and (ii) Rank-Score Fusion (RSF) to robustly aggregate results and provide stable rewards. Training uses Curriculum Reinforcement Learning (CRL) with a two-stage progression to gradually expose harder queries.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows state-of-the-art performance on three complex query benchmarks, with significant gains over baselines. ACQO also improves computational efficiency and is compatible with various retrieval architectures.
AI.conclusion: ACQO is a powerful, generalizable solution for next-generation RAG systems, enabling adaptive handling of complex queries and stable RL training across diverse retrieval setups.

title: Do Pathology Foundation Models Encode Disease Progression? A Pseudotime Analysis of Visual Representations
summary: Vision foundation models trained on discretely sampled images achieve strong performance on classification benchmarks, yet whether their representations encode the continuous processes underlying their training data remains unclear. This question is especially pertinent in computational pathology, where we posit that models whose latent representations implicitly capture continuous disease progression may better reflect underlying biology, support more robust generalization, and enable quantitative analyses of features associated with disease transitions. Using diffusion pseudotime, a method developed to infer developmental trajectories from single-cell transcriptomics, we probe whether foundation models organize disease states along coherent progression directions in representation space. Across four cancer progressions and six models, we find that all pathology-specific models recover trajectory orderings significantly exceeding null baselines, with vision-only models achieving the highest fidelities $(τ> 0.78$ on CRC-Serrated). Model rankings by trajectory fidelity on reference diseases strongly predict few-shot classification performance on held-out diseases ($ρ= 0.92$), and exploratory analysis shows cell-type composition varies smoothly along inferred trajectories in patterns consistent with known stromal remodeling. Together, these results demonstrate that vision foundation models can implicitly learn to represent continuous processes from independent static observations, and that trajectory fidelity provides a complementary measure of representation quality beyond downstream performance. While demonstrated in pathology, this framework could be applied to other domains where continuous processes are observed through static snapshots.

AI.tldr: Vision foundation models trained on discretely sampled pathology images implicitly encode continuous disease progression; diffusion pseudotime reveals trajectories in latent space, with high trajectory fidelity correlating with few-shot classification performance; suggests continuous-process representations beyond supervised tasks.
AI.motivation: Assess whether static pathology training data lead to latent representations that reflect underlying continuous disease progression and whether trajectory fidelity complements traditional downstream metrics.
AI.method: Apply diffusion pseudotime to latent representations from four cancer progressions across six models (pathology-specific and vision-only). Quantify trajectory fidelity (tau) against null baselines; examine correlation between fidelity and few-shot performance on held-out diseases; analyze cell-type composition along inferred trajectories to assess biological plausibility.
AI.result: All pathology-specific models recover trajectory orderings significantly better than null baselines; vision-only models achieve the highest fidelity (tau > 0.78 on CRC-Serrated). Rankings by trajectory fidelity predict held-out-disease few-shot performance (rho ≈ 0.92). Cell-type composition varies smoothly along trajectories, aligning with known stromal remodeling patterns.
AI.conclusion: Vision foundation models can implicitly learn continuous biological processes from static data. Trajectory fidelity offers a complementary metric of representation quality beyond downstream accuracy and could generalize to other domains with continuous processes.

title: CoFreeVLA: Collision-Free Dual-Arm Manipulation via Vision-Language-Action Model and Risk Estimation
summary: Vision Language Action (VLA) models enable instruction following manipulation, yet dualarm deployment remains unsafe due to under modeled selfcollisions between arms and grasped objects. We introduce CoFreeVLA, which augments an endtoend VLA with a short horizon selfcollision risk estimator that predicts collision likelihood from proprioception, visual embeddings, and planned actions. The estimator gates risky commands, recovers to safe states via risk-guided adjustments, and shapes policy refinement for safer rollouts. It is pre-trained with model-based collision labels and posttrained on real robot rollouts for calibration. On five bimanual tasks with the PiPER robot arm, CoFreeVLA reduces selfcollisions and improves success rates versus RDT and APEX.

AI.tldr: CoFreeVLA augments Vision-Language Action models with a short-horizon self-collision risk estimator to mitigate inter-arm and grasped-object collisions in dual-arm manipulation, improving safety and success on bimanual tasks.
AI.motivation: Dual-arm VLA systems risk self-collisions due to under-modeling of arm-object interactions; a proactive, proprioceptive- and vision-based risk estimator can gate unsafe actions and enable safer, more reliable manipulation.
AI.method: Introduce CoFreeVLA: an end-to-end VLA augmented with a short-horizon self-collision risk estimator that uses proprioception, visual embeddings, and planned actions to predict collision likelihood. The estimator gates risky commands, enables risk-guided recovery to safe states, and shapes policy refinement during rollouts. Pre-training with model-based collision labels and real-robot rollouts for calibration.
AI.result: Evaluated on five bimanual tasks with the PiPER robot arm; CoFreeVLA reduces self-collisions and improves success rates relative to baselines RDT and APEX.
AI.conclusion: The hazard-aware gating and recovery mechanism improves safety and success of dual-arm manipulation by embedding a calibrated self-collision risk model into the VLA loop, effective across simulation-informed pre-training and real-world calibration.

title: Uncovering Hidden Correctness in LLM Causal Reasoning via Symbolic Verification
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being applied to tasks that involve causal reasoning. However, current benchmarks often rely on string matching or surface-level metrics that do not capture whether the output of a model is formally valid under the semantics of causal reasoning. To address this, we propose DoVerifier, a simple symbolic verifier that checks whether LLM-generated causal expressions are derivable from a given causal graph using rules from do-calculus and probability theory. This allows us to recover correct answers to causal queries that would otherwise be marked incorrect due to superficial differences in their causal semantics. Our evaluations on synthetic data and causal QA benchmarks show that DoVerifier more accurately captures semantic correctness of causal reasoning traces, offering a more rigorous and informative way to evaluate LLMs on causal reasoning.

AI.tldr: A symbolic verifier (DoVerifier) checks LLM-generated causal expressions for derivability from a causal graph using do-calculus and probability theory, improving semantic correctness evaluation beyond string matching.
AI.motivation: Current causal-reasoning benchmarks rely on surface-level string matches that miss semantic validity; formal semantics are needed.
AI.method: Introduce DoVerifier, a symbolic verifier that tests whether a generated causal expression can be derived from a given causal graph via do-calculus and probability theory rules.
AI.result: DoVerifier better captures semantic correctness, recovering correct causal-queries that surface-mismatch would mark incorrect, validated on synthetic data and causal QA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic semantic verification provides a more rigorous, informative evaluation framework for LLM-based causal reasoning.

title: Snowball: A Scalable All-to-All Ising Machine with Dual-Mode Markov Chain Monte Carlo Spin Selection and Asynchronous Spin Updates for Fast Combinatorial Optimization
summary:
Ising machines have emerged as accelerators for combinatorial optimization. To enable practical deployment, this work aims to reduce time-to-solution by addressing three challenges: (1) hardware topology, (2) spin selection and update algorithms, and (3) scalable coupling-coefficient precision. Restricted topologies require minor embedding; naive parallel updates can oscillate or stall; and limited precision can preclude feasible mappings or degrade solution quality.
  This work presents Snowball, a digital, scalable, all-to-all coupled Ising machine that integrates dual-mode Markov chain Monte Carlo spin selection with asynchronous spin updates to promote convergence and reduce time-to-solution. The digital architecture supports wide, configurable coupling precision, unlike many analog realizations at high bit widths. A prototype on an AMD Alveo U250 accelerator card achieves an 8$\times$ reduction in time-to-solution relative to a state-of-the-art Ising machine on the same benchmark instance.

AI.tldr: Snowball is a digital, scalable Ising machine with all-to-all coupling and dual-mode MCMC spin selection and asynchronous updates, achieving 8× faster time-to-solution on a benchmark compared to a state-of-the-art Ising machine.
AI.motivation: To enable practical deployment of Ising machines by addressing three core challenges: hardware topology (avoiding minor embedding), spin selection/update algorithms (preventing oscillations and stalls), and scalable coupling-precision (enabling feasible mappings and maintaining solution quality).
AI.method: Propose Snowball: a digital, scalable, all-to-all coupled Ising machine that uses dual-mode Markov chain Monte Carlo spin selection combined with asynchronous spin updates; supports wide, configurable coupling precision; implemented on an AMD Alveo U250 accelerator card to test performance.
AI.result: Prototype demonstrates an 8× reduction in time-to-solution relative to a state-of-the-art Ising machine on the same benchmark instance.
AI.conclusion: Snowball shows that a digitally implemented, scalable, all-to-all Ising machine with MCMC-based spin selection and asynchronous updates can achieve substantial speedups, with wide coupling-precision support, suggesting practical viability versus analog isolates.

title: SR$^{2}$-Net: A General Plug-and-Play Model for Spectral Refinement in Hyperspectral Image Super-Resolution
summary: HSI-SR aims to enhance spatial resolution while preserving spectrally faithful and physically plausible characteristics. Recent methods have achieved great progress by leveraging spatial correlations to enhance spatial resolution. However, these methods often neglect spectral consistency across bands, leading to spurious oscillations and physically implausible artifacts. While spectral consistency can be addressed by designing the network architecture, it results in a loss of generality and flexibility. To address this issue, we propose a lightweight plug-and-play rectifier, physically priors Spectral Rectification Super-Resolution Network (SR$^{2}$-Net), which can be attached to a wide range of HSI-SR models without modifying their architectures. SR$^{2}$-Net follows an enhance-then-rectify pipeline consisting of (i) Hierarchical Spectral-Spatial Synergy Attention (H-S$^{3}$A) to reinforce cross-band interactions and (ii) Manifold Consistency Rectification (MCR) to constrain the reconstructed spectra to a compact, physically plausible spectral manifold. In addition, we introduce a degradation-consistency loss to enforce data fidelity by encouraging the degraded SR output to match the observed low resolution input. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks and diverse backbones demonstrate consistent improvements in spectral fidelity and overall reconstruction quality with negligible computational overhead. Our code will be released upon publication.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, plug-and-play rectifier for hyperspectral SR (SR^2-Net) that enhances spatial resolution while enforcing spectral fidelity without altering base models.
AI.motivation: Address spectral inconsistency and physically implausible artifacts in HSI-SR without sacrificing model generality, flexibility, or requiring architecture-specific changes.
AI.method: Propose SR^2-Net as a modular add-on with an enhance-then-rectify pipeline: (i) Hierarchical Spectral-Spatial Synergy Attention (H-S^3A) to strengthen cross-band interactions, and (ii) Manifold Consistency Rectification (MCR) to keep reconstructions within a compact physical spectral manifold. A degradation-consistency loss enforces fidelity by aligning degraded SR outputs with the LR input.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across benchmarks and backbones show consistent gains in spectral fidelity and overall reconstruction quality with negligible computational overhead; code will be released.
AI.conclusion: SR^2-Net offers a general, flexible rectification module that improves spectral fidelity in HSI-SR without constraining base architectures, enabling better performance with minimal extra cost.

title: Disentangling perception and reasoning for improving data efficiency in learning cloth manipulation without demonstrations
summary: Cloth manipulation is a ubiquitous task in everyday life, but it remains an open challenge for robotics. The difficulties in developing cloth manipulation policies are attributed to the high-dimensional state space, complex dynamics, and high propensity to self-occlusion exhibited by fabrics. As analytical methods have not been able to provide robust and general manipulation policies, reinforcement learning (RL) is considered a promising approach to these problems. However, to address the large state space and complex dynamics, data-based methods usually rely on large models and long training times. The resulting computational cost significantly hampers the development and adoption of these methods. Additionally, due to the challenge of robust state estimation, garment manipulation policies often adopt an end-to-end learning approach with workspace images as input. While this approach enables a conceptually straightforward sim-to-real transfer via real-world fine-tuning, it also incurs a significant computational cost by training agents on a highly lossy representation of the environment state. This paper questions this common design choice by exploring an efficient and modular approach to RL for cloth manipulation. We show that, through careful design choices, model size and training time can be significantly reduced when learning in simulation. Furthermore, we demonstrate how the resulting simulation-trained model can be transferred to the real world. We evaluate our approach on the SoftGym benchmark and achieve significant performance improvements over available baselines on our task, while using a substantially smaller model.

AI.tldr: Presents an efficient modular RL pipeline for cloth manipulation that reduces model size and training time in simulation and enables sim-to-real transfer, achieving superior performance on SoftGym vs baselines.
AI.motivation: Cloth manipulation presents high-dimensional state, complex, and occlusion-prone dynamics; RL is promising but traditionally computationally expensive, especially with end-to-end visual inputs. The work argues for a more efficient, modular RL approach to enable practical training and transfer.
AI.method: Proposes a modular RL architecture with smaller networks and a decoupled perception–control design, trained in simulation with careful design choices to reduce computation. Demonstrates sim-to-real transfer by transferring the simulation-trained model to real-world cloth manipulation and evaluates on the SoftGym benchmark.
AI.result: The approach yields significant performance improvements over available baselines on SoftGym while using a substantially smaller model and reduced training time; real-world transfer is demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: Efficient, modular RL for cloth manipulation can substantially reduce computational cost without sacrificing performance, enabling practical sim-to-real deployment.

title: Intelli-Planner: Towards Customized Urban Planning via Large Language Model Empowered Reinforcement Learning
summary: Effective urban planning is crucial for enhancing residents' quality of life and ensuring societal stability, playing a pivotal role in the sustainable development of cities. Current planning methods heavily rely on human experts, which are time-consuming and labor-intensive, or utilize deep learning algorithms, often limiting stakeholder involvement. To bridge these gaps, we propose Intelli-Planner, a novel framework integrating Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) with large language models (LLMs) to facilitate participatory and customized planning scheme generation. Intelli-Planner utilizes demographic, geographic data, and planning preferences to determine high-level planning requirements and demands for each functional type. During training, a knowledge enhancement module is employed to enhance the decision-making capability of the policy network. Additionally, we establish a multi-dimensional evaluation system and employ LLM-based stakeholders for satisfaction scoring. Experimental validation across diverse urban settings shows that Intelli-Planner surpasses traditional baselines and achieves comparable performance to state-of-the-art DRL-based methods in objective metrics, while enhancing stakeholder satisfaction and convergence speed. These findings underscore the effectiveness and superiority of our framework, highlighting the potential for integrating the latest advancements in LLMs with DRL approaches to revolutionize tasks related to functional areas planning.

AI.tldr: Intelli-Planner combines deep reinforcement learning (DRL) with large language models (LLMs) to enable participatory, customized urban planning; it includes a knowledge enhancement module and LLM-based stakeholder evaluation, achieving strong objective performance and faster convergence while improving stakeholder satisfaction.
AI.motivation: Addresses the heavy reliance on human experts and limited stakeholder involvement in planning. Seeks to accelerate planning cycles, enhance participation, and improve customization by integrating DRL with LLM capabilities.
AI.method: A DRL-based policy network determines high-level planning requirements for functional types using demographic, geographic data, and planning preferences. A knowledge enhancement module augments decision-making. A multi-dimensional evaluation framework and LLM-based stakeholders provide satisfaction scoring. Training and evaluation are conducted across diverse urban settings, comparing against baselines and state-of-the-art DRL methods.
AI.result: Intelli-Planner surpasses traditional baselines and matches state-of-the-art DRL methods on objective metrics, while increasing stakeholder satisfaction and speeding up convergence.
AI.conclusion: The integration of LLMs with DRL is effective for functional-area planning, offering participatory, customized solutions and promising broader applications in urban planning tasks.

title: Human-LLM Collaborative Feature Engineering for Tabular Data
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to automate feature engineering in tabular learning. Given task-specific information, LLMs can propose diverse feature transformation operations to enhance downstream model performance. However, current approaches typically assign the LLM as a black-box optimizer, responsible for both proposing and selecting operations based solely on its internal heuristics, which often lack calibrated estimations of operation utility and consequently lead to repeated exploration of low-yield operations without a principled strategy for prioritizing promising directions. In this paper, we propose a human-LLM collaborative feature engineering framework for tabular learning. We begin by decoupling the transformation operation proposal and selection processes, where LLMs are used solely to generate operation candidates, while the selection is guided by explicitly modeling the utility and uncertainty of each proposed operation. Since accurate utility estimation can be difficult especially in the early rounds of feature engineering, we design a mechanism within the framework that selectively elicits and incorporates human expert preference feedback, comparing which operations are more promising, into the selection process to help identify more effective operations. Our evaluations on both the synthetic study and the real user study demonstrate that the proposed framework improves feature engineering performance across a variety of tabular datasets and reduces users' cognitive load during the feature engineering process.

AI.tldr: Human–LLM collaborative framework for tabular feature engineering that decouples operation proposal (LLM-generated candidates) from operation selection (utility/uncertainty modeling with optional human feedback).
AI.motivation: LLMs as black-box optimizers in feature engineering often lack calibrated estimates of operation utility, causing inefficient exploration; a principled selection mechanism and human-in-the-loop can improve efficiency and performance.
AI.method: Decouple proposal and selection: the LLM generates candidate transformation operations; a utility–uncertainty model evaluates proposals; selective human expert feedback is elicited to refine selection by signaling which operations seem more promising; evaluate via synthetic and real-user studies.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved feature engineering performance across diverse tabular datasets and reduces users' cognitive load.
AI.conclusion: A practical, interpretable framework that improves tabular feature engineering by separating generation from selection and incorporating human guidance to focus exploration on likely-beneficial transformations.

title: Dynamical Adapter Fusion: Constructing A Global Adapter for Pre-Trained Model-based Class-Incremental Learning
summary: Class-Incremental Learning (CIL) requires models to continuously acquire new classes without forgetting previously learned ones. A dominant paradigm involves freezing a pre-trained model and training lightweight, task-specific adapters. However, maintaining task-specific parameters hinders knowledge transfer and incurs high retrieval costs, while naive parameter fusion often leads to destructive interference and catastrophic forgetting. To address these challenges, we propose Dynamical Adapter Fusion (DAF) to construct a single robust global adapter. Grounded in the PAC-Bayes theorem, we derive a fusion mechanism that explicitly integrates three components: the optimized task-specific adapter parameters, the previous global adapter parameters, and the initialization parameters. We utilize the Taylor expansion of the loss function to derive the optimal fusion coefficients, dynamically achieving the best balance between stability and plasticity. Furthermore, we propose a Robust Initialization strategy to effectively capture global knowledge patterns. Experiments on multiple CIL benchmarks demonstrate that DAF achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance.

AI.tldr: DAF: a PAC-Bayes-guided fusion to build a single robust global adapter for class-incremental learning by dynamically combining task adapters, past global adapter, and initial parameters; uses Taylor expansion to derive optimal fusion coefficients and a Robust Initialization strategy; reports SOTA results.
AI.motivation: In CIL, freezing a pretrained backbone and training task-specific adapters preserves memory but harms knowledge transfer, increases retrieval cost, and naive fusion causes destructive interference and catastrophic forgetting. A single, robust global adapter is desirable for better transfer and efficiency.
AI.method: Propose Dynamical Adapter Fusion (DAF) that fuses three components: (i) task-specific adapter parameters, (ii) previous global adapter parameters, and (iii) initialization parameters. Grounded in PAC-Bayes, derive a fusion mechanism with coefficients optimized via a Taylor expansion of the loss to balance stability and plasticity. Introduce Robust Initialization to better capture global knowledge patterns and enable efficient knowledge transfer.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multiple class-incremental learning benchmarks shows state-of-the-art performance, surpassing baselines and demonstrating strong transfer with a single global adapter.
AI.conclusion: DAF yields a single robust global adapter by dynamically fusing task adapters, past global parameters, and initialization, guided by PAC-Bayes and Taylor-expanded loss, achieving SOTA in CIL while reducing parameter overhead and preserving knowledge.

title: Flocking behavior for dynamic and complex swarm structures
summary: Maintaining the formation of complex structures with multiple UAVs and achieving complex trajectories remains a major challenge. This work presents an algorithm for implementing the flocking behavior of UAVs based on the concept of Virtual Centroid to easily develop a structure for the flock. The approach builds on the classical virtual-based behavior, providing a theoretical framework for incorporating enhancements to dynamically control both the number of agents and the formation of the structure. Simulation tests and real-world experiments were conducted, demonstrating its simplicity even with complex formations and complex trajectories.

AI.tldr: Virtual Centroid-based flocking enabling dynamic formation and varying agent counts for UAVs, validated by simulations and real-world tests.
AI.motivation: Maintaining complex formations and trajectories with a multi-UAV system; need flexible, scalable control that accommodates changing numbers of agents and formation structures.
AI.method: An algorithm for UAV flocking based on Virtual Centroid, extending classical virtual-based behavior with a theoretical framework to dynamically adjust agent count and formation topology; includes simulations and real-world experiments.
AI.result: Demonstrates simplicity and effectiveness in achieving complex formations and trajectories under varying agent numbers, validated through both simulations and real-world tests.
AI.conclusion: A Virtual Centroid-based approach provides a simple, scalable, and adaptable method for multi-UAV flocking and formation control, suitable for complex trajectories.

title: Causal Discovery for Explainable AI: A Dual-Encoding Approach
summary: Understanding causal relationships among features is fundamental for explaining machine learning model decisions. However, traditional causal discovery methods face challenges with categorical variables due to numerical instability in conditional independence testing. We propose a dual-encoding causal discovery approach that addresses these limitations by running constraint-based algorithms with complementary encoding strategies and merging results through majority voting. Applied to the Titanic dataset, our method identifies causal structures that align with established explainable methods.

AI.tldr: A dual-encoding causal discovery method improves causal structure learning for categorical features by combining two complementary encodings via constraint-based discovery and majority voting; applied to Titanic dataset with results aligning to explainable methods.
AI.motivation: Causal discovery on categorical variables suffers from numerical instability in conditional independence tests. A robust approach is needed to reliably infer causal relations in such data, enabling explainability in ML models.
AI.method: Apply constraint-based causal discovery twice under complementary encodings of categorical features; merge edge decisions by majority vote across the two runs; validate on Titanic dataset and compare to established explainable methods.
AI.result: The fused approach yields causal structures that agree with established explainable methods on Titanic data, demonstrating stability and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: Dual-encoding with majority voting improves robustness of causal discovery for categorical data and produces causal graphs that align with explainable ML practices.

title: Signal from Structure: Exploiting Submodular Upper Bounds in Generative Flow Networks
summary: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets; GFNs) are a class of generative models that learn to sample compositional objects proportionally to their a priori unknown value, their reward. We focus on the case where the reward has a specified, actionable structure, namely that it is submodular. We show submodularity can be harnessed to retrieve upper bounds on the reward of compositional objects that have not yet been observed. We provide in-depth analyses of the probability of such bounds occurring, as well as how many unobserved compositional objects can be covered by a bound. Following the Optimism in the Face of Uncertainty principle, we then introduce SUBo-GFN, which uses the submodular upper bounds to train a GFN. We show that SUBo-GFN generates orders of magnitude more training data than classical GFNs for the same number of queries to the reward function. We demonstrate the effectiveness of SUBo-GFN in terms of distribution matching and high-quality candidate generation on synthetic and real-world submodular tasks.

AI.tldr: GFlowNets with submodular rewards (SUBo-GFN) leverage upper bounds from submodularity to generate more training data per reward query, improving efficiency and performance on submodular compositional tasks.
AI.motivation: To exploit submodular reward structure to bound unseen objects, enabling data-efficient training of GFlowNets and improved candidate generation.
AI.method: Derive probabilistic submodular upper bounds on rewards for unobserved compositional objects; analyze bound-coverage probability and bounds’ reach; adopt an Optimism in the Face of Uncertainty principle to train a GFlowNet using these bounds (SUBo-GFN); compare data efficiency and distribution matching to classical GFNs across synthetic and real-world submodular tasks.
AI.result: SUBo-GFN achieves orders of magnitude more training data per reward query than classical GFNs, while maintaining good distribution matching and generating high-quality candidates on both synthetic and real-world submodular tasks.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting submodular structure via upper bounds enables data-efficient GFlowNet training and enhanced candidate generation, offering a scalable approach for submodular compositional objectives.

title: Semantic-Guided Dynamic Sparsification for Pre-Trained Model-based Class-Incremental Learning
summary: Class-Incremental Learning (CIL) requires a model to continually learn new classes without forgetting old ones. A common and efficient solution freezes a pre-trained model and employs lightweight adapters, whose parameters are often forced to be orthogonal to prevent inter-task interference. However, we argue that this parameter-constraining method is detrimental to plasticity. To this end, we propose Semantic-Guided Dynamic Sparsification (SGDS), a novel method that proactively guides the activation space by governing the orientation and rank of its subspaces through targeted sparsification. Specifically, SGDS promotes knowledge transfer by encouraging similar classes to share a compact activation subspace, while simultaneously preventing interference by assigning non-overlapping activation subspaces to dissimilar classes. By sculpting class-specific sparse subspaces in the activation space, SGDS effectively mitigates interference without imposing rigid constraints on the parameter space. Extensive experiments on various benchmark datasets demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance of SGDS.

AI.tldr: SGDS dynamically sparsifies activation subspaces guided by semantic relations to balance knowledge transfer and interference in class-incremental learning, avoiding rigid parameter constraints and achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Conventional CIL methods often freeze a pre-trained backbone and impose orthogonality on adapters to prevent inter-task interference, which harms plasticity and limits transfer.
AI.method: SGDS proposes semantic-guided dynamic sparsification: shaping the orientation and rank of activation subspaces via targeted sparsity. It encourages similar classes to share compact activation subspaces and assigns non-overlapping subspaces to dissimilar classes, operating in activation space rather than constraining parameters.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets show state-of-the-art performance of SGDS, demonstrating improved balance between plasticity and stability without rigid parameter-space constraints.
AI.conclusion: SGDS effectively mitigates interference in CIL by sculpting class-specific sparse activation subspaces guided by semantic relations, achieving superior performance while preserving plasticity.

title: GAZELOAD A Multimodal Eye-Tracking Dataset for Mental Workload in Industrial Human-Robot Collaboration
summary: This article describes GAZELOAD, a multimodal dataset for mental workload estimation in industrial human-robot collaboration. The data were collected in a laboratory assembly testbed where 26 participants interacted with two collaborative robots (UR5 and Franka Emika Panda) while wearing Meta ARIA smart glasses. The dataset time-synchronizes eye-tracking signals (pupil diameter, fixations, saccades, eye gaze, gaze transition entropy, fixation dispersion index) with environmental real-time and continuous measurements (illuminance) and task and robot context (bench, task block, induced faults), under controlled manipulations of task difficulty and ambient conditions. For each participant and workload-graded task block, we provide CSV files with ocular metrics aggregated into 250 ms windows, environmental logs, and self-reported mental workload ratings on a 1-10 Likert scale, organized in participant-specific folders alongside documentation. These data can be used to develop and benchmark algorithms for mental workload estimation, feature extraction, and temporal modeling in realistic industrial HRC scenarios, and to investigate the influence of environmental factors such as lighting on eye-based workload markers.

AI.tldr: GAZELOAD is a synchronized multimodal dataset for mental workload estimation in industrial HRC, combining eye-tracking, environmental sensing, task/robot context, and subjective workload ratings across 26 participants in a lab with two collaborative robots, enabling development, benchmarking, and study of lighting effects on eye-based workload markers.
AI.motivation: To provide a realistic, richly labeled resource for developing and validating algorithms for mental workload estimation and understanding how environmental factors influence ocular indicators in human-robot collaboration.
AI.method: Experimental study in a lab with 26 participants interacting with UR5 and Panda robots, wearing Meta ARIA glasses; collection of eye-tracking signals (pupil diameter, fixations, saccades, gaze, gaze transition entropy, fixation dispersion index), environmental data (illuminance), and task/robot context (bench, task block, induced faults); data aggregated in 250 ms windows; self-reported workload ratings (1-10 Likert); dataset organized per participant with documentation.
AI.result: A publicly available dataset consisting of CSV files with ocular metrics (aggregated 250 ms), environmental logs, and workload ratings; suitable for developing/benchmarking mental workload estimation, feature extraction, and temporal modeling in realistic industrial HRC, and for assessing lighting effects on eye-based workload markers.
AI.conclusion: GAZELOAD provides a realistic, multimodal benchmark resource for HRC workload research, enabling robust algorithm development and insights into environmental impacts on eye-based workload cues.

title: Delegation Without Living Governance
summary: Most governance frameworks assume that rules can be defined in advance, systems can be engineered to comply, and accountability can be applied after outcomes occur. This model worked when machines replaced physical labor or accelerated calculation. It no longer holds when judgment itself is delegated to agentic AI systems operating at machine speed. The central issue here is not safety, efficiency, or employment. It is whether humans remain relevant participants in systems that increasingly shape social, economic, and political outcomes. This paper argues that static, compliance-based governance fails once decision-making moves to runtime and becomes opaque. It further argues that the core challenge is not whether AI is conscious, but whether humans can maintain meaningful communication, influence, and co-evolution with increasingly alien forms of intelligence. We position runtime governance, specifically, a newly proposed concept called the Governance Twin [1]; as a strong candidate for preserving human relevance, while acknowledging that accountability, agency, and even punishment must be rethought in this transition.

AI.tldr: Runtime governance via a Governance Twin offers a path to keep humans relevant in AI‑driven decision-making, but requires rethinking accountability, agency, and punishment.
AI.motivation: Traditional, static, compliance‑based governance fails when decision‑making shifts to runtime and becomes opaque as agentic AI operates at machine speed; humans risk losing influence over social, economic, and political outcomes.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis that introduces Governance Twin as a runtime governance approach and argues for its role in preserving human relevance amid opaque autonomous decision‑making; discusses rethinking accountability, agency, and punishment.
AI.result: Static governance is inadequate once decisions are made at runtime and become opaque. Governance Twin is proposed as a strong candidate for preserving human relevance, though it necessitates rethinking accountability, agency, and punishment in this transition.
AI.conclusion: To maintain meaningful human participation in AI‑shaped systems, runtime governance with Governance Twin should be pursued, with explicit attention to redefining accountability, agency, and punitive mechanisms to fit the runtime, opaque decision‑making landscape.

title: Textual Equilibrium Propagation for Deep Compound AI Systems
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as part of compound AI systems that coordinate multiple modules (e.g., retrievers, tools, verifiers) over long-horizon workflows. Recent approaches that propagate textual feedback globally (e.g., TextGrad) make it feasible to optimize such pipelines, but we find that performance degrades as system depth grows. In particular, long-horizon agentic workflows exhibit two depth-scaling failure modes: 1) exploding textual gradient, where textual feedback grows exponentially with depth, leading to prohibitively long message and amplifies evaluation biases; and 2) vanishing textual gradient, where limited long-context ability causes models overemphasize partial feedback and compression of lengthy feedback causes downstream messages to lose specificity gradually as they propagate many hops upstream. To mitigate these issues, we introduce Textual Equilibrium Propagation (TEP), a local learning principle inspired by Equilibrium Propagation in energy-based models. TEP includes two phases: 1) a free phase where a local LLM critics iteratively refine prompts until reaching equilibrium (no further improvements are suggested); and 2) a nudged phase which applies proximal prompt edits with bounded modification intensity, using task-level objectives that propagate via forward signaling rather than backward feedback chains. This design supports local prompt optimization followed by controlled adaptation toward global goals without the computational burden and signal degradation of global textual backpropagation. Across long-horizon QA benchmarks and multi-agent tool-use dataset, TEP consistently improves accuracy and efficiency over global propagation methods such as TextGrad. The gains grows with depth, while preserving the practicality of black-box LLM components in deep compound AI system.

AI.tldr: TEP introduces a local learning paradigm for deep compound AI systems with LLMs, addressing depth-scaling failures in global textual feedback (exploding/vanishing gradients). It uses a two-phase process (free phase with local critics reaching prompt equilibrium; nudged phase with bounded edits and forward-signaled objectives) to optimize prompts, improving accuracy and efficiency over TextGrad, with gains that grow with depth.
AI.motivation: To overcome degradation of performance when propagating textual feedback through many modules in long-horizon LLM-based pipelines, caused by exploding or vanishing textual gradients under global backpropagation-like methods.
AI.method: Two-phase local learning: (1) free phase where local LLM critics iteratively refine prompts until equilibrium; (2) nudged phase applying bounded prompt edits guided by task-level objectives that propagate via forward signaling, avoiding backpropagation of textual gradients and reducing computational burden.
AI.result: TEP yields consistent accuracy and efficiency improvements over global propagation baselines (e.g., TextGrad) on long-horizon QA benchmarks and multi-agent tool-use datasets; improvements scale with system depth and maintain practicality for black-box LLM components.
AI.conclusion: TEP demonstrates a scalable, gradient-stable alternative to global textual backpropagation for deep AI systems, enabling local optimization that aligns with global goals without suffering gradient explosion or vanishing, and is especially beneficial as pipeline depth increases.

title: LLM-Driven Scenario-Aware Planning for Autonomous Driving
summary: Hybrid planner switching framework (HPSF) for autonomous driving needs to reconcile high-speed driving efficiency with safe maneuvering in dense traffic. Existing HPSF methods often fail to make reliable mode transitions or sustain efficient driving in congested environments, owing to heuristic scene recognition and low-frequency control updates. To address the limitation, this paper proposes LAP, a large language model (LLM) driven, adaptive planning method, which switches between high-speed driving in low-complexity scenes and precise driving in high-complexity scenes, enabling high qualities of trajectory generation through confined gaps. This is achieved by leveraging LLM for scene understanding and integrating its inference into the joint optimization of mode configuration and motion planning. The joint optimization is solved using tree-search model predictive control and alternating minimization. We implement LAP by Python in Robot Operating System (ROS). High-fidelity simulation results show that the proposed LAP outperforms other benchmarks in terms of both driving time and success rate.

AI.tldr: An LLM-driven adaptive planning framework (LAP) for hybrid planner switching in autonomous driving, enabling mode switching between high-speed in simple scenes and precise driving in complex scenes; integrates LLM-based scene understanding into a joint optimization of mode configuration and motion planning, solved via tree-search MPC and alternating minimization; implemented in ROS; shows superior driving time and success rate in high-fidelity simulations.
AI.motivation: Current HPSF methods struggle with unreliable mode transitions and inefficiencies in dense traffic due to heuristic scene recognition and low-frequency control updates.
AI.method: Use a Large Language Model for scene understanding and embed its inference into a joint optimization of mode selection and motion planning; solve with tree-search model predictive control and alternating minimization; implemented in Python within ROS.
AI.result: High-fidelity simulations indicate LAP outperforms benchmarks in driving time and success rate.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven adaptive planning enables reliable, high-quality trajectory generation by aligning planner mode with scene complexity, demonstrating the feasibility of joint optimization-based HPSF with ROS implementation.

title: Just Ask: Curious Code Agents Reveal System Prompts in Frontier LLMs
summary: Autonomous code agents built on large language models are reshaping software and AI development through tool use, long-horizon reasoning, and self-directed interaction. However, this autonomy introduces a previously unrecognized security risk: agentic interaction fundamentally expands the LLM attack surface, enabling systematic probing and recovery of hidden system prompts that guide model behavior. We identify system prompt extraction as an emergent vulnerability intrinsic to code agents and present \textbf{\textsc{JustAsk}}, a self-evolving framework that autonomously discovers effective extraction strategies through interaction alone. Unlike prior prompt-engineering or dataset-based attacks, \textsc{JustAsk} requires no handcrafted prompts, labeled supervision, or privileged access beyond standard user interaction. It formulates extraction as an online exploration problem, using Upper Confidence Bound-based strategy selection and a hierarchical skill space spanning atomic probes and high-level orchestration. These skills exploit imperfect system-instruction generalization and inherent tensions between helpfulness and safety. Evaluated on \textbf{41} black-box commercial models across multiple providers, \textsc{JustAsk} consistently achieves full or near-complete system prompt recovery, revealing recurring design- and architecture-level vulnerabilities. Our results expose system prompts as a critical yet largely unprotected attack surface in modern agent systems.

AI.tldr: The paper presents JustAsk, an autonomous framework that discovers strategies to extract system prompts from code agents through online exploration, showing that system prompts are a widespread, attack-prone surface.
AI.motivation: As autonomous code agents leverage large language models, the attack surface expands to include the system prompts that guide model behavior. Prompt leakage or manipulation could undermine safety, enable jailbreaks, or reveal hidden instructions. Existing defenses (handcrafted prompts, curated datasets) are insufficient against self-evolving, interaction-based attacks.
AI.method: JustAsk formulates system-prompt extraction as an online exploration problem using an Upper Confidence Bound-based strategy. It employs a hierarchical skill space combining atomic probes and high-level orchestration to adaptively probe for prompts without any handcrafted prompts, labeled data, or privileged access beyond normal user interaction.
AI.result: Evaluated on 41 black-box commercial LLM models across multiple providers. JustAsk achieved full or near-complete recovery of system prompts, revealing design- and architecture-level vulnerabilities and indicating that system prompts are a critical, largely unprotected attack surface in modern agent systems.
AI.conclusion: System prompts represent a critical security vulnerability in modern autonomous agents. The work highlights the need for defense strategies at design/architecture level to mitigates prompt leakage and manipulation, and raises questions about how to secure prompt boundaries and prevent illicit extraction.

title: Out-of-Distribution Generalization in Graph Foundation Models
summary: Graphs are a fundamental data structure for representing relational information in domains such as social networks, molecular systems, and knowledge graphs. However, graph learning models often suffer from limited generalization when applied beyond their training distributions. In practice, distribution shifts may arise from changes in graph structure, domain semantics, available modalities, or task formulations. To address these challenges, graph foundation models (GFMs) have recently emerged, aiming to learn general-purpose representations through large-scale pretraining across diverse graphs and tasks. In this survey, we review recent progress on GFMs from the perspective of out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization. We first discuss the main challenges posed by distribution shifts in graph learning and outline a unified problem setting. We then organize existing approaches based on whether they are designed to operate under a fixed task specification or to support generalization across heterogeneous task formulations, and summarize the corresponding OOD handling strategies and pretraining objectives. Finally, we review common evaluation protocols and discuss open directions for future research. To the best of our knowledge, this paper is the first survey for OOD generalization in GFMs.

AI.tldr: A survey on graph foundation models (GFMs) centered on out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization, proposing a unified problem setting and a taxonomy of approaches by fixed-task vs multi-task generalization, with evaluation protocols and future directions.
AI.motivation: Graph learning suffers from distribution shifts across structure, semantics, modalities, and tasks; GFMs aim to learn general representations through large-scale pretraining across diverse graphs and tasks, necessitating understanding of OOD behavior.
AI.method: Comprehensive literature review and taxonomy; discuss OOD handling strategies, pretraining objectives, and problem settings; categorize methods by fixed-task vs heterogeneous-task generalization; outline evaluation protocols and open directions.
AI.result: Synthesizes progress on GFMs for OOD generalization; provides a unified problem setting; maps existing methods to OOD strategies and pretraining objectives; highlights evaluation standards and gaps.
AI.conclusion: First survey addressing OOD generalization in GFMs; identifies key challenges and future research directions to improve robust generalization across diverse graphs and tasks.

title: Rectifying Geometry-Induced Similarity Distortions for Real-World Aerial-Ground Person Re-Identification
summary:
Aerial-ground person re-identification (AG-ReID) is fundamentally challenged by extreme viewpoint and distance discrepancies between aerial and ground cameras, which induce severe geometric distortions and invalidate the assumption of a shared similarity space across views. Existing methods primarily rely on geometry-aware feature learning or appearance-conditioned prompting, while implicitly assuming that the geometry-invariant dot-product similarity used in attention mechanisms remains reliable under large viewpoint and scale variations. We argue that this assumption does not hold. Extreme camera geometry systematically distorts the query-key similarity space and degrades attention-based matching, even when feature representations are partially aligned.
  To address this issue, we introduce Geometry-Induced Query-Key Transformation (GIQT), a lightweight low-rank module that explicitly rectifies the similarity space by conditioning query-key interactions on camera geometry. Rather than modifying feature representations or the attention formulation itself, GIQT adapts the similarity computation to compensate for dominant geometry-induced anisotropic distortions. Building on this local similarity rectification, we further incorporate a geometry-conditioned prompt generation mechanism that provides global, view-adaptive representation priors derived directly from camera geometry.
  Experiments on four aerial-ground person re-identification benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently improves robustness under extreme and previously unseen geometric conditions, while introducing minimal computational overhead compared to state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: Introduces Geometry-Induced Query-Key Transformation (GIQT) to rectify geometry-induced distortions in AG-ReID by conditioning query-key interactions on camera geometry, plus a geometry-conditioned prompt mechanism, achieving robust cross-view matching with low overhead on four benchmarks.
AI.motivation: In aerial-ground ReID, extreme viewpoint and distance induce anisotropic geometry distortions that invalidate the assumption of a geometry-invariant similarity space used by attention. Existing methods relying on geometry-aware features or appearance-conditioned prompts do not fix the degraded query-key similarity space, leading to suboptimal cross-view matching.
AI.method: Propose GIQT, a lightweight low-rank module that explicitly rectifies the query-key similarity space by conditioning QK interactions on camera geometry, without altering feature representations or the base attention formulation. Complement with a geometry-conditioned prompt generation mechanism that yields global, view-adaptive priors derived from camera geometry.
AI.result: On four aerial-ground ReID benchmarks, the framework consistently improves robustness under extreme and unseen geometric conditions and does so with minimal computational overhead compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Explicit geometry-aware rectification of the similarity space, via GIQT and geometry-conditioned prompts, enhances cross-view AG-ReID performance and generalization with low added complexity.

title: Multi-Modular MANTA-RAY: A Modular Soft Surface Platform for Distributed Multi-Object Manipulation
summary: Manipulation surfaces control objects by actively deforming their shape rather than directly grasping them. While dense actuator arrays can generate complex deformations, they also introduce high degrees of freedom (DOF), increasing system complexity and limiting scalability. The MANTA-RAY (Manipulation with Adaptive Non-rigid Textile Actuation with Reduced Actuation densitY) platform addresses these challenges by leveraging a soft, fabric-based surface with reduced actuator density to manipulate fragile and heterogeneous objects. Previous studies focused on single-module implementations supported by four actuators, whereas the feasibility and benefits of a scalable, multi-module configuration remain unexplored. In this work, we present a distributed, modular, and scalable variant of the MANTA-RAY platform that maintains manipulation performance with a reduced actuator density. The proposed multi-module MANTA-RAY platform and control strategy employs object passing between modules and a geometric transformation driven PID controller that directly maps tilt-angle control outputs to actuator commands, eliminating the need for extensive data-driven or black-box training. We evaluate system performance in simulation across surface configurations of varying modules (3x3 and 4x4) and validate its feasibility through experiments on a physical 2x2 hardware prototype. The system successfully manipulates objects with diverse geometries, masses, and textures including fragile items such as eggs and apples as well as enabling parallel manipulation. The results demonstrate that the multi-module MANTA-RAY improves scalability and enables coordinated manipulation of multiple objects across larger areas, highlighting its potential for practical, real-world applications.

AI.tldr: A scalable, modular soft manipulation surface (MANTA-RAY) with reduced actuator density preserves manipulation performance across multiple modules, enabling object passing and parallel manipulation; validated by simulations (3x3, 4x4) and a 2x2 hardware prototype on diverse objects, including fragile items.
AI.motivation: High degrees of freedom and complexity in dense actuator arrays limit scalability for manipulation surfaces. A distributed, modular approach with reduced actuation density aims to maintain performance while expanding usable area and enabling multi-object manipulation.
AI.method: Develop a distributed, modular MANTA-RAY platform using a soft, fabric-based surface with reduced actuator density. Implement a geometry-based PID controller that maps tilt-angle outputs directly to actuator commands. Enable object passing between modules and assess via simulations (3x3 and 4x4 configurations) and hardware experiments on a 2x2 prototype.
AI.result: The multi-module MANTA-RAY preserves manipulation performance with fewer actuators, enables parallel and coordinated manipulation, and handles objects with varying geometry, mass, and texture (including fragile items like eggs and apples). Simulations show scalability gains; hardware validation confirms feasibility.
AI.conclusion: A modular, reduced-density actuator design can achieve scalable, coordinated manipulation across larger surfaces without relying on extensive data-driven training, making the approach viable for real-world tasks.

title: TIDE: Tuning-Integrated Dynamic Evolution for LLM-Based Automated Heuristic Design
summary: Although Large Language Models have advanced Automated Heuristic Design, treating algorithm evolution as a monolithic text generation task overlooks the coupling between discrete algorithmic structures and continuous numerical parameters. Consequently, existing methods often discard promising algorithms due to uncalibrated constants and suffer from premature convergence resulting from simple similarity metrics. To address these limitations, we propose TIDE, a Tuning-Integrated Dynamic Evolution framework designed to decouple structural reasoning from parameter optimization. TIDE features a nested architecture where an outer parallel island model utilizes Tree Similarity Edit Distance to drive structural diversity, while an inner loop integrates LLM-based logic generation with a differential mutation operator for parameter tuning. Additionally, a UCB-based scheduler dynamically prioritizes high-yield prompt strategies to optimize resource allocation. Extensive experiments across nine combinatorial optimization problems demonstrate that TIDE discovers heuristics that significantly outperform state-of-the-art baselines in solution quality while achieving improved search efficiency and reduced computational costs.

AI.tldr: TIDE is a tuning-integrated dynamic evolution framework that decouples structural reasoning from parameter optimization for combinatorial optimization, combining an outer island model with Tree Similarity Edit Distance, an inner LLM-based logic generation and differential mutation for parameters, and a UCB scheduler for prompt strategies; it yields superior heuristics with better efficiency across nine problems.
AI.motivation: Treating algorithm evolution as a single text-generation task couples discrete algorithmic structures with continuous parameters, causing uncalibrated constants and premature convergence. A decoupled approach aims to optimize structure and parameters separately to improve search performance.
AI.method: Proposes a nested architecture: (1) outer parallel island model driven by Tree Similarity Edit Distance to promote structural diversity; (2) inner loop uses LLM-based logic generation with a differential mutation operator for continuous parameter tuning; (3) a UCB-based scheduler prioritizes high-yield prompt strategies to allocate resources efficiently; evaluated on nine combinatorial optimization problems.
AI.result: Empirical results show TIDE discovers heuristics that significantly outperform state-of-the-art baselines in solution quality, with improved search efficiency and reduced computational costs.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling structure search from parameter optimization via TIDE yields superior heuristics and more efficient search across diverse combinatorial optimization problems, validating the proposed framework.

title: LOCUS: Low-Dimensional Model Embeddings for Efficient Model Exploration, Comparison, and Selection
summary: The rapidly growing ecosystem of Large Language Models (LLMs) makes it increasingly challenging to manage and utilize the vast and dynamic pool of models effectively. We propose LOCUS, a method that produces low-dimensional vector embeddings that compactly represent a language model's capabilities across queries. LOCUS is an attention-based approach that generates embeddings by a deterministic forward pass over query encodings and evaluation scores via an encoder model, enabling seamless incorporation of new models to the pool and refinement of existing model embeddings without having to perform any retraining. We additionally train a correctness predictor that uses model embeddings and query encodings to achieve state-of-the-art routing accuracy on unseen queries. Experiments show that LOCUS needs up to 4.8x fewer query evaluation samples than baselines to produce informative and robust embeddings. Moreover, the learned embedding space is geometrically meaningful: proximity reflects model similarity, enabling a range of downstream applications including model comparison and clustering, model portfolio selection, and resilient proxies of unavailable models.

AI.tldr: LOCUS is an attention-based framework that learns low-dimensional embeddings of LLM capabilities from query evaluations, enabling scalable model management with reduced sampling, plus a correctness predictor for routing unseen queries.
AI.motivation: To address the fast-growing, dynamic pool of LLMs by providing compact, updatable representations and reliable routing without retraining, facilitating comparison, clustering, and deployment decisions.
AI.method: Generate embeddings via a deterministic forward pass over query encodings and evaluation scores using an encoder; support adding new models without retraining; train a correctness predictor that uses embeddings and query encodings to route unseen queries.
AI.result: LOCUS achieves up to 4.8x reduction in required query evaluations to produce informative embeddings; the embedding space is geometrically meaningful, with proximity reflecting model similarity, enabling model comparison, clustering, portfolio selection, and proxies for unavailable models.
AI.conclusion: LOCUS offers an efficient, robust representation of a heterogeneous LLM pool, enabling scalable management and a range of downstream tasks through meaningful, low-dimensional embeddings.

title: Generation Enhances Understanding in Unified Multimodal Models via Multi-Representation Generation
summary: Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) integrate both visual understanding and generation within a single framework. Their ultimate aspiration is to create a cycle where understanding and generation mutually reinforce each other. While recent post-training methods have successfully leveraged understanding to enhance generation, the reverse direction of utilizing generation to improve understanding remains largely unexplored. In this work, we propose UniMRG (Unified Multi-Representation Generation), a simple yet effective architecture-agnostic post-training method. UniMRG enhances the understanding capabilities of UMMs by incorporating auxiliary generation tasks. Specifically, we train UMMs to generate multiple intrinsic representations of input images, namely pixel (reconstruction), depth (geometry), and segmentation (structure), alongside standard visual understanding objectives. By synthesizing these diverse representations, UMMs capture complementary information regarding appearance, spatial relations, and structural layout. Consequently, UMMs develop a deeper and more comprehensive understanding of visual inputs. Extensive experiments across diverse UMM architectures demonstrate that our method notably enhances fine-grained perception, reduces hallucinations, and improves spatial understanding, while simultaneously boosting generation capabilities.

AI.tldr: Proposes UniMRG: an architecture-agnostic post-training method that adds auxiliary generation tasks (pixel reconstruction, depth, segmentation) to unified multimodal models to improve understanding and generation.
AI.motivation: To close the loop between understanding and generation in unified multimodal models by enabling generation tasks to enhance understanding, addressing the current focus on leveraging understanding to boost generation.
AI.method: Post-training framework UniMRG; trains existing UMMs with auxiliary generation objectives to produce multiple intrinsic image representations (pixel, depth, segmentation) in addition to standard understanding tasks; architecture-agnostic.
AI.result: Across diverse UMM architectures, UniMRG improves fine-grained perception, reduces hallucinations, and enhances spatial understanding, while simultaneously boosting generation capabilities.
AI.conclusion: UniMRG demonstrates that incorporating multi-representation generation as an auxiliary objective yields reciprocal gains for understanding and generation in UMMs, offering a simple, effective, architecture-agnostic post-training approach.

title: Information Filtering via Variational Regularization for Robot Manipulation
summary: Diffusion-based visuomotor policies built on 3D visual representations have achieved strong performance in learning complex robotic skills. However, most existing methods employ an oversized denoising decoder. While increasing model capacity can improve denoising, empirical evidence suggests that it also introduces redundancy and noise in intermediate feature blocks. Crucially, we find that randomly masking backbone features at inference time (without changing training) can improve performance, confirming the presence of task-irrelevant noise in intermediate features. To this end, we propose Variational Regularization (VR), a lightweight module that imposes a timestep-conditioned Gaussian over backbone features and applies a KL-divergence regularizer, forming an adaptive information bottleneck. Extensive experiments on three simulation benchmarks (RoboTwin2.0, Adroit, and MetaWorld) show that, compared to the baseline DP3, our approach improves the success rate by 6.1% on RoboTwin2.0 and by 4.1% on Adroit and MetaWorld, achieving new state-of-the-art results. Real-world experiments further demonstrate that our method performs well in practical deployments. Code will released.

AI.tldr: Introduces Variational Regularization (VR) for diffusion-based visuomotor policies to suppress task-irrelevant noise in backbone features via a timestep-conditioned Gaussian prior and KL regularization, yielding an adaptive information bottleneck and improved performance.
AI.motivation: Excessive capacity in denoising decoders causes redundancy and noise in intermediate features. Inference-time masking suggests task-irrelevant noise can hurt performance; aim to enable robust representations without retraining or oversized decoders.
AI.method: Add VR module that imposes a timestep-conditioned Gaussian distribution over backbone features and applies a KL-divergence penalty, forming an adaptive information bottleneck. The approach is lightweight and compatible with existing diffusion-based policies, leveraging inference-time masking insights.
AI.result: On RoboTwin2.0, DP3 baseline gains 6.1% in success rate; on Adroit and MetaWorld, gains of 4.1%. Achieves state-of-the-art results across three simulation benchmarks; real-world experiments confirm practical viability; code to be released.
AI.conclusion: VR effectively reduces task-irrelevant noise in intermediate features with minimal computational overhead, enabling strong performance gains and better generalization for diffusion-based visuomotor policies in both simulation and real-world settings.

title: Position: Certifiable State Integrity in Cyber-Physical Systems -- Why Modular Sovereignty Solves the Plasticity-Stability Paradox
comment: 14 pages, (8 main text, 6 references and appendices), 2 figures
summary: The machine learning community has achieved remarkable success with universal foundation models for time-series and physical dynamics, largely overcoming earlier approximation barriers in smooth or slowly varying regimes through scale and specialized architectures. However, deploying these monolithic models in safety-critical Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), governed by non-stationary lifecycle dynamics and strict reliability requirements, reveals persistent challenges. Recent evidence shows that fine-tuning time-series foundation models induces catastrophic forgetting, degrading performance on prior regimes.
