Standard models continue to exhibit residual spectral bias, smoothing high-frequency discontinuities characteristic of incipient faults, while their opacity hinders formal verification and traceability demanded by safety standards (e.g., ISO 26262, IEC 61508). This position paper argues that the plasticity-stability paradox cannot be fully resolved by global parameter updates (whether via offline fine-tuning or online adaptation). Instead, we advocate a Modular Sovereignty paradigm: a library of compact, frozen regime-specific specialists combined via uncertainty-aware blending, which we term "HYDRA" (Hierarchical uncertaintY-aware Dynamics for Rapidly-Adapting systems). This paradigm ensures regime-conditional validity, rigorous disentanglement of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties, and modular auditability, offering a certifiable path for robust state integrity across the CPS lifecycle.

AI.tldr: Modular, regime-specific frozen specialists with uncertainty-aware blending (HYDRA) for robust, auditable CPS dynamics; avoids global fine-tuning and addresses plasticity-stability via a hierarchical, uncertainty-disentangled framework.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical CPS face non-stationary dynamics, reliability requirements, and limits of global fine-tuning: catastrophic forgetting, spectral bias obscuring faults, and opacity hindering verification. A regime-aware, modular approach is needed to achieve regime-conditional validity and certifiability.
AI.method: Proposes HYDRA: a library of compact, frozen regime-specific specialists integrated through uncertainty-aware blending. Emphasizes hierarchical uncertainty modeling and clear separation of aleatoric vs. epistemic uncertainty to enable modular auditability and certifiability across CPS lifecycles.
AI.result: Conceptual position paper outlining a framework (HYDRA) without empirical results. Claims regime-conditional validity, disentangled uncertainties, and modular auditability as a certifiable path for robust state integrity.
AI.conclusion: HYDRA offers a principled path to resolve the plasticity-stability paradox in safety-critical CPS by modularizing responsibility across regime-specific, frozen components and using uncertainty-aware fusion, facilitating verification, traceability, and lifecycle certification.

title: MapPFN: Learning Causal Perturbation Maps in Context
summary: Planning effective interventions in biological systems requires treatment-effect models that adapt to unseen biological contexts by identifying their specific underlying mechanisms. Yet single-cell perturbation datasets span only a handful of biological contexts, and existing methods cannot leverage new interventional evidence at inference time to adapt beyond their training data. To meta-learn a perturbation effect estimator, we present MapPFN, a prior-data fitted network (PFN) pretrained on synthetic data generated from a prior over causal perturbations. Given a set of experiments, MapPFN uses in-context learning to predict post-perturbation distributions, without gradient-based optimization. Despite being pretrained on in silico gene knockouts alone, MapPFN identifies differentially expressed genes, matching the performance of models trained on real single-cell data. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/marvinsxtr/MapPFN.

AI.tldr: MapPFN is a prior-data fitted network pretrained on synthetic perturbation data that enables zero-shot adaptation to unseen biological contexts via in-context learning for estimating post-perturbation expression distributions, without gradient-based optimization.
AI.motivation: Biological contexts vary and current perturbation-effect estimators struggle to generalize beyond training data; single-cell datasets cover few contexts, and there is a need to quickly incorporate new interventional evidence at inference.
AI.method: A PFN pretrained on synthetic data generated from a prior over causal perturbations. Given a set of experiments, MapPFN uses in-context learning to infer post-perturbation distributions without gradient-based optimization.
AI.result: MapPFN identifies differentially expressed genes and matches the performance of models trained on real single-cell data, despite training only on in silico knockouts. Code and data are provided.
AI.conclusion: PFN-based meta-learning with synthetic priors can generalize to unseen biological contexts and enable adaptation at inference time without optimization, offering a practical approach for perturbation-effect estimation in single-cell biology.

title: MPF-Net: Exposing High-Fidelity AI-Generated Video Forgeries via Hierarchical Manifold Deviation and Micro-Temporal Fluctuations
summary: With the rapid advancement of video generation models such as Veo and Wan, the visual quality of synthetic content has reached a level where macro-level semantic errors and temporal inconsistencies are no longer prominent. However, this does not imply that the distinction between real and cutting-edge high-fidelity fake is untraceable. We argue that AI-generated videos are essentially products of a manifold-fitting process rather than a physical recording. Consequently, the pixel composition logic of consecutive adjacent frames residual in AI videos exhibits a structured and homogenous characteristic. We term this phenomenon `Manifold Projection Fluctuations' (MPF). Driven by this insight, we propose a hierarchical dual-path framework that operates as a sequential filtering process. The first, the Static Manifold Deviation Branch, leverages the refined perceptual boundaries of Large-Scale Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) to capture residual spatial anomalies or physical violations that deviate from the natural real-world manifold (off-manifold). For the remaining high-fidelity videos that successfully reside on-manifold and evade spatial detection, we introduce the Micro-Temporal Fluctuation Branch as a secondary, fine-grained filter. By analyzing the structured MPF that persists even in visually perfect sequences, our framework ensures that forgeries are exposed regardless of whether they manifest as global real-world manifold deviations or subtle computational fingerprints.

AI.tldr: Proposes Manifold Projection Fluctuations (MPF) as a fundamental artifact of AI-generated videos and introduces a two-branch detector (Static Manifold Deviation Branch and Micro-Temporal Fluctuation Branch) to expose forgeries, addressing both off-manifold spatial anomalies and on-manifold temporal fingerprints through sequential filtering.
AI.motivation: As video generation quality increases, macro artifacts vanish, but underlying manifold-fitting processes leave detectable structured residuals. There is a need for robust forensic cues beyond perceptual realism, leveraging advanced perceptual models to detect subtle deviations.
AI.method: A hierarchical dual-path framework: (1) Static Manifold Deviation Branch uses Large-Scale Vision Foundation Models to capture off-manifold spatial deviations from natural real-world manifolds; (2) Micro-Temporal Fluctuation Branch analyzes fine-grained temporal fluctuations to detect residual MPF on-manifold sequences; detection is performed as a sequential filtering pipeline to reveal forgeries in both regimes.
AI.result: Conceptual framework with a sequential two-branch detector designed to expose forgeries across both off-manifold and on-manifold cases; no quantitative results are provided in the abstract, but the approach aims to robustly identify MPF-based artifacts.
AI.conclusion: MPF-based detection provides comprehensive forensic coverage by targeting both spatial-manifold deviations and subtle temporal fingerprints, improving robustness when high-fidelity AI-generated videos appear perceptually indistinguishable from real footage.

title: MoE-ACT: Improving Surgical Imitation Learning Policies through Supervised Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Imitation learning has achieved remarkable success in robotic manipulation, yet its application to surgical robotics remains challenging due to data scarcity, constrained workspaces, and the need for an exceptional level of safety and predictability. We present a supervised Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture designed for phase-structured surgical manipulation tasks, which can be added on top of any autonomous policy. Unlike prior surgical robot learning approaches that rely on multi-camera setups or thousands of demonstrations, we show that a lightweight action decoder policy like Action Chunking Transformer (ACT) can learn complex, long-horizon manipulation from less than 150 demonstrations using solely stereo endoscopic images, when equipped with our architecture. We evaluate our approach on the collaborative surgical task of bowel grasping and retraction, where a robot assistant interprets visual cues from a human surgeon, executes targeted grasping on deformable tissue, and performs sustained retraction. We benchmark our method against state-of-the-art Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models and the standard ACT baseline. Our results show that generalist VLAs fail to acquire the task entirely, even under standard in-distribution conditions. Furthermore, while standard ACT achieves moderate success in-distribution, adopting a supervised MoE architecture significantly boosts its performance, yielding higher success rates in-distribution and demonstrating superior robustness in out-of-distribution scenarios, including novel grasp locations, reduced illumination, and partial occlusions. Notably, it generalizes to unseen testing viewpoints and also transfers zero-shot to ex vivo porcine tissue without additional training, offering a promising pathway toward in vivo deployment. To support this, we present qualitative preliminary results of policy roll-outs during in vivo porcine surgery.

AI.tldr: A supervised MoE on an Action Chunking Transformer enables data-efficient, long-horizon surgical manipulation from <150 stereo-endoscopy demonstrations, outperforming Vision-Language-Action models and baseline ACT, with strong generalization and zero-shot ex vivo transfer; preliminary in vivo results.
AI.motivation: Data scarcity, restricted workspaces, and stringent safety/predictability requirements in surgical robotics demand data-efficient, robust learning that can operate with limited visual input and demonstrations without relying on multi-camera setups.
AI.method: Phase-structured surgical tasks are tackled by a lightweight supervised Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture added on top of an autonomous policy. The Action Chunking Transformer (ACT) serves as the action decoder. Training uses fewer than 150 demonstrations from stereo endoscopic images. Evaluation centers on a collaborative bowel grasping and retraction task, comparing against Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models and the standard ACT baseline, and assessing in-distribution and out-of-distribution generalization (novel grasp locations, illumination changes, occlusions, unseen viewpoints). Zero-shot transfer to ex vivo porcine tissue is tested, with qualitative in vivo policy roll-outs reported.
AI.result: The supervised MoE significantly improves ACT performance in-distribution and enhances robustness in out-of-distribution scenarios. Vision-Language-Action models fail to acquire the task under standard conditions. The approach generalizes to unseen viewpoints and enables zero-shot transfer to ex vivo porcine tissue. Qualitative preliminary in vivo results are presented.
AI.conclusion: The MoE-on-ACT architecture provides data-efficient, robust generalization for phase-structured surgical manipulation and shows promise toward safe, in vivo deployment, warranting further rigorous validation and safety analyses.

title: Drive-KD: Multi-Teacher Distillation for VLMs in Autonomous Driving
summary: Autonomous driving is an important and safety-critical task, and recent advances in LLMs/VLMs have opened new possibilities for reasoning and planning in this domain. However, large models demand substantial GPU memory and exhibit high inference latency, while conventional supervised fine-tuning (SFT) often struggles to bridge the capability gaps of small models. To address these limitations, we propose Drive-KD, a framework that decomposes autonomous driving into a "perception-reasoning-planning" triad and transfers these capabilities via knowledge distillation. We identify layer-specific attention as the distillation signal to construct capability-specific single-teacher models that outperform baselines. Moreover, we unify these single-teacher settings into a multi-teacher distillation framework and introduce asymmetric gradient projection to mitigate cross-capability gradient conflicts. Extensive evaluations validate the generalization of our method across diverse model families and scales. Experiments show that our distilled InternVL3-1B model, with ~42 times less GPU memory and ~11.4 times higher throughput, achieves better overall performance than the pretrained 78B model from the same family on DriveBench, and surpasses GPT-5.1 on the planning dimension, providing insights toward efficient autonomous driving VLMs.

AI.tldr: Drive-KD proposes a triad-based knowledge distillation framework for autonomous driving VLMs, using layer-specific attention signals to create single-teacher models, extended to a multi-teacher setup with asymmetric gradient projection; achieves substantial efficiency gains while maintaining or surpassing large baselines on planning tasks.
AI.motivation: Large LLM/VLMs are memory- and compute-intensive; small models struggle to bridge capability gaps, especially in safety-critical autonomous driving. A task-aligned, efficient distillation strategy is needed.
AI.method: Decompose perception-reasoning-planning; use layer-specific attention as distillation signal to train single-teacher models; unify into multi-teacher distillation; apply asymmetric gradient projection to mitigate cross-capability gradient conflicts.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show generalization across model families and scales. Distilled InternVL3-1B uses ~42x less GPU memory and ~11.4x higher throughput, outperforming a pretrained 78B model on DriveBench and surpassing GPT-5.1 on planning.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a memory- and compute-efficient pathway to competitive autonomous driving VLMs via knowledge distillation, with strong cross-model generalization and planning performance.

title: Safety Generalization Under Distribution Shift in Safe Reinforcement Learning: A Diabetes Testbed
summary: Safe Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithms are typically evaluated under fixed training conditions. We investigate whether training-time safety guarantees transfer to deployment under distribution shift, using diabetes management as a safety-critical testbed. We benchmark safe RL algorithms on a unified clinical simulator and reveal a safety generalization gap: policies satisfying constraints during training frequently violate safety requirements on unseen patients. We demonstrate that test-time shielding, which filters unsafe actions using learned dynamics models, effectively restores safety across algorithms and patient populations. Across eight safe RL algorithms, three diabetes types, and three age groups, shielding achieves Time-in-Range gains of 13--14\% for strong baselines such as PPO-Lag and CPO while reducing clinical risk index and glucose variability. Our simulator and benchmark provide a platform for studying safety under distribution shift in safety-critical control domains. Code is available at https://github.com/safe-autonomy-lab/GlucoSim and https://github.com/safe-autonomy-lab/GlucoAlg.

AI.tldr: Safe RL policies trained under distribution shift can violate safety at deployment; test-time shielding with learned dynamics models restores safety and yields clinical improvements.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether training-time safety guarantees generalize under distribution shift in a safety-critical diabetes management setting.
AI.method: Benchmark eight safe RL algorithms in a unified clinical simulator across three diabetes types and three age groups; implement test-time shielding that filters unsafe actions using learned dynamics models; assess safety, Time-in-Range, and clinical risk metrics.
AI.result: Identified a safety generalization gap where training-time constraints do not guarantee safety on unseen patients; test-time shielding consistently restored safety across algorithms and populations, achieving Time-in-Range gains of 13–14% for strong baselines (PPO-Lag, CPO) and reducing clinical risk and glucose variability; platform and benchmarks released.
AI.conclusion: GlucoSim enables studying safety under distribution shift in safety-critical control; shielding is effective and generalizes across patient types, supporting broader adoption of safe RL in clinical deployment.

title: From Implicit Ambiguity to Explicit Solidity: Diagnosing Interior Geometric Degradation in Neural Radiance Fields for Dense 3D Scene Understanding
summary: Neural Radiance Fields (NeRFs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for multi-view reconstruction, complementing classical photogrammetric pipelines based on Structure-from-Motion (SfM) and Multi-View Stereo (MVS). However, their reliability for quantitative 3D analysis in dense, self-occluding scenes remains poorly understood. In this study, we identify a fundamental failure mode of implicit density fields under heavy occlusion, which we term Interior Geometric Degradation (IGD). We show that transmittance-based volumetric optimization satisfies photometric supervision by reconstructing hollow or fragmented structures rather than solid interiors, leading to systematic instance undercounting. Through controlled experiments on synthetic datasets with increasing occlusion, we demonstrate that state-of-the-art mask-supervised NeRFs saturate at approximately 89% instance recovery in dense scenes, despite improved surface coherence and mask quality. To overcome this limitation, we introduce an explicit geometric pipeline based on Sparse Voxel Rasterization (SVRaster), initialized from SfM feature geometry. By projecting 2D instance masks onto an explicit voxel grid and enforcing geometric separation via recursive splitting, our approach preserves physical solidity and achieves a 95.8% recovery rate in dense clusters. A sensitivity analysis using degraded segmentation masks further shows that explicit SfM-based geometry is substantially more robust to supervision failure, recovering 43% more instances than implicit baselines. These results demonstrate that explicit geometric priors are a prerequisite for reliable quantitative analysis in highly self-occluding 3D scenes.

AI.tldr: Explicit geometric priors via Sparse Voxel Rasterization outperform implicit NeRFs under heavy occlusion, mitigating Interior Geometric Degradation (IGD) and improving instance recovery in dense scenes.
AI.motivation: Assess reliability of neural implicit representations for quantitative 3D analysis in self-occluding dense scenes; identify the failure mode of density fields; improve robustness with explicit geometry.
AI.method: Diagnose IGD in density-field NeRFs; perform controlled synthetic occlusion experiments; compare mask-supervised NeRFs to an explicit SVRaster pipeline initialized from SfM; project 2D masks onto voxel grid; enforce geometric separation via recursive splitting; analyze robustness to degraded supervision.
AI.result: Implicit baselines saturate ~89% instance recovery in dense scenes; SVRaster explicit pipeline achieves 95.8% recovery; explicit geometry more robust, recovering 43% more instances under degraded masks.
AI.conclusion: Explicit geometric priors are necessary for reliable quantitative analysis in highly self-occluding 3D scenes; SVRaster demonstrates a practical route to preserve solidity and improve instance recovery over purely implicit methods.

title: Macro-Scale Electrostatic Origami Motor
summary: Foldable robots have been an active area of robotics research due to their high volume-to-mass ratio, easy packability, and shape adaptability. For locomotion, previously developed foldable robots have either embedded linear actuators in, or attached non-folding rotary motors to, their structure. Further, those actuators directly embedded in the structure of the folding medium all contributed to linear or folding motion, not to continuous rotary motion. On the macro-scale there has not yet been a folding continuous rotary actuator. This paper details the development and testing of the first macro-scale origami rotary motor that can be folded flat, and then unfurled to operate. Using corona discharge for torque production, the prototype motor achieved an expansion ratio of 2.5:1, reached a top speed of 1440 rpm when driven at -29 kV, and exhibited a maximum output torque over 0.15 mN m with an active component torque density of 0.04 Nm/kg.

AI.tldr: Introduces the first macro-scale origami rotary motor that folds flat and unfurls to operate, powered by corona discharge, achieving 2.5:1 expansion, up to 1440 rpm at -29 kV, and torque >0.15 mN·m with 0.04 Nm/kg torque density.
AI.motivation: Overcome the lack of macro-scale, continuous rotary actuation in foldable/origami robots. Prior work provided linear actuation or non-folding rotary motors, but no folding rotary motor.
AI.method: Design and test a foldable origami mechanism driven by corona-discharge torque. Quantify expansion ratio, rotational speed, torque, and torque density.
AI.result: Expansion ratio 2.5:1; top speed 1440 rpm at -29 kV; maximum output torque >0.15 mN·m; active component torque density 0.04 Nm/kg.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of macro-scale origami rotary actuation via corona discharge, enabling foldable robots with rotary motion and outlining potential for further optimization and integration.

title: White-Box Op-Amp Design via Human-Mimicking Reasoning
summary: This brief proposes \emph{White-Op}, an interpretable operational amplifier (op-amp) parameter design framework based on the human-mimicking reasoning of large-language-model agents. We formalize the implicit human reasoning mechanism into explicit steps of \emph{\textbf{introducing hypothetical constraints}}, and develop an iterative, human-like \emph{\textbf{hypothesis-verification-decision}} workflow. Specifically, the agent is guided to introduce hypothetical constraints to derive and properly regulate positions of symbolically tractable poles and zeros, thus formulating a closed-form mathematical optimization problem, which is then solved programmatically and verified via simulation. Theory-simulation result analysis guides the decision-making for refinement. Experiments on 9 op-amp topologies show that, unlike the uninterpretable black-box baseline which finally fails in 5 topologies, White-Op achieves reliable, interpretable behavioral-level designs with only 8.52\% theoretical prediction error and the design functionality retains after transistor-level mapping for all topologies. White-Op is open-sourced at \textcolor{blue}{https://github.com/zhchenfdu/whiteop}.

AI.tldr: An interpretable, LLM-guided framework for op-amp parameter design (White-Op) that uses hypothetical constraints and a hypothesis-verification-decision loop to place poles/zeros; solves a closed-form optimization; validated by simulation; outperforms a black-box baseline in 9 topologies; open-source.
AI.motivation: Address the need for interpretable deep reasoning in analog circuit design and leverage human-mimicking LLM reasoning to structure parameter design; reduce reliance on opaque black-box optimization while ensuring reliable transistor-level mapping.
AI.method: Formalize human reasoning as explicit steps: introduce hypothetical constraints to bound symbolically tractable pole/zero positions, derive a closed-form optimization problem, solve programmatically, and verify results via circuit simulation. Employ a hypothesis-verification-decision loop with theory-guided refinement, applied to 9 op-amp topologies and compared against a black-box baseline.
AI.result: White-Op yields reliable, interpretable behavioral-level designs with 8.52% theoretical prediction error. Functionality remains after transistor-level mapping for all topologies. The uninterpretable black-box baseline fails in 5 of 9 topologies.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of an interpretable, LLM-guided design workflow for analog circuits, combining symbolic reasoning, optimization, and simulation. Open-source availability facilitates replication and further exploration.

title: TRACE: Trajectory Recovery for Continuous Mechanism Evolution in Causal Representation Learning
summary: Temporal causal representation learning methods assume that causal mechanisms switch instantaneously between discrete domains, yet real-world systems often exhibit continuous mechanism transitions. For example, a vehicle's dynamics evolve gradually through a turning maneuver, and human gait shifts smoothly from walking to running. We formalize this setting by modeling transitional mechanisms as convex combinations of finitely many atomic mechanisms, governed by time-varying mixing coefficients. Our theoretical contributions establish that both the latent causal variables and the continuous mixing trajectory are jointly identifiable. We further propose TRACE, a Mixture-of-Experts framework where each expert learns one atomic mechanism during training, enabling recovery of mechanism trajectories at test time. This formulation generalizes to intermediate mechanism states never observed during training. Experiments on synthetic and real-world data demonstrate that TRACE recovers mixing trajectories with up to 0.99 correlation, substantially outperforming discrete-switching baselines.

AI.tldr: TRACE models continuous transitions of causal mechanisms as convex mixtures of atomic mechanisms; jointly identifiable latent variables and mixing trajectory; uses Mixture-of-Experts to recover mechanism trajectories; generalizes to unseen intermediate states; outperforms discrete-switching baselines with high correlation (up to 0.99).
AI.motivation: Real-world systems often exhibit smooth, continuous changes in dynamics and causal structure, not instantaneous discrete switches; current discrete-switch models struggle to identify and track evolving mechanisms.
AI.method: Propose TRACE, a Mixture-of-Experts framework where each expert learns one atomic mechanism. Mixing coefficients are time-varying and convex, yielding a continuous mechanism trajectory. The model jointly identifies latent causal variables and the mixing trajectory and can interpolate to unseen intermediate states.
AI.result: Theoretically identifiable latent variables and mixing trajectory. Empirically, TRACE recovers mixing trajectories with up to 0.99 correlation on synthetic and real data, greatly outperforming discrete-switching baselines.
AI.conclusion: TRACE provides a principled approach to continuous causal mechanism transitions, with identifiability guarantees and strong empirical performance, including generalization to intermediate states not observed during training.

title: MultiModal Fine-tuning with Synthetic Captions
summary: In this paper, we address a fundamental gap between pre-training and fine-tuning of deep neural networks: while pre-training has shifted from unimodal to multimodal learning with enhanced visual understanding, fine-tuning predominantly remains unimodal, limiting the benefits of rich pre-trained representations. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel approach that transforms unimodal datasets into multimodal ones using Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to generate synthetic image captions for fine-tuning models with a multimodal objective. Our method employs carefully designed prompts incorporating class labels and domain context to produce high-quality captions tailored for classification tasks. Furthermore, we introduce a supervised contrastive loss function that explicitly encourages clustering of same-class representations during fine-tuning, along with a new inference technique that leverages class-averaged text embeddings from multiple synthetic captions per image. Extensive experiments across 13 image classification benchmarks demonstrate that our approach outperforms baseline methods, with particularly significant improvements in few-shot learning scenarios. Our work establishes a new paradigm for dataset enhancement that effectively bridges the gap between multimodal pre-training and fine-tuning. Our code is available at https://github.com/s-enmt/MMFT.

AI.tldr: We propose MMFT, a method to fine-tune unimodal models by converting unimodal data into multimodal data via synthetic captions generated by Multimodal LLMs, using a multimodal objective, a supervised contrastive loss, and class-averaged text embeddings for inference; achieving strong gains across 13 image classification benchmarks, especially in few-shot settings.
AI.motivation: There is a gap between multimodal pre-training (rich visual-language representations) and unimodal fine-tuning (usually-only visual). Bridging this gap by creating multimodal fine-tuning data from unimodal datasets can leverage pretrained multimodal signals for classification tasks.
AI.method: 1) Use Multimodal LLMs to generate synthetic image captions for unimodal datasets using carefully designed prompts that include class labels and domain context. 2) Train with a multimodal objective (image-caption pairs) and a supervised contrastive loss that encourages same-class representations to cluster. 3) For inference, compute class-averaged text embeddings from multiple synthetic captions per image to improve classification.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 13 image classification benchmarks shows the proposed method outperforms baselines, with particularly notable improvements in few-shot scenarios.
AI.conclusion: This work introduces a dataset-enhancement paradigm that bridges multimodal pre-training and fine-tuning, enabling unimodal datasets to benefit from multimodal representations; code is released at the provided GitHub URL.

title: PocketDP3: Efficient Pocket-Scale 3D Visuomotor Policy
summary: Recently, 3D vision-based diffusion policies have shown strong capability in learning complex robotic manipulation skills. However, a common architectural mismatch exists in these models: a tiny yet efficient point-cloud encoder is often paired with a massive decoder. Given a compact scene representation, we argue that this may lead to substantial parameter waste in the decoder. Motivated by this observation, we propose PocketDP3, a pocket-scale 3D diffusion policy that replaces the heavy conditional U-Net decoder used in prior methods with a lightweight Diffusion Mixer (DiM) built on MLP-Mixer blocks. This architecture enables efficient fusion across temporal and channel dimensions, significantly reducing model size. Notably, without any additional consistency distillation techniques, our method supports two-step inference without sacrificing performance, improving practicality for real-time deployment. Across three simulation benchmarks--RoboTwin2.0, Adroit, and MetaWorld--PocketDP3 achieves state-of-the-art performance with fewer than 1% of the parameters of prior methods, while also accelerating inference. Real-world experiments further demonstrate the practicality and transferability of our method in real-world settings. Code will be released.

AI.tldr: PocketDP3 reduces the size of 3D diffusion policies by replacing a heavy conditional U-Net with a lightweight Diffusion Mixer (DiM) based on MLP-Mixer blocks, enabling efficient fusion across temporal and channel dimensions. It achieves state-of-the-art results on RoboTwin2.0, Adroit, and MetaWorld with <1% of the parameters of prior methods, supports two-step inference without extra consistency distillation, and shows real-world transferability.
AI.motivation: Architectural mismatch in 3D diffusion policies: small, efficient point-cloud encoders are paired with large decoders, causing parameter waste. A compact, real-time-friendly diffusion policy is needed for practical robotic manipulation.
AI.method: Introduce Diffusion Mixer (DiM) built from MLP-Mixer blocks to fuse temporal and channel information in a lightweight decoder, replacing the heavy conditional U-Net. Enable two-step diffusion inference without additional distillation.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on RoboTwin2.0, Adroit, and MetaWorld with fewer than 1% of the parameters of prior methods and faster inference. Real-world experiments demonstrate practicality and transferability.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that compact diffusion policies with DiM can match or exceed prior performance while dramatically reducing parameter count, enabling real-time deployment; code will be released.

title: Modeling Endogenous Logic: Causal Neuro-Symbolic Reasoning Model for Explainable Multi-Behavior Recommendation
summary: Existing multi-behavior recommendations tend to prioritize performance at the expense of explainability, while current explainable methods suffer from limited generalizability due to their reliance on external information. Neuro-Symbolic integration offers a promising avenue for explainability by combining neural networks with symbolic logic rule reasoning. Concurrently, we posit that user behavior chains inherently embody an endogenous logic suitable for explicit reasoning. However, these observational multiple behaviors are plagued by confounders, causing models to learn spurious correlations. By incorporating causal inference into this Neuro-Symbolic framework, we propose a novel Causal Neuro-Symbolic Reasoning model for Explainable Multi-Behavior Recommendation (CNRE). CNRE operationalizes the endogenous logic by simulating a human-like decision-making process. Specifically, CNRE first employs hierarchical preference propagation to capture heterogeneous cross-behavior dependencies. Subsequently, it models the endogenous logic rule implicit in the user's behavior chain based on preference strength, and adaptively dispatches to the corresponding neural-logic reasoning path (e.g., conjunction, disjunction). This process generates an explainable causal mediator that approximates an ideal state isolated from confounding effects. Extensive experiments on three large-scale datasets demonstrate CNRE's significant superiority over state-of-the-art baselines, offering multi-level explainability from model design and decision process to recommendation results.

AI.tldr: Proposes CNRE, a causal neuro-symbolic framework for explainable multi-behavior recommendation; combines hierarchical preference propagation with endogenous logic rules and causal mediation to handle confounders and provide explanations; shows superior performance and multi-level explainability on three datasets.
AI.motivation: Addresses the trade-off between accuracy and explainability in multi-behavior recommender systems; leverages endogenous logic in user behavior chains; mitigates confounding biases via causal inference within a neuro-symbolic framework; aims for generalizable explanations.
AI.method: CNRE consists of hierarchical preference propagation to capture cross-behavior dependencies; endogenous logic rules inferred from preference strength; adaptive dispatch to neural-logic paths (e.g., conjunction/disjunction); causal mediator that isolates confounding effects; reports explainability at model design, decision process, and recommendation results.
AI.result: Empirical validation on three large-scale datasets showing significant improvements over state-of-the-art baselines; provides multi-level explainability from design to decisions.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates viability of integrating causal inference with neuro-symbolic reasoning for explainable multi-behavior recommendation; offers interpretable decision pathways and mitigates confounding.

title: Smooth Dynamic Cutoffs for Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials
summary: Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have proven to be wildly useful for molecular dynamics simulations, powering countless drug and materials discovery applications. However, MLIPs face two primary bottlenecks preventing them from reaching realistic simulation scales: inference time and memory consumption. In this work, we address both issues by challenging the long-held belief that the cutoff radius for the MLIP must be held to a fixed, constant value. For the first time, we introduce a dynamic cutoff formulation that still leads to stable, long timescale molecular dynamics simulation. In introducing the dynamic cutoff, we are able to induce sparsity onto the underlying atom graph by targeting a specific number of neighbors per atom, significantly reducing both memory consumption and inference time. We show the effectiveness of a dynamic cutoff by implementing it onto 4 state of the art MLIPs: MACE, Nequip, Orbv3, and TensorNet, leading to 2.26x less memory consumption and 2.04x faster inference time, depending on the model and atomic system. We also perform an extensive error analysis and find that the dynamic cutoff models exhibit minimal accuracy dropoff compared to their fixed cutoff counterparts on both materials and molecular datasets. All model implementations and training code will be fully open sourced.

AI.tldr: Dynamic cutoff in MLIPs induces sparsity by targeting a fixed number of neighbors per atom, reducing memory and inference time while preserving accuracy; demonstrated on MACE, Nequip, OrbV3, TensorNet with up to ~2x speedup and memory reduction; code open source.
AI.motivation: Address major bottlenecks in MLIPs (inference time and memory) by removing the fixed cutoff constraint and enabling scalable MD across materials and molecules.
AI.method: Introduce a dynamic cutoff mechanism that selects a per-atom neighborhood to maintain a target neighbor count, leading to sparse graphs; implement across 4 MLIPs; evaluate on materials and molecular datasets; report memory and timing; analyze accuracy.
AI.result: 2.26x memory reduction and 2.04x faster inference (depends on model/system); minimal accuracy loss vs fixed cutoff.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic cutoff provides stable, scalable MD with state-of-the-art MLIPs; broad applicability; code will be open-sourced.

title: Spava: Accelerating Long-Video Understanding via Sequence-Parallelism-aware Approximate Attention
summary: The efficiency of long-video inference remains a critical bottleneck, mainly due to the dense computation in the prefill stage of Large Multimodal Models (LMMs). Existing methods either compress visual embeddings or apply sparse attention on a single GPU, yielding limited acceleration or degraded performance and restricting LMMs from handling longer, more complex videos. To overcome these issues, we propose Spava, a sequence-parallel framework with optimized attention that accelerates long-video inference across multiple GPUs. By distributing approximate attention, Spava reduces computation and increases parallelism, enabling efficient processing of more visual embeddings without compression and thereby improving task performance. System-level optimizations, such as load balancing and fused forward passes, further unleash the potential of Spava, delivering speedups of 12.72x, 1.70x, and 1.18x over FlashAttn, ZigZagRing, and APB, without notable performance loss. Code available at https://github.com/thunlp/APB

AI.tldr: Spava is a sequence-parallel framework with optimized attention that accelerates long-video inference in Large Multimodal Models by distributing approximate attention across multiple GPUs, avoiding embedding compression, and applying system-level optimizations to achieve substantial speedups.
AI.motivation: Long-video inference is bottlenecked by dense computation in the prefill stage of LMMs. Prior approaches either compress visual embeddings or apply sparse attention on a single GPU, which limits speedups and degrades performance on longer, more complex videos.
AI.method: Spava distributes approximate attention in a sequence-parallel fashion across multiple GPUs, enabling reduced computation and increased parallelism. It avoids embedding compression and includes system-level optimizations such as load balancing and fused forward passes.
AI.result: Achieves speedups of 12.72x, 1.70x, and 1.18x over FlashAttn, ZigZagRing, and APB, respectively, without notable performance loss.
AI.conclusion: Spava enables efficient long-video inference on multi-GPU systems, improving scalability and performance of LMMs in handling longer and more complex videos.

title: mjlab: A Lightweight Framework for GPU-Accelerated Robot Learning
summary: We present mjlab, a lightweight, open-source framework for robot learning that combines GPU-accelerated simulation with composable environments and minimal setup friction. mjlab adopts the manager-based API introduced by Isaac Lab, where users compose modular building blocks for observations, rewards, and events, and pairs it with MuJoCo Warp for GPU-accelerated physics. The result is a framework installable with a single command, requiring minimal dependencies, and providing direct access to native MuJoCo data structures. mjlab ships with reference implementations of velocity tracking, motion imitation, and manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: mjlab is a lightweight, open-source robot-learning framework that unifies GPU-accelerated simulation with a modular, manager-based API and single-command installation, enabling native MuJoCo data access and ready-made tasks for velocity tracking, motion imitation, and manipulation.
AI.motivation: Lower setup friction and provide high-performance, modular components for reinforcement learning in robotics, combining GPU physics with composable environment blocks.
AI.method: Integrates MuJoCo Warp for GPU physics with a manager-based API (blocks for observations, rewards, events) and a simple one-command installation; exposes native MuJoCo data structures; includes reference implementations for velocity tracking, motion imitation, and manipulation tasks.
AI.result: A framework that installs via a single command with minimal dependencies, offers direct access to MuJoCo data, and ships with reference task implementations.
AI.conclusion: mjlab delivers a practical, low-friction platform for rapid robot-learning research, leveraging GPU-accelerated physics and modular environment composition to streamline development.

title: Within-Model vs Between-Prompt Variability in Large Language Models for Creative Tasks
summary: How much of LLM output variance is explained by prompts versus model choice versus stochasticity through sampling? We answer this by evaluating 12 LLMs on 10 creativity prompts with 100 samples each (N = 12,000). For output quality (originality), prompts explain 36.43% of variance, comparable to model choice (40.94%). But for output quantity (fluency), model choice (51.25%) and within-LLM variance (33.70%) dominate, with prompts explaining only 4.22%. Prompts are powerful levers for steering output quality, but given the substantial within-LLM variance (10-34%), single-sample evaluations risk conflating sampling noise with genuine prompt or model effects.

AI.tldr: Prompts and model choice explain comparable portions of output originality variance, while sampling randomness dominates fluency variance; substantial within-LLM variance (10-34%) implies single-sample evaluations are noisy.
AI.motivation: Quantify how different sources of variation (prompts, model, stochastic sampling) contribute to LLM output quality and quantity, to inform evaluation design and reproducibility.
AI.method: Empirical variance decomposition by evaluating 12 LLMs on 10 creativity prompts with 100 samples each (N=12,000) and partitioning variance of two outcomes (originality and fluency) across prompts, models, and within-LLM sampling noise.
AI.result: Originality: prompts explain 36.43% of variance, model choice 40.94%, within-LLM variance not explicitly stated but implied; Fluency: model choice 51.25%, within-LLM 33.70%, prompts 4.22%. Prompts can steer output quality; substantial within-LLM variance (10-34%) makes single-sample evaluations potentially confounded by sampling noise.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation of LLMs should account for substantial sampling variability and within-model noise; to assess prompt quality vs model effects, use multiple samples per prompt-model pair and consider variance components; prompts are effective for quality but reliability requires repeated sampling and robust statistical design.

title: Mobility-Embedded POIs: Learning What A Place Is and How It Is Used from Human Movement
summary: Recent progress in geospatial foundation models highlights the importance of learning general-purpose representations for real-world locations, particularly points-of-interest (POIs) where human activity concentrates. Existing approaches, however, focus primarily on place identity derived from static textual metadata, or learn representations tied to trajectory context, which capture movement regularities rather than how places are actually used (i.e., POI's function). We argue that POI function is a missing but essential signal for general POI representations. We introduce Mobility-Embedded POIs (ME-POIs), a framework that augments POI embeddings derived, from language models with large-scale human mobility data to learn POI-centric, context-independent representations grounded in real-world usage. ME-POIs encodes individual visits as temporally contextualized embeddings and aligns them with learnable POI representations via contrastive learning to capture usage patterns across users and time. To address long-tail sparsity, we propose a novel mechanism that propagates temporal visit patterns from nearby, frequently visited POIs across multiple spatial scales. We evaluate ME-POIs on five newly proposed map enrichment tasks, testing its ability to capture both the identity and function of POIs. Across all tasks, augmenting text-based embeddings with ME-POIs consistently outperforms both text-only and mobility-only baselines. Notably, ME-POIs trained on mobility data alone can surpass text-only models on certain tasks, highlighting that POI function is a critical component of accurate and generalizable POI representations.

AI.tldr: ME-POIs augments text-based POI embeddings with mobility-derived, temporally contextualized embeddings and cross-modal contrastive learning to capture POI function, addressing long-tail sparsity via multi-scale propagation; improves map enrichment tasks.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in POI representations: existing work emphasizes static identity from textual metadata or trajectory context, but not the actual usage (POI function). Incorporating usage signals should yield more generalizable, functional POI representations.
AI.method: Encode individual visits as temporally contextualized embeddings; align them with learnable POI embeddings through contrastive learning to capture cross-user and cross-time usage patterns; propagate temporal visit signals from nearby, frequently visited POIs across multiple spatial scales to mitigate long-tail sparsity; evaluate by augmenting text-based POI embeddings on five new map enrichment tasks.
AI.result: ME-POIs augmented embeddings outperform both text-only and mobility-only baselines across all five tasks; ME-POIs trained on mobility data alone can surpass text-only models on some tasks, indicating POI function is a key signal for generalizable representations.
AI.conclusion: POI function is a critical missing signal for general POI representations. ME-POIs successfully integrates mobility-derived usage data with language-based POI embeddings, yielding improved generalization. Further work should address robustness, privacy, and broader evaluation.

title: Variance & Greediness: A comparative study of metric-learning losses
comment: 5 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables. Accepted by ICASSP 2026
summary: Metric learning is central to retrieval, yet its effects on embedding geometry and optimization dynamics are not well understood. We introduce a diagnostic framework, VARIANCE (intra-/inter-class variance) and GREEDINESS (active ratio and gradient norms), to compare seven representative losses, i.e., Contrastive, Triplet, N-pair, InfoNCE, ArcFace, SCL, and CCL, across five image-retrieval datasets. Our analysis reveals that Triplet and SCL preserve higher within-class variance and clearer inter-class margins, leading to stronger top-1 retrieval in fine-grained settings. In contrast, Contrastive and InfoNCE compact embeddings are achieved quickly through many small updates, accelerating convergence but potentially oversimplifying class structures. N-pair achieves a large mean separation but with uneven spacing. These insights reveal a form of efficiency-granularity trade-off and provide practical guidance: prefer Triplet/SCL when diversity preservation and hard-sample discrimination are critical, and Contrastive/InfoNCE when faster embedding compaction is desired.

AI.tldr: A diagnostic framework (VARIANCE and GREEDINESS) compares seven metric-learning losses (Contrastive, Triplet, N-pair, InfoNCE, ArcFace, SCL, CCL) across five image-retrieval datasets. Triplet and SCL preserve higher within-class variance and clearer inter-class margins, yielding stronger top-1 results in fine-grained settings; Contrastive and InfoNCE produce compact embeddings quickly via many small updates, accelerating convergence but risking oversimplified class structure; N-pair achieves large mean separation with uneven spacing. This reveals an efficiency-granularity trade-off and practical guidelines for loss choice depending on diversity preservation vs. fast embedding compaction.
AI.motivation: To understand how different metric-learning losses shape embedding geometry and optimization dynamics in image retrieval, addressing a gap in characterizing efficiency-granularity trade-offs.
AI.method: Introduce VARIANCE (intra-/inter-class variance) and GREEDINESS (active ratio and gradient norms) as diagnostic probes; evaluate seven losses (Contrastive, Triplet, N-pair, InfoNCE, ArcFace, SCL, CCL) on five image-retrieval datasets; compare embedding geometry, margins, convergence behavior, and top-1 retrieval.
AI.result: Triplet and SCL maintain higher within-class variance and clearer inter-class margins, leading to stronger top-1 retrieval in fine-grained tasks. Contrastive and InfoNCE achieve rapid embedding compaction through many small updates, speeding convergence but potentially oversimplifying class structure. N-pair yields large mean separation but with uneven spacing. Overall, an efficiency-granularity trade-off emerges, informing practical guidance.
AI.conclusion: Prefer Triplet or SCL when diversity preservation and hard-sample discrimination are critical; opt for Contrastive or InfoNCE when fast embedding compaction is desired; use the diagnostic framework to guide loss selection based on retrieval needs.

title: ReactEMG Stroke: Healthy-to-Stroke Few-shot Adaptation for sEMG-Based Intent Detection
summary: Surface electromyography (sEMG) is a promising control signal for assist-as-needed hand rehabilitation after stroke, but detecting intent from paretic muscles often requires lengthy, subject-specific calibration and remains brittle to variability. We propose a healthy-to-stroke adaptation pipeline that initializes an intent detector from a model pretrained on large-scale able-bodied sEMG, then fine-tunes it for each stroke participant using only a small amount of subject-specific data. Using a newly collected dataset from three individuals with chronic stroke, we compare adaptation strategies (head-only tuning, parameter-efficient LoRA adapters, and full end-to-end fine-tuning) and evaluate on held-out test sets that include realistic distribution shifts such as within-session drift, posture changes, and armband repositioning. Across conditions, healthy-pretrained adaptation consistently improves stroke intent detection relative to both zero-shot transfer and stroke-only training under the same data budget; the best adaptation methods improve average transition accuracy from 0.42 to 0.61 and raw accuracy from 0.69 to 0.78. These results suggest that transferring a reusable healthy-domain EMG representation can reduce calibration burden while improving robustness for real-time post-stroke intent detection.

AI.tldr: Healthy-to-stroke adaptation pipeline uses a model pretrained on able-bodied sEMG and fine-tunes it with limited stroke data, improving robustness and reducing calibration burden for stroke intent detection. Best methods (head-only tuning, LoRA adapters, full fine-tuning) yield substantial gains under distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Address calibration burden and robustness in sEMG-based intent detection for post-stroke rehabilitation, where paretic muscle signals vary greatly and require subject-specific calibration.
AI.method: Pretrain on large-scale able-bodied sEMG; fine-tune per stroke participant with small subject-specific data. Compare adaptation strategies: head-only tuning, LoRA adapters (low-rank adapters), and full end-to-end fine-tuning. Evaluate on held-out test sets including distribution shifts (within-session drift, posture changes, armband repositioning) using a new dataset from three chronic stroke subjects.
AI.result: Healthy-pretrained adaptation consistently improves stroke intent detection over zero-shot transfer and stroke-only training under the same data budget. Best methods raise average transition accuracy from 0.42 to 0.61 and raw accuracy from 0.69 to 0.78.
AI.conclusion: Transferring a reusable healthy-domain EMG representation can reduce calibration burden and enhance robustness for real-time post-stroke intent detection.

title: EHR-RAG: Bridging Long-Horizon Structured Electronic Health Records and Large Language Models via Enhanced Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Electronic Health Records (EHRs) provide rich longitudinal clinical evidence that is central to medical decision-making, motivating the use of retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to ground large language model (LLM) predictions. However, long-horizon EHRs often exceed LLM context limits, and existing approaches commonly rely on truncation or vanilla retrieval strategies that discard clinically relevant events and temporal dependencies. To address these challenges, we propose EHR-RAG, a retrieval-augmented framework designed for accurate interpretation of long-horizon structured EHR data. EHR-RAG introduces three components tailored to longitudinal clinical prediction tasks: Event- and Time-Aware Hybrid EHR Retrieval to preserve clinical structure and temporal dynamics, Adaptive Iterative Retrieval to progressively refine queries in order to expand broad evidence coverage, and Dual-Path Evidence Retrieval and Reasoning to jointly retrieves and reasons over both factual and counterfactual evidence. Experiments across four long-horizon EHR prediction tasks show that EHR-RAG consistently outperforms the strongest LLM-based baselines, achieving an average Macro-F1 improvement of 10.76%. Overall, our work highlights the potential of retrieval-augmented LLMs to advance clinical prediction on structured EHR data in practice.

AI.tldr: Proposes EHR-RAG, a retrieval-augmented framework for long-horizon structured EHR data to improve clinical predictions, addressing context-length and temporal dependencies via event/time-aware retrieval, adaptive iterative retrieval, and dual-path retrieval/reasoning; achieves ~10.8% Macro-F1 gain over strong LLM baselines across four tasks.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon EHRs exceed typical LLM context windows and existing retrieval strategies discard clinically relevant events and temporal dynamics, hindering accurate predictions; there is a need to preserve clinical structure and enable reasoning over both factual and counterfactual evidence.
AI.method: Introduce three components: (1) Event- and Time-Aware Hybrid EHR Retrieval to preserve clinical structure and temporal dynamics; (2) Adaptive Iterative Retrieval to progressively refine queries and expand evidence coverage; (3) Dual-Path Evidence Retrieval and Reasoning to jointly retrieve and reason over both factual and counterfactual evidence. Evaluate on four long-horizon clinical prediction tasks with macro-F1 as the metric.
AI.result: EHR-RAG consistently outperforms the strongest LLM-based baselines, achieving an average Macro-F1 improvement of 10.76% across the tasks.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-augmented LLMs with the proposed components effectively handle long-horizon structured EHR data and enhance clinical prediction performance, illustrating practical potential in real-world structured EHR applications.

title: Can Neural Networks Learn Small Algebraic Worlds? An Investigation Into the Group-theoretic Structures Learned By Narrow Models Trained To Predict Group Operations
summary: While a real-world research program in mathematics may be guided by a motivating question, the process of mathematical discovery is typically open-ended. Ideally, exploration needed to answer the original question will reveal new structures, patterns, and insights that are valuable in their own right. This contrasts with the exam-style paradigm in which the machine learning community typically applies AI to math. To maximize progress in mathematics using AI, we will need to go beyond simple question answering. With this in mind, we explore the extent to which narrow models trained to solve a fixed mathematical task learn broader mathematical structure that can be extracted by a researcher or other AI system. As a basic test case for this, we use the task of training a neural network to predict a group operation (for example, performing modular arithmetic or composition of permutations). We describe a suite of tests designed to assess whether the model captures significant group-theoretic notions such as the identity element, commutativity, or subgroups. Through extensive experimentation we find evidence that models learn representations capable of capturing abstract algebraic properties. For example, we find hints that models capture the commutativity of modular arithmetic. We are also able to train linear classifiers that reliably distinguish between elements of certain subgroups (even though no labels for these subgroups are included in the data). On the other hand, we are unable to extract notions such as the concept of the identity element. Together, our results suggest that in some cases the representations of even small neural networks can be used to distill interesting abstract structure from new mathematical objects.

AI.tldr: Neural nets trained on fixed mathematical tasks can internalize partial higher-order algebraic structure, enabling extraction of some properties (e.g., commutativity, subgroup membership) but failing to capture others (e.g., identity). This suggests potential for AI-assisted mathematical discovery, with limitations.
AI.motivation: To move AI for mathematics beyond mere problem answering by testing whether narrow models can reveal broader algebraic structures useful for researchers, using group theory as a testbed.
AI.method: Train neural networks to predict a group operation (e.g., modular arithmetic, permutation composition). Evaluate learned representations by designing tests for key group-theoretic notions (identity, commutativity, subgroups). Use linear classifiers on representations to detect subgroup membership without subgroup labels in the data.
AI.result: The models' representations show partial capture of algebraic structure: hints of commutativity in modular arithmetic; linear classifiers reliably distinguish certain subgroup elements without explicit subgroup labels. However, the identity element concept remains not extractable.
AI.conclusion: Small neural networks can distill nontrivial abstract structure from new mathematical objects, indicating promise for AI-assisted mathematical discovery. However, essential concepts like the identity element may resist extraction, highlighting limits and the need for further methodological development.

title: Mining Forgery Traces from Reconstruction Error: A Weakly Supervised Framework for Multimodal Deepfake Temporal Localization
summary: Modern deepfakes have evolved into localized and intermittent manipulations that require fine-grained temporal localization. The prohibitive cost of frame-level annotation makes weakly supervised methods a practical necessity, which rely only on video-level labels. To this end, we propose Reconstruction-based Temporal Deepfake Localization (RT-DeepLoc), a weakly supervised temporal forgery localization framework that identifies forgeries via reconstruction errors. Our framework uses a Masked Autoencoder (MAE) trained exclusively on authentic data to learn its intrinsic spatiotemporal patterns; this allows the model to produce significant reconstruction discrepancies for forged segments, effectively providing the missing fine-grained cues for localization. To robustly leverage these indicators, we introduce a novel Asymmetric Intra-video Contrastive Loss (AICL). By focusing on the compactness of authentic features guided by these reconstruction cues, AICL establishes a stable decision boundary that enhances local discrimination while preserving generalization to unseen forgeries. Extensive experiments on large-scale datasets, including LAV-DF, demonstrate that RT-DeepLoc achieves state-of-the-art performance in weakly-supervised temporal forgery localization.

AI.tldr: RT-DeepLoc introduces a weakly supervised temporal forgery localization framework that uses a masked autoencoder trained on authentic videos to produce reconstruction errors for forged segments, enhanced by a novel Asymmetric Intra-video Contrastive Loss (AICL); achieves state-of-the-art results on LAV-DF.
AI.motivation: Frame-level annotation for localizing deepfake forgery is costly; video-level labels are more practical. There is a need for fine-grained temporal localization under weak supervision.
AI.method: Train a Masked Autoencoder (MAE) on authentic data to learn authentic spatiotemporal patterns. Use reconstruction discrepancies to highlight forged segments. Employ Asymmetric Intra-video Contrastive Loss (AICL) to promote compact authentic features guided by reconstruction cues, stabilizing decision boundaries and improving local discrimination while generalizing to unseen forgeries.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on large-scale datasets, including LAV-DF, show RT-DeepLoc achieves state-of-the-art performance in weakly supervised temporal forgery localization.
AI.conclusion: Combining MAE-based reconstruction signals with AICL yields robust temporal localization under weak supervision, achieving strong generalization to unseen forgery patterns.

title: DynamicVLA: A Vision-Language-Action Model for Dynamic Object Manipulation
summary: Manipulating dynamic objects remains an open challenge for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, which, despite strong generalization in static manipulation, struggle in dynamic scenarios requiring rapid perception, temporal anticipation, and continuous control. We present DynamicVLA, a framework for dynamic object manipulation that integrates temporal reasoning and closed-loop adaptation through three key designs: 1) a compact 0.4B VLA using a convolutional vision encoder for spatially efficient, structurally faithful encoding, enabling fast multimodal inference; 2) Continuous Inference, enabling overlapping reasoning and execution for lower latency and timely adaptation to object motion; and 3) Latent-aware Action Streaming, which bridges the perception-execution gap by enforcing temporally aligned action execution. To fill the missing foundation of dynamic manipulation data, we introduce the Dynamic Object Manipulation (DOM) benchmark, built from scratch with an auto data collection pipeline that efficiently gathers 200K synthetic episodes across 2.8K scenes and 206 objects, and enables fast collection of 2K real-world episodes without teleoperation. Extensive evaluations demonstrate remarkable improvements in response speed, perception, and generalization, positioning DynamicVLA as a unified framework for general dynamic object manipulation across embodiments.

AI.tldr: DynamicVLA integrates a compact vision-language-action model with temporal reasoning and continuous inference, supported by a large synthetic/real-world Dynamic Object Manipulation benchmark to enable fast, general dynamic object manipulation.
AI.motivation: Dynamic manipulation remains challenging for VLA models due to the need for rapid perception, temporal anticipation, and closed-loop control in dynamic scenarios; existing static-focused VLA systems often fail to adapt quickly to moving objects.
AI.method: Three core designs: (1) a compact 0.4B VLA using a convolutional vision encoder for spatially efficient, structurally faithful encoding; (2) Continuous Inference enabling overlapping reasoning and execution to reduce latency; (3) Latent-aware Action Streaming enforcing temporal alignment between perception and action execution. Dataset: Dynamic Object Manipulation (DOM) benchmark with an auto data-collection pipeline yielding 200K synthetic episodes across 2.8K scenes and 206 objects, plus 2K real-world episodes without teleoperation.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations report improvements in response speed, perception, and generalization, positioning DynamicVLA as a unified framework for dynamic object manipulation across different embodiments.
AI.conclusion: DynamicVLA provides a scalable framework that couples a compact, fast VLA with continuous inference and latent-action alignment, supported by a large DOM benchmark to advance general dynamic object manipulation across embodiments.

title: Ostrakon-VL: Towards Domain-Expert MLLM for Food-Service and Retail Stores
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have recently achieved substantial progress in general-purpose perception and reasoning. Nevertheless, their deployment in Food-Service and Retail Stores (FSRS) scenarios encounters two major obstacles: (i) real-world FSRS data, collected from heterogeneous acquisition devices, are highly noisy and lack auditable, closed-loop data curation, which impedes the construction of high-quality, controllable, and reproducible training corpora; and (ii) existing evaluation protocols do not offer a unified, fine-grained and standardized benchmark spanning single-image, multi-image, and video inputs, making it challenging to objectively gauge model robustness. To address these challenges, we first develop Ostrakon-VL, an FSRS-oriented MLLM based on Qwen3-VL-8B. Second, we introduce ShopBench, the first public benchmark for FSRS. Third, we propose QUAD (Quality-aware Unbiased Automated Data-curation), a multi-stage multimodal instruction data curation pipeline. Leveraging a multi-stage training strategy, Ostrakon-VL achieves an average score of 60.1 on ShopBench, establishing a new state of the art among open-source MLLMs with comparable parameter scales and diverse architectures. Notably, it surpasses the substantially larger Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B (59.4) by +0.7, and exceeds the same-scale Qwen3-VL-8B (55.3) by +4.8, demonstrating significantly improved parameter efficiency. These results indicate that Ostrakon-VL delivers more robust and reliable FSRS-centric perception and decision-making capabilities. To facilitate reproducible research, we will publicly release Ostrakon-VL and the ShopBench benchmark.

AI.tldr: Ostrakon-VL is an FSRS-focused multimodal LLM built on Qwen3-VL-8B, introducing ShopBench and QUAD for data curation; it achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models of similar scale, indicating improved robustness and efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address noisy, heterogeneous real-world FSRS data and the lack of unified, fine-grained benchmarks for evaluating MLLMs across image and video inputs; to enable reproducible, high-quality data curation and robust FSRS perception and decision-making.
AI.method: Develop Ostrakon-VL based on Qwen3-VL-8B; create ShopBench as a public FSRS benchmark (covering single-image, multi-image, and video inputs); propose QUAD, a multi-stage data-curation pipeline for multimodal instruction data; employ a multi-stage training strategy to fuse modality and instruction-following capabilities.
AI.result: Ostrakon-VL achieves an average ShopBench score of 60.1, setting a new SOTA among open-source MLLMs of comparable parameter scales; outperforms larger models (Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B by 0.7, and Qwen3-VL-8B by 4.8), demonstrating superior parameter efficiency and robustness in FSRS tasks.
AI.conclusion: Public release of Ostrakon-VL and ShopBench to facilitate reproducible FSRS research; the combination of robust data curation (QUAD) and multi-stage training yields strong, efficient FSRS-oriented perception and decision-making.

title: Learning to Advect: A Neural Semi-Lagrangian Architecture for Weather Forecasting
summary: Recent machine-learning approaches to weather forecasting often employ a monolithic architecture, where distinct physical mechanisms (advection, transport), diffusion-like mixing, thermodynamic processes, and forcing are represented implicitly within a single large network. This representation is particularly problematic for advection, where long-range transport must be treated with expensive global interaction mechanisms or through deep, stacked convolutional layers. To mitigate this, we present PARADIS, a physics-inspired global weather prediction model that imposes inductive biases on network behavior through a functional decomposition into advection, diffusion, and reaction blocks acting on latent variables. We implement advection through a Neural Semi-Lagrangian operator that performs trajectory-based transport via differentiable interpolation on the sphere, enabling end-to-end learning of both the latent modes to be transported and their characteristic trajectories. Diffusion-like processes are modeled through depthwise-separable spatial mixing, while local source terms and vertical interactions are modeled via pointwise channel interactions, enabling operator-level physical structure. PARADIS provides state-of-the-art forecast skill at a fraction of the training cost. On ERA5-based benchmarks, the 1 degree PARADIS model, with a total training cost of less than a GPU month, meets or exceeds the performance of 0.25 degree traditional and machine-learning baselines, including the ECMWF HRES forecast and DeepMind's GraphCast.

AI.tldr: A physics-inspired modular ML model, PARADIS, splits atmospheric dynamics into advection, diffusion, and reaction blocks with a Neural Semi-Lagrangian advection operator on the sphere, achieving state-of-the-art forecast skill at low training cost; on ERA5 benchmarks, 1° PARADIS matches or surpasses 0.25° baselines such as ECMWF HRES and GraphCast.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of monolithic ML weather models, especially expensive long-range advection handling, by injecting inductive biases and a physics-inspired operator structure to improve data efficiency, interpretability, and generalization.
AI.method: Functional decomposition into three latent-operator blocks (advection via Neural Semi-Lagrangian trajectory-based transport on the sphere; diffusion via depthwise-separable spatial mixing; reaction via pointwise channel interactions); end-to-end differentiable on latent variables; 1° model trained with total cost < GPU-month; evaluated on ERA5 benchmarks; latent transport trajectories learned jointly with modes.
AI.result: PARADIS achieves state-of-the-art forecast skill at a fraction of the training cost; on ERA5-based benchmarks, the 1° model meets or exceeds the performance of 0.25° traditional and ML baselines, including ECMWF HRES and DeepMind GraphCast.
AI.conclusion: A physics-inspired modular design with explicit advection, diffusion, and reaction operators provides competitive global weather forecasts with substantially reduced training cost, illustrating the value of operator-level inductive biases and modularity for scalable weather prediction.

title: Hypernetwork-Based Adaptive Aggregation for Multimodal Multiple-Instance Learning in Predicting Coronary Calcium Debulking
summary: In this paper, we present the first attempt to estimate the necessity of debulking coronary artery calcifications from computed tomography (CT) images. We formulate this task as a Multiple-instance Learning (MIL) problem. The difficulty of this task lies in that physicians adjust their focus and decision criteria for device usage according to tabular data representing each patient's condition. To address this issue, we propose a hypernetwork-based adaptive aggregation transformer (HyperAdAgFormer), which adaptively modifies the feature aggregation strategy for each patient based on tabular data through a hypernetwork. The experiments using the clinical dataset demonstrated the effectiveness of HyperAdAgFormer. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/Shiku-Kaito/HyperAdAgFormer.

AI.tldr: First MIL approach to estimate the necessity of debulking coronary artery calcifications from CT; introduces HyperAdAgFormer, a hypernetwork-based adaptive aggregation transformer that conditions aggregation on patient-specific tabular data; shows effective results on a clinical dataset; code released.
AI.motivation: Physician decisions depend on tabular patient data; fixed MIL aggregation may fail to capture individualized decision criteria; adapt aggregation per patient to improve necessity estimation.
AI.method: Propose HyperAdAgFormer: a hypernetwork-driven adaptive aggregation transformer for MIL. It uses tabular data to personalize how instance-level CT features are aggregated into a bag-level prediction; hypernetwork outputs aggregation parameters; evaluated on a clinical CT dataset; code available.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate effectiveness of HyperAdAgFormer in estimating debulking necessity; indicates better adaptation to patient data; likely outperforming baselines; code available.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, patient-aware MIL via hypernetwork-conditioned aggregation improves CT-based decision support for debulking CAD calcifications; contributes a novel architecture for MIL with conditioning; public code.

title: EmboCoach-Bench: Benchmarking AI Agents on Developing Embodied Robots
summary: The field of Embodied AI is witnessing a rapid evolution toward general-purpose robotic systems, fueled by high-fidelity simulation and large-scale data collection. However, this scaling capability remains severely bottlenecked by a reliance on labor-intensive manual oversight from intricate reward shaping to hyperparameter tuning across heterogeneous backends. Inspired by LLMs' success in software automation and science discovery, we introduce \textsc{EmboCoach-Bench}, a benchmark evaluating the capacity of LLM agents to autonomously engineer embodied policies. Spanning 32 expert-curated RL and IL tasks, our framework posits executable code as the universal interface. We move beyond static generation to assess a dynamic closed-loop workflow, where agents leverage environment feedback to iteratively draft, debug, and optimize solutions, spanning improvements from physics-informed reward design to policy architectures such as diffusion policies. Extensive evaluations yield three critical insights: (1) autonomous agents can qualitatively surpass human-engineered baselines by 26.5\% in average success rate; (2) agentic workflow with environment feedback effectively strengthens policy development and substantially narrows the performance gap between open-source and proprietary models; and (3) agents exhibit self-correction capabilities for pathological engineering cases, successfully resurrecting task performance from near-total failures through iterative simulation-in-the-loop debugging. Ultimately, this work establishes a foundation for self-evolving embodied intelligence, accelerating the paradigm shift from labor-intensive manual tuning to scalable, autonomous engineering in embodied AI field.

AI.tldr: EmboCoach-Bench evaluates LLM agents' ability to autonomously engineer embodied policies via an executable-code interface in a closed-loop, simulation-in-the-loop setup across 32 RL/IL tasks, achieving self-evolved, policy-design improvements that outperform human baselines, reduce reliance on proprietary models, and demonstrate self-correction.
AI.motivation: Manual reward shaping and hyperparameter tuning across heterogeneous backends bottleneck scaling Embodied AI; inspired by LLMs' automation capabilities to enable self-driven policy engineering and reduce human oversight.
AI.method: 32 expert-curated RL/IL tasks; executable code as universal interface; closed-loop workflow with environment feedback; iterative drafting, debugging, and optimization; exploration of physics-informed rewards and diffusion-based policies.
AI.result: Three key findings: (1) autonomous agents surpass human-engineered baselines by 26.5% average success; (2) environment feedback enhances policy development and narrows the gap between open-source and proprietary models; (3) agents self-correct, reviving task performance from near-failure through simulation-in-the-loop debugging.
AI.conclusion: EmboCoach-Bench lays groundwork for self-evolving embodied intelligence, enabling scalable, autonomous engineering in embodied AI and accelerating the shift away from labor-intensive manual tuning.

title: A Federated Generalized Expectation-Maximization Algorithm for Mixture Models with an Unknown Number of Components
summary: We study the problem of federated clustering when the total number of clusters $K$ across clients is unknown, and the clients have heterogeneous but potentially overlapping cluster sets in their local data. To that end, we develop FedGEM: a federated generalized expectation-maximization algorithm for the training of mixture models with an unknown number of components. Our proposed algorithm relies on each of the clients performing EM steps locally, and constructing an uncertainty set around the maximizer associated with each local component. The central server utilizes the uncertainty sets to learn potential cluster overlaps between clients, and infer the global number of clusters via closed-form computations. We perform a thorough theoretical study of our algorithm, presenting probabilistic convergence guarantees under common assumptions. Subsequently, we study the specific setting of isotropic GMMs, providing tractable, low-complexity computations to be performed by each client during each iteration of the algorithm, as well as rigorously verifying assumptions required for algorithm convergence. We perform various numerical experiments, where we empirically demonstrate that our proposed method achieves comparable performance to centralized EM, and that it outperforms various existing federated clustering methods.

AI.tldr: FedGEM: federated generalized EM for clustering with unknown global K. Clients perform local EM, build uncertainty sets around local component maximizers, server fuses these to infer overlaps and the global number of clusters via closed-form updates; provides convergence guarantees; specializes to isotropic GMMs for efficient per-iteration computation; empirically competitive with centralized EM and superior to baselines.
