AI.motivation: Tackle federated clustering when the total number of clusters is unknown and local datasets have heterogeneous/overlapping cluster sets. Aims to reconcile local components into a coherent global clustering without centralized data access.
AI.method: Clients run EM locally and form uncertainty sets around their local component maximizers. The server analyzes these uncertainty sets to infer overlaps across clients and to determine the global number of clusters using closed-form computations. The paper also specializes the framework to isotropic GMMs to obtain tractable per-iteration updates and provides convergence guarantees under standard assumptions.
AI.result: Theoretical probabilistic convergence guarantees are established. In the isotropic GMM setting, the method yields low-complexity client updates. Empirically, FedGEM achieves performance comparable to centralized EM and outperforms several existing federated clustering methods across experiments.
AI.conclusion: FedGEM provides a principled and theoretically grounded solution for federated clustering with unknown K and heterogeneous local cluster sets. It balances local EM computations with a global aggregation mechanism based on uncertainty sets, delivering competitive accuracy with favorable computational properties. Further work could explore non-isotropic covariances, privacy-preserving variants, and communication-efficient implementations.

title: SimGraph: A Unified Framework for Scene Graph-Based Image Generation and Editing
summary: Recent advancements in Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) have significantly enhanced the capabilities of both image generation and editing. However, current approaches often treat these tasks separately, leading to inefficiencies and challenges in maintaining spatial consistency and semantic coherence between generated content and edits. Moreover, a major obstacle is the lack of structured control over object relationships and spatial arrangements. Scene graph-based methods, which represent objects and their interrelationships in a structured format, offer a solution by providing greater control over composition and interactions in both image generation and editing. To address this, we introduce SimGraph, a unified framework that integrates scene graph-based image generation and editing, enabling precise control over object interactions, layouts, and spatial coherence. In particular, our framework integrates token-based generation and diffusion-based editing within a single scene graph-driven model, ensuring high-quality and consistent results. Through extensive experiments, we empirically demonstrate that our approach outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: A unified, scene-graph-driven framework (SimGraph) integrates generation and editing for GenAI imagery, addressing spatial coherence, semantic consistency, and object-relationship control by combining token-based generation with diffusion-based editing under a single scene graph model; claims state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Current GenAI workflows treat generation and editing separately, causing inefficiencies and mismatches in spatial layout and semantic relations; scene graphs offer structured control over object interactions and layouts, enabling coherent composition across generation and editing.
AI.method: Proposes SimGraph, a unified framework that uses a scene graph to drive both token-based generation and diffusion-based editing. The framework enables precise control over object interactions, layouts, and spatial coherence, integrating generation and editing in a single model driven by scene graphs.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that SimGraph outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on relevant tasks, demonstrating improved spatial coherence, semantic consistency, and control over object relationships and layouts.
AI.conclusion: A scene-graph-driven, unified model can effectively couple generation and editing in GenAI, providing precise control over objects and their relations while maintaining coherence; this approach advances efficiency and quality in image synthesis/editing workflows.

title: Generalized Information Gathering Under Dynamics Uncertainty
summary: An agent operating in an unknown dynamical system must learn its dynamics from observations. Active information gathering accelerates this learning, but existing methods derive bespoke costs for specific modeling choices: dynamics models, belief update procedures, observation models, and planners. We present a unifying framework that decouples these choices from the information-gathering cost by explicitly exposing the causal dependencies between parameters, beliefs, and controls. Using this framework, we derive a general information-gathering cost based on Massey's directed information that assumes only Markov dynamics with additive noise and is otherwise agnostic to modeling choices. We prove that the mutual information cost used in existing literature is a special case of our cost. Then, we leverage our framework to establish an explicit connection between the mutual information cost and information gain in linearized Bayesian estimation, thereby providing theoretical justification for mutual information-based active learning approaches. Finally, we illustrate the practical utility of our framework through experiments spanning linear, nonlinear, and multi-agent systems.

AI.tldr: A unifying, causal-information framework for active information gathering using Massey’s directed information; mutual information is a special case; connects to Bayesian information gain; validated on linear, nonlinear, and multi-agent systems.
AI.motivation: Decouple information-gathering costs from specific modeling choices to obtain a model-agnostic, principled cost and to expose the causal dependencies between parameters, beliefs, and controls.
AI.method: Derive a general information-gathering cost based on directed information under Markov dynamics with additive noise; demonstrate that mutual information is a special case; establish a link between the mutual information cost and information gain in linearized Bayesian estimation; provide theoretical justification for MI-based active learning; validate with experiments across linear, nonlinear, and multi-agent systems.
AI.result: Formalization shows MI cost as a special case of the directed-information cost; explicit connection to information gain in Bayesian estimation; empirical validation across diverse dynamical systems.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a general, principled, model-agnostic tool for active information gathering with broad applicability and a theoretical bridge between existing MI-based methods and information-theoretic measures.

title: BEAP-Agent: Backtrackable Execution and Adaptive Planning for GUI Agents
summary: GUI agents are designed to automate repetitive tasks and enhance productivity. However, existing GUI agents struggle to recover once they follow an incorrect exploration path, often leading to task failure. In this work, we model GUI task execution as a DFS process and propose BEAP-Agent, a DFS-based framework that supports long-range, multi-level state backtracking with dynamic task tracking and updating. The framework consists of three collaborative components: Planner, Executor, and Tracker. Together, they enable effective task exploration and execution. BEAP-Agent fills the gap in systematic backtracking mechanisms for GUI agents, offering a systematic solution for long-horizon task exploration. We conducted a systematic evaluation on the OSWorld benchmark, where BEAP-Agent achieved an accuracy of 28.2%, validating the effectiveness of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: BEAP-Agent: a DFS-based GUI task framework with Planner, Executor, and Tracker enabling long-range backtracking; validated on OSWorld with 28.2% accuracy.
AI.motivation: Address the inability of existing GUI agents to recover from incorrect exploration paths, which often leads to task failure during long-horizon tasks.
AI.method: Model GUI task execution as a DFS process and implement a three-component architecture (Planner, Executor, Tracker) that supports long-range, multi-level state backtracking and dynamic task tracking/updating.
AI.result: Systematic evaluation on the OSWorld benchmark showing BEAP-Agent achieving 28.2% accuracy, indicating effectiveness of the backtracking approach for GUI tasks.
AI.conclusion: BEAP-Agent fills a gap in systematic backtracking mechanisms for GUI agents, providing a structured solution for long-horizon task exploration.

title: Efficient Simple Regret Algorithms for Stochastic Contextual Bandits
summary: We study stochastic contextual logistic bandits under the simple regret objective. While simple regret guarantees have been established for the linear case, no such results were previously known for the logistic setting. Building on ideas from contextual linear bandits and self-concordant analysis, we propose the first algorithm that achieves simple regret $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d/\sqrt{T})$. Notably, the leading term of our regret bound is free of the constant $κ= \mathcal O(\exp(S))$, where $S$ is a bound on the magnitude of the unknown parameter vector. The algorithm is shown to be fully tractable when the action set is finite. We also introduce a new variant of Thompson Sampling tailored to the simple-regret setting. This yields the first simple regret guarantee for randomized algorithms in stochastic contextual linear bandits, with regret $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d^{3/2}/\sqrt{T})$. Extending this method to the logistic case, we obtain a similarly structured Thompson Sampling algorithm that achieves the same regret bound -- $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d^{3/2}/\sqrt{T})$ -- again with no dependence on $κ$ in the leading term. The randomized algorithms, as expected, are cheaper to run than their deterministic counterparts. Finally, we conducted a series of experiments to empirically validate these theoretical guarantees.

AI.tldr: First simple-regret guarantees for stochastic contextual logistic bandits; deterministic and randomized algorithms achieving near-optimal bounds with leading term independent of the κ-parameter; finite-action tractability; Thompson Sampling variants; empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap in simple-regret guarantees for logistic contextual bandits, extending ideas from contextual linear bandits and self-concordant analysis to obtain regret bounds without κ dependence.
AI.method: Develops a logistic contextual bandit algorithm achieving rac{d}{√T} simple regret (up to log factors) using self-concordant analysis. Introduces a variant of Thompson Sampling tailored to simple-regret; extends to logistic case for randomized algorithm with rac{d^{3/2}}{√T} regret; tractability for finite action sets.
AI.result: Achieves simple-regret  ~ d/√T with leading term independent of κ; randomized algorithm yields ~ d^{3/2}/√T; logistic extension yields the same bound; experiments corroborate theoretical guarantees.
AI.conclusion: First simple-regret guarantees for stochastic contextual logistic bandits; randomized methods are cheaper; finite-action sets allow full tractability; empirical results validate theory.

title: HERS: Hidden-Pattern Expert Learning for Risk-Specific Vehicle Damage Adaptation in Diffusion Models
summary: Recent advances in text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models have enabled increasingly realistic synthesis of vehicle damage, raising concerns about their reliability in automated insurance workflows. The ability to generate crash-like imagery challenges the boundary between authentic and synthetic data, introducing new risks of misuse in fraud or claim manipulation. To address these issues, we propose HERS (Hidden-Pattern Expert Learning for Risk-Specific Damage Adaptation), a framework designed to improve fidelity, controllability, and domain alignment of diffusion-generated damage images. HERS fine-tunes a base diffusion model via domain-specific expert adaptation without requiring manual annotation. Using self-supervised image-text pairs automatically generated by a large language model and T2I pipeline, HERS models each damage category, such as dents, scratches, broken lights, or cracked paint, as a separate expert. These experts are later integrated into a unified multi-damage model that balances specialization with generalization. We evaluate HERS across four diffusion backbones and observe consistent improvements: plus 5.5 percent in text faithfulness and plus 2.3 percent in human preference ratings compared to baselines. Beyond image fidelity, we discuss implications for fraud detection, auditability, and safe deployment of generative models in high-stakes domains. Our findings highlight both the opportunities and risks of domain-specific diffusion, underscoring the importance of trustworthy generation in safety-critical applications such as auto insurance.

AI.tldr: HERS is a domain-aware diffusion fine-tuning framework for vehicle-damage generation that uses per-damage experts trained via self-supervised data (LLM-generated image-text pairs) and integrates them into a unified model. It yields gains in text fidelity and human preference across four backbones, with discussion on fraud detection, auditability, and safe deployment in auto-insurance.
AI.motivation: The surge of realistic T2I damage imagery threatens the integrity of automated insurance workflows, enabling fraud or claim manipulation. There is a need for controllable, domain-aligned, high-fidelity generation without heavy manual labeling.
AI.method: Fine-tune a base diffusion model via domain-specific expert adaptation. Create damage-category experts (e.g., dents, scratches, broken lights, cracked paint) using self-supervised image-text pairs generated automatically by a large language model and a T2I pipeline. Integrate experts into a unified multi-damage model that balances specialization and generalization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across four diffusion backbones shows consistent improvements: +5.5% text faithfulness and +2.3% in human preference ratings over baselines.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific diffusion with expert adaptation can enhance fidelity and controllability for safety-critical, high-stakes applications like auto insurance, while also highlighting the need for risk mitigation in fraud detection, auditability, and responsible deployment.

title: Causal World Modeling for Robot Control
summary: This work highlights that video world modeling, alongside vision-language pre-training, establishes a fresh and independent foundation for robot learning. Intuitively, video world models provide the ability to imagine the near future by understanding the causality between actions and visual dynamics. Inspired by this, we introduce LingBot-VA, an autoregressive diffusion framework that learns frame prediction and policy execution simultaneously. Our model features three carefully crafted designs: (1) a shared latent space, integrating vision and action tokens, driven by a Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture, (2) a closed-loop rollout mechanism, allowing for ongoing acquisition of environmental feedback with ground-truth observations, (3) an asynchronous inference pipeline, parallelizing action prediction and motor execution to support efficient control. We evaluate our model on both simulation benchmarks and real-world scenarios, where it shows significant promise in long-horizon manipulation, data efficiency in post-training, and strong generalizability to novel configurations. The code and model are made publicly available to facilitate the community.

AI.tldr: LingBot-VA: an autoregressive diffusion-based video world model for robot learning that jointly predicts frames and actions using a shared latent space and three design pillars, achieving long-horizon manipulation with data-efficient post-training and good generalization; code released.
AI.motivation: Video world models combined with vision-language pre-training offer a more independent, capable foundation for robot learning by enabling imagined futures through action-to-visual dynamics, addressing data efficiency and generalization challenges in manipulation tasks.
AI.method: Proposes LingBot-VA, an autoregressive diffusion framework with (1) a shared latent space integrating vision and action tokens via a Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT), (2) a closed-loop rollout to continuously acquire environment feedback with ground-truth observations, and (3) an asynchronous inference pipeline that parallelizes action prediction and motor execution for efficient control; learns frame prediction and policy execution jointly.
AI.result: Evaluated on simulation benchmarks and real-world scenarios, LingBot-VA demonstrates promise for long-horizon manipulation, improved data efficiency after training, and robust generalization to novel configurations; code and model are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: The integration of video world modeling with diffusion-based frame prediction and policy learning, enabled by a MoT-based shared latent space and asynchronous inference, provides a strong, data-efficient foundation for robot learning with scalable feedback loops and broad generalization potential.

title: Latent Chain-of-Thought as Planning: Decoupling Reasoning from Verbalization
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) empowers Large Language Models (LLMs) to tackle complex problems, but remains constrained by the computational cost and reasoning path collapse when grounded in discrete token spaces. Recent latent reasoning approaches attempt to optimize efficiency by performing reasoning within continuous hidden states. However, these methods typically operate as opaque end-to-end mappings from explicit reasoning steps to latent states, and often require a pre-defined number of latent steps during inference. In this work, we introduce PLaT (Planning with Latent Thoughts), a framework that reformulates latent reasoning as planning by fundamentally decouple reasoning from verbalization. We model reasoning as a deterministic trajectory of latent planning states, while a separate Decoder grounds these thoughts into text when necessary. This decoupling allows the model to dynamically determine when to terminate reasoning rather than relying on fixed hyperparameters. Empirical results on mathematical benchmarks reveal a distinct trade-off: while PLaT achieves lower greedy accuracy than baselines, it demonstrates superior scalability in terms of reasoning diversity. This indicates that PLaT learns a robust, broader solution space, offering a transparent and scalable foundation for inference-time search.

AI.tldr: PLaT introduces a decoupled latent reasoning framework that plans in latent space and only grounds to text when needed, enabling dynamic termination and broader solution diversity at the cost of greedy accuracy.
AI.motivation: To overcome CoT's computational cost and token-space path collapse, and to address opacity and fixed-step limits of prior latent reasoning approaches by separating planning from verbalization and enabling dynamic termination.
AI.method: Model reasoning as a deterministic trajectory of latent planning states, with a separate decoder that grounds thoughts into text as required. This decouples inference-time planning from natural language generation and allows dynamic termination without fixed latent-step hyperparameters.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on mathematical benchmarks shows lower greedy accuracy than baselines but stronger scalability in reasoning diversity, indicating a broader and more robust solution space suitable for inference-time search.
AI.conclusion: PLaT provides a transparent, scalable foundation for latent reasoning by decoupling planning from grounding and enabling dynamic termination, trading some greedy performance for improved diversity and search efficiency.

title: The Powers of Precision: Structure-Informed Detection in Complex Systems -- From Customer Churn to Seizure Onset
summary: Emergent phenomena -- onset of epileptic seizures, sudden customer churn, or pandemic outbreaks -- often arise from hidden causal interactions in complex systems. We propose a machine learning method for their early detection that addresses a core challenge: unveiling and harnessing a system's latent causal structure despite the data-generating process being unknown and partially observed. The method learns an optimal feature representation from a one-parameter family of estimators -- powers of the empirical covariance or precision matrix -- offering a principled way to tune in to the underlying structure driving the emergence of critical events. A supervised learning module then classifies the learned representation. We prove structural consistency of the family and demonstrate the empirical soundness of our approach on seizure detection and churn prediction, attaining competitive results in both. Beyond prediction, and toward explainability, we ascertain that the optimal covariance power exhibits evidence of good identifiability while capturing structural signatures, thus reconciling predictive performance with interpretable statistical structure.

AI.tldr: A principled ML approach for early detection of emergent events by learning from a one-parameter family of covariance/precision matrix powers and classifying the resulting representations; shows structural consistency and competitive performance on seizure and churn tasks with interpretable structure.
AI.motivation: Emergent phenomena in complex systems (e.g., seizures, churn, outbreaks) arise from hidden causal interactions; there is a need to uncover latent structure from partially observed data for early detection and explainability without assuming a known data-generating process.
AI.method: Learn an optimal feature representation by optimizing over a one-parameter family of estimators: powers of the empirical covariance or precision matrix. A supervised learning module then classifies the learned representation. The authors prove structural consistency of the family and evaluate on seizure detection and churn prediction, examining identifiability and structural signatures.
AI.result: The method achieves competitive results in both seizure detection and churn prediction. It demonstrates empirical soundness, and the optimal covariance power exhibits identifiability while capturing structural signatures, reconciling predictive performance with interpretable statistical structure.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a theoretically grounded, interpretable approach to early detection of emergent phenomena by leveraging latent causal structure—even when the data-generating process is unknown and only partially observed—balancing predictive accuracy with identifiable structural information.

title: Vision KAN: Towards an Attention-Free Backbone for Vision with Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
summary: Attention mechanisms have become a key module in modern vision backbones due to their ability to model long-range dependencies. However, their quadratic complexity in sequence length and the difficulty of interpreting attention weights limit both scalability and clarity. Recent attention-free architectures demonstrate that strong performance can be achieved without pairwise attention, motivating the search for alternatives. In this work, we introduce Vision KAN (ViK), an attention-free backbone inspired by the Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks. At its core lies MultiPatch-RBFKAN, a unified token mixer that combines (a) patch-wise nonlinear transform with Radial Basis Function-based KANs, (b) axis-wise separable mixing for efficient local propagation, and (c) low-rank global mapping for long-range interaction. Employing as a drop-in replacement for attention modules, this formulation tackles the prohibitive cost of full KANs on high-resolution features by adopting a patch-wise grouping strategy with lightweight operators to restore cross-patch dependencies. Experiments on ImageNet-1K show that ViK achieves competitive accuracy with linear complexity, demonstrating the potential of KAN-based token mixing as an efficient and theoretically grounded alternative to attention.

AI.tldr: ViK is an attention-free Vision backbone using MultiPatch-RBFKAN, achieving competitive ImageNet performance with linear complexity by combining patch-wise nonlinear transforms, axis-wise separable mixing, and low-rank global mapping; demonstrates a theoretically grounded alternative to attention.
AI.motivation: Reduce the quadratic complexity and interpretability issues of attention while retaining competitive performance in vision backbones; explore attention-free architectures based on Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs).
AI.method: Introduce ViK with a unified token mixer (MultiPatch-RBFKAN) that combines: (i) patch-wise nonlinear transform with Radial Basis Function-based KANs, (ii) axis-wise separable mixing for efficient local propagation, and (iii) low-rank global mapping for long-range interactions; employ patch-wise grouping to manage high-resolution features and enable a drop-in replacement for attention modules.
AI.result: On ImageNet-1K, ViK achieves competitive accuracy with linear (O(N)) complexity, demonstrating the viability of KAN-based token mixing as an efficient and theoretically grounded alternative to attention.
AI.conclusion: ViK demonstrates the potential of attention-free KAN-based backbones for scalable vision models, offering both efficiency and interpretability advantages over traditional attention-based architectures.

title: Hebbian Learning with Global Direction
summary: Backpropagation algorithm has driven the remarkable success of deep neural networks, but its lack of biological plausibility and high computational costs have motivated the ongoing search for alternative training methods. Hebbian learning has attracted considerable interest as a biologically plausible alternative to backpropagation. Nevertheless, its exclusive reliance on local information, without consideration of global task objectives, fundamentally limits its scalability. Inspired by the biological synergy between neuromodulators and local plasticity, we introduce a novel model-agnostic Global-guided Hebbian Learning (GHL) framework, which seamlessly integrates local and global information to scale up across diverse networks and tasks. In specific, the local component employs Oja's rule with competitive learning to ensure stable and effective local updates. Meanwhile, the global component introduces a sign-based signal that guides the direction of local Hebbian plasticity updates. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms existing Hebbian approaches. Notably, on large-scale network and complex datasets like ImageNet, our framework achieves the competitive results and significantly narrows the gap with standard backpropagation.

AI.tldr: Global-guided Hebbian Learning (GHL) combines local Oja-based updates with a global sign-based modulator to align Hebbian plasticity with global objectives, achieving competitive performance on large-scale tasks and reducing the gap to backprop.
AI.motivation: Hebbian learning is biologically plausible but lacks global task objectives and scalability; a neuromodulator-inspired global signal could guide local updates toward the desired objective.
AI.method: Model-agnostic framework: local updates via Oja's rule with competitive learning; global component injects a sign-based signal determining the direction of local Hebbian updates; aims to scale across architectures and datasets.
AI.result: Experimentally outperforms existing Hebbian methods; on large networks and datasets like ImageNet, achieves competitive results and narrows the gap with standard backprop.
AI.conclusion: GHL demonstrates that integrating a global guidance signal with local Hebbian plasticity yields scalable, biologically plausible training closely approaching backprop performance, meriting further study.

title: AC2L-GAD: Active Counterfactual Contrastive Learning for Graph Anomaly Detection
summary: Graph anomaly detection aims to identify abnormal patterns in networks, but faces significant challenges from label scarcity and extreme class imbalance. While graph contrastive learning offers a promising unsupervised solution, existing methods suffer from two critical limitations: random augmentations break semantic consistency in positive pairs, while naive negative sampling produces trivial, uninformative contrasts. We propose AC2L-GAD, an Active Counterfactual Contrastive Learning framework that addresses both limitations through principled counterfactual reasoning. By combining information-theoretic active selection with counterfactual generation, our approach identifies structurally complex nodes and generates anomaly-preserving positive augmentations alongside normal negative counterparts that provide hard contrasts, while restricting expensive counterfactual generation to a strategically selected subset. This design reduces computational overhead by approximately 65% compared to full-graph counterfactual generation while maintaining detection quality. Experiments on nine benchmark datasets, including real-world financial transaction graphs from GADBench, show that AC2L-GAD achieves competitive or superior performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines, with notable gains in datasets where anomalies exhibit complex attribute-structure interactions.

AI.tldr: AC2L-GAD introduces Active Counterfactual Contrastive Learning for graph anomaly detection to tackle label scarcity and extreme class imbalance. It uses information-theoretic active node selection and counterfactually generated, anomaly-preserving positives with hard negatives, while restricting costly counterfactuals to a subset, achieving ~65% computational savings.
AI.motivation: Graph anomaly detection is hampered by limited labels and severe class imbalance. Standard graph contrastive learning relies on random augmentations and naive negatives, which破 semantic consistency and contrasts. A principled, efficient framework is needed to select informative nodes and generate meaningful augmentations.
AI.method: Propose AC2L-GAD that combines information-theoretic active selection with counterfactual generation. It identifies structurally complex nodes, creates anomaly-preserving positive augmentations, and provides normal hard negatives. Counterfactual generation is applied to a strategically chosen subset to reduce cost.
AI.result: On nine benchmark datasets, including GADBench real-world financial graphs, AC2L-GAD is competitive or superior to state-of-the-art baselines, with notable gains when anomalies involve complex attribute-structure interactions.
AI.conclusion: AC2L-GAD delivers efficient, high-quality graph anomaly detection by leveraging counterfactual reasoning and active selection to balance informative positives and hard negatives, achieving substantial computational savings without compromising detection performance.

title: Bi-Anchor Interpolation Solver for Accelerating Generative Modeling
summary: Flow Matching (FM) models have emerged as a leading paradigm for high-fidelity synthesis. However, their reliance on iterative Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) solving creates a significant latency bottleneck. Existing solutions face a dichotomy: training-free solvers suffer from significant performance degradation at low Neural Function Evaluations (NFEs), while training-based one- or few-steps generation methods incur prohibitive training costs and lack plug-and-play versatility. To bridge this gap, we propose the Bi-Anchor Interpolation Solver (BA-solver). BA-solver retains the versatility of standard training-free solvers while achieving significant acceleration by introducing a lightweight SideNet (1-2% backbone size) alongside the frozen backbone. Specifically, our method is founded on two synergistic components: \textbf{1) Bidirectional Temporal Perception}, where the SideNet learns to approximate both future and historical velocities without retraining the heavy backbone; and 2) Bi-Anchor Velocity Integration, which utilizes the SideNet with two anchor velocities to efficiently approximate intermediate velocities for batched high-order integration. By utilizing the backbone to establish high-precision ``anchors'' and the SideNet to densify the trajectory, BA-solver enables large interval sizes with minimized error. Empirical results on ImageNet-256^2 demonstrate that BA-solver achieves generation quality comparable to 100+ NFEs Euler solver in just 10 NFEs and maintains high fidelity in as few as 5 NFEs, incurring negligible training costs. Furthermore, BA-solver ensures seamless integration with existing generative pipelines, facilitating downstream tasks such as image editing.

AI.tldr: Bi-Anchor Interpolation Solver (BA-solver) speeds up flow-matching models by pairing a lightweight SideNet with a frozen backbone to interpolate velocities, achieving high-quality results with 5–10 NFEs on ImageNet-256^2 and minimal training cost.
AI.motivation: Address the latency bottleneck from iterative ODE solving in Flow Matching models by combining training-free flexibility with acceleration, aiming for high fidelity at low NFEs without costly retraining.
AI.method: Introduce SideNet (1-2% backbone) with freezing of the backbone. Two key components: (1) Bidirectional Temporal Perception where SideNet predicts future and past velocities; (2) Bi-Anchor Velocity Integration using two anchor velocities to interpolate intermediate velocities for batched high-order integration. Backbone provides high-precision anchors; SideNet densifies the trajectory with efficient interpolation across large intervals.
AI.result: Empirical results on ImageNet-256^2 show BA-solver matches 100+ NFEs Euler solver quality using only 10 NFEs, and preserves high fidelity with as few as 5 NFEs, with negligible training cost and seamless integration into existing pipelines, enabling downstream tasks such as image editing.
AI.conclusion: BA-solver delivers a versatile, fast, and plug-and-play acceleration for Flow Matching models—reducing inference latency while maintaining high fidelity and keeping training overhead negligible, broadening practical applicability.

title: NEMO: Execution-Aware Optimization Modeling via Autonomous Coding Agents
summary:
In this paper, we present NEMO, a system that translates Natural-language descriptions of decision problems into formal Executable Mathematical Optimization implementations, operating collaboratively with users or autonomously. Existing approaches typically rely on specialized large language models (LLMs) or bespoke, task-specific agents. Such methods are often brittle, complex and frequently generating syntactically invalid or non-executable code.
  NEMO instead centers on remote interaction with autonomous coding agents (ACAs), treated as a first-class abstraction analogous to API-based interaction with LLMs. This design enables the construction of higher-level systems around ACAs that structure, consolidate, and iteratively refine task specifications. Because ACAs execute within sandboxed environments, code produced by NEMO is executable by construction, allowing automated validation and repair.
  Building on this, we introduce novel coordination patterns with and across ACAs, including asymmetric validation loops between independently generated optimizer and simulator implementations (serving as a high-level validation mechanism), external memory for experience reuse, and robustness enhancements via minimum Bayes risk (MBR) decoding and self-consistency. We evaluate NEMO on nine established optimization benchmarks. As depicted in Figure 1, it achieves state-of-the-art performance on the majority of tasks, with substantial margins on several datasets, demonstrating the power of execution-aware agentic architectures for automated optimization modeling.

AI.tldr: NEMO translates natural-language descriptions into executable optimization code via autonomous coding agents, enabling validation, repair, and achieving state-of-the-art performance on nine benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address brittleness and non-executable outputs of existing LLM-based optimization approaches by introducing execution-aware, sandboxed agent collaboration and reusable experience.
AI.method: Proposes ACAs as first-class abstractions; sandboxed execution; novel coordination patterns including asymmetric validation loops between optimizer and simulator implementations; external memory; robustness via MBR decoding and self-consistency.
AI.result: Evaluated on nine optimization benchmarks; achieves state-of-the-art performance on the majority of tasks with substantial margins on several datasets.
AI.conclusion: Execution-aware agentic architectures are powerful for automated optimization modeling, enabling robust, executable formulations and improved performance.

title: Breaking the Reasoning Horizon in Entity Alignment Foundation Models
summary: Entity alignment (EA) is critical for knowledge graph (KG) fusion. Existing EA models lack transferability and are incapable of aligning unseen KGs without retraining. While using graph foundation models (GFMs) offer a solution, we find that directly adapting GFMs to EA remains largely ineffective. This stems from a critical "reasoning horizon gap": unlike link prediction in GFMs, EA necessitates capturing long-range dependencies across sparse and heterogeneous KG structuresTo address this challenge, we propose a EA foundation model driven by a parallel encoding strategy. We utilize seed EA pairs as local anchors to guide the information flow, initializing and encoding two parallel streams simultaneously. This facilitates anchor-conditioned message passing and significantly shortens the inference trajectory by leveraging local structural proximity instead of global search. Additionally, we incorporate a merged relation graph to model global dependencies and a learnable interaction module for precise matching. Extensive experiments verify the effectiveness of our framework, highlighting its strong generalizability to unseen KGs.

AI.tldr: A parallel-encoding EA foundation model to improve transferability and unseen KG alignment by seed-anchor guided dual streams, merged relation graph, and interaction module, addressing the reasoning horizon gap.
AI.motivation: EA requires long-range reasoning over sparse, heterogeneous KGs and generalization to unseen graphs; GFMs adaptation for EA suffers from insufficient ability to capture global dependencies and long inference paths.
AI.method: Seed pairs initialize two parallel encoders executing anchor-conditioned message passing; two streams propagate locally with anchors; a merged relation graph models global dependencies; a learnable interaction module matches entity representations across streams.
AI.result: Empirical results on multiple datasets demonstrate strong zero-shot/generalization to unseen KGs and superior alignment accuracy compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed EA foundation model with parallel encoding mitigates the reasoning horizon gap and enhances transferability, enabling effective EA without retraining on new KGs.

title: Unifying Heterogeneous Degradations: Uncertainty-Aware Diffusion Bridge Model for All-in-One Image Restoration
summary: All-in-One Image Restoration (AiOIR) faces the fundamental challenge in reconciling conflicting optimization objectives across heterogeneous degradations. Existing methods are often constrained by coarse-grained control mechanisms or fixed mapping schedules, yielding suboptimal adaptation. To address this, we propose an Uncertainty-Aware Diffusion Bridge Model (UDBM), which innovatively reformulates AiOIR as a stochastic transport problem steered by pixel-wise uncertainty. By introducing a relaxed diffusion bridge formulation which replaces the strict terminal constraint with a relaxed constraint, we model the uncertainty of degradations while theoretically resolving the drift singularity inherent in standard diffusion bridges. Furthermore, we devise a dual modulation strategy: the noise schedule aligns diverse degradations into a shared high-entropy latent space, while the path schedule adaptively regulates the transport trajectory motivated by the viscous dynamics of entropy regularization. By effectively rectifying the transport geometry and dynamics, UDBM achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse restoration tasks within a single inference step.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty-Aware Diffusion Bridge Model (UDBM) for All-in-One Image Restoration (AiOIR) that uses a relaxed diffusion-bridge formulation and dual modulation to unify heterogeneous degradations into a shared latent space, achieving SOTA in a single inference step.
AI.motivation: AiOIR suffers from conflicting optimization objectives across heterogeneous degradations and coarse control mechanisms or fixed mapping schedules. A probabilistic transport viewpoint with pixel-wise uncertainty can better reconcile degradations and enable adaptive restoration.
AI.method: Formulates AiOIR as a stochastic transport problem steered by pixel-wise uncertainty. Introduces a relaxed diffusion bridge that replaces the strict terminal constraint, modeling degradation uncertainty and resolving drift singularities. Proposes a dual modulation strategy: (1) a noise schedule that maps diverse degradations into a shared high-entropy latent space; (2) a path schedule that adaptively regulates the transport trajectory under entropy-regularized viscous dynamics. Enables effective transport geometry and dynamics correction with single-step inference.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art restoration performance across diverse tasks within a single inference step.
AI.conclusion: The relaxed diffusion bridge and dual modulation rectify transport geometry and dynamics, enabling robust, unified AiOIR with efficient inference.

title: TeachBench: A Syllabus-Grounded Framework for Evaluating Teaching Ability in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) show promise as teaching assistants, yet their teaching capability remains insufficiently evaluated. Existing benchmarks mainly focus on problem-solving or problem-level guidance, leaving knowledge-centered teaching underexplored. We propose a syllabus-grounded evaluation framework that measures LLM teaching capability via student performance improvement after multi-turn instruction. By restricting teacher agents to structured knowledge points and example problems, the framework avoids information leakage and enables reuse of existing benchmarks. We instantiate the framework on Gaokao data across multiple subjects. Experiments reveal substantial variation in teaching effectiveness across models and domains: some models perform well in mathematics, while teaching remains challenging in physics and chemistry. We also find that incorporating example problems does not necessarily improve teaching, as models often shift toward example-specific error correction. Overall, our results highlight teaching ability as a distinct and measurable dimension of LLM behavior.

AI.tldr: Proposes a syllabus-grounded, multi-turn evaluation framework to measure LLM teaching ability via student performance, instantiated on Gaokao data; finds model/domain variation and that example problems may not always help; supports treating teaching as a distinct LLM capability.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in evaluating LLMs as teachers, not just problem solvers. Aims to quantify knowledge-centered teaching while preventing information leakage and enabling reuse of existing benchmarks.
AI.method: Defines a structured teaching protocol restricted to knowledge points and example problems, uses multi-turn instruction, and measures student performance gains. Validated on Gaokao data across several subjects.
AI.result: Significant variation in teaching effectiveness across models and domains; math more tractable than physics/chemistry. Adding example problems does not always improve teaching and can induce example-specific error correction.
AI.conclusion: Teaching ability is a distinct, measurable dimension of LLM behavior. The framework provides a controlled, reusable way to evaluate LLMs as teachers.

authors: A. S. M. Ahsanul Sarkar Akib
title: HydroSense: A Dual-Microcontroller IoT Framework for Real-Time Multi-Parameter Water Quality Monitoring with Edge Processing and Cloud Analytics
summary: The global water crisis necessitates affordable, accurate, and real-time water quality monitoring solutions. Traditional approaches relying on manual sampling or expensive commercial systems fail to address accessibility challenges in resource-constrained environments. This paper presents HydroSense, an innovative Internet of Things framework that integrates six critical water quality parameters including pH, dissolved oxygen (DO), temperature, total dissolved solids (TDS), estimated nitrogen, and water level into a unified monitoring system. HydroSense employs a novel dual-microcontroller architecture, utilizing Arduino Uno for precision analog measurements with five-point calibration algorithms and ESP32 for wireless connectivity, edge processing, and cloud integration. The system implements advanced signal processing techniques including median filtering for TDS measurement, temperature compensation algorithms, and robust error handling. Experimental validation over 90 days demonstrates exceptional performance metrics: pH accuracy of plus or minus 0.08 units across the 0 to 14 range, DO measurement stability within plus or minus 0.2 mg/L, TDS accuracy of plus or minus 1.9 percent across 0 to 1000 ppm, and 99.8 percent cloud data transmission reliability. With a total implementation cost of 32,983 BDT (approximately 300 USD), HydroSense achieves an 85 percent cost reduction compared to commercial systems while providing enhanced connectivity through the Firebase real-time database. This research establishes a new paradigm for accessible environmental monitoring, demonstrating that professional-grade water quality assessment can be achieved through intelligent system architecture and cost-effective component selection.

AI.tldr: HydroSense offers a dual- MCU IoT water quality monitor (pH, DO, temp, TDS, nitrogen estimate, water level) with Arduino Uno and ESP32, median filtering, calibration/compensation, and Firebase cloud integration; 90-day validation shows strong accuracy, high reliability, and ~85% cost reduction.
AI.motivation: Address the global water crisis by delivering affordable, accurate, and real-time water quality monitoring accessible in resource-limited environments, replacing manual sampling and costly systems.
AI.method: Dual- MCU architecture (Arduino Uno for precision analog measurement with five-point calibration; ESP32 for wireless connectivity, edge processing, cloud integration). Signal processing includes median filtering for TDS, temperature compensation, and robust error handling. Firebase real-time database used for cloud storage. Validation over 90 days with specified accuracy metrics. Cost about 300 USD (~32,983 BDT), claiming 85% cost reduction vs. commercial systems.
AI.result: Achieves pH accuracy ±0.08 pH units (0–14), DO ±0.2 mg/L, TDS ±1.9% (0–1000 ppm), and 99.8% cloud data transmission reliability over 90 days; demonstrated overall system cost reduction and enhanced connectivity.
AI.conclusion: HydroSense demonstrates that professional-grade water quality assessment is attainable with a thoughtfully designed low-cost hardware–software stack and cloud integration, potentially redefining accessible environmental monitoring in resource-constrained settings.

title: DataCross: A Unified Benchmark and Agent Framework for Cross-Modal Heterogeneous Data Analysis
summary: In real-world data science and enterprise decision-making, critical information is often fragmented across directly queryable structured sources (e.g., SQL, CSV) and "zombie data" locked in unstructured visual documents (e.g., scanned reports, invoice images). Existing data analytics agents are predominantly limited to processing structured data, failing to activate and correlate this high-value visual information, thus creating a significant gap with industrial needs. To bridge this gap, we introduce DataCross, a novel benchmark and collaborative agent framework for unified, insight-driven analysis across heterogeneous data modalities. DataCrossBench comprises 200 end-to-end analysis tasks across finance, healthcare, and other domains. It is constructed via a human-in-the-loop reverse-synthesis pipeline, ensuring realistic complexity, cross-source dependency, and verifiable ground truth. The benchmark categorizes tasks into three difficulty tiers to evaluate agents' capabilities in visual table extraction, cross-modal alignment, and multi-step joint reasoning. We also propose the DataCrossAgent framework, inspired by the "divide-and-conquer" workflow of human analysts. It employs specialized sub-agents, each an expert on a specific data source, which are coordinated via a structured workflow of Intra-source Deep Exploration, Key Source Identification, and Contextual Cross-pollination. A novel reReAct mechanism enables robust code generation and debugging for factual verification. Experimental results show that DataCrossAgent achieves a 29.7% improvement in factuality over GPT-4o and exhibits superior robustness on high-difficulty tasks, effectively activating fragmented "zombie data" for insightful, cross-modal analysis.

AI.tldr: DataCross proposes a benchmark (DataCrossBench) and a multi-agent framework (DataCrossAgent) to unify analysis across structured data and unstructured visual documents, achieving substantial gains in factuality and robustness over baseline models.
AI.motivation: Critical information is dispersed across queryable sources and locked in visual, unstructured documents, creating a gap between existing analytics tools and industrial needs for cross-modal reasoning and data activation.
AI.method: DataCrossBench comprises 200 end-to-end tasks across domains (finance, healthcare, etc.) built via human-in-the-loop reverse-synthesis. DataCrossAgent follows a divide-and-conquer workflow with specialized sub-agents per data source, coordinated by Intra-source Deep Exploration, Key Source Identification, and Contextual Cross-pollination. A reReAct mechanism supports robust code generation and debugging for factual verification.
AI.result: Experimental results show a 29.7% improvement in factuality over GPT-4o and improved robustness on high-difficulty tasks, indicating effective activation of fragmented 'zombie data' for cross-modal analysis.
AI.conclusion: DataCrossAgent provides a scalable framework for unified, insight-driven analysis across heterogeneous data modalities and a realistic benchmark suite for evaluating cross-modal agents, with clear potential for industrial deployment.

title: Rethinking Refinement: Correcting Generative Bias without Noise Injection
summary: Generative models, including diffusion and flow-based models, often exhibit systematic biases that degrade sample quality, particularly in high-dimensional settings. We revisit refinement methods and show that effective bias correction can be achieved as a post-hoc procedure, without noise injection or multi-step resampling of the sampling process. We propose a flow-matching-based \textbf{Bi-stage Flow Refinement (BFR)} framework with two refinement strategies operating at different stages: latent space alignment for approximately invertible generators and data space refinement trained with lightweight augmentations. Unlike previous refiners that perturb sampling dynamics, BFR preserves the original ODE trajectory and applies deterministic corrections to generated samples. Experiments on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and FFHQ at 256x256 resolution demonstrate consistent improvements in fidelity and coverage; notably, starting from base samples with FID 3.95, latent space refinement achieves a \textbf{state-of-the-art} FID of \textbf{1.46} on MNIST using only a single additional function evaluation (1-NFE), while maintaining sample diversity.

AI.tldr: Bi-stage Flow Refinement (BFR) is a two-stage, post-hoc bias correction framework for diffusion/flow-based generators that preserves the original ODE trajectory and applies deterministic corrections, achieving substantial fidelity/coverage gains with minimal extra computation. On MNIST, CIFAR-10, and FFHQ (256x256), latent-space refinement yields state-of-the-art MNIST FID of 1.46 with only 1 extra function evaluation (1-NFE).
AI.motivation: Generative models exhibit systematic biases that degrade sample quality, especially in high dimensions. Prior refiners often perturb sampling dynamics or require multi-step resampling and noise injection. A post-hoc, deterministic correction that preserves the sampling trajectory offers a potentially efficient, stable improvement.
AI.method: A flow-matching-based Bi-stage Flow Refinement (BFR) with two strategies: (1) latent space alignment for approximately invertible generators, aligning latent representations to reduce bias; (2) data-space refinement trained with lightweight augmentations to correct residual artifacts. Corrections are deterministic and do not modify the ODE trajectory; only a single additional function evaluation is needed (1-NFE).
AI.result: Empirical results on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and FFHQ (256x256) show consistent improvements in both fidelity and coverage. Notably, starting from base samples with FID 3.95 on MNIST, latent space refinement achieves a new state-of-the-art FID of 1.46 using 1-NFE, while maintaining sample diversity.
AI.conclusion: BFR provides an effective, efficient post-hoc refinement that preserves sampling dynamics and yields substantial quality gains across datasets. The two-stage design—latent-space alignment and data-space refinement—complements each other to improve both fidelity and coverage.

title: WMVLM: Evaluating Diffusion Model Image Watermarking via Vision-Language Models
summary: Digital watermarking is essential for securing generated images from diffusion models. Accurate watermark evaluation is critical for algorithm development, yet existing methods have significant limitations: they lack a unified framework for both residual and semantic watermarks, provide results without interpretability, neglect comprehensive security considerations, and often use inappropriate metrics for semantic watermarks. To address these gaps, we propose WMVLM, the first unified and interpretable evaluation framework for diffusion model image watermarking via vision-language models (VLMs). We redefine quality and security metrics for each watermark type: residual watermarks are evaluated by artifact strength and erasure resistance, while semantic watermarks are assessed through latent distribution shifts. Moreover, we introduce a three-stage training strategy to progressively enable the model to achieve classification, scoring, and interpretable text generation. Experiments show WMVLM outperforms state-of-the-art VLMs with strong generalization across datasets, diffusion models, and watermarking methods.

AI.tldr: A unified, interpretable evaluation framework WMVLM for diffusion model image watermarking using vision-language models (VLMs). It introduces stage-wise training (classification, scoring, interpretable text generation), and defines metrics for residual (artifact strength, erasure resistance) and semantic watermarks (latent distribution shifts). Demonstrates strong generalization and outperforms SOTA across datasets, diffusion models, and watermarking methods.
AI.motivation: There is no unified framework that handles both residual and semantic watermarks with interpretable metrics and comprehensive security considerations in diffusion-model watermarking; existing metrics are ad hoc and inadequately assess semantic watermarks.
AI.method: Introduce WMVLM, a three-stage training strategy enabling classification, scoring, and interpretable text generation for watermark evaluation via vision-language models. Redefine quality and security metrics for residual (artifact strength, erasure resistance) and semantic watermarks (latent distribution shifts). Extensive experiments show strong generalization across datasets, diffusion models, and watermarking methods, outperforming state-of-the-art VLMs.
AI.result: WMVLM outperforms state-of-the-art vision-language models with strong generalization across datasets, diffusion models, and watermarking methods.
AI.conclusion: WMVLM provides a unified, interpretable evaluation framework for diffusion-model image watermarking via VLMs, with redesigned metrics and a three-stage training approach that enhances interpretability and generalization.

title: System 1&2 Synergy via Dynamic Model Interpolation
summary: Training a unified language model that adapts between intuitive System 1 and deliberative System 2 remains challenging due to interference between their cognitive modes. Recent studies have thus pursued making System 2 models more efficient. However, these approaches focused on output control, limiting what models produce. We argue that this paradigm is misaligned: output length is merely a symptom of the model's cognitive configuration, not the root cause. In this work, we shift the focus to capability control, which modulates \textit{how models think} rather than \textit{what they produce}. To realize this, we leverage existing Instruct and Thinking checkpoints through dynamic parameter interpolation, without additional training. Our pilot study establishes that linear interpolation yields a convex, monotonic Pareto frontier, underpinned by representation continuity and structural connectivity. Building on this, we propose \textbf{DAMI} (\textbf{D}yn\textbf{A}mic \textbf{M}odel \textbf{I}nterpolation), a framework that estimates a query-specific Reasoning Intensity $λ(q)$ to configure cognitive depth. For training-based estimation, we develop a preference learning method encoding accuracy and efficiency criteria. For zero-shot deployment, we introduce a confidence-based method leveraging inter-model cognitive discrepancy. Experiments on five mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that DAMI achieves higher accuracy than the Thinking model while remaining efficient, effectively combining the efficiency of System 1 with the reasoning depth of System 2.

AI.tldr: DAMI is a dynamic interpolation framework that tunes a model's cognitive depth to balance accuracy and efficiency, achieving better math reasoning performance than a Thinking baseline while staying efficient.
AI.motivation: Unified language models suffer interference between System 1 (fast, intuitive) and System 2 (deliberate) modes. Prior work focused on output control, but root causes lie in cognitive configuration; the paper seeks capability control to modulate how models think rather than what they produce.
AI.method: Linearly interpolate between Instruct and Thinking checkpoints without additional training to realize dynamic parameter interpolation. Introduce a query-specific Reasoning Intensity λ(q) to configure cognitive depth. For training-based estimation, use a preference-learning method encoding accuracy and efficiency; for zero-shot deployment, use a confidence-based method leveraging inter-model cognitive discrepancy.
AI.result: Linear interpolation yields a convex, monotonic Pareto frontier supported by representation continuity and structural connectivity. On five mathematical reasoning benchmarks, DAMI achieves higher accuracy than the Thinking model while maintaining efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic capability control via DAMI effectively combines the efficiency of System 1 with the reasoning depth of System 2, offering a practical approach to adaptable reasoning across tasks.

title: Rethinking Self-Training Based Cross-Subject Domain Adaptation for SSVEP Classification
summary: Steady-state visually evoked potentials (SSVEP)-based brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) are widely used due to their high signal-to-noise ratio and user-friendliness. Accurate decoding of SSVEP signals is crucial for interpreting user intentions in BCI applications. However, signal variability across subjects and the costly user-specific annotation limit recognition performance. Therefore, we propose a novel cross-subject domain adaptation method built upon the self-training paradigm. Specifically, a Filter-Bank Euclidean Alignment (FBEA) strategy is designed to exploit frequency information from SSVEP filter banks. Then, we propose a Cross-Subject Self-Training (CSST) framework consisting of two stages: Pre-Training with Adversarial Learning (PTAL), which aligns the source and target distributions, and Dual-Ensemble Self-Training (DEST), which refines pseudo-label quality. Moreover, we introduce a Time-Frequency Augmented Contrastive Learning (TFA-CL) module to enhance feature discriminability across multiple augmented views. Extensive experiments on the Benchmark and BETA datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across varying signal lengths, highlighting its superiority.

AI.tldr: A cross-subject self-training framework for SSVEP-BCIs integrating Filter-Bank Euclidean Alignment (FBEA), Cross-Subject Self-Training (CSST) with PTAL and DEST, and Time-Frequency Augmented Contrastive Learning (TFA-CL) to achieve state-of-the-art results with reduced annotation.
AI.motivation: Subject variability and costly user-specific annotations limit SSVEP decoding performance; cross-subject unsupervised or semi-supervised adaptation can leverage unlabeled target data.
AI.method: FBEA exploits filter-bank frequency information for SSVEP alignment; CSST has two stages: Pre-Training with Adversarial Learning (PTAL) to align source and target distributions, and Dual-Ensemble Self-Training (DEST) to refine pseudo-labels; TFA-CL enhances discriminability via contrastive learning across multiple time-frequency augmented views.
AI.result: Experiments on Benchmark and BETA datasets report state-of-the-art performance across varying signal lengths, demonstrating robustness and effective cross-subject transfer.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively enables cross-subject SSVEP decoding with improved robustness and reduced labeling requirements, holding promise for practical and scalable BCI deployments; further analyses could explore ablations, computational cost, and generalization to additional datasets.

title: PathReasoner-R1: Instilling Structured Reasoning into Pathology Vision-Language Model via Knowledge-Guided Policy Optimization
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are advancing computational pathology with superior visual understanding capabilities. However, current systems often reduce diagnosis to directly output conclusions without verifiable evidence-linked reasoning, which severely limits clinical trust and hinders expert error rectification. To address these barriers, we construct PathReasoner, the first large-scale dataset of whole-slide image (WSI) reasoning. Unlike previous work reliant on unverified distillation, we develop a rigorous knowledge-guided generation pipeline. By leveraging medical knowledge graphs, we explicitly align structured pathological findings and clinical reasoning with diagnoses, generating over 20K high-quality instructional samples. Based on the database, we propose PathReasoner-R1, which synergizes trajectory-masked supervised fine-tuning with reasoning-oriented reinforcement learning to instill structured chain-of-thought capabilities. To ensure medical rigor, we engineer a knowledge-aware multi-granular reward function incorporating an Entity Reward mechanism strictly aligned with knowledge graphs. This effectively guides the model to optimize for logical consistency rather than mere outcome matching, thereby enhancing robustness. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PathReasoner-R1 achieves state-of-the-art performance on both PathReasoner and public benchmarks across various image scales, equipping pathology models with transparent, clinically grounded reasoning capabilities. Dataset and code are available at https://github.com/cyclexfy/PathReasoner-R1.

AI.tldr: Proposes PathReasoner dataset and PathReasoner-R1 model to enable verifiable, knowledge-grounded chain-of-thought reasoning in pathology VLMs via knowledge graphs and reinforcement learning; achieves state-of-the-art results and provides code/dataset.
AI.motivation: Clinical trust requires evidence-linked reasoning. Current vision-language pathology models often output diagnoses without explanations, hindering expert verification and error rectification; a knowledge-guided, explainable framework is needed to align reasoning with medical knowledge.
AI.method: Create PathReasoner, a knowledge-guided dataset (>20k instructional samples) built from whole-slide images using medical knowledge graphs to align pathologic findings with diagnoses. Develop PathReasoner-R1 with trajectory-masked supervised fine-tuning plus reasoning-oriented reinforcement learning, including a knowledge-aware multi-granular reward and an Entity Reward to enforce alignment with knowledge graphs.
AI.result: PathReasoner-R1 achieves state-of-the-art performance on PathReasoner and public benchmarks across multiple image scales; demonstrates robust, transparent, clinically grounded reasoning. Dataset and code are released.
AI.conclusion: Integrating structured medical knowledge with trajectory-aware fine-tuning and knowledge-guided RL yields verifiable, clinically grounded reasoning in pathology VLMs, laying groundwork for trusted AI-assisted pathology.

title: When Prohibitions Become Permissions: Auditing Negation Sensitivity in Language Models
summary: When a user tells an AI system that someone "should not" take an action, the system ought to treat this as a prohibition. Yet many large language models do the opposite: they interpret negated instructions as affirmations. We audited 16 models across 14 ethical scenarios and found that open-source models endorse prohibited actions 77% of the time under simple negation and 100% under compound negation -- a 317% increase over affirmative framing. Commercial models fare better but still show swings of 19-128%. Agreement between models drops from 74% on affirmative prompts to 62% on negated ones, and financial scenarios prove twice as fragile as medical ones. These patterns hold under deterministic decoding, ruling out sampling noise. We present case studies showing how these failures play out in practice, propose the Negation Sensitivity Index (NSI) as a governance metric, and outline a tiered certification framework with domain-specific thresholds. The findings point to a gap between what current alignment techniques achieve and what safe deployment requires: models that cannot reliably distinguish "do X" from "do not X" should not be making autonomous decisions in high-stakes contexts.

AI.tldr: Negation sensitivity is poor in LLMs. A broad audit shows open-source models frequently misinterpret negated instructions as affirmations, with high risk in high-stakes domains; NSI and a tiered certification framework are proposed for safer deployment.
AI.motivation: To quantify how well large language models distinguish prohibitions ('do not X') from affirmative actions ('do X'), which is critical for safe autonomous decision-making in high-stakes contexts.
AI.method: Audit 16 models across 14 ethical scenarios, testing simple negation and compound negation under deterministic decoding to rule out sampling noise. Measured endorsement of prohibited actions, cross-model agreement, and domain differences (e.g., financial vs medical). Included case studies and comparison between open-source and commercial models.
AI.result: Open-source models endorse prohibited actions 77% under simple negation and 100% under compound negation (317% increase vs affirmative framing). Commercial models show swings of 19–128%. Agreement across models drops from 74% (affirmative prompts) to 62% (negated prompts). Financial scenarios are twice as fragile as medical. Findings persist under deterministic decoding, supporting a real failure mode rather than noise.
AI.conclusion: There is a gap between current alignment methods and safe deployment requirements. The authors propose the Negation Sensitivity Index (NSI) as a governance metric and outline a tiered certification framework with domain-specific thresholds to ensure models do not autonomously act on misinterpreted negations.

title: A Sheaf-Theoretic and Topological Perspective on Complex Network Modeling and Attention Mechanisms in Graph Neural Models
summary: Combinatorial and topological structures, such as graphs, simplicial complexes, and cell complexes, form the foundation of geometric and topological deep learning (GDL and TDL) architectures. These models aggregate signals over such domains, integrate local features, and generate representations for diverse real-world applications. However, the distribution and diffusion behavior of GDL and TDL features during training remains an open and underexplored problem. Motivated by this gap, we introduce a cellular sheaf theoretic framework for modeling and analyzing the local consistency and harmonicity of node features and edge weights in graph-based architectures. By tracking local feature alignments and agreements through sheaf structures, the framework offers a topological perspective on feature diffusion and aggregation. Furthermore, a multiscale extension inspired by topological data analysis (TDA) is proposed to capture hierarchical feature interactions in graph models. This approach enables a joint characterization of GDL and TDL architectures based on their underlying geometric and topological structures and the learned signals defined on them, providing insights for future studies on conventional tasks such as node classification, substructure detection, and community detection.

AI.tldr: A cellular sheaf-based framework is introduced to study local consistency and diffusion of features in graph neural architectures, with a multiscale, TDA-inspired extension to capture hierarchical interactions, enabling a joint geometric/topological view of GDL/TDL models and guiding future tasks.
AI.motivation: Understanding how GDL/TDL features distribute and diffuse during training is underexplored. A topological framework to analyze local alignment and harmonicity can reveal diffusion patterns and improve interpretability and design.
AI.method: Model node features and edge weights as sections of a cellular sheaf over graphs; track local feature alignments and agreements via sheaf morphisms; introduce a multiscale extension inspired by topological data analysis to capture hierarchical feature interactions.
AI.result: Proposes a formal framework with theoretical insights on local consistency and harmonicity of features; introduces a multiscale extension; outlines potential applications to node classification, substructure detection, and community detection, pending empirical validation.
AI.conclusion: Provides a unified topological-geometric lens for GDL/TDL architectures, enabling joint analysis of learned signals on graphs and guiding future theoretical and applied research.

title: Similarity of Processing Steps in Vision Model Representations
summary: Recent literature suggests that the bigger the model, the more likely it is to converge to similar, ``universal'' representations, despite different training objectives, datasets, or modalities. While this literature shows that there is an area where model representations are similar, we study here how vision models might get to those representations -- in particular, do they also converge to the same intermediate steps and operations? We therefore study the processes that lead to convergent representations in different models. First, we quantify distance between different model representations at different stages. We follow the evolution of distances between models throughout processing, identifying the processing steps which are most different between models. We find that while layers at similar positions in different models have the most similar representations, strong differences remain. Classifier models, unlike the others, will discard information about low-level image statistics in their final layers. CNN- and transformer-based models also behave differently, with transformer models applying smoother changes to representations from one layer to the next. These distinctions clarify the level and nature of convergence between model representations, and enables a more qualitative account of the underlying processes in image models.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether intermediate representations in vision models converge across architectures by tracking representation distances across layers; finds layer-wise convergence but persistent differences; transformer representations change more smoothly; classifiers discard low-level information; overall partial, architecture-dependent convergence.
AI.motivation: To understand the dynamics of convergent representations beyond final similarity, especially whether different models align at intermediate processing steps.
AI.method: Quantify representation distances between models at different stages/layers during processing; compare CNNs, transformers, and classifier heads; analyze how information content (low-level statistics) is preserved or discarded; examine how distances evolve across layers.
AI.result: Layers at similar positions have the most similar representations, but strong differences remain; transformers show smoother progression; CNNs and transformers differ in progression; classifiers discard low-level statistics in final layers.
AI.conclusion: Convergence is partial and architecture-dependent; to interpret model representations, examine both layerwise alignment and processing dynamics; results enable qualitative accounts of underlying processes.

title: The Paradox of Robustness: Decoupling Rule-Based Logic from Affective Noise in High-Stakes Decision-Making
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) are widely documented to be sensitive to minor prompt perturbations and prone to sycophantic alignment with user biases, their robustness in consequential, rule-bound decision-making remains under-explored. In this work, we uncover a striking "Paradox of Robustness": despite their known lexical brittleness, instruction-tuned LLMs exhibit a behavioral and near-total invariance to emotional framing effects. Using a novel controlled perturbation framework across three high-stakes domains (healthcare, law, and finance), we quantify a robustness gap where LLMs demonstrate 110-300 times greater resistance to narrative manipulation than human subjects. Specifically, we find a near-zero effect size for models (Cohen's h = 0.003) compared to the substantial biases observed in humans (Cohen's h in [0.3, 0.8]). This result is highly counterintuitive and suggests the mechanisms driving sycophancy and prompt sensitivity do not necessarily translate to a failure in logical constraint satisfaction. We show that this invariance persists across models with diverse training paradigms. Our findings show that while LLMs may be "brittle" to how a query is formatted, they are remarkably "stable" against why a decision should be biased. Our findings establish that instruction-tuned models can decouple logical rule-adherence from persuasive narratives, offering a source of decision stability that complements, and even potentially de-biases, human judgment in institutional contexts. We release the 162-scenario benchmark, code, and data to facilitate the rigorous evaluation of narrative-induced bias and robustness on GitHub.com.

AI.tldr: Instruction-tuned LLMs exhibit strong robustness to emotional framing in high-stakes decisions, showing near-zero framing effects versus substantial human biases; a 110-300x resistance, with a 162-scenario benchmark released.
AI.motivation: To assess whether instruction-tuned LLMs exhibit narrative-induced bias and whether their rule-following remains stable under persuasive framing across critical domains (healthcare, law, finance).
AI.method: Controlled perturbation framework applied to three high-stakes domains; compare LLMs with humans; measure framing effects via effect size (Cohen's h); examine models with diverse training paradigms; release a 162-scenario benchmark.
AI.result: LLMs show near-zero framing effects (Cohen's h ≈ 0.003) whereas humans display moderate to large biases (Cohen's h ≈ 0.3–0.8). LLMs are 110–300× more robust to narrative manipulation; invariance persists across training paradigms; lexical brittleness does not translate to failure in logical constraint satisfaction.
AI.conclusion: Instruction-tuned models can decouple logical rule-adherence from persuasive narratives, offering decision stability that can complement or de-bias human judgment in institutional contexts; a practical benchmark (162 scenarios) and code/data are released for evaluation.

title: Temporal Context and Architecture: A Benchmark for Naturalistic EEG Decoding
summary: We study how model architecture and temporal context interact in naturalistic EEG decoding. Using the HBN movie-watching dataset, we benchmark five architectures, CNN, LSTM, a stabilized Transformer (EEGXF), S4, and S5, on a 4-class task across segment lengths from 8s to 128s. Accuracy improves with longer context: at 64s, S5 reaches 98.7%+/-0.6 and CNN 98.3%+/-0.3, while S5 uses ~20x fewer parameters than CNN. To probe real-world robustness, we evaluate zero-shot cross-frequency shifts, cross-task OOD inputs, and leave-one-subject-out generalization. S5 achieves stronger cross-subject accuracy but makes over-confident errors on OOD tasks; EEGXF is more conservative and stable under frequency shifts, though less calibrated in-distribution. These results reveal a practical efficiency-robustness trade-off: S5 for parameter-efficient peak accuracy; EEGXF when robustness and conservative uncertainty are critical.

AI.tldr: Longer temporal context improves naturalistic EEG decoding; among five architectures, S5 delivers near-peak accuracy with ~20x fewer parameters than CNN, revealing an efficiency-robustness trade-off: S5 favors peak accuracy and efficiency, EEGXF favors robustness and conservative uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Investigate how model architecture and temporal context interact in EEG decoding under realistic, naturalistic stimuli, to inform model selection for accuracy, efficiency, and robustness in practical BCI settings.
AI.method: Benchmark five architectures (CNN, LSTM, EEGXF, S4, S5) on the HBN movie-watching EEG dataset for a 4-class task across segment lengths from 8s to 128s. Evaluate zero-shot cross-frequency shifts, cross-task out-of-distribution inputs, and leave-one-subject-out generalization. Report accuracy and parameter counts.
AI.result: With 64s context, S5 achieves ~98.7% ±0.6 accuracy, CNN ~98.3% ±0.3; S5 uses ~20x fewer parameters than CNN. S5 generalizes better across subjects but is over-confident on OOD tasks. EEGXF is more conservative and stable under frequency shifts, though less calibrated in-distribution. Overall, a practical efficiency-robustness trade-off: S5 for parameter-efficient peak accuracy; EEGXF for robustness and conservative uncertainty.
