AI.result: ARGORA achieves competitive accuracy and exhibits corrective behavior: when experts disagree, it tends to resolve toward correct answers more often than introducing new errors, and provides causal diagnostics of decisive arguments.
AI.conclusion: ARGORA offers interpretable, fault-tolerant multi-expert reasoning with causal analysis and targeted corrections, enabling identification of decisive arguments and robust dispute resolution.

title: TimeSliver : Symbolic-Linear Decomposition for Explainable Time Series Classification
summary: Identifying the extent to which every temporal segment influences a model's predictions is essential for explaining model decisions and increasing transparency. While post-hoc explainable methods based on gradients and feature-based attributions have been popular, they suffer from reference state sensitivity and struggle to generalize across time-series datasets, as they treat time points independently and ignore sequential dependencies. Another perspective on explainable time-series classification is through interpretable components of the model, for instance, leveraging self-attention mechanisms to estimate temporal attribution; however, recent findings indicate that these attention weights often fail to provide faithful measures of temporal importance. In this work, we advance this perspective and present a novel explainability-driven deep learning framework, TimeSliver, which jointly utilizes raw time-series data and its symbolic abstraction to construct a representation that maintains the original temporal structure. Each element in this representation linearly encodes the contribution of each temporal segment to the final prediction, allowing us to assign a meaningful importance score to every time point. For time-series classification, TimeSliver outperforms other temporal attribution methods by 11% on 7 distinct synthetic and real-world multivariate time-series datasets. TimeSliver also achieves predictive performance within 2% of state-of-the-art baselines across 26 UEA benchmark datasets, positioning it as a strong and explainable framework for general time-series classification.

AI.tldr: TimeSliver is an explainability-driven framework that fuses raw time-series data with symbolic abstractions to produce a per-time-point contribution representation, enabling faithful temporal attributions while maintaining competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Explaining temporal predictions is hard due to sequential dependencies and limitations of gradient/feature-based methods and attention-based explanations, with poor generalization across time-series datasets.
AI.method: TimeSliver jointly leverages raw time-series and symbolic abstraction to construct a representation that linearly encodes each temporal segment's contribution to the final prediction, preserving temporal structure and enabling per-time-point importance scores.
AI.result: TimeSliver achieves an 11% improvement over competing temporal attribution methods on 7 synthetic/real-world multivariate datasets and attains within 2% of state-of-the-art baselines on 26 UEA datasets.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a robust, explainable approach for general time-series classification, balancing faithful temporal attribution with strong predictive performance; it offers a promising direction for integrating interpretable components with end-to-end learning.

title: Synthetic-to-Real Domain Bridging for Single-View 3D Reconstruction of Ships for Maritime Monitoring
summary: Three-dimensional (3D) reconstruction of ships is an important part of maritime monitoring, allowing improved visualization, inspection, and decision-making in real-world monitoring environments. However, most state-ofthe-art 3D reconstruction methods require multi-view supervision, annotated 3D ground truth, or are computationally intensive, making them impractical for real-time maritime deployment. In this work, we present an efficient pipeline for single-view 3D reconstruction of real ships by training entirely on synthetic data and requiring only a single view at inference. Our approach uses the Splatter Image network, which represents objects as sparse sets of 3D Gaussians for rapid and accurate reconstruction from single images. The model is first fine-tuned on synthetic ShapeNet vessels and further refined with a diverse custom dataset of 3D ships, bridging the domain gap between synthetic and real-world imagery. We integrate a state-of-the-art segmentation module based on YOLOv8 and custom preprocessing to ensure compatibility with the reconstruction network. Postprocessing steps include real-world scaling, centering, and orientation alignment, followed by georeferenced placement on an interactive web map using AIS metadata and homography-based mapping. Quantitative evaluation on synthetic validation data demonstrates strong reconstruction fidelity, while qualitative results on real maritime images from the ShipSG dataset confirm the potential for transfer to operational maritime settings. The final system provides interactive 3D inspection of real ships without requiring real-world 3D annotations. This pipeline provides an efficient, scalable solution for maritime monitoring and highlights a path toward real-time 3D ship visualization in practical applications. Interactive demo: https://dlr-mi.github.io/ship3d-demo/.

AI.tldr: Single-view 3D ship reconstruction trained on synthetic data using the Splatter Image network; integrates segmentation and georeferenced visualization for real-time maritime monitoring without real 3D ground-truth.
AI.motivation: Enable real-time, scalable 3D visualization of ships in maritime monitoring by avoiding multi-view supervision and expensive real-world 3D annotations.
AI.method: Fine-tune Splatter Image network on synthetic ShapeNet vessel data; further refine with a diverse custom 3D ship dataset; incorporate a YOLOv8-based segmentation module and preprocessing; apply postprocessing for real-world scaling, centering, and orientation; place reconstructed ships on an interactive web map using AIS metadata and homography-based mapping.
AI.result: Quantitative evaluation on synthetic validation data shows strong reconstruction fidelity; qualitative validation on real ShipSG imagery demonstrates transfer potential; an interactive demo is provided; system enables interactive 3D inspection without real 3D annotations.
AI.conclusion: An efficient, scalable single-view 3D ship reconstruction pipeline that bridges synthetic and real data, enabling near real-time 3D ship visualization for practical maritime monitoring without real-world 3D ground-truth annotations.

title: ShardMemo: Masked MoE Routing for Sharded Agentic LLM Memory
summary: Agentic large language model (LLM) systems rely on external memory for long-horizon state and concurrent multi-agent execution, but centralized indexes and heuristic partitions become bottlenecks as memory volume and parallel access grow. We present ShardMemo, a budgeted tiered memory service with Tier A per-agent working state, Tier B sharded evidence with shard-local approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) indexes, and Tier C, a versioned skill library. Tier B enforces scope-before-routing: structured eligibility constraints mask ineligible shards before routing or ANN search. We cast shard probing as masked mixture-of-experts (MoE) routing over eligible shards, probing up to $B_{\mathrm{probe}}$ shards via Top-$B_{\mathrm{probe}}$ or adaptive Top-$P$, and use cost-aware gating over profile/observation/session shard families; the router is trained from evidence-to-shard supervision. On LoCoMo, ShardMemo improves over the strongest baseline (GAM) by +5.11 to +6.82 F1 across question categories. Under a fixed-budget routing setting ($B_{\mathrm{probe}}=3$), ShardMemo improves over cosine-to-prototype shard routing by +6.87 F1 while reducing retrieval work (VecScan 521->414, -20.5%) and p95 latency (95->76 ms). On long-context HotpotQA, ShardMemo achieves 63.41/61.88/57.95 F1 at 56K/224K/448K tokens. On ToolBench, Tier C reaches 0.97 Precision@3 and 1.94 StepRed (+10.2% and +7.2% over embedding-similarity retrieval).

AI.tldr: ShardMemo introduces a budgeted, tiered memory service for agentic LLMs with per-agent state (Tier A), sharded evidence with local ANN indices (Tier B), and a versioned skill library (Tier C). It uses scope-before-routing and masked MoE-style shard routing, probing up to B_probe shards with Top-B_probe or adaptive Top-P, plus cost-aware gating. Demonstrates consistent gains across multiple benchmarks (LoCoMo, HotpotQA, ToolBench) with reduced retrieval cost and latency.
AI.motivation: As memory volume and the need for concurrent multi-agent execution grow, centralized indexes and manual partitions become bottlenecks. A scalable, budget-aware, tiered memory system is needed to maintain performance without excessive retrieval cost.
AI.method: Three-tier architecture: Tier A for per-agent working state; Tier B with sharded evidence and shard-local ANN indices; Tier C a versioned skill library. Tier B enforces scope-before-routing by masking ineligible shards prior to routing or ANN search. Shard probing is cast as masked mixture-of-experts routing over eligible shards, probing up to B_probe shards via Top-B_probe or adaptive Top-P. A cost-aware router, trained with evidence-to-shard supervision, selects among profile/observation/session shard families for routing.
AI.result: Empirical gains across benchmarks: On LoCoMo, +5.11 to +6.82 F1 over GAM. With fixed-budget routing (B_probe=3), +6.87 F1 over cosine-to-prototype routing, while reducing retrieval work (VecScan 521→414, -20.5%) and p95 latency (95→76 ms). On long-context HotpotQA, F1s of 63.41/61.88/57.95 at 56K/224K/448K tokens. On ToolBench, Tier C achieves 0.97 Precision@3 and 1.94 StepRed ( +10.2% and +7.2% over embedding-similarity retrieval).
AI.conclusion: Tiered, budget-aware memory with scope-controlled routing and MoE-style shard probing yields higher retrieval accuracy and lower cost/latency across diverse tasks, demonstrating practicality for scalable agentic LLM systems.

title: Physics-Guided Tiny-Mamba Transformer for Reliability-Aware Early Fault Warning
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Reliability
summary: Reliability-centered prognostics for rotating machinery requires early warning signals that remain accurate under nonstationary operating conditions, domain shifts across speed/load/sensors, and severe class imbalance, while keeping the false-alarm rate small and predictable. We propose the Physics-Guided Tiny-Mamba Transformer (PG-TMT), a compact tri-branch encoder tailored for online condition monitoring. A depthwise-separable convolutional stem captures micro-transients, a Tiny-Mamba state-space branch models near-linear long-range dynamics, and a lightweight local Transformer encodes cross-channel resonances. We derive an analytic temporal-to-spectral mapping that ties the model's attention spectrum to classical bearing fault-order bands, yielding a band-alignment score that quantifies physical plausibility and provides physics-grounded explanations. To ensure decision reliability, healthy-score exceedances are modeled with extreme-value theory (EVT), which yields an on-threshold achieving a target false-alarm intensity (events/hour); a dual-threshold hysteresis with a minimum hold time further suppresses chatter. Under a leakage-free streaming protocol with right-censoring of missed detections on CWRU, Paderborn, XJTU-SY, and an industrial pilot, PG-TMT attains higher precision-recall AUC (primary under imbalance), competitive or better ROC AUC, and shorter mean time-to-detect at matched false-alarm intensity, together with strong cross-domain transfer. By coupling physics-aligned representations with EVT-calibrated decision rules, PG-TMT delivers calibrated, interpretable, and deployment-ready early warnings for reliability-centric prognostics and health management.

AI.tldr: Proposes PG-TMT: a physics-guided tri-branch encoder for online bearing prognostics, combining a conv stem, Tiny-Mamba state-space branch, and a local Transformer; analytic mapping to fault-order bands; EVT-based decision with dual thresholds; leakage-free streaming; strong cross-domain transfer and calibrated, interpretable warnings under imbalance.
AI.motivation: Reliability-centered prognostics require early, accurate warnings under nonstationary operating conditions, domain shifts, and severe class imbalance while maintaining low, predictable false alarms.
AI.method: Three-branch encoder: (1) depthwise-separable conv stem for micro-transients; (2) Tiny-Mamba state-space branch for near-linear long-range dynamics; (3) lightweight local Transformer for cross-channel resonances. An analytic temporal-to-spectral mapping ties attention to bearing fault-order bands, producing a band-alignment score for physical plausibility. Healthy-score exceedances modeled with EVT to set a target false-alarm intensity; dual-threshold hysteresis with a minimum hold time to reduce chatter. Leakage-free streaming with right-censoring of missed detections; evaluation across CWRU, Paderborn, XJTU-SY, and an industrial pilot.
AI.result: PG-TMT achieves higher precision-recall AUC under class imbalance, competitive or better ROC AUC, and shorter mean time-to-detect at matched false-alarm intensity, with strong cross-domain transfer.
AI.conclusion: Coupling physics-aligned representations with EVT-calibrated decision rules yields calibrated, interpretable, and deployment-ready early warnings for reliability-centered prognostics and health management.

title: CG-MLLM: Captioning and Generating 3D content via Multi-modal Large Language Models
summary: Large Language Models(LLMs) have revolutionized text generation and multimodal perception, but their capabilities in 3D content generation remain underexplored. Existing methods compromise by producing either low-resolution meshes or coarse structural proxies, failing to capture fine-grained geometry natively. In this paper, we propose CG-MLLM, a novel Multi-modal Large Language Model (MLLM) capable of 3D captioning and high-resolution 3D generation in a single framework. Leveraging the Mixture-of-Transformer architecture, CG-MLLM decouples disparate modeling needs, where the Token-level Autoregressive (TokenAR) Transformer handles token-level content, and the Block-level Autoregressive (BlockAR) Transformer handles block-level content. By integrating a pre-trained vision-language backbone with a specialized 3D VAE latent space, CG-MLLM facilitates long-context interactions between standard tokens and spatial blocks within a single integrated architecture. Experimental results show that CG-MLLM significantly outperforms existing MLLMs in generating high-fidelity 3D objects, effectively bringing high-resolution 3D content creation into the mainstream LLM paradigm.

AI.tldr: CG-MLLM is a Mixture-of-Transformer MLLM enabling high-resolution 3D captioning and generation, decoupling token-level and block-level content via TokenAR and BlockAR, integrating a vision-language backbone with a 3D VAE latent space for long-context interactions, and claims superior 3D fidelity over prior MLLMs.
AI.motivation: Current MLLMs either produce low-resolution meshes or coarse proxies for 3D content, failing to capture fine-grained geometry natively. There is a need for a single framework that supports high-resolution 3D generation and captioning with long-context interactions between textual tokens and spatial blocks.
AI.method: Mixture-of-Transformer architecture: TokenAR Transformer handles token-level content; BlockAR Transformer handles block-level content. Integrates a pre-trained vision-language backbone with a specialized 3D VAE latent space to enable long-context interactions between standard tokens and spatial blocks within a single architecture.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate CG-MLLM significantly outperforms existing MLLMs in generating high-fidelity, high-resolution 3D objects, demonstrating effective 3D content creation within the mainstream LLM framework.
AI.conclusion: CG-MLLM brings high-resolution 3D content creation into the mainstream LLM paradigm by decoupling token- and block-level modeling and leveraging a vision-language backbone with 3D VAE latent space to enable rich cross-modal, long-context interactions.

title: Meta Context Engineering via Agentic Skill Evolution
summary: The operational efficacy of large language models relies heavily on their inference-time context. This has established Context Engineering (CE) as a formal discipline for optimizing these inputs. Current CE methods rely on manually crafted harnesses, such as rigid generation-reflection workflows and predefined context schemas. They impose structural biases and restrict context optimization to a narrow, intuition-bound design space. To address this, we introduce Meta Context Engineering (MCE), a bi-level framework that supersedes static CE heuristics by co-evolving CE skills and context artifacts. In MCE iterations, a meta-level agent refines engineering skills via agentic crossover, a deliberative search over the history of skills, their executions, and evaluations. A base-level agent executes these skills, learns from training rollouts, and optimizes context as flexible files and code. We evaluate MCE across five disparate domains under offline and online settings. MCE demonstrates consistent performance gains, achieving 5.6--53.8% relative improvement over state-of-the-art agentic CE methods (mean of 16.9%), while maintaining superior context adaptability, transferability, and efficiency in both context usage and training.

AI.tldr: Meta Context Engineering (MCE) introduces a bi-level, co-evolutionary framework for context engineering in large language models, outperforming static CE methods and improving adaptability and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current Context Engineering relies on manually crafted, rigid workflows and predefined schemas, which inject biases and confine optimization to a narrow design space. There is a need for automatic, adaptive CE that can co-evolve capabilities and context artifacts to enhance inference-time performance and transferability.
AI.method: MCE uses a bi-level architecture: a meta-level agent performs agentic crossover and deliberative search over the history of CE skills, executions, and evaluations to refine engineering strategies, while a base-level agent executes these skills, learns from training rollouts, and optimizes the context as flexible files and code. The approach is evaluated across five domains in offline and online settings.
AI.result: MCE achieves 5.6–53.8% relative improvement over state-of-the-art agentic CE methods (mean ~16.9%), with consistent gains and improved context adaptability, transferability, and efficiency in both context usage and training.
AI.conclusion: Co-evolving CE skills and context artifacts via a bi-level MCE framework yields robust performance gains and better context management, suggesting a promising direction for adaptive, transferable context engineering in LLMs.

title: Missing-Data-Induced Phase Transitions in Spectral PLS for Multimodal Learning
summary: Partial Least Squares (PLS) learns shared structure from paired data via the top singular vectors of the empirical cross-covariance (PLS-SVD), but multimodal datasets often have missing entries in both views. We study PLS-SVD under independent entry-wise missing-completely-at-random masking in a proportional high-dimensional spiked model. After appropriate normalization, the masked cross-covariance behaves like a spiked rectangular random matrix whose effective signal strength is attenuated by $\sqrtρ$, where $ρ$ is the joint entry retention probability. As a result, PLS-SVD exhibits a sharp BBP-type phase transition: below a critical signal-to-noise threshold the leading singular vectors are asymptotically uninformative, while above it they achieve nontrivial alignment with the latent shared directions, with closed-form asymptotic overlap formulas. Simulations and semi-synthetic multimodal experiments corroborate the predicted phase diagram and recovery curves across aspect ratios, signal strengths, and missingness levels.

AI.tldr: In high-dimensional PLS-SVD with independently missing data, the masked cross-covariance behaves like a spiked rectangular random matrix with the signal attenuated by sqrt(ρ). This yields a BBP-type phase transition: when the signal exceeds a critical threshold, the leading singular vectors coherently align with the latent directions; otherwise they are asymptotically uninformative. The authors provide closed-form asymptotic overlap formulas and validate them with simulations and semi-synthetic experiments across various aspect ratios, signal strengths, and missingness levels.
AI.motivation: Multimodal datasets frequently contain missing entries. Understanding how PLS-SVD behaves under elementwise missingness is crucial for reliable recovery of shared structure and for guiding data collection and preprocessing.
AI.method: Model the masked cross-covariance within a proportional high-dimensional spiked framework. After normalization, the observed cross-covariance acts as a spiked rectangular random matrix with effective signal strength attenuated by sqrt(ρ), where ρ is the joint entry retention probability. The analysis yields a BBP-type phase transition with explicit asymptotic overlap formulas for the leading singular vectors. The results are corroborated by simulations and semi-synthetic experiments to map phase diagrams over aspect ratios, signal strengths, and missingness.
AI.result: Identification of a sharp BBP-type phase transition in PLS-SVD under missing data. Below the critical threshold, the leading singular vectors are asymptotically uninformative; above it, they achieve nontrivial alignment with latent shared directions. The authors derive closed-form asymptotic overlap expressions and validate the predictions via simulations and semi-synthetic experiments, producing consistent phase diagrams across varying aspect ratios, signal strengths, and missingness levels.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a rigorous characterization of when PLS-SVD can recover shared structure in the presence of random entrywise missingness, via a spiked rectangular random-matrix framework with signal attenuation by sqrt(ρ). The phase transition and overlap formulas offer practical benchmarks and are supported by empirical validation.

title: MMFineReason: Closing the Multimodal Reasoning Gap via Open Data-Centric Methods
summary: Recent advances in Vision Language Models (VLMs) have driven significant progress in visual reasoning. However, open-source VLMs still lag behind proprietary systems, largely due to the lack of high-quality reasoning data. Existing datasets offer limited coverage of challenging domains such as STEM diagrams and visual puzzles, and lack consistent, long-form Chain-of-Thought (CoT) annotations essential for eliciting strong reasoning capabilities. To bridge this gap, we introduce MMFineReason, a large-scale multimodal reasoning dataset comprising 1.8M samples and 5.1B solution tokens, featuring high-quality reasoning annotations distilled from Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Thinking. The dataset is established via a systematic three-stage pipeline: (1) large-scale data collection and standardization, (2) CoT rationale generation, and (3) comprehensive selection based on reasoning quality and difficulty awareness. The resulting dataset spans STEM problems, visual puzzles, games, and complex diagrams, with each sample annotated with visually grounded reasoning traces. We fine-tune Qwen3-VL-Instruct on MMFineReason to develop MMFineReason-2B/4B/8B versions. Our models establish new state-of-the-art results for their size class. Notably, MMFineReason-4B succesfully surpasses Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking, and MMFineReason-8B even outperforms Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Thinking while approaching Qwen3-VL-32B-Thinking, demonstrating remarkable parameter efficiency. Crucially, we uncover a "less is more" phenomenon via our difficulty-aware filtering strategy: a subset of just 7\% (123K samples) achieves performance comparable to the full dataset. Notably, we reveal a synergistic effect where reasoning-oriented data composition simultaneously boosts general capabilities.

AI.tldr: MMFineReason is a large-scale multimodal reasoning dataset (1.8M samples, 5.1B solution tokens) distilled from Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Thinking, enabling efficient fine-tuning of MMFineReason-2B/4B/8B models. It achieves state-of-the-art results for its size classes, with 4B surpassing 8B-thinking and 8B approaching 32B-thinking, and reveals a “less is more” finding where a 7% subset matches full-data performance; highlights a synergistic benefit where reasoning-focused data improves general capabilities.
AI.motivation: Open-source Vision-Language Models lag proprietary systems due to a lack of high-quality, long-form reasoning data across challenging domains (e.g., STEM diagrams, visual puzzles). CoT annotations are essential to elicit strong reasoning in multimodal models.
AI.method: A three-stage pipeline: (1) large-scale data collection and standardization; (2) generation of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) rationales; (3) difficulty-aware filtering to select high-quality, challenging samples. The dataset is distilled from Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Thinking. MMFineReason-2B/4B/8B are obtained by fine-tuning Qwen3-VL-Instruct on MMFineReason.
AI.result: New state-of-the-art for their size classes. MMFineReason-4B beats Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking; MMFineReason-8B beats Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Thinking and nears Qwen3-VL-32B-Thinking. A 7% subset (123K samples) matches full-dataset performance, demonstrating a strong data-efficiency and the beneficial synergy between reasoning data and general capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The dataset demonstrates efficient scaling and efficacy of reasoning-focused data in multimodal models, enabling compact models to achieve strong performance and providing practical guidance for data selection via difficulty-aware filtering.

title: Grounding and Enhancing Informativeness and Utility in Dataset Distillation
comment: Accepted by ICLR 2026, 20 pages, 9 figures, 11 tables
summary: Dataset Distillation (DD) seeks to create a compact dataset from a large, real-world dataset. While recent methods often rely on heuristic approaches to balance efficiency and quality, the fundamental relationship between original and synthetic data remains underexplored. This paper revisits knowledge distillation-based dataset distillation within a solid theoretical framework. We introduce the concepts of Informativeness and Utility, capturing crucial information within a sample and essential samples in the training set, respectively. Building on these principles, we define optimal dataset distillation mathematically. We then present InfoUtil, a framework that balances informativeness and utility in synthesizing the distilled dataset. InfoUtil incorporates two key components: (1) game-theoretic informativeness maximization using Shapley Value attribution to extract key information from samples, and (2) principled utility maximization by selecting globally influential samples based on Gradient Norm. These components ensure that the distilled dataset is both informative and utility-optimized. Experiments demonstrate that our method achieves a 6.1\% performance improvement over the previous state-of-the-art approach on ImageNet-1K dataset using ResNet-18.

AI.tldr: InfoUtil proposes a theoretically grounded framework for dataset distillation that balances informativeness and utility. It uses Shapley-value-based informativeness and gradient-norm-based utility to synthesize a compact, high-quality dataset, reporting a 6.1% accuracy gain on ImageNet-1K with ResNet-18.
AI.motivation: The relationship between original and synthetic data in dataset distillation is not well understood. A formal framework with clear notions of information (informativeness) and training efficacy (utility) can guide principled distillation and improve performance.
AI.method: Define Informativeness and Utility; introduce InfoUtil framework. Component 1 uses Shapley Value attributions to quantify and maximize informativeness of samples. Component 2 selects globally influential samples via Gradient Norm to maximize utility. Integrates these components into a distillation objective to synthesize a compact dataset.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows a 6.1% improvement in performance over the previous state-of-the-art on ImageNet-1K with ResNet-18, indicating strong practical gains for high-resolution datasets and large models.
AI.conclusion: InfoUtil provides a principled, two-pronged approach to dataset distillation that combines theoretical informativeness with practical utility optimization, achieving notable gains on large-scale benchmarks. Future work may explore computational cost, ablations, and applicability across architectures and datasets.

title: Trajectory-Guided Diffusion for Foreground-Preserving Background Generation in Multi-Layer Documents
summary: We present a diffusion-based framework for document-centric background generation that achieves foreground preservation and multi-page stylistic consistency through latent-space design rather than explicit constraints. Instead of suppressing diffusion updates or applying masking heuristics, our approach reinterprets diffusion as the evolution of stochastic trajectories through a structured latent space. By shaping the initial noise and its geometric alignment, background generation naturally avoids designated foreground regions, allowing readable content to remain intact without auxiliary mechanisms. To address the long-standing issue of stylistic drift across pages, we decouple style control from text conditioning and introduce cached style directions as persistent vectors in latent space. Once selected, these directions constrain diffusion trajectories to a shared stylistic subspace, ensuring consistent appearance across pages and editing iterations. This formulation eliminates the need for repeated prompt-based style specification and provides a more stable foundation for multi-page generation. Our framework admits a geometric and physical interpretation, where diffusion paths evolve on a latent manifold shaped by preferred directions, and foreground regions are rarely traversed as a consequence of trajectory initialization rather than explicit exclusion. The proposed method is training-free, compatible with existing diffusion backbones, and produces visually coherent, foreground-preserving results across complex documents. By reframing diffusion as trajectory design in latent space, we offer a principled approach to consistent and structured generative modeling.

AI.tldr: Diffusion is reframed as latent-trajectory design for document background generation, achieving foreground preservation and multi-page stylistic consistency without explicit constraints or training.
AI.motivation: Address foreground preservation and consistent appearance across multiple document pages without reliance on masking, prompt-based style specification, or retraining, while remaining compatible with existing diffusion backbones.
AI.method: Reinterpret diffusion as the evolution of stochastic trajectories within a structured latent space. Shape initial noise and its geometry so black-box diffusion naturally avoids designated foreground regions. Decouple style control from text conditioning and introduce cached style directions as persistent vectors in latent space. These directions constrain trajectories to a shared stylistic subspace, yielding cross-page consistency. The approach is training-free and leverages trajectory initialization to implicitly respect foregrounds.
AI.result: Foreground regions remain readable while backgrounds are generated with consistent style across pages. The method provides stable results across editing iterations and complex documents, without requiring prompt-based style specification. It is compatible with existing diffusion backbones and does not rely on explicit masking.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion is interpreted as trajectory design on a latent manifold shaped by preferred directions. Foreground avoidance emerges from initialization rather than explicit exclusion, enabling principled, structured, and stable multi-page document generation without additional training or masking strategies.

title: Chain Of Thought Compression: A Theoritical Analysis
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) has unlocked advanced reasoning abilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) with intermediate steps, yet incurs prohibitive computational costs due to generation of extra tokens. Recent studies empirically show that compressing reasoning steps into latent states, or implicit CoT compression, offers a token-efficient alternative. However, the mechanism behind CoT compression remains unclear. In this paper, we provide the first theoretical analysis of the difficulty of learning to internalize intermediate reasoning steps. By introducing Order-r Interaction, we prove that the learning signal for high-order logical dependencies exponentially decays to solve irreducible problem, where skipping intermediate steps inevitably leads to high-order interaction barriers. To empirically validate this, we introduce NatBool-DAG, a challenging benchmark designed to enforce irreducible logical reasoning and eliminate semantic shortcuts. Guided by our theoretical findings, we propose ALiCoT (Aligned Implicit CoT), a novel framework that overcomes the signal decay by aligning latent token distributions with intermediate reasoning states. Experimental results demonstrate that ALiCoT successfully unlocks efficient reasoning: it achieves a 54.4x speedup while maintaining performance comparable to explicit CoT.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes why implicit CoT works and why learning to internalize intermediate steps is hard. It introduces Order-r Interaction to show signal decay for high-order logic, presents NatBool-DAG to test irreducible reasoning, and proposes ALiCoT to align latent distributions with intermediate states, achieving 54.4x speedup with comparable accuracy to explicit CoT.
AI.motivation: Reduce token costs of chain-of-thought (CoT) by compressing reasoning into latent states while understanding the theoretical limits of such compression.
AI.method: Theoretically define Order-r Interaction and prove exponential decay of learning signals for high-order dependencies; construct NatBool-DAG benchmark to enforce irreducible reasoning; develop ALiCoT to align latent token distributions with intermediate reasoning states and evaluate empirically.
AI.result: The analysis shows information loss barriers (signal decay) for high-order reasoning; NatBool-DAG challenges irreducible reasoning; ALiCoT delivers up to 54.4x speedup with performance on par with explicit CoT.
AI.conclusion: CoT compression can be theoretically characterized via Order-r Interaction; latent alignment (ALiCoT) mitigates signal decay enabling efficient implicit CoT without sacrificing performance.

title: Achieving $\varepsilon^{-2}$ Dependence for Average-Reward Q-Learning with a New Contraction Principle
summary: We present the convergence rates of synchronous and asynchronous Q-learning for average-reward Markov decision processes, where the absence of contraction poses a fundamental challenge. Existing non-asymptotic results overcome this challenge by either imposing strong assumptions to enforce seminorm contraction or relying on discounted or episodic Markov decision processes as successive approximations, which either require unknown parameters or result in suboptimal sample complexity. In this work, under a reachability assumption, we establish optimal $\widetilde{O}(\varepsilon^{-2})$ sample complexity guarantees (up to logarithmic factors) for a simple variant of synchronous and asynchronous Q-learning that samples from the lazified dynamics, where the system remains in the current state with some fixed probability. At the core of our analysis is the construction of an instance-dependent seminorm and showing that, after a lazy transformation of the Markov decision process, the Bellman operator becomes one-step contractive under this seminorm.

AI.tldr: Near-optimal non-asymptotic sample complexity ~O~(ε^-2) for synchronous and asynchronous Q-learning in average-reward MDPs by using lazified dynamics and an instance-dependent seminorm, yielding one-step contraction of the Bellman operator.
AI.motivation: Average-reward MDPs lack contraction, complicating non-asymptotic analysis. Existing results rely on strong contraction assumptions or discounted/episodic reductions, often with unknown parameters or suboptimal sample complexity.
AI.method: Introduce lazified dynamics where the process stays in the current state with a fixed probability; construct an instance-dependent seminorm; show that after lazy transformation the Bellman operator is one-step contractive under this seminorm; analyze both synchronous and asynchronous Q-learning.
AI.result: Prove optimal ~O~(ε^-2) sample complexity (up to log factors) for a simple lazy Q-learning variant under a reachability assumption.
AI.conclusion: Lazy dynamics plus an instance-dependent seminorm restore contraction and yield tight non-asymptotic guarantees for average-reward Q-learning without requiring strong contraction or discounting, expanding applicability.

title: Improving Classifier-Free Guidance of Flow Matching via Manifold Projection
summary: Classifier-free guidance (CFG) is a widely used technique for controllable generation in diffusion and flow-based models. Despite its empirical success, CFG relies on a heuristic linear extrapolation that is often sensitive to the guidance scale. In this work, we provide a principled interpretation of CFG through the lens of optimization. We demonstrate that the velocity field in flow matching corresponds to the gradient of a sequence of smoothed distance functions, which guides latent variables toward the scaled target image set. This perspective reveals that the standard CFG formulation is an approximation of this gradient, where the prediction gap, the discrepancy between conditional and unconditional outputs, governs guidance sensitivity. Leveraging this insight, we reformulate the CFG sampling as a homotopy optimization with a manifold constraint. This formulation necessitates a manifold projection step, which we implement via an incremental gradient descent scheme during sampling. To improve computational efficiency and stability, we further enhance this iterative process with Anderson Acceleration without requiring additional model evaluations. Our proposed methods are training-free and consistently refine generation fidelity, prompt alignment, and robustness to the guidance scale. We validate their effectiveness across diverse benchmarks, demonstrating significant improvements on large-scale models such as DiT-XL-2-256, Flux, and Stable Diffusion 3.5.

AI.tldr: CFG is interpreted as a gradient flow of smoothed distance functions; standard CFG is a crude gradient approximation. The paper reformulates CFG sampling as a homotopy optimization with a manifold constraint, requiring a manifold projection implemented via incremental gradient descent and accelerated by Anderson Acceleration. Training-free and improves fidelity, prompt alignment, and robustness to guidance scale on large models.
AI.motivation: CFG relies on a heuristic linear extrapolation and exhibits sensitivity to the guidance scale. A principled, training-free interpretation and stabilization method is sought to improve controllable generation in diffusion/flow-based models.
AI.method: Demonstrate that the velocity field in flow matching equals the gradient of a sequence of smoothed distance functions toward a scaled target image set. Show that the CFG prediction gap governs guidance sensitivity. Reformulate CFG sampling as a homotopy optimization with a manifold constraint. Implement a manifold projection step via incremental gradient descent during sampling. Improve efficiency/stability with Anderson Acceleration without extra model evaluations.
AI.result: Empirical results show improved generation fidelity, prompt alignment, and robustness to guidance scale on large-scale models such as DiT-XL-2-256, Flux, and Stable Diffusion 3.5.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a principled, training-free framework for CFG that yields more stable and accurate controllable generation across large diffusion/flow models, with broad applicability and reduced sensitivity to guidance strength.

title: Depth-Recurrent Attention Mixtures: Giving Latent Reasoning the Attention it Deserves
summary: Depth-recurrence facilitates latent reasoning by sharing parameters across depths. However, prior work lacks combined FLOP-, parameter-, and memory-matched baselines, underutilizes depth-recurrence due to partially fixed layer stacks, and ignores the bottleneck of constant hidden-sizes that restricts many-step latent reasoning. To address this, we introduce a modular framework of depth-recurrent attention mixtures (Dreamer), combining sequence attention, depth attention, and sparse expert attention. It alleviates the hidden-size bottleneck through attention along depth, decouples scaling dimensions, and allows depth-recurrent models to scale efficiently and effectively. Across language reasoning benchmarks, our models require 2 to 8x fewer training tokens for the same accuracy as FLOP-, parameter-, and memory-matched SOTA, and outperform ca. 2x larger SOTA models with the same training tokens. We further present insights into knowledge usage across depths, e.g., showing 2 to 11x larger expert selection diversity than SOTA MoEs.

AI.tldr: Dreamer introduces depth-recurrent attention mixtures that overcome the hidden-size bottleneck by enabling attention along depth and combining sequence, depth, and sparse expert attention. It achieves strong token-efficient performance and richer expert usage for depth-based reasoning.
AI.motivation: To enable fair, FLOP-/parameter-/memory-matched baselines while mitigating underutilization of depth-recurrence and the hidden-size bottleneck that limits multi-step latent reasoning.
AI.method: A modular framework (Dreamer) that combines sequence attention, depth attention, and sparse expert attention to form depth-recurrent attention mixtures. It allows attention along depth to decouple scaling dimensions and scale depth-recurrent models efficiently.
AI.result: On language reasoning benchmarks, the model requires 2–8x fewer training tokens to reach the same accuracy as FLOP-, parameter-, and memory-matched SOTA, and outperforms roughly 2x larger SOTA models given the same tokens. It also shows 2–11x larger expert selection diversity than SOTA MoEs.
AI.conclusion: Depth-recurrent attention mixtures enable efficient and effective scaling of depth-recurrent models, improving token efficiency and revealing greater diversity in expert usage across depths.

title: The Surprising Difficulty of Search in Model-Based Reinforcement Learning
summary: This paper investigates search in model-based reinforcement learning (RL). Conventional wisdom holds that long-term predictions and compounding errors are the primary obstacles for model-based RL. We challenge this view, showing that search is not a plug-and-play replacement for a learned policy. Surprisingly, we find that search can harm performance even when the model is highly accurate. Instead, we show that mitigating distribution shift matters more than improving model or value function accuracy. Building on this insight, we identify key techniques for enabling effective search, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple popular benchmark domains.

AI.tldr: Search in model-based RL is not a plug-and-play replacement for learned policies: mitigating distribution shift is more critical than model or value accuracy, and with techniques addressing distribution shift, search achieves state-of-the-art results across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Challenge the view that long-horizon predictions and compounding errors are the primary obstacles in model-based RL; propose that distribution shift during search is a more crucial bottleneck requiring targeted techniques.
AI.method: Empirical analysis of search in model-based RL, examining the effects of distribution shift and model accuracy; synthesizing insights to identify techniques that enable effective search; demonstration across multiple benchmark domains.
AI.result: Demonstrates that search can harm performance even with accurate models; improving distribution-shift robustness yields state-of-the-art results across benchmarks; provides practical techniques for enabling effective search.
AI.conclusion: Mitigating distribution shift is more important than improving model/value accuracy for search in model-based RL; future work should focus on strategies that reduce distributional mismatch between imagined trajectories and the true environment.

title: Past- and Future-Informed KV Cache Policy with Salience Estimation in Autoregressive Video Diffusion
summary: Video generation is pivotal to digital media creation, and recent advances in autoregressive video generation have markedly enhanced the efficiency of real-time video synthesis. However, existing approaches generally rely on heuristic KV Cache policies, which ignore differences in token importance in long-term video generation. This leads to the loss of critical spatiotemporal information and the accumulation of redundant, invalid cache, thereby degrading video generation quality and efficiency. To address this limitation, we first observe that token contributions to video generation are highly time-heterogeneous and accordingly propose a novel Past- and Future-Informed KV Cache Policy (PaFu-KV). Specifically, PaFu-KV introduces a lightweight Salience Estimation Head distilled from a bidirectional teacher to estimate salience scores, allowing the KV cache to retain informative tokens while discarding less relevant ones. This policy yields a better quality-efficiency trade-off by shrinking KV cache capacity and reducing memory footprint at inference time. Extensive experiments on benchmarks demonstrate that our method preserves high-fidelity video generation quality while enables accelerated inference, thereby enabling more efficient long-horizon video generation. Our code will be released upon paper acceptance.

AI.tldr: PaFu-KV: a past- and future-informed KV cache policy with a salience head distilled from a bidirectional teacher improves inference efficiency and preserves video quality in autoregressive long-horizon video generation.
AI.motivation: Current KV Cache policies are heuristic and treat tokens equally, causing information loss and inefficiency in long-horizon video autoregression.
AI.method: Introduce PaFu-KV with a Salience Estimation Head trained via knowledge distillation from a bidirectional teacher; uses salience scores to retain informative tokens and discard less relevant ones; reduces cache capacity and memory footprint.
AI.result: Benchmarks show maintained high-fidelity video quality with accelerated inference; demonstrates better quality-efficiency trade-off and enabling efficient long-horizon generation.
AI.conclusion: PaFu-KV yields efficient long-horizon video generation without compromising quality; code will be released.

title: Beyond Imitation: Reinforcement Learning for Active Latent Planning
summary: Aiming at efficient and dense chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning, latent reasoning methods fine-tune Large Language Models (LLMs) to substitute discrete language tokens with continuous latent tokens. These methods consume fewer tokens compared to the conventional language CoT reasoning and have the potential to plan in a dense latent space. However, current latent tokens are generally supervised based on imitating language labels. Considering that there can be multiple equivalent but diverse CoT labels for a question, passively imitating an arbitrary one may lead to inferior latent token representations and latent reasoning policies, undermining the potential planning ability and resulting in clear gaps between training and testing. In this work, we emphasize the importance of active planning over the representation space of latent tokens in achieving the optimal latent reasoning policy. So, we propose the \underline{A}c\underline{t}ive Latent \underline{P}lanning method (ATP-Latent), which models the supervision process of latent tokens as a conditional variational auto-encoder (VAE) to obtain a smoother latent space. Moreover, to facilitate the most reasonable latent reasoning policy, ATP-Latent conducts reinforcement learning (RL) with an auxiliary coherence reward, which is calculated based on the consistency between VAE-decoded contents of latent tokens, enabling a guided RL process. In experiments on LLaMA-1B, ATP-Latent demonstrates +4.1\% accuracy and -3.3\% tokens on four benchmarks compared to advanced baselines. Codes are available on https://github.com/zz1358m/ATP-Latent-master.

AI.tldr: Active latent planning (ATP-Latent) learns smoother latent tokens for CoT reasoning via a conditional VAE for supervision and a coherence-based RL objective, yielding accuracy gains and reduced token usage on LLaMA-1B across four benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Latent reasoning methods replace discrete tokens with continuous latent tokens to enable dense planning but often rely on supervised imitation of language labels. Multiple equivalent CoT labels can produce suboptimal latent representations and misaligned training/testing objectives; active planning in the latent space is needed to improve the latent policy.
AI.method: Model latent token supervision as a conditional variational auto-encoder (VAE) to produce a smoother latent space. Use reinforcement learning with an auxiliary coherence reward, computed from the consistency between VAE-decoded contents of latent tokens, to guide policy optimization.
AI.result: On LLaMA-1B, ATP-Latent achieves +4.1 percentage points accuracy and -3.3% tokens compared to advanced baselines across four benchmarks. Code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: ATP-Latent demonstrates that active planning in the latent space, via VAE-based supervision and coherence-guided RL, can improve latent reasoning policies and reduce token usage, addressing the training-testing gap in latent CoT methods.

title: Transferable Graph Condensation from the Causal Perspective
summary: The increasing scale of graph datasets has significantly improved the performance of graph representation learning methods, but it has also introduced substantial training challenges. Graph dataset condensation techniques have emerged to compress large datasets into smaller yet information-rich datasets, while maintaining similar test performance. However, these methods strictly require downstream applications to match the original dataset and task, which often fails in cross-task and cross-domain scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose a novel causal-invariance-based and transferable graph dataset condensation method, named \textbf{TGCC}, providing effective and transferable condensed datasets. Specifically, to preserve domain-invariant knowledge, we first extract domain causal-invariant features from the spatial domain of the graph using causal interventions. Then, to fully capture the structural and feature information of the original graph, we perform enhanced condensation operations. Finally, through spectral-domain enhanced contrastive learning, we inject the causal-invariant features into the condensed graph, ensuring that the compressed graph retains the causal information of the original graph. Experimental results on five public datasets and our novel \textbf{FinReport} dataset demonstrate that TGCC achieves up to a 13.41\% improvement in cross-task and cross-domain complex scenarios compared to existing methods, and achieves state-of-the-art performance on 5 out of 6 datasets in the single dataset and task scenario.

AI.tldr: TGCC introduces a causal-invariance-based, transferable graph dataset condensation method that preserves domain-invariant causal features and uses spectral-domain contrastive learning to produce condensed graphs with strong cross-task/domain transfer and competitive single-task performance.
AI.motivation: Existing graph dataset condensation methods often require downstream tasks to match the original data, limiting cross-task and cross-domain transferability. There is a need to preserve causal-invariant information to enable transfer across tasks and domains.
AI.method: 1) Extract domain causal-invariant features from the spatial domain of the graph via causal interventions. 2) Apply enhanced condensation to capture both structural and feature information of the original graph. 3) Use spectral-domain enhanced contrastive learning to inject causal-invariant features into the condensed graph, ensuring preservation of original causal information.
AI.result: Experimental results on five public datasets and the FinReport dataset show up to 13.41% improvement in cross-task and cross-domain scenarios. TGCC achieves state-of-the-art performance on 5 of 6 datasets in the single dataset/task scenario.
AI.conclusion: TGCC provides effective and transferable condensed graphs by combining causal-invariance with spectral-domain contrastive learning. It demonstrates strong cross-task/domain transfer while maintaining strong performance in standard settings, suggesting a viable strategy for cross-domain graph dataset condensation.

title: TraceRouter: Robust Safety for Large Foundation Models via Path-Level Intervention
summary: Despite their capabilities, large foundation models (LFMs) remain susceptible to adversarial manipulation. Current defenses predominantly rely on the "locality hypothesis", suppressing isolated neurons or features. However, harmful semantics act as distributed, cross-layer circuits, rendering such localized interventions brittle and detrimental to utility. To bridge this gap, we propose \textbf{TraceRouter}, a path-level framework that traces and disconnects the causal propagation circuits of illicit semantics. TraceRouter operates in three stages: (1) it pinpoints a sensitive onset layer by analyzing attention divergence; (2) it leverages sparse autoencoders (SAEs) and differential activation analysis to disentangle and isolate malicious features; and (3) it maps these features to downstream causal pathways via feature influence scores (FIS) derived from zero-out interventions. By selectively suppressing these causal chains, TraceRouter physically severs the flow of harmful information while leaving orthogonal computation routes intact. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TraceRouter significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a superior trade-off between adversarial robustness and general utility. Our code will be publicly released. WARNING: This paper contains unsafe model responses.

AI.tldr: TraceRouter is a path-level defense for large foundation models that traces and disconnects illicit semantic propagation by identifying onset layers, disentangling malicious features with sparse autoencoders and differential activation analysis, and mapping features to downstream causal pathways via feature influence scores to sever harmful information flows while preserving other computations. It reportedly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines and will release code; it also flags unsafe model responses in the paper.
AI.motivation: Current defenses based on the locality hypothesis (suppressing isolated neurons/features) misjudge the distributed, cross-layer nature of harmful semantics, making them brittle and utility-reducing. A path-level approach is needed to identify and sever entire causal propagation circuits of illicit content in LFMs.
AI.method: 1) Identify a sensitive onset layer by analyzing attention divergence. 2) Use sparse autoencoders (SAEs) and differential activation analysis to disentangle and isolate malicious features. 3) Map these features to downstream causal pathways using feature influence scores (FIS) derived from zero-out interventions, then selectively suppress the causal chains to sever harmful information while preserving orthogonal computation routes.
AI.result: Empirical results claim significant improvements over state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a better trade-off between adversarial robustness and general utility across tasks.
AI.conclusion: TraceRouter provides a physically motivated mechanism to sap the flow of illicit semantics by disconnecting causal propagation paths in LFMs, potentially offering robust defenses with preserved utility; code release anticipated; cautions noted about unsafe model responses.

title: Few-Shot Learning for Dynamic Operations of Automated Electric Taxi Fleets under Evolving Charging Infrastructure: A Meta-Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach
summary: With the rapid expansion of electric vehicles (EVs) and charging infrastructure, the effective management of Autonomous Electric Taxi (AET) fleets faces a critical challenge in environments with dynamic and uncertain charging availability. While most existing research assumes a static charging network, this simplification creates a significant gap between theoretical models and real-world operations. To bridge this gap, we propose GAT-PEARL, a novel meta-reinforcement learning framework that learns an adaptive operational policy. Our approach integrates a graph attention network (GAT) to effectively extract robust spatial representations under infrastructure layouts and model the complex spatiotemporal relationships of the urban environment, and employs probabilistic embeddings for actor-critic reinforcement learning (PEARL) to enable rapid, inference-based adaptation to changes in charging network layouts without retraining. Through extensive simulations on real-world data in Chengdu, China, we demonstrate that GAT-PEARL significantly outperforms conventional reinforcement learning baselines, showing superior generalization to unseen infrastructure layouts and achieving higher overall operational efficiency in dynamic settings.

AI.tldr: Meta-RL framework GAT-PEARL combines graph attention networks with PEARL to enable rapid adaptation of autonomous electric taxi fleet policies to dynamic and uncertain charging layouts, improving generalization and efficiency.
AI.motivation: There is a gap between static charging network assumptions in existing research and real-world operations where charging availability is dynamic and uncertain. An adaptive policy that generalizes to unseen infrastructure layouts is needed.
AI.method: Use a Graph Attention Network (GAT) to extract robust spatial representations from infrastructure layouts and model complex spatiotemporal relationships; apply PEARL (probabilistic embeddings for actor-critic RL) to enable rapid, inference-based adaptation to changing charging network layouts without retraining.
AI.result: GAT-PEARL significantly outperforms conventional RL baselines, with superior generalization to unseen infrastructure layouts and higher overall operational efficiency in dynamic settings, demonstrated on real-world Chengdu data.
AI.conclusion: GAT-PEARL provides a robust, adaptive policy framework for AET fleet management in dynamic charging environments, addressing the static-network limitation and showing strong potential for real-world deployment.

title: Beyond Global Alignment: Fine-Grained Motion-Language Retrieval via Pyramidal Shapley-Taylor Learning
summary: As a foundational task in human-centric cross-modal intelligence, motion-language retrieval aims to bridge the semantic gap between natural language and human motion, enabling intuitive motion analysis, yet existing approaches predominantly focus on aligning entire motion sequences with global textual representations. This global-centric paradigm overlooks fine-grained interactions between local motion segments and individual body joints and text tokens, inevitably leading to suboptimal retrieval performance. To address this limitation, we draw inspiration from the pyramidal process of human motion perception (from joint dynamics to segment coherence, and finally to holistic comprehension) and propose a novel Pyramidal Shapley-Taylor (PST) learning framework for fine-grained motion-language retrieval. Specifically, the framework decomposes human motion into temporal segments and spatial body joints, and learns cross-modal correspondences through progressive joint-wise and segment-wise alignment in a pyramidal fashion, effectively capturing both local semantic details and hierarchical structural relationships. Extensive experiments on multiple public benchmark datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving precise alignment between motion segments and body joints and their corresponding text tokens. The code of this work will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Proposes Pyramidal Shapley-Taylor (PST) learning for fine-grained motion-language retrieval by hierarchically aligning local motion segments, body joints, and text tokens, improving over global-centric methods.
AI.motivation: Global-centric methods ignore local interactions between segments, joints, and tokens. A pyramidal perceptual analogy motivates decomposing motion and progressively aligning finer-grained components to bridge semantic gaps in cross-modal retrieval.
AI.method: Decompose motion into temporal segments and spatial joints. Learn cross-modal correspondences via progressive joint-wise and segment-wise alignment in a pyramidal architecture, instantiated as the Pyramidal Shapley-Taylor (PST) framework; aligns local semantics and hierarchical structure across modalities.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multiple public benchmarks shows state-of-the-art performance with precise segment–joint–text token alignment; significant retrieval gains reported. Code to be released upon acceptance.
AI.conclusion: PST effectively captures local semantics and hierarchical structure for motion-language retrieval, demonstrating the value of pyramidal, progressive alignment and joint/segment-level interactions.

title: Search-Based Risk Feature Discovery in Document Structure Spaces under a Constrained Budget
summary: Enterprise-grade Intelligent Document Processing (IDP) systems support high-stakes workflows across finance, insurance, and healthcare. Early-phase system validation under limited budgets mandates uncovering diverse failure mechanisms, rather than identifying a single worst-case document. We formalize this challenge as a Search-Based Software Testing (SBST) problem, aiming to identify complex interactions between document variables, with the objective to maximize the number of distinct failure types discovered within a fixed evaluation budget. Our methodology operates on a combinatorial space of document configurations, rendering instances of structural \emph{risk features} to induce realistic failure conditions. We benchmark a diverse portfolio of search strategies spanning evolutionary, swarm-based, quality-diversity, learning-based, and quantum under identical budget constraints. Through configuration-level exclusivity, win-rate, and cross-temporal overlap analyses, we show that different solvers consistently uncover failure modes that remain undiscovered by specific alternatives at comparable budgets. Crucially, cross-temporal analysis reveals persistent solver-specific discoveries across all evaluated budgets, with no single strategy exhibiting absolute dominance. While the union of all solvers eventually recovers the observed failure space, reliance on any individual method systematically delays the discovery of important risks. These results demonstrate intrinsic solver complementarity and motivate portfolio-based SBST strategies for robust industrial IDP validation.

AI.tldr: Formulates industrial IDP validation as a search-based software testing (SBST) problem over document configurations to maximize discovery of distinct failure types within a fixed budget; shows solver complementarity and advocates portfolio SBST for robust validation.
AI.motivation: Industrial IDP systems operate in high-stakes domains; validating under budget requires uncovering diverse failure mechanisms across many failure types, not just the single worst-case. There is a need to understand how different SBST solvers contribute to uncovering a broader failure space and to encourage robust validation strategies.
AI.method: Model a combinatorial space of document configurations with structural risk features to induce realistic failures. Evaluate a broad set of SBST strategies (evolutionary, swarm-based, quality-diversity, learning-based, and quantum) under identical budgets. Use configuration-level exclusivity, win-rate, and cross-temporal overlap analyses to compare solvers and assess complementarity.
AI.result: Different solvers tend to uncover distinct, non-overlapping failure modes; cross-temporal analysis shows solver-specific discoveries persist across budgets; no single strategy dominates. The union of all solvers recovers the observed failure space, but relying on any single method delays discovery. The study demonstrates solver complementarity and supports portfolio-based SBST for industrial IDP validation.
AI.conclusion: Portfolio-based, diversified SBST strategies are beneficial for robust industrial IDP validation, leveraging complementary strengths across solvers to rapidly and comprehensively uncover failure modes within practical budgets.

title: Distributionally Robust Classification for Multi-source Unsupervised Domain Adaptation
summary: Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) is a statistical learning problem when the distribution of training (source) data is different from that of test (target) data. In this setting, one has access to labeled data only from the source domain and unlabeled data from the target domain. The central objective is to leverage the source data and the unlabeled target data to build models that generalize to the target domain. Despite its potential, existing UDA approaches often struggle in practice, particularly in scenarios where the target domain offers only limited unlabeled data or spurious correlations dominate the source domain. To address these challenges, we propose a novel distributionally robust learning framework that models uncertainty in both the covariate distribution and the conditional label distribution. Our approach is motivated by the multi-source domain adaptation setting but is also directly applicable to the single-source scenario, making it versatile in practice. We develop an efficient learning algorithm that can be seamlessly integrated with existing UDA methods. Extensive experiments under various distribution shift scenarios show that our method consistently outperforms strong baselines, especially when target data are extremely scarce.

AI.tldr: Proposes a distributionally robust UDA framework that accounts for uncertainty in covariate and conditional distributions, spanning multi-source and single-source settings, with an efficient algorithm and strong empirical gains when target data are scarce.
AI.motivation: UDA often underperforms when target unlabeled data are limited or when spurious correlations in the source domain mislead learning. A robust framework that explicitly models uncertainty in both covariate distributions and label-conditionals is needed to improve generalization under distribution shift.
AI.method: A distributionally robust learning framework that models uncertainty in both the covariate distribution and the conditional label distribution. Motivated by multi-source domain adaptation but directly applicable to single-source. An efficient learning algorithm designed to integrate with existing UDA methods, enabling robust adaptation without overhauling existing pipelines.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse distribution shift scenarios show that the proposed method consistently outperforms strong baselines, with particularly large gains when target data are extremely scarce.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework is versatile (works for single- and multi-source settings), effective under severe distribution shift and data scarcity, and can be readily integrated into existing UDA frameworks to improve robustness and generalization.

title: VideoAesBench: Benchmarking the Video Aesthetics Perception Capabilities of Large Multimodal Models
summary: Large multimodal models (LMMs) have demonstrated outstanding capabilities in various visual perception tasks, which has in turn made the evaluation of LMMs significant. However, the capability of video aesthetic quality assessment, which is a fundamental ability for human, remains underexplored for LMMs. To address this, we introduce VideoAesBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LMMs' understanding of video aesthetic quality. VideoAesBench has several significant characteristics: (1) Diverse content including 1,804 videos from multiple video sources including user-generated (UGC), AI-generated (AIGC), compressed, robotic-generated (RGC), and game videos. (2) Multiple question formats containing traditional single-choice questions, multi-choice questions, True or False questions, and a novel open-ended questions for video aesthetics description. (3) Holistic video aesthetics dimensions including visual form related questions from 5 aspects, visual style related questions from 4 aspects, and visual affectiveness questions from 3 aspects. Based on VideoAesBench, we benchmark 23 open-source and commercial large multimodal models. Our findings show that current LMMs only contain basic video aesthetics perception ability, their performance remains incomplete and imprecise. We hope our VideoAesBench can be served as a strong testbed and offer insights for explainable video aesthetics assessment.

AI.tldr: Introduces VideoAesBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating large multimodal models on video aesthetic quality using diverse content, multiple QA formats (including open-ended), and dimensions across form, style, and affective aesthetics; benchmarks 23 models and finds current LMMs have limited, imprecise aesthetics understanding.
AI.motivation: Video aesthetic understanding is fundamental to human perception and is underexplored in large multimodal models; a standardized benchmark is needed to assess and advance LMMs' capabilities in this area.
AI.method: Construct a dataset of 1,804 videos from multiple sources (UGC, AI-generated, compressed, robotic-generated, games); design diverse question formats: single-choice, multiple-choice, True/False, and novel open-ended questions for video aesthetics description; define holistic aesthetics dimensions across visual form (5 aspects), visual style (4 aspects), and visual affectiveness (3 aspects); evaluate 23 open-source and commercial LMMs.
AI.result: Current LMMs possess only basic video aesthetics perception; performance is incomplete and imprecise across the benchmark.
AI.conclusion: VideoAesBench provides a strong testbed and yields insights for explainable video aesthetics assessment, guiding future evaluation and development of LMMs in this domain.

title: RecNet: Self-Evolving Preference Propagation for Agentic Recommender Systems
summary: Agentic recommender systems leverage Large Language Models (LLMs) to model complex user behaviors and support personalized decision-making. However, existing methods primarily model preference changes based on explicit user-item interactions, which are sparse, noisy, and unable to reflect the real-time, mutual influences among users and items. To address these limitations, we propose RecNet, a self-evolving preference propagation framework that proactively propagates real-time preference updates across related users and items. RecNet consists of two complementary phases. In the forward phase, the centralized preference routing mechanism leverages router agents to integrate preference updates and dynamically propagate them to the most relevant agents. To ensure accurate and personalized integration of propagated preferences, we further introduce a personalized preference reception mechanism, which combines a message buffer for temporary caching and an optimizable, rule-based filter memory to guide selective preference assimilation based on past experience and interests. In the backward phase, the feedback-driven propagation optimization mechanism simulates a multi-agent reinforcement learning framework, using LLMs for credit assignment, gradient analysis, and module-level optimization, enabling continuous self-evolution of propagation strategies. Extensive experiments on various scenarios demonstrate the effectiveness of RecNet in modeling preference propagation for recommender systems.

AI.tldr: RecNet enables self-evolving preference propagation in agentic recommender systems via forward routing with router agents and a backward reinforcement-like optimization to continuously adapt propagation strategies.
AI.motivation: To overcome sparsity/noise in explicit user-item interactions and to capture real-time, mutual influences among users and items that existing methods miss.
AI.method: Two-phase design: (1) Forward phase with centralized preference routing using router agents to propagate updates to relevant agents; a personalized reception mechanism with a message buffer and a rule-based filter memory to guide selective assimilation; (2) Backward phase with feedback-driven propagation optimization simulating multi-agent RL, using LLMs for credit assignment, gradient analysis, and module-level optimization to continuously evolve propagation strategies.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse scenarios demonstrate RecNet's effectiveness in modeling and propagating user preferences in recommender systems.
AI.conclusion: RecNet provides a proactive, real-time, self-evolving framework for preference propagation, improving personalization by accounting for dynamic, interrelated user-item influences; future work may focus on scalability, robustness, and evaluation in real-world deployments.

title: Heterogeneous Vertiport Selection Optimization for On-Demand Air Taxi Services: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach
summary: Urban Air Mobility (UAM) has emerged as a transformative solution to alleviate urban congestion by utilizing low-altitude airspace, thereby reducing pressure on ground transportation networks. To enable truly efficient and seamless door-to-door travel experiences, UAM requires close integration with existing ground transportation infrastructure. However, current research on optimal integrated routing strategies for passengers in air-ground mobility systems remains limited, with a lack of systematic exploration.To address this gap, we first propose a unified optimization model that integrates strategy selection for both air and ground transportation. This model captures the dynamic characteristics of multimodal transport networks and incorporates real-time traffic conditions alongside passenger decision-making behavior. Building on this model, we propose a Unified Air-Ground Mobility Coordination (UAGMC) framework, which leverages deep reinforcement learning (RL) and Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication to optimize vertiport selection and dynamically plan air taxi routes. Experimental results demonstrate that UAGMC achieves a 34\% reduction in average travel time compared to conventional proportional allocation methods, enhancing overall travel efficiency and providing novel insights into the integration and optimization of multimodal transportation systems. This work lays a solid foundation for advancing intelligent urban mobility solutions through the coordination of air and ground transportation modes. The related code can be found at https://github.com/Traffic-Alpha/UAGMC.

AI.tldr: Unified optimization and RL-based framework for integrated air-ground mobility (UAGMC) coordinating vertiport choice and air taxi routing, achieving 34% travel-time reduction over proportional methods.
AI.motivation: Urban congestion and the demand for seamless door-to-door multimodal travel necessitate integrated, real-time optimization of air-ground routing and passenger decision-making, which is lacking in current work.
AI.method: A unified optimization model for strategy selection across air and ground modes, incorporating real-time traffic and passenger decisions. A Unified Air-Ground Mobility Coordination (UAGMC) framework uses deep reinforcement learning and V2X communication to optimize vertiport selection and dynamic air taxi routing.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate a 34% reduction in average travel time compared with conventional proportional allocation methods, improving overall travel efficiency; useful insights for multimodal system integration; code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: Provides a solid foundation for intelligent urban mobility via coordinated air and ground transport, with potential for scalable, adaptive multimodal routing and avenues for further validation and extension.

title: Zero-Shot Video Restoration and Enhancement with Assistance of Video Diffusion Models
summary: Although diffusion-based zero-shot image restoration and enhancement methods have achieved great success, applying them to video restoration or enhancement will lead to severe temporal flickering. In this paper, we propose the first framework that utilizes the rapidly-developed video diffusion model to assist the image-based method in maintaining more temporal consistency for zero-shot video restoration and enhancement. We propose homologous latents fusion, heterogenous latents fusion, and a COT-based fusion ratio strategy to utilize both homologous and heterogenous text-to-video diffusion models to complement the image method. Moreover, we propose temporal-strengthening post-processing to utilize the image-to-video diffusion model to further improve temporal consistency. Our method is training-free and can be applied to any diffusion-based image restoration and enhancement methods. Experimental results demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: A training-free framework that combines image-based restoration with both homologous and heterogeneous text-to-video diffusion models to reduce temporal flicker in zero-shot video restoration/enhancement, via homologous latents fusion, heterogenous latents fusion, COT-based fusion ratio, and temporal-strengthening post-processing.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot image restoration methods often suffer from severe temporal flicker when applied to videos. Video diffusion models offer temporal coherence, but integrating them with image-based methods without additional training remains challenging. The work aims to fuse information from homologous and heterogenous video diffusion models to stabilize video outputs while remaining training-free.
AI.method: Introduce homologous latents fusion and heterogenous latents fusion to combine latent representations from text-to-video diffusion models with an image-based restoration method. Employ a COT-based fusion ratio strategy to balance contributions from different models. Add temporal-strengthening post-processing to further improve temporal consistency.
