The framework is training-free and compatible with any diffusion-based image restoration/enhancement method.
AI.result: Experimental results show superiority of the proposed framework in achieving temporal consistency and restoration quality over baselines (specific metrics not provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: A training-free, model-agnostic fusion framework leveraging both homologous and heterogeneous video diffusion models, with a COT-based ratio and temporal-strengthening post-processing, can improve temporal stability in zero-shot video restoration/enhancement.

title: Semantic Content Determines Algorithmic Performance
summary: Counting should not depend on what is being counted; more generally, any algorithm's behavior should be invariant to the semantic content of its arguments. We introduce WhatCounts to test this property in isolation. Unlike prior work that conflates semantic sensitivity with reasoning complexity or prompt variation, WhatCounts is atomic: count items in an unambiguous, delimited list with no duplicates, distractors, or reasoning steps for different semantic types. Frontier LLMs show over 40% accuracy variation depending solely on what is being counted - cities versus chemicals, names versus symbols. Controlled ablations rule out confounds. The gap is semantic, and it shifts unpredictably with small amounts of unrelated fine-tuning. LLMs do not implement algorithms; they approximate them, and the approximation is argument-dependent. As we show with an agentic example, this has implications beyond counting: any LLM function may carry hidden dependencies on the meaning of its inputs.

AI.tldr: WhatCounts probes whether counting behavior in LLMs is invariant to semantic content by using atomic, unambiguous counting tasks; results show large content-driven variation (e.g., counting cities vs. chemicals), implying semantic sensitivity despite task simplicity.
AI.motivation: Disentangle semantic sensitivity from reasoning complexity or prompt variation; demonstrate whether LLMs implement algorithms or rely on content-dependent heuristics.
AI.method: A minimal, atomic counting task: count items in a delimited, duplicate-free list with no distractors or reasoning steps, across multiple semantic domains. Use controlled ablations to rule out confounds and test across frontier LLMs, with tests showing how small fine-tuning can shift behavior.
AI.result: Frontier LLMs exhibit >40% accuracy variation solely based on what is being counted. Ablations rule out simple confounds; the semantic gap appears robust and shifts with limited, unrelated fine-tuning. LLMs seem to approximate algorithms, with results that depend on input meaning.
AI.conclusion: LLMs do not faithfully implement algorithmic invariances; their behavior is argment-dependent. Counting and potentially any LLM function can carry hidden dependencies on input semantics, with broader implications for reliability and generalization.

title: Just Noticeable Difference Modeling for Deep Visual Features
summary: Deep visual features are increasingly used as the interface in vision systems, motivating the need to describe feature characteristics and control feature quality for machine perception. Just noticeable difference (JND) characterizes the maximum imperceptible distortion for images under human or machine vision. Extending it to deep visual features naturally meets the above demand by providing a task-aligned tolerance boundary in feature space, offering a practical reference for controlling feature quality under constrained resources. We propose FeatJND, a task-aligned JND formulation that predicts the maximum tolerable per-feature perturbation map while preserving downstream task performance. We propose a FeatJND estimator at standardized split points and validate it across image classification, detection, and instance segmentation. Under matched distortion strength, FeatJND-based distortions consistently preserve higher task performance than unstructured Gaussian perturbations, and attribution visualizations suggest FeatJND can suppress non-critical feature regions. As an application, we further apply FeatJND to token-wise dynamic quantization and show that FeatJND-guided step-size allocation yields clear gains over random step-size permutation and global uniform step size under the same noise budget. Our code will be released after publication.

AI.tldr: FeatJND introduces a task-aligned JND for deep visual features, predicting per-feature perturbation caps to preserve downstream performance; outperforms Gaussian perturbations and enables efficient token-wise quantization under a fixed noise budget.
AI.motivation: Deep features serve as the interface for vision systems and require a controllable tolerance boundary; extending JND to feature space enables resource-constrained distortion control aligned with downstream tasks.
AI.method: Propose FeatJND estimator at standardized split points to generate a per-feature perturbation map; validate across image classification, detection, and instance segmentation; compare with unstructured Gaussian noise; use attribution visualizations to assess region importance; apply FeatJND to token-wise dynamic quantization with guided step-size allocation.
AI.result: Under matched distortion strength, FeatJND-based distortions preserve higher task performance than Gaussian perturbations; attribution suggests suppression of non-critical feature regions; FeatJND-guided step-size allocation achieves gains over random permutation and global uniform step sizes under the same noise budget.
AI.conclusion: FeatJND provides a practical, task-aligned framework for controlling feature quality in deep representations, enabling more efficient and robust vision systems and quantization strategies; code release planned.

title: ScholarGym: Benchmarking Deep Research Workflows on Academic Literature Retrieval
summary:
Tool-augmented large language models have advanced from single-turn question answering to deep research workflows that iteratively plan queries, invoke external tools, and synthesize information to address complex information needs. Evaluating such workflows presents a fundamental challenge: reliance on live APIs introduces non-determinism, as tool invocations may yield different results across runs due to temporal drift, rate limiting, and evolving backend states. This variance undermines reproducibility and invalidates cross-system comparisons.
  We present ScholarGym, a simulation environment for reproducible evaluation of deep research workflows on academic literature. The environment decouples workflow components into query planning, tool invocation, and relevance assessment, enabling fine-grained analysis of each stage under controlled conditions. Built on a static corpus of 570K papers with deterministic retrieval, ScholarGym provides 2,536 queries with expert-annotated ground truth. Experiments across diverse backbone models reveal how reasoning capabilities, planning strategies, and selection mechanisms interact over iterative refinement.

AI.tldr: Deterministic, simulation-based platform ScholarGym enables reproducible evaluation of tool-augmented, multi-step research workflows over academic literature using a static corpus and ground-truth queries.
AI.motivation: Nondeterminism from live API tool calls undermines reproducibility and fair cross-system comparisons; a controlled evaluation environment is needed to study planning, reasoning, and tool usage in deep workflows.
AI.method: Proposes ScholarGym: a simulation environment that separates query planning, tool invocation, and relevance assessment; uses a static corpus of 570k papers with deterministic retrieval; provides 2,536 expert-annotated queries; conducts experiments across diverse backbone models to study interactions during iterative refinement.
AI.result: Demonstrates the framework's capacity for detailed analysis of component interactions and confirms reproducibility benefits; yields insights into how reasoning, planning, and selection strategies affect performance across iterations.
AI.conclusion: ScholarGym enables reproducible, fine-grained evaluation of deep research workflows for scholarly information tasks and supports robust cross-system comparisons by removing API nondeterminism.

title: An introductory Generalization of the standard SVMs loss and its applications to Shallow and Deep Neural Networks
summary: We propose a new convex loss for SVMs, both for the binary classification and for the regression models. Therefore, we show the mathematical derivation of the dual problems and we experiment them with several small data-sets. The minimal dimension of those data-sets is due to the difficult scalability of the SVM method to bigger instances. This preliminary study should prove that using pattern correlations inside the loss function could enhance the generalisation performances. Coherently, results show that generalisation measures are never worse than the standard losses and several times they are better. In our opinion, it should be considered a careful study of this loss, coupled with shallow and deep neural networks. In fact, we present some novel results obtained with those architectures.

AI.tldr: A convex loss for SVMs (classification and regression) with pattern correlations is proposed, with dual derivations and experiments on small datasets. The approach yields generalization that is at least as good as standard losses, often better, and is shown to be compatible with shallow and deep neural networks in preliminary results.
AI.motivation: Improve SVM generalization and address scalability limitations by incorporating pattern correlations into the loss; extend the approach to neural networks.
AI.method: Introduce a new convex loss function for SVMs (binary classification and regression); derive the dual formulations; conduct experiments on several small datasets due to scalability constraints; evaluate the loss in conjunction with shallow and deep neural networks.
AI.result: Generalization measures are never worse than those with standard losses and are often better; experiments constrained by small data sizes; preliminary results with NN architectures show promising improvements.
AI.conclusion: The loss is a promising direction worthy of further investigation, particularly in combination with shallow and deep neural networks; results suggest potential generalization gains and compatibility across models.

title: BookNet: Book Image Rectification via Cross-Page Attention Network
summary: Book image rectification presents unique challenges in document image processing due to complex geometric distortions from binding constraints, where left and right pages exhibit distinctly asymmetric curvature patterns. However, existing single-page document image rectification methods fail to capture the coupled geometric relationships between adjacent pages in books. In this work, we introduce BookNet, the first end-to-end deep learning framework specifically designed for dual-page book image rectification. BookNet adopts a dual-branch architecture with cross-page attention mechanisms, enabling it to estimate warping flows for both individual pages and the complete book spread, explicitly modeling how left and right pages influence each other. Moreover, to address the absence of specialized datasets, we present Book3D, a large-scale synthetic dataset for training, and Book100, a comprehensive real-world benchmark for evaluation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BookNet outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on book image rectification. Code and dataset will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: A dual-page book image rectification framework, BookNet, with a dual-branch architecture and cross-page attention to jointly rectify left/right pages; introduces synthetic Book3D and real Book100 datasets; achieves state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Rectifying book pages is hindered by binding-induced geometric distortions that couple the deformation of adjacent pages; existing single-page rectification methods fail to capture inter-page relationships, necessitating an end-to-end dual-page solution.
AI.method: BookNet uses a dual-branch network with cross-page attention to estimate warping flows for each page and the full book spread, explicitly modeling inter-page influence and producing coherent rectifications.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show BookNet outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on book image rectification across synthetic Book3D and real Book100 benchmarks; code and datasets will be released.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents the first end-to-end dual-page rectification framework, along with synthetic and real-world datasets, demonstrating improved performance and offering publicly available resources.

title: SONIC-O1: A Real-World Benchmark for Evaluating Multimodal Large Language Models on Audio-Video Understanding
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are a major focus of recent AI research. However, most prior work focuses on static image understanding, while their ability to process sequential audio-video data remains underexplored. This gap highlights the need for a high-quality benchmark to systematically evaluate MLLM performance in a real-world setting. We introduce SONIC-O1, a comprehensive, fully human-verified benchmark spanning 13 real-world conversational domains with 4,958 annotations and demographic metadata. SONIC-O1 evaluates MLLMs on key tasks, including open-ended summarization, multiple-choice question (MCQ) answering, and temporal localization with supporting rationales (reasoning). Experiments on closed- and open-source models reveal limitations. While the performance gap in MCQ accuracy between two model families is relatively small, we observe a substantial 22.6% performance difference in temporal localization between the best performing closed-source and open-source models. Performance further degrades across demographic groups, indicating persistent disparities in model behavior. Overall, SONIC-O1 provides an open evaluation suite for temporally grounded and socially robust multimodal understanding. We release SONIC-O1 for reproducibility and research: Project page: https://vectorinstitute.github.io/sonic-o1/ Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/vector-institute/sonic-o1 Github: https://github.com/vectorinstitute/sonic-o1 Leaderboard: https://huggingface.co/spaces/vector-institute/sonic-o1-leaderboard

AI.tldr: SONIC-O1 is a new, fully human-verified benchmark for temporally grounded multimodal understanding, spanning 13 real-world conversational domains with 4,958 annotations and demographic metadata. It evaluates open-ended summarization, MCQ answering, and temporal localization with rationales; reveals small MCQ gaps but a 22.6% gap in temporal localization between model families and demographic disparities; released as an open evaluation suite and resources.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of robust benchmarks for sequential audio–video data in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and enable real-world, socially aware evaluation.
AI.method: Develop SONIC-O1 with 13 domains, 4,958 annotations, and demographic metadata; ensure full human verification; evaluate MLLMs on open-ended summarization, MCQ, and temporal localization with rationales; compare closed- and open-source models; release dataset, code, and leaderboard.
AI.result: Demonstrates limitations in current MLLMs: small MCQ accuracy gap between model families but a substantial 22.6% gap in temporal localization; performance declines across demographic groups, indicating disparities; provides an open, reproducible evaluation suite for temporally grounded and socially robust multimodal understanding.
AI.conclusion: SONIC-O1 is a valuable resource for reproducible evaluation of temporally grounded multimodal understanding and fairness, enabling researchers to benchmark and improve models on temporal tasks and demographic robustness; resources are openly released (dataset, project page, GitHub, leaderboard).

title: Memorization Control in Diffusion Models from Denoising-centric Perspective
summary: Controlling memorization in diffusion models is critical for applications that require generated data to closely match the training distribution. Existing approaches mainly focus on data centric or model centric modifications, treating the diffusion model as an isolated predictor. In this paper, we study memorization in diffusion models from a denoising centric perspective. We show that uniform timestep sampling leads to unequal learning contributions across denoising steps due to differences in signal to noise ratio, which biases training toward memorization. To address this, we propose a timestep sampling strategy that explicitly controls where learning occurs along the denoising trajectory. By adjusting the width of the confidence interval, our method provides direct control over the memorization generalization trade off. Experiments on image and 1D signal generation tasks demonstrate that shifting learning emphasis toward later denoising steps consistently reduces memorization and improves distributional alignment with training data, validating the generality and effectiveness of our approach.

AI.tldr: Denoising-centric control of memorization in diffusion models via adjustable timestep sampling.
AI.motivation: Address memorization in diffusion models by moving beyond data/model-centric fixes and examining learning dynamics along the denoising trajectory to reduce overfitting to training data.
AI.method: Analyze uniform timestep sampling and its unequal learning contributions due to varying signal-to-noise ratio; propose a timestep sampling strategy that controls where learning occurs along the denoising path by tuning the width of a confidence interval to steer memorization-generalization trade-off.
AI.result: Empirical results on image and 1D signal generation show that emphasizing later denoising steps reduces memorization and yields better distributional alignment with training data, indicating the approach’s generality.
AI.conclusion: A denoising-centric sampling mechanism provides direct, controllable mitigation of memorization in diffusion models by adjusting where learning occurs along the denoising trajectory.

title: Deep Models, Shallow Alignment: Uncovering the Granularity Mismatch in Neural Decoding
summary: Neural visual decoding is a central problem in brain computer interface research, aiming to reconstruct human visual perception and to elucidate the structure of neural representations. However, existing approaches overlook a fundamental granularity mismatch between human and machine vision, where deep vision models emphasize semantic invariance by suppressing local texture information, whereas neural signals preserve an intricate mixture of low-level visual attributes and high-level semantic content. To address this mismatch, we propose Shallow Alignment, a novel contrastive learning strategy that aligns neural signals with intermediate representations of visual encoders rather than their final outputs, thereby striking a better balance between low-level texture details and high-level semantic features. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that Shallow Alignment significantly outperforms standard final-layer alignment, with performance gains ranging from 22% to 58% across diverse vision backbones. Notably, our approach effectively unlocks the scaling law in neural visual decoding, enabling decoding performance to scale predictably with the capacity of pre-trained vision backbones. We further conduct systematic empirical analyses to shed light on the mechanisms underlying the observed performance gains.

AI.tldr: Shallow Alignment uses contrastive learning to align neural signals with intermediate visual encoder representations, addressing a granularity mismatch between human vision (texture details) and machine vision (semantic invariance). It outperforms final-layer alignment by 22–58% across backbones and reveals scalable decoding with backbone capacity; includes empirical analyses of underlying mechanisms.
AI.motivation: To resolve the granularity mismatch in neural visual decoding: neural signals retain low-level and mid-level features, while models trained for vision often emphasize semantic invariance by suppressing texture details; aligning to intermediate representations could leverage richer signal content.
AI.method: Introduce Shallow Alignment, a contrastive learning objective that aligns neural data with intermediate-layer features from pre-trained vision encoders (rather than final outputs). Evaluate across multiple vision backbones and benchmarks; perform ablations and analyses to understand mechanisms and scaling behavior.
AI.result: Significant performance gains (22%–58%) over final-layer alignment across diverse backbones; unlocking a scaling law whereby decoding performance scales with backbone capacity; comprehensive empirical analyses provide insights into why intermediate representations yield benefits.
AI.conclusion: Aligning neural signals with intermediate representations (shallow alignment) is a more faithful mapping to neural data, yielding better decoding and scalable improvements, suggesting a principled path for neural visual decoding research.

title: TCAP: Tri-Component Attention Profiling for Unsupervised Backdoor Detection in MLLM Fine-Tuning
summary: Fine-Tuning-as-a-Service (FTaaS) facilitates the customization of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) but introduces critical backdoor risks via poisoned data. Existing defenses either rely on supervised signals or fail to generalize across diverse trigger types and modalities. In this work, we uncover a universal backdoor fingerprint-attention allocation divergence-where poisoned samples disrupt the balanced attention distribution across three functional components: system instructions, vision inputs, and user textual queries, regardless of trigger morphology. Motivated by this insight, we propose Tri-Component Attention Profiling (TCAP), an unsupervised defense framework to filter backdoor samples. TCAP decomposes cross-modal attention maps into the three components, identifies trigger-responsive attention heads via Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) statistical profiling, and isolates poisoned samples through EM-based vote aggregation. Extensive experiments across diverse MLLM architectures and attack methods demonstrate that TCAP achieves consistently strong performance, establishing it as a robust and practical backdoor defense in MLLMs.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised defense TCAP filters backdoor samples in Fine-Tuning-as-a-Service for Multimodal LLMs by analyzing tri-component attention distribution and using Gaussian Mixture Modeling and EM-based aggregation to identify and remove poisoned data.
AI.motivation: Backdoors during FTaaS create universal risks across modalities and trigger types. Existing defenses struggle to generalize and often rely on supervised signals. There is a need for a general, unsupervised defense that works across different triggers and modalities.
AI.method: Decompose cross-modal attention maps into three components: system instructions, vision inputs, and user textual queries. Use Gaussian Mixture Model profiling to identify trigger-responsive attention heads. Apply EM-based vote aggregation across samples to isolate poisoned data.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse MLLM architectures and attack methods show that TCAP consistently achieves strong backdoor defense performance, indicating robustness and practicality across settings.
AI.conclusion: TCAP provides a robust, unsupervised defense for backdoor detection in MLLMs, leveraging tri-component attention profiling to generalize across trigger morphologies and modalities; it offers practical applicability and potential for broader adoption.

title: L2R: Low-Rank and Lipschitz-Controlled Routing for Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models scale neural networks by conditionally activating a small subset of experts, where the router plays a central role in determining expert specialization and overall model performance. However, many modern MoE systems still adopt linear routers in raw high-dimensional representation spaces, where representation mismatch, angular concentration, and scale-sensitive scoring can jointly undermine routing discriminability and stable expert specialization. In this work, we propose Low-rank \& Lipschitz-controlled Routing (L2R), a unified routing framework that reshapes both the routing space and scoring geometry. L2R performs expert assignment in a shared low-rank latent routing space and introduces Saturated Inner-Product Scoring (SIPS) to explicitly control the Lipschitz behavior of routing functions, yielding smoother and more stable routing geometry. In addition, L2R incorporates a parameter-efficient multi-anchor routing mechanism to enhance expert expressiveness. Extensive experiments on a large-scale language MoE model and a vision MoE setting on ImageNet demonstrate that L2R consistently improves routing stability, expert specialization, and overall model performance.

AI.tldr: Low-rank & Lipschitz-controlled Routing (L2R) for Mixture-of-Experts; reshapes routing space via a shared low-rank latent space and controls scoring Lipschitzness with Saturated Inner-Product Scoring (SIPS), plus multi-anchor routing; yields smoother, more stable routing and better expert specialization, improving language and vision MoE performance.
AI.motivation: Standard linear routers in high-dimensional feature spaces suffer representation mismatch, angular concentration, and scale-sensitive scoring, which degrade routing discriminability and stable expert specialization.
AI.method: Introduce L2R: routing in a shared low-rank latent space; SIPS to bound Lipschitzness of scoring; multi-anchor routing for expressiveness; efficient parameterization.
AI.result: Empirical results on large-scale language MoE and ImageNet-vision MoE show improved routing stability, expert specialization, and overall model performance.
AI.conclusion: L2R provides a unified, geometry-aware routing framework that improves MoE routing stability and performance by restructuring the routing space and scoring mechanism.

title: PaddleOCR-VL-1.5: Towards a Multi-Task 0.9B VLM for Robust In-the-Wild Document Parsing
summary: We introduce PaddleOCR-VL-1.5, an upgraded model achieving a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) accuracy of 94.5% on OmniDocBench v1.5. To rigorously evaluate robustness against real-world physical distortions, including scanning, skew, warping, screen-photography, and illumination, we propose the Real5-OmniDocBench benchmark. Experimental results demonstrate that this enhanced model attains SOTA performance on the newly curated benchmark. Furthermore, we extend the model's capabilities by incorporating seal recognition and text spotting tasks, while remaining a 0.9B ultra-compact VLM with high efficiency. Code: https://github.com/PaddlePaddle/PaddleOCR

AI.tldr: PaddleOCR-VL-1.5 achieves SOTA accuracy on OmniDocBench v1.5 (94.5%), introduces Real5-OmniDocBench to evaluate robustness to real-world distortions, extends to seal recognition and text spotting, while remaining a compact 0.9B VLM; code released.
AI.motivation: Assess OCR robustness to physical distortions and broaden understanding of document-level visual-language models with a compact, deployable size.
AI.method: Upgrade the PaddleOCR-VL-1.5 model; curate Real5-OmniDocBench for real-world distortions (scanning, skew, warping, screen-photography, illumination); add seal recognition and text spotting heads; maintain a 0.9B parameter budget; release code.
AI.result: The model attains state-of-the-art results on OmniDocBench v1.5 and Real5-OmniDocBench benchmarks, demonstrating robustness to real-world distortions while preserving compactness and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: PaddleOCR-VL-1.5 provides a compact, robust VLM for OCR tasks, with expanded capabilities (seal recognition and text spotting) and access to code, targeting practical deployment.

title: FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer
summary: Large language models (LLMs) excel across many tasks, yet inference is still dominated by strictly token-by-token autoregression. Existing acceleration methods largely patch this pipeline and miss core human-reading ingredients: content-adaptive foresight, chunk-structure-aware compute allocation, and train--test consistency for preview/skimming. We propose the \textbf{Fovea-Block-Skip Transformer} (FBS), which injects a causal, trainable loop into Transformers via Parafovea-Attention Window (PAW), Chunk-Head (CH), and Skip-Gate (SG). Across diverse benchmarks, FBS improves the quality-efficiency trade-off without increasing parameters, and ablations show the three modules are complementary.

AI.tldr: Fovea-Block-Skip Transformer (FBS) introduces a trainable causal loop in Transformers via Parafovea-Attention Window (PAW), Chunk-Head (CH), and Skip-Gate (SG) to enable content-adaptive foresight and chunk-aware compute allocation, improving quality-efficiency without adding parameters; three modules are complementary according to ablations.
AI.motivation: Current inference remains token-by-token autoregressive and many acceleration methods patch the pipeline rather than incorporating human-reading-like foresight, chunking, and test-time preview consistency. The aim is to reduce compute while preserving or improving quality without increasing model size.
AI.method: Incorporate a trainable loop through: Parafovea-Attention Window (PAW) to focus attention with a parafoveal-like window; Chunk-Head (CH) to process tokens in chunks; Skip-Gate (SG) to selectively skip or gate computations. The paradigm forms a Fovea-Block-Skip Transformer (FBS) that enables content-adaptive foresight and test-time preview consistency.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across diverse benchmarks shows improved quality–efficiency trade-off without increasing parameters. Ablation studies indicate that PAW, CH, and SG are complementary and contribute to performance.
AI.conclusion: FBS provides a principled method to accelerate Transformers by injecting a trainable, causal loop that leverages attention windowing, chunked computation, and gating. It achieves better efficiency without parameter growth and highlights the synergy of the three modules; future work could explore broader tasks and deployment considerations.

title: Factored Causal Representation Learning for Robust Reward Modeling in RLHF
summary: A reliable reward model is essential for aligning large language models with human preferences through reinforcement learning from human feedback. However, standard reward models are susceptible to spurious features that are not causally related to human labels. This can lead to reward hacking, where high predicted reward does not translate into better behavior. In this work, we address this problem from a causal perspective by proposing a factored representation learning framework that decomposes the model's contextual embedding into (1) causal factors that are sufficient for reward prediction and (2) non-causal factors that capture reward-irrelevant attributes such as length or sycophantic bias. The reward head is then constrained to depend only on the causal component. In addition, we introduce an adversarial head trained to predict reward from the non-causal factors, while applying gradient reversal to discourage them from encoding reward-relevant information. Experiments on both mathematical and dialogue tasks demonstrate that our method learns more robust reward models and consistently improves downstream RLHF performance over state-of-the-art baselines. Analyses on length and sycophantic bias further validate the effectiveness of our method in mitigating reward hacking behaviors.

AI.tldr: A causal, factored-representation approach for reward models in RLHF that isolates causal factors for reward prediction while suppressing non-causal, bias-prone signals; uses an adversarial head with gradient reversal to mitigate reward hacking; shows improved robustness and RLHF performance on math and dialogue tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard reward models rely on features that may be spuriously correlated with human labels, leading to reward hacking where high predicted rewards do not yield better behavior. A causal perspective seeks to ensure the reward depends only on causally relevant information.
AI.method: Decompose the model's contextual embedding into two components: causal factors sufficient for reward prediction and non-causal factors capturing reward-irrelevant attributes (e.g., length, sycophancy). Constrain the reward head to depend only on the causal component. Introduce an adversarial head trained to predict rewards from non-causal factors, with gradient reversal to discourage encoding reward-relevant information in non-causal factors.
AI.result: Empirical results on mathematical and dialogue tasks show the proposed method yields more robust reward models and consistently improves downstream RLHF performance over strong baselines. Analyses on length and sycophantic bias support reduced reward hacking.
AI.conclusion: Factored representations with causal-constrained reward prediction and adversarial control of non-causal factors yield robust reward models and improved RLHF outcomes, offering a principled approach to mitigating reward hacking.

title: E-mem: Multi-agent based Episodic Context Reconstruction for LLM Agent Memory
summary: The evolution of Large Language Model (LLM) agents towards System~2 reasoning, characterized by deliberative, high-precision problem-solving, requires maintaining rigorous logical integrity over extended horizons. However, prevalent memory preprocessing paradigms suffer from destructive de-contextualization. By compressing complex sequential dependencies into pre-defined structures (e.g., embeddings or graphs), these methods sever the contextual integrity essential for deep reasoning. To address this, we propose E-mem, a framework shifting from Memory Preprocessing to Episodic Context Reconstruction. Inspired by biological engrams, E-mem employs a heterogeneous hierarchical architecture where multiple assistant agents maintain uncompressed memory contexts, while a central master agent orchestrates global planning. Unlike passive retrieval, our mechanism empowers assistants to locally reason within activated segments, extracting context-aware evidence before aggregation. Evaluations on the LoCoMo benchmark demonstrate that E-mem achieves over 54\% F1, surpassing the state-of-the-art GAM by 7.75\%, while reducing token cost by over 70\%.

AI.tldr: E-mem reframes memory as episodic context reconstruction for LLM agents, enabling local reasoning within activated segments and centralized planning, achieving strong performance and efficiency gains.
AI.motivation: Conventional memory preprocessing compresses complex sequential dependencies into fixed structures, destroying contextual integrity and hindering long-horizon, high-precision reasoning.
AI.method: A heterogeneous, hierarchical system where multiple assistant agents retain uncompressed memory contexts; a central master agent orchestrates global planning. Instead of passive retrieval, agents reason within activated memory segments to extract context-aware evidence before aggregation.
AI.result: On the LoCoMo benchmark, E-mem achieves 54% F1, surpassing the state-of-the-art GAM by 7.75 percentage points and reducing token cost by over 70%.
AI.conclusion: E-mem demonstrates that episodic context reconstruction with uncompressed memories and coordinated planning improves reasoning quality and efficiency, suggesting a shift away from memory preprocessing toward dynamic, context-preserving architectures.

title: Theoretically Optimal Attention/FFN Ratios in Disaggregated LLM Serving
summary: Attention-FFN disaggregation (AFD) is an emerging architecture for LLM decoding that separates state-heavy, KV-cache-dominated Attention computation from stateless, compute-intensive FFN computation, connected by per-step communication. While AFD enables independent scaling of memory and compute resources, its performance is highly sensitive to the Attention/FFN provisioning ratio: mis-sizing induces step-level blocking and costly device idle time. We develop a tractable analytical framework for sizing AFD bundles in an $r$A-$1$F topology, where the key difficulty is that Attention-side work is nonstationary-token context grows and requests are continuously replenished with random lengths-while FFN work is stable given the aggregated batch. Using a probabilistic workload model, we derive closed-form rules for the optimal A/F ratio that maximize average throughput per instance across the system. A trace-calibrated AFD simulator validates the theory: across workloads, the theoretical optimal A/F ratio matches the simulation-optimal within 10%, and consistently reduces idle time.

AI.tldr: Analytical sizing of Attention-FFN disaggregation (AFD) for LLM decoding in an rA-1F topology, deriving closed-form optimal Attention/FFN (A/F) ratio under nonstationary attention workloads and validating with a trace-calibrated simulator.
AI.motivation: Address performance sensitivity of AFD to provisioning; provide tractable, closed-form sizing rules to balance memory-heavy Attention with compute-heavy FFN.
AI.method: Develop a probabilistic workload model with nonstationary attention context growth and replenished requests; obtain closed-form A/F ratio expressions; validate via trace-calibrated AFD simulator across workloads.
AI.result: Derivation of closed-form rules for the optimal A/F ratio that maximize average throughput; simulation confirms theory within ~10% of the optimum and shows reduced idle time across workloads.
AI.conclusion: Analytical sizing guides enable near-optimal AFD throughput in scalable LLM decoding by balancing memory and compute resources, mitigating step-level blocking and idle time.

title: Drive-JEPA: Video JEPA Meets Multimodal Trajectory Distillation for End-to-End Driving
summary: End-to-end autonomous driving increasingly leverages self-supervised video pretraining to learn transferable planning representations. However, pretraining video world models for scene understanding has so far brought only limited improvements. This limitation is compounded by the inherent ambiguity of driving: each scene typically provides only a single human trajectory, making it difficult to learn multimodal behaviors. In this work, we propose Drive-JEPA, a framework that integrates Video Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (V-JEPA) with multimodal trajectory distillation for end-to-end driving. First, we adapt V-JEPA for end-to-end driving, pretraining a ViT encoder on large-scale driving videos to produce predictive representations aligned with trajectory planning. Second, we introduce a proposal-centric planner that distills diverse simulator-generated trajectories alongside human trajectories, with a momentum-aware selection mechanism to promote stable and safe behavior. When evaluated on NAVSIM, the V-JEPA representation combined with a simple transformer-based decoder outperforms prior methods by 3 PDMS in the perception-free setting. The complete Drive-JEPA framework achieves 93.3 PDMS on v1 and 87.8 EPDMS on v2, setting a new state-of-the-art.

AI.tldr: Drive-JEPA fuses Video Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture with multimodal trajectory distillation for end-to-end driving, achieving state-of-the-art on NAVSIM.
AI.motivation: Overcome limited gains from self-supervised video pretraining for scene understanding in driving and address the inherent single-trajectory ambiguity by learning multimodal planning representations.
AI.method: Adapt V-JEPA to end-to-end driving by pretraining a ViT encoder on large-scale driving videos to produce predictive representations aligned with trajectory planning; introduce a proposal-centric planner that distills diverse simulator-generated trajectories alongside human trajectories with a momentum-aware selection mechanism for stability and safety.
AI.result: On NAVSIM, the V-JEPA representation with a transformer-based decoder outperforms prior methods by 3 PDMS in the perception-free setting. Drive-JEPA achieves 93.3 PDMS on v1 and 87.8 EPDMS on v2, setting a new state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: Drive-JEPA demonstrates that combining self-supervised predictive video representations with multimodal trajectory distillation yields strong end-to-end driving performance, achieving state-of-the-art on NAVSIM.

title: DropoutTS: Sample-Adaptive Dropout for Robust Time Series Forecasting
summary: Deep time series models are vulnerable to noisy data ubiquitous in real-world applications. Existing robustness strategies either prune data or rely on costly prior quantification, failing to balance effectiveness and efficiency. In this paper, we introduce DropoutTS, a model-agnostic plugin that shifts the paradigm from "what" to learn to "how much" to learn. DropoutTS employs a Sample-Adaptive Dropout mechanism: leveraging spectral sparsity to efficiently quantify instance-level noise via reconstruction residuals, it dynamically calibrates model learning capacity by mapping noise to adaptive dropout rates - selectively suppressing spurious fluctuations while preserving fine-grained fidelity. Extensive experiments across diverse noise regimes and open benchmarks show DropoutTS consistently boosts superior backbones' performance, delivering advanced robustness with negligible parameter overhead and no architectural modifications. Our code is available at https://github.com/CityMind-Lab/DropoutTS.

AI.tldr: DropoutTS is a model-agnostic plugin for robust time-series learning. It uses a Sample-Adaptive Dropout driven by spectral sparsity to quantify instance-level noise via reconstruction residuals and adaptively adjust dropout rates, suppressing noise while preserving signal with negligible overhead and no architecture changes.
AI.motivation: Deep time series models are highly susceptible to real-world noisy data. Existing robustness methods either prune data or require expensive prior quantification, which hurts efficiency and universality.
AI.method: Introduce Sample-Adaptive Dropout (DropoutTS). It leverages spectral sparsity to estimate instance-level noise through reconstruction residuals and maps this noise to adaptive dropout rates to selectively suppress noisy fluctuations while preserving fine-grained information. The approach is model-agnostic and does not require architectural modifications.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse noise regimes and open benchmarks show DropoutTS consistently improves performance of strong backbones, delivering robust accuracy with negligible parameter overhead.
AI.conclusion: DropoutTS provides superior robustness for time-series models with minimal overhead and no architectural changes; code is released at the provided GitHub URL.

title: Expected Improvement via Gradient Norms
summary: Bayesian Optimization (BO) is a principled approach for optimizing expensive black-box functions, with Expected Improvement (EI) being one of the most widely used acquisition functions. Despite its empirical success, EI is known to be overly exploitative and can converge to suboptimal stationary points. We propose Expected Improvement via Gradient Norms (EI-GN), a novel acquisition function that applies the improvement principle to a gradient-aware auxiliary objective, thereby promoting sampling in regions that are both high-performing and approaching first-order stationarity. EI-GN relies on gradient observations used to learn gradient-enhanced surrogate models that enable principled gradient inference from function evaluations. We derive a tractable closed-form expression for EI-GN that allows efficient optimization and show that the proposed acquisition is consistent with the improvement-based acquisition framework. Empirical evaluations on standard BO benchmarks demonstrate that EI-GN yields consistent improvements against standard baselines. We further demonstrate applicability of EI-GN to control policy learning problems.

AI.tldr: Introduces EI-GN, a gradient-aware acquisition for Bayesian Optimization that applies the improvement principle to a gradient-based auxiliary objective, leveraging gradient-enhanced surrogates to guide sampling toward high-performing regions near first-order stationarity; derives a closed-form for efficient optimization and shows empirical gains on benchmarks and in control policy learning.
AI.motivation: Standard Expected Improvement is often overly exploitative and can converge to suboptimal stationary points. Incorporating gradient information aims to promote exploration toward regions near stationarity and improve convergence on expensive black-box functions.
AI.method: Define an auxiliary objective based on gradient norm; apply the EI improvement principle to this gradient-aware objective. Use gradient observations to train gradient-enhanced surrogates, enabling principled gradient inference. Derive a tractable closed-form EI-GN expression that fits the improvement-based framework.
AI.result: Empirical results on standard BO benchmarks show consistent improvements over baselines; the method also demonstrates applicability to control policy learning tasks.
AI.conclusion: EI-GN provides a principled, tractable, gradient-informed acquisition that mitigates excessive exploitation of EI and improves optimization performance; suitable for expensive black-box problems, with potential applicability to other gradient-aware settings.

title: Epistemic Context Learning: Building Trust the Right Way in LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems
comment: Codes and data are available at https://github.com/skyriver-2000/epistemic-context-learning
summary: Individual agents in multi-agent (MA) systems often lack robustness, tending to blindly conform to misleading peers. We show this weakness stems from both sycophancy and inadequate ability to evaluate peer reliability. To address this, we first formalize the learning problem of history-aware reference, introducing the historical interactions of peers as additional input, so that agents can estimate peer reliability and learn from trustworthy peers when uncertain. This shifts the task from evaluating peer reasoning quality to estimating peer reliability based on interaction history. We then develop Epistemic Context Learning (ECL): a reasoning framework that conditions predictions on explicitly-built peer profiles from history. We further optimize ECL by reinforcement learning using auxiliary rewards. Our experiments reveal that our ECL enables small models like Qwen 3-4B to outperform a history-agnostic baseline 8x its size (Qwen 3-30B) by accurately identifying reliable peers. ECL also boosts frontier models to near-perfect (100%) performance. We show that ECL generalizes well to various MA configurations and we find that trust is modeled well by LLMs, revealing a strong correlation in trust modeling accuracy and final answer quality.

AI.tldr: Epistemic Context Learning (ECL) uses history-based peer profiles to estimate reliability and weigh peer input, improving robustness in multi-agent systems. Small models outperform larger baselines, and frontier models reach near-perfect performance through RL with auxiliary rewards.
AI.motivation: Robustness issues in multi-agent systems arise when agents blindly conform to misleading peers. Estimating peer reliability from history can reduce sycophancy and improve decision quality.
AI.method: Formalize history-aware reference; construct peer profiles from historical interactions; develop Epistemic Context Learning that conditions predictions on these profiles; train with reinforcement learning using auxiliary rewards to optimize reliability-based weighting.
AI.result: Experiments show small models (Qwen 3-4B) exceed a history-agnostic baseline 8x larger (Qwen 3-30B). ECL improves frontier models to near-perfect performance; generalizes across MA configurations; trust modeling accuracy correlates with final answer quality.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for interaction history to estimate peer reliability enables more robust, scalable MA reasoning. ECL provides a practical framework, with consistent empirical gains and a link between trust estimation and answer quality.

title: Urban Neural Surface Reconstruction from Constrained Sparse Aerial Imagery with 3D SAR Fusion
summary: Neural surface reconstruction (NSR) has recently shown strong potential for urban 3D reconstruction from multi-view aerial imagery. However, existing NSR methods often suffer from geometric ambiguity and instability, particularly under sparse-view conditions. This issue is critical in large-scale urban remote sensing, where aerial image acquisition is limited by flight paths, terrain, and cost. To address this challenge, we present the first urban NSR framework that fuses 3D synthetic aperture radar (SAR) point clouds with aerial imagery for high-fidelity reconstruction under constrained, sparse-view settings. 3D SAR can efficiently capture large-scale geometry even from a single side-looking flight path, providing robust priors that complement photometric cues from images. Our framework integrates radar-derived spatial constraints into an SDF-based NSR backbone, guiding structure-aware ray selection and adaptive sampling for stable and efficient optimization. We also construct the first benchmark dataset with co-registered 3D SAR point clouds and aerial imagery, facilitating systematic evaluation of cross-modal 3D reconstruction. Extensive experiments show that incorporating 3D SAR markedly enhances reconstruction accuracy, completeness, and robustness compared with single-modality baselines under highly sparse and oblique-view conditions, highlighting a viable route toward scalable high-fidelity urban reconstruction with advanced airborne and spaceborne optical-SAR sensing.

AI.tldr: Fuse 3D SAR point clouds with aerial imagery to guide neural surface reconstruction (NSR) of urban scenes under sparse views, using an SDF backbone with radar-informed constraints; introduces a cross-modal benchmark.
AI.motivation: Geometric ambiguity and instability in NSR under sparse-view conditions for large-scale urban remote sensing; 3D SAR captures large-scale geometry from limited flight paths; cross-modal fusion with imagery aims to improve accuracy, completeness, and robustness.
AI.method: Integrates radar-derived spatial constraints into an SDF-based NSR backbone; implements structure-aware ray selection and adaptive sampling during optimization; builds co-registered 3D SAR points with aerial imagery; first urban NSR framework with SAR fusion and a cross-modal dataset.
AI.result: Notable improvements in reconstruction accuracy, completeness, and robustness over single-modality baselines under sparse and oblique-view settings; dataset enables systematic evaluation of cross-modal 3D reconstruction; demonstrates viability of optical-SAR sensing for scalable urban NSR.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal fusion of 3D SAR priors with aerial NSR is a viable path to high-fidelity, scalable urban reconstruction under constrained data; the framework and benchmark provide a foundation for future research and practical deployment.

title: Language-based Trial and Error Falls Behind in the Era of Experience
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in language-based agentic tasks, their applicability to unseen, nonlinguistic environments (e.g., symbolic or spatial tasks) remains limited. Previous work attributes this performance gap to the mismatch between the pretraining distribution and the testing distribution. In this work, we demonstrate the primary bottleneck is the prohibitive cost of exploration: mastering these tasks requires extensive trial-and-error, which is computationally unsustainable for parameter-heavy LLMs operating in a high dimensional semantic space. To address this, we propose SCOUT (Sub-Scale Collaboration On Unseen Tasks), a novel framework that decouples exploration from exploitation. We employ lightweight "scouts" (e.g., small MLPs) to probe environmental dynamics at a speed and scale far exceeding LLMs. The collected trajectories are utilized to bootstrap the LLM via Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), followed by multi-turn Reinforcement Learning (RL) to activate its latent world knowledge. Empirically, SCOUT enables a Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct model to achieve an average score of 0.86, significantly outperforming proprietary models, including Gemini-2.5-Pro (0.60), while saving about 60% GPU hours consumption.

AI.tldr: SCOUT decouples exploration from exploitation by using lightweight scouts to cheaply probe nonlinguistic environments, bootstrapping an LLM via supervised fine-tuning and subsequent RL. This reduces exploration cost and improves unseen-task performance, achieving 0.86 average score (Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct) versus 0.60 for Gemini-2.5-Pro while saving ~60% GPU hours.
AI.motivation: The main bottleneck for LLMs on unseen, nonlinguistic tasks is the expensive exploration required to learn in high-dimensional spaces. A distribution shift between pretraining and testing remains, but exploration cost is primary.
AI.method: Introduce scouts (e.g., small MLPs) to rapidly probe environmental dynamics and generate trajectories. Use these trajectories to perform supervised fine-tuning (SFT) of the LLM, followed by multi-turn reinforcement learning (RL) to leverage latent world knowledge.
AI.result: Empirically, SCOUT enables Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct to achieve an average score of 0.86, outperforming Gemini-2.5-Pro (0.60) and reducing GPU hours by ~60%.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling exploration from exploitation via lightweight scouts to bootstrap LLMs is effective for unseen, nonlinguistic tasks, enabling better performance with substantial computational savings and broader applicability.

title: Perceptrons and localization of attention's mean-field landscape
summary: The forward pass of a Transformer can be seen as an interacting particle system on the unit sphere: time plays the role of layers, particles that of token embeddings, and the unit sphere idealizes layer normalization. In some weight settings the system can even be seen as a gradient flow for an explicit energy, and one can make sense of the infinite context length (mean-field) limit thanks to Wasserstein gradient flows. In this paper we study the effect of the perceptron block in this setting, and show that critical points are generically atomic and localized on subsets of the sphere.

AI.tldr: Transformer forward pass as a particle system on the unit sphere; in a mean-field gradient-flow regime, the perceptron block leads to critical points that are generically atomic and localized on subsets of the sphere.
AI.motivation: Understand the optimization landscape and representation structure of Transformers under a mean-field, Wasserstein gradient-flow framework, and assess how the perceptron block influences stationary states.
AI.method: Model tokens as particles on the unit sphere with layer normalization as the sphere constraint; identify dynamics as a Wasserstein gradient flow of an explicit energy for certain weights; analyze the infinite-context (mean-field) limit; analyze the perceptron block to characterize its critical points.
AI.result: Establishes a well-defined mean-field limit and shows that critical points of the perceptron block are generically atomic and supported on subsets of the sphere (i.e., localized).
AI.conclusion: Suggests emergent sparsity and discrete attractors in Transformer representations under the mean-field regime, with implications for interpretability and network design.

title: PLANING: A Loosely Coupled Triangle-Gaussian Framework for Streaming 3D Reconstruction
summary: Streaming reconstruction from monocular image sequences remains challenging, as existing methods typically favor either high-quality rendering or accurate geometry, but rarely both. We present PLANING, an efficient on-the-fly reconstruction framework built on a hybrid representation that loosely couples explicit geometric primitives with neural Gaussians, enabling geometry and appearance to be modeled in a decoupled manner. This decoupling supports an online initialization and optimization strategy that separates geometry and appearance updates, yielding stable streaming reconstruction with substantially reduced structural redundancy. PLANING improves dense mesh Chamfer-L2 by 18.52% over PGSR, surpasses ARTDECO by 1.31 dB PSNR, and reconstructs ScanNetV2 scenes in under 100 seconds, over 5x faster than 2D Gaussian Splatting, while matching the quality of offline per-scene optimization. Beyond reconstruction quality, the structural clarity and computational efficiency of \modelname~make it well suited for a broad range of downstream applications, such as enabling large-scale scene modeling and simulation-ready environments for embodied AI. Project page: https://city-super.github.io/PLANING/ .

AI.tldr: PLANING introduces a hybrid explicit-implicit representation for streaming monocular reconstruction that decouples geometry and appearance, enabling online initialization/optimization and faster, stable reconstructions with improved mesh quality and PSNR.
AI.motivation: Streaming monocular 3D reconstruction struggles to achieve both high-quality rendering and accurate geometry with existing methods; a decoupled representation could reduce redundancy and enable online, efficient updates.
AI.method: A hybrid representation combining explicit geometric primitives with neural Gaussians, coupled with online initialization and optimization that separately updates geometry and appearance to achieve stable streaming reconstruction with reduced redundancy.
AI.result: PLANING improves dense mesh Chamfer-L2 by 18.52% over PGSR, surpasses ARTDECO by 1.31 dB PSNR, reconstructs ScanNetV2 scenes in under 100 seconds (over 5x faster than 2D Gaussian Splatting), and matches offline per-scene optimization quality.
AI.conclusion: The decoupled geometry-appearance framework offers both reconstruction quality and computational efficiency, enabling scalable scene modeling and simulation-ready environments for embodied AI; demonstrated via quantitative gains and fast online performance.

title: Zero-Shot Statistical Downscaling via Diffusion Posterior Sampling
summary: Conventional supervised climate downscaling struggles to generalize to Global Climate Models (GCMs) due to the lack of paired training data and inherent domain gaps relative to reanalysis. Meanwhile, current zero-shot methods suffer from physical inconsistencies and vanishing gradient issues under large scaling factors. We propose Zero-Shot Statistical Downscaling (ZSSD), a zero-shot framework that performs statistical downscaling without paired data during training. ZSSD leverages a Physics-Consistent Climate Prior learned from reanalysis data, conditioned on geophysical boundaries and temporal information to enforce physical validity. Furthermore, to enable robust inference across varying GCMs, we introduce Unified Coordinate Guidance. This strategy addresses the vanishing gradient problem in vanilla DPS and ensures consistency with large-scale fields. Results show that ZSSD significantly outperforms existing zero-shot baselines in 99th percentile errors and successfully reconstructs complex weather events, such as tropical cyclones, across heterogeneous GCMs.

AI.tldr: Proposes Zero-Shot Statistical Downscaling (ZSSD): a zero-data-learning framework using a physics-consistent climate prior and unified coordinate guidance to enable robust downscaling across heterogeneous GCMs, improving high-percentile errors and reconstructing events like tropical cyclones.
AI.motivation: Conventional supervised downscaling requires paired data and struggles with GCM domain gaps; zero-shot methods suffer from physical inconsistencies and gradient issues at large scaling.
AI.method: Learn a Physics-Consistent Climate Pri​or from reanalysis data, conditioned on geophysical boundaries and time; apply Unified Coordinate Guidance to stabilize training and ensure consistency with large-scale fields for inference across GCMs.
AI.result: ZSSD outperforms zero-shot baselines on 99th percentile errors and reconstructs complex weather events (e.g., tropical cyclones) across diverse GCMs.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of physics-informed zero-shot statistical downscaling across heterogeneous climate models; addresses data scarcity and domain shift while maintaining physical validity.

title: Rethinking Federated Graph Foundation Models: A Graph-Language Alignment-based Approach
summary:
Recent studies of federated graph foundational models (FedGFMs) break the idealized and untenable assumption of having centralized data storage to train graph foundation models, and accommodate the reality of distributed, privacy-restricted data silos. Despite their simplicity and intuition, existing studies that project aligned generalizable knowledge onto a discrete token space via vector-quantized backbones suffer from irreversible knowledge loss during the quantization process. In this context, we argue that reconciling the semantic-structural orthogonality and integrity between pre-trained language models (PLMs) and graph neural networks (GNNs) is paramount for developing effective FedGFMs while simultaneously mitigating the severe data heterogeneity and communication constraints inherent in distributed, resource-limited environments.
  To address these issues, we propose FedGALA (Federated Graph And Language Alignment), a framework that resolves graph-based semantic-structural orthogonality and integrity in federated settings by employing unsupervised contrastive learning to align GNNs and frozen PLMs within a continuous embedding space, thereby capturing robust, transferable general knowledge. Subsequently, FedGALA leverages a communication-efficient prompt tuning mechanism to steer these pre-aligned encoders and frozen PLMs, facilitating effective adaptation to diverse downstream tasks while circumventing the prohibitive overhead of full-parameter fine-tuning. The comprehensive experiments validate that FedGALA outperforms all competitive baselines across multi-domain datasets on multiple tasks with up to 14.37% performance improvement.

AI.tldr: FedGALA aligns GNNs with frozen PLMs in a federated setting using unsupervised contrastive learning in a shared embedding space, followed by prompt-tuned, communication-efficient adaptation, to improve multi-domain graph tasks without full-parameter fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Federated graph foundation models operate under distributed, privacy-restricted data silos with no centralized storage. Quantization-based backbones incur irreversible knowledge loss and fail to preserve semantic-structural integrity between PLMs and GNNs. There is a need to reconcile semantic-structural orthogonality and robustness under data heterogeneity and limited communication.
AI.method: FedGALA uses unsupervised contrastive learning to align GNN encoders with frozen PLMs in a continuous embedding space, addressing semantic-structural orthogonality. It then employs a communication-efficient prompt-tuning mechanism to adapt to downstream tasks without full-parameter fine-tuning, leveraging pre-aligned encoders and frozen PLMs.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows FedGALA outperforms competitive baselines across multi-domain datasets and multiple tasks, with up to 14.37% performance improvement.
AI.conclusion: FedGALA effectively reconciles semantic-structural alignment between PLMs and GNNs in federated settings, yielding robust, transferable graph knowledge while reducing communication and computation via prompt tuning.

title: MetricAnything: Scaling Metric Depth Pretraining with Noisy Heterogeneous Sources
summary: Scaling has powered recent advances in vision foundation models, yet extending this paradigm to metric depth estimation remains challenging due to heterogeneous sensor noise, camera-dependent biases, and metric ambiguity in noisy cross-source 3D data. We introduce Metric Anything, a simple and scalable pretraining framework that learns metric depth from noisy, diverse 3D sources without manually engineered prompts, camera-specific modeling, or task-specific architectures. Central to our approach is the Sparse Metric Prompt, created by randomly masking depth maps, which serves as a universal interface that decouples spatial reasoning from sensor and camera biases. Using about 20M image-depth pairs spanning reconstructed, captured, and rendered 3D data across 10000 camera models, we demonstrate-for the first time-a clear scaling trend in the metric depth track. The pretrained model excels at prompt-driven tasks such as depth completion, super-resolution and Radar-camera fusion, while its distilled prompt-free student achieves state-of-the-art results on monocular depth estimation, camera intrinsics recovery, single/multi-view metric 3D reconstruction, and VLA planning. We also show that using pretrained ViT of Metric Anything as a visual encoder significantly boosts Multimodal Large Language Model capabilities in spatial intelligence. These results show that metric depth estimation can benefit from the same scaling laws that drive modern foundation models, establishing a new path toward scalable and efficient real-world metric perception. We open-source MetricAnything at http://metric-anything.github.io/metric-anything-io/ to support community research.

AI.tldr: Metric Anything: a scalable pretraining framework for metric depth from noisy, diverse 3D sources using Sparse Metric Prompt, showing scaling trends and state-of-the-art results on monocular depth estimation and related tasks; open-sourced.
AI.motivation: Tackle challenges in scaling metric depth estimation caused by heterogeneous sensor noise, camera biases, and metric ambiguity in cross-source 3D data by leveraging large, diverse datasets and prompts-free design.
AI.method: Pretrain a universal metric-depth model (Metric Anything) on ~20M image-depth pairs from reconstructed, captured, and rendered data across ~10k camera models. Use Sparse Metric Prompt—randomly masked depth maps—as a universal interface to separate spatial reasoning from sensor/camera biases. No manual prompts, no camera-specific modeling, or task-specific architectures. Includes a pretrained prompt-enabled model and a distilled prompt-free student, with ViT visual encoder to boost multimodal LLM spatial capabilities.
AI.result: Shows a clear scaling trend in metric depth estimation. The pretrained model excels on prompt-driven tasks (depth completion, super-resolution, Radar-camera fusion). The distilled student achieves state-of-the-art in monocular depth estimation, camera intrinsics recovery, single/multi-view metric 3D reconstruction, and VLA planning. Using the pretrained ViT as encoder improves multimodal LLM spatial reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Metric depth estimation can benefit from foundation-model-like scaling laws. The approach enables scalable, real-world metric perception and provides open-source resources to the community.

title: Abstract Concept Modelling in Conceptual Spaces: A Study on Chess Strategies
summary: We present a conceptual space framework for modelling abstract concepts that unfold over time, demonstrated through a chess-based proof-of-concept. Strategy concepts, such as attack or sacrifice, are represented as geometric regions across interpretable quality dimensions, with chess games instantiated and analysed as trajectories whose directional movement toward regions enables recognition of intended strategies. This approach also supports dual-perspective modelling, capturing how players interpret identical situations differently. Our implementation demonstrates the feasibility of trajectory-based concept recognition, with movement patterns aligning with expert commentary. This work explores extending the conceptual spaces theory to temporally realised, goal-directed concepts. The approach establishes a foundation for broader applications involving sequential decision-making and supports integration with knowledge evolution mechanisms for learning and refining abstract concepts over time.

AI.tldr: Temporal conceptual spaces for abstract concepts demonstrated via chess trajectories; trajectory-based recognition; dual-perspective modelling; foundation for sequential decision-making.
AI.motivation: Extend conceptual spaces to temporally realized, goal-directed concepts; capture dynamic, interpretable concept regions and learning over time.
AI.method: Represent strategies (attack, sacrifice) as geometric regions in quality dimensions; instantiate games as trajectories; analyze movement toward regions; support dual-perspective interpretations; align with expert commentary.
AI.result: Feasible trajectory-based concept recognition; movement patterns align with expert commentary; dual-perspective modelling validated; trajectories can capture temporal unfolding.
AI.conclusion: Foundational step for broader sequential decision-making applications; enables learning and refinement of abstract concepts over time and integration with knowledge evolution mechanisms.

title: DA-SPS: A Dual-stage Network based on Singular Spectrum Analysis, Patching-strategy and Spearman-correlation for Multivariate Time-series Prediction
comment: 12 pages, 7 figures, 6 tables, submitted to IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
summary: Multivariate time-series forecasting, as a typical problem in the field of time series prediction, has a wide range of applications in weather forecasting, traffic flow prediction, and other scenarios. However, existing works do not effectively consider the impact of extraneous variables on the prediction of the target variable. On the other hand, they fail to fully extract complex sequence information based on various time patterns of the sequences. To address these drawbacks, we propose a DA-SPS model, which adopts different modules for feature extraction based on the information characteristics of different variables. DA-SPS mainly consists of two stages: the target variable processing stage (TVPS) and the extraneous variables processing stage (EVPS). In TVPS, the model first uses Singular Spectrum Analysis (SSA) to process the target variable sequence and then uses Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and P-Conv-LSTM which deploys a patching strategy to extract features from trend and seasonality components, respectively. In EVPS, the model filters extraneous variables that have a strong correlation with the target variate by using Spearman correlation analysis and further analyses them using the L-Attention module which consists of LSTM and attention mechanism. Finally, the results obtained by TVPS and EVPS are combined through weighted summation and linear mapping to produce the final prediction. The results on four public datasets demonstrate that the DA-SPS model outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, its performance in real-world scenarios is further validated using a private dataset collected by ourselves, which contains the test items' information on laptop motherboards.

AI.tldr: A two-stage DA-SPS model for multivariate time-series forecasting that separates target and extraneous variables, uses SSA/LSTM-based modules to capture trend/seasonality, filters correlated extraneous variables with Spearman analysis, and fuses outputs; shows state-of-the-art results on four public datasets and a private laptop motherboard dataset.
AI.motivation: Extraneous variables can degrade forecast accuracy and limit the extraction of complex sequence information. A variable-aware architecture is needed to exploit different information characteristics of target vs. extraneous variables.
AI.method: Two-stage design: TVPS processes the target variable with SSA, then LSTM and P-Conv-LSTM (with a patching strategy) to capture trend and seasonality components. EVPS filters extraneous variables via Spearman correlation with the target, then analyzes them with an L-Attention module (LSTM + attention). Outputs from TVPS and EVPS are combined via weighted summation and a linear mapping to produce the final forecast.
AI.result: DA-SPS outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on four public datasets. Real-world validation is provided on a private dataset containing laptop motherboard test-item information.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-stage, variable-aware architecture effectively leverages target-pattern and extraneous-variable information for improved forecasting, with demonstrated generalization to real-world data.

title: Unsupervised Decomposition and Recombination with Discriminator-Driven Diffusion Models
summary: Decomposing complex data into factorized representations can reveal reusable components and enable synthesizing new samples via component recombination. We investigate this in the context of diffusion-based models that learn factorized latent spaces without factor-level supervision. In images, factors can capture background, illumination, and object attributes; in robotic videos, they can capture reusable motion components. To improve both latent factor discovery and quality of compositional generation, we introduce an adversarial training signal via a discriminator trained to distinguish between single-source samples and those generated by recombining factors across sources. By optimizing the generator to fool this discriminator, we encourage physical and semantic consistency in the resulting recombinations. Our method outperforms implementations of prior baselines on CelebA-HQ, Virtual KITTI, CLEVR, and Falcor3D, achieving lower FID scores and better disentanglement as measured by MIG and MCC. Furthermore, we demonstrate a novel application to robotic video trajectories: by recombining learned action components, we generate diverse sequences that significantly increase state-space coverage for exploration on the LIBERO benchmark.

AI.tldr: An adversarially regularized, factorized latent diffusion framework learns reusable latent components without supervision, enabling high-quality, semantically consistent recombinations and improved disentanglement. It outperforms baselines on multiple datasets and extends to robotic trajectories to boost exploration.
AI.motivation: Decompose complex data into reusable, recombinable latent factors to enable controllable synthesis. Without factor-level supervision, factor discovery can be unstable and recombinations may be incoherent. The work adds an adversarial signal to enforce consistency and plausibility of recombined samples, improving both discovery and generation quality.
AI.method: Train a diffusion-based generator with an adversarial discriminator that distinguishes single-source samples from recombined (cross-source) samples. The generator learns to produce recombinations that fool the discriminator, enforcing physical and semantic consistency in factor-level recombinations. Evaluation spans visual domains (CelebA-HQ, Virtual KITTI, CLEVR, Falcor3D) and robotic trajectories (LIBERO), using FID for quality and MIG/MCC for disentanglement; additionally demonstrates diversity in action components for exploration.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves lower FID and higher disentanglement scores (MIG, MCC) than prior baselines across CelebA-HQ, Virtual KITTI, CLEVR, and Falcor3D. It also yields diverse, improved robotic trajectories by recombining learned action components, increasing state-space coverage on the LIBERO benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Adversarially guided recombination fosters semantic/physical consistency in factorized latent spaces, improving both unsupervised factor discovery and the quality of compositional generation. The approach generalizes across vision and motion domains and enables practical benefits for exploration in robotics.

title: BioAgent Bench: An AI Agent Evaluation Suite for Bioinformatics
summary: This paper introduces BioAgent Bench, a benchmark dataset and an evaluation suite designed for measuring the performance and robustness of AI agents in common bioinformatics tasks. The benchmark contains curated end-to-end tasks (e.g., RNA-seq, variant calling, metagenomics) with prompts that specify concrete output artifacts to support automated assessment, including stress testing under controlled perturbations. We evaluate frontier closed-source and open-weight models across multiple agent harnesses, and use an LLM-based grader to score pipeline progress and outcome validity. We find that frontier agents can complete multi-step bioinformatics pipelines without elaborate custom scaffolding, often producing the requested final artifacts reliably. However, robustness tests reveal failure modes under controlled perturbations (corrupted inputs, decoy files, and prompt bloat), indicating that correct high-level pipeline construction does not guarantee reliable step-level reasoning. Finally, because bioinformatics workflows may involve sensitive patient data, proprietary references, or unpublished IP, closed-source models can be unsuitable under strict privacy constraints; in such settings, open-weight models may be preferable despite lower completion rates. We release the dataset and evaluation suite publicly.

AI.tldr: Introduces BioAgent Bench, a benchmark for evaluating AI agents on end-to-end bioinformatics tasks, with stress testing and LLM-based grading; compares frontier closed-source and open-weight models; finds capable pipeline construction but notable robustness failures; discusses privacy implications and releases dataset publicly.
AI.motivation: There is a need for standardized, automated evaluation of AI agents in bioinformatics pipelines to measure not only end results but also stepwise reasoning, robustness to perturbations, and privacy constraints.
AI.method: Constructs BioAgent Bench with curated end-to-end tasks (RNA-seq, variant calling, metagenomics) using concrete prompts for output artifacts; evaluates multiple frontier models across various agent harnesses; uses an LLM-based grader to assess pipeline progress and artifact validity; subjects models to controlled perturbations (corrupted inputs, decoy files, prompt bloat) to test robustness.
AI.result: Frontier agents can complete multi-step pipelines without extensive scaffolding and often produce the requested final artifacts; however, robustness tests reveal failure modes under perturbations, indicating that correct high-level pipeline construction does not guarantee reliable step-level reasoning; in privacy-sensitive contexts, closed-source models may be unsuitable, making open-weight models preferable despite lower completion rates.
AI.conclusion: The dataset and evaluation suite are released publicly, enabling standardized benchmarking and further study of robustness, privacy considerations, and the trade-offs between closed-source and open-weight models in bioinformatics AI agents.

title: Sim-MSTNet: sim2real based Multi-task SpatioTemporal Network Traffic Forecasting
summary: Network traffic forecasting plays a crucial role in intelligent network operations, but existing techniques often perform poorly when faced with limited data. Additionally, multi-task learning methods struggle with task imbalance and negative transfer, especially when modeling various service types.
