Although recent process-aware approaches attempt to mitigate this via static preference learning or heuristic reward shaping, they often lack the on-policy exploration capabilities required to decouple step-level credit from global outcomes. To address these challenges, we propose ProRAG, a process-supervised reinforcement learning framework designed to integrate learned step-level supervision into the online optimization loop. Our framework consists of four stages: (1) Supervised Policy Warmup to initialize the model with a structured reasoning format; (2) construction of an MCTS-based Process Reward Model (PRM) to quantify intermediate reasoning quality; (3) PRM-Guided Reasoning Refinement to align the policy with fine-grained process preferences; and (4) Process-Supervised Reinforcement Learning with a dual-granularity advantage mechanism. By aggregating step-level process rewards with global outcome signals, ProRAG provides precise feedback for every action. Extensive experiments on five multi-hop reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that ProRAG achieves superior overall performance compared to strong outcome-based and process-aware RL baselines, particularly on complex long-horizon tasks, validating the effectiveness of fine-grained process supervision. The code and model are available at https://github.com/lilinwz/ProRAG.

AI.tldr: ProRAG introduces process-supervised reinforcement learning to address reward sparsity and process hallucinations in Retrieval-Augmented Generation for long-horizon reasoning, using a four-stage pipeline (supervised warmup, MCTS-based PRM, PRM-guided refinement, and process-supervised RL) to provide fine-grained step-level feedback and improve performance on multi-hop tasks.
AI.motivation: Outcome-based RL suffers from sparse rewards and weak credit assignment, leading to process hallucinations where the model's reasoning is flawed even when final answers are correct. Existing process-aware methods lack on-policy step-level exploration to disentangle credit, necessitating a method that couples fine-grained supervision with online optimization.
AI.method: A four-stage framework: (1) Supervised Policy Warmup to bootstrap reasoning structure; (2) build an MCTS-based Process Reward Model (PRM) to quantify intermediate reasoning quality; (3) PRM-Guided Reasoning Refinement to align policy with process-level preferences; (4) Process-Supervised RL with a dual-granularity advantage mechanism that aggregates step-level process rewards with global outcomes during online learning.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five multi-hop reasoning benchmarks shows ProRAG outperforms strong outcome-based and process-aware RL baselines, especially on complex long-horizon tasks, demonstrating the value of fine-grained process supervision.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained, process-level supervision integrated into an online RL loop improves credit assignment and reduces process hallucinations in RAG tasks; the approach is effective on challenging long-horizon reasoning problems, with code available.

title: SAGE: Sequence-level Adaptive Gradient Evolution for Generative Recommendation
comment: arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2506.19235
summary: While works such as OneRec have validated the scaling laws of Large Language Models (LLMs) in recommender systems, they rely on a cumbersome separate vocabulary. This dependency prevents the model architecture from reusing native LLM vocabularies, resulting in high maintenance costs and poor scalability. In response, we aim to efficiently reuse open-source LLM architectures without constructing a separate tokenization vocabulary. Furthermore, we identify that the optimization strategy of OneRec Gradient Bounded Policy Optimization (GBPO),suffers from a "Symmetric Conservatism" problem: its static gradient boundaries structurally suppress the update momentum required for cold-start items and fail to prevent diversity collapse in high-noise environments.To address this issue, we propose SAGE (Sequence-level Adaptive Gradient Evolution), a unified optimization framework tailored for list-wise generative recommendation. SAGE introduces two key innovations:(1) Sequence-level Signal Decoupling: By combining a geometric mean importance ratio with decoupled multi-objective advantages, we eliminate token-level variance and resolve the "Reward Collapse" problem. (2) Asymmetric Adaptive Dynamics: We construct a dynamic gradient manifold that applies a "Boost Factor" to high-potential cold start items to achieve super-linear updates and employs an "Entropy Aware Penalty" to break information cocoons. Theoretical analysis and empirical results demonstrate that SAGE effectively unblocks cold-start traffic and sustains recommendation diversity, all while retaining the numerical stability of GBPO.

AI.tldr: SAGE: Sequence-level Adaptive Gradient Evolution for list-wise generative recommendation that reuses open-source LLM vocab, addressing OneRec GBPO symmetry conservatism by sequence-level signal decoupling and asymmetric adaptive dynamics, yielding improved cold-start and diversity with stable optimization.
AI.motivation: Overcome dependency on a separate tokenization vocabulary that hinders reuse of native LLM vocabularies in scaling LLM-based recommenders; address GBPO's Symmetric Conservatism that suppresses update momentum and causes diversity collapse.
AI.method: Introduce two innovations: (1) Sequence-level Signal Decoupling: use geometric mean importance ratio with decoupled multi-objective advantages to remove token-level variance and avoid Reward Collapse. (2) Asymmetric Adaptive Dynamics: dynamic gradient manifold with a Boost Factor for high-potential cold-start items enabling super-linear updates and an Entropy Aware Penalty to prevent information cocoons; theoretical and empirical validation.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis and empirical experiments demonstrate SAGE unblocks cold-start traffic, preserves recommendation diversity, and maintains GBPO's numerical stability.
AI.conclusion: SAGE provides a unified optimization framework enabling efficient reuse of open-source LLM architectures for list-wise generative recommendations, mitigating vocabulary maintenance costs and addressing gradient bias without sacrificing stability or diversity.

title: One-step Latent-free Image Generation with Pixel Mean Flows
summary: Modern diffusion/flow-based models for image generation typically exhibit two core characteristics: (i) using multi-step sampling, and (ii) operating in a latent space. Recent advances have made encouraging progress on each aspect individually, paving the way toward one-step diffusion/flow without latents. In this work, we take a further step towards this goal and propose "pixel MeanFlow" (pMF). Our core guideline is to formulate the network output space and the loss space separately. The network target is designed to be on a presumed low-dimensional image manifold (i.e., x-prediction), while the loss is defined via MeanFlow in the velocity space. We introduce a simple transformation between the image manifold and the average velocity field. In experiments, pMF achieves strong results for one-step latent-free generation on ImageNet at 256x256 resolution (2.22 FID) and 512x512 resolution (2.48 FID), filling a key missing piece in this regime. We hope that our study will further advance the boundaries of diffusion/flow-based generative models.

AI.tldr: Proposes pixel MeanFlow (pMF): one-step, latent-free diffusion/flow via predicting images on a low-dimensional manifold while training with a velocity-space MeanFlow loss, achieving ImageNet results at 256×256 and 512×512.
AI.motivation: Move toward one-step diffusion/flow without latent variables by decoupling the network output space from the loss space, enabling efficient, direct image generation.
AI.method: Predict images on a presumed low-dimensional manifold (x-prediction) and define the loss in velocity space using MeanFlow; introduce a transformation between the image manifold and the mean velocity field to couple the two spaces.
AI.result: pMF attains competitive one-step latent-free generation on ImageNet, achieving 2.22 FID at 256×256 and 2.48 FID at 512×512, indicating strong performance in the one-step latent-free regime.
AI.conclusion: The approach advances diffusion/flow-based models toward true one-step, latent-free generation, filling a key gap and warranting further investigation into its generality and efficiency.

title: JADE: Bridging the Strategic-Operational Gap in Dynamic Agentic RAG
summary: The evolution of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has shifted from static retrieval pipelines to dynamic, agentic workflows where a central planner orchestrates multi-turn reasoning. However, existing paradigms face a critical dichotomy: they either optimize modules jointly within rigid, fixed-graph architectures, or empower dynamic planning while treating executors as frozen, black-box tools. We identify that this \textit{decoupled optimization} creates a ``strategic-operational mismatch,'' where sophisticated planning strategies fail to materialize due to unadapted local executors, often leading to negative performance gains despite increased system complexity. In this paper, we propose \textbf{JADE} (\textbf{J}oint \textbf{A}gentic \textbf{D}ynamic \textbf{E}xecution), a unified framework for the joint optimization of planning and execution within dynamic, multi-turn workflows. By modeling the system as a cooperative multi-agent team unified under a single shared backbone, JADE enables end-to-end learning driven by outcome-based rewards. This approach facilitates \textit{co-adaptation}: the planner learns to operate within the capability boundaries of the executors, while the executors evolve to align with high-level strategic intent. Empirical results demonstrate that JADE transforms disjoint modules into a synergistic system, yielding remarkable performance improvements via joint optimization and enabling a flexible balance between efficiency and effectiveness through dynamic workflow orchestration.

AI.tldr: JADE is a unified framework for joint optimization of planning and execution in dynamic, multi-turn RAG workflows, addressing the strategic-operational mismatch by training planners and executors as a cooperative multi-agent team under a shared backbone, enabling end-to-end learning and co-adaptation.
AI.motivation: Address the decoupled optimization problem where advanced planning fails to realize benefits because executors are fixed and unadaptive, leading to inefficiencies or negative gains despite added complexity.
AI.method: Model the system as a cooperative multi-agent team with a single shared backbone; jointly optimize planning and execution through end-to-end learning driven by outcome-based rewards; enable co-adaptation between the planner and executors.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate substantial performance improvements over baselines that separate planning and execution, demonstrating improved synergy and a tunable balance between efficiency and effectiveness via dynamic workflow orchestration.
AI.conclusion: JADE enables end-to-end joint optimization of planning and execution in dynamic RAG workflows, transforming modular components into a synergistic system capable of adapting plan and execution to each other.

title: HER: Human-like Reasoning and Reinforcement Learning for LLM Role-playing
summary: LLM role-playing, i.e., using LLMs to simulate specific personas, has emerged as a key capability in various applications, such as companionship, content creation, and digital games. While current models effectively capture character tones and knowledge, simulating the inner thoughts behind their behaviors remains a challenge. Towards cognitive simulation in LLM role-play, previous efforts mainly suffer from two deficiencies: data with high-quality reasoning traces, and reliable reward signals aligned with human preferences. In this paper, we propose HER, a unified framework for cognitive-level persona simulation. HER introduces dual-layer thinking, which distinguishes characters' first-person thinking from LLMs' third-person thinking. To bridge these gaps, we curate reasoning-augmented role-playing data via reverse engineering and construct human-aligned principles and reward models. Leveraging these resources, we train \method models based on Qwen3-32B via supervised and reinforcement learning. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of our approach. Notably, our models significantly outperform the Qwen3-32B baseline, achieving a 30.26 improvement on the CoSER benchmark and a 14.97 gain on the Minimax Role-Play Bench. Our datasets, principles, and models will be released to facilitate future research.

AI.tldr: HER is a unified cognitive-level persona simulation framework for LLM role-play that separates first-person thinking from the model's third-person reasoning, using reasoning-augmented data, principles, and reward models; it improves over a strong baseline on two benchmarks and will release resources.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of inner-thought modeling in LLM role-play due to insufficient high-quality reasoning traces and reliable reward signals aligned with human preferences.
AI.method: Dual-layer thinking; reverse-engineered reasoning-augmented role-playing data; human-aligned principles and reward models; supervised learning and reinforcement learning to train models (based on Qwen3-32B).
AI.result: Significant performance gains: +30.26 on CoSER; +14.97 on Minimax Role-Play Bench, outperforming Qwen3-32B baseline.
AI.conclusion: Datasets, principles, and models will be released to facilitate future research; HER demonstrates effectiveness in cognitive-level persona simulation.

title: Self-Compression of Chain-of-Thought via Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: The inference overhead induced by redundant reasoning undermines the interactive experience and severely bottlenecks the deployment of Large Reasoning Models. Existing reinforcement learning (RL)-based solutions tackle this problem by coupling a length penalty with outcome-based rewards. This simplistic reward weighting struggles to reconcile brevity with accuracy, as enforcing brevity may compromise critical reasoning logic. In this work, we address this limitation by proposing a multi-agent RL framework that selectively penalizes redundant chunks, while preserving essential reasoning logic. Our framework, Self-Compression via MARL (SCMA), instantiates redundancy detection and evaluation through two specialized agents: \textbf{a Segmentation Agent} for decomposing the reasoning process into logical chunks, and \textbf{a Scoring Agent} for quantifying the significance of each chunk. The Segmentation and Scoring agents collaboratively define an importance-weighted length penalty during training, incentivizing \textbf{a Reasoning Agent} to prioritize essential logic without introducing inference overhead during deployment. Empirical evaluations across model scales demonstrate that SCMA reduces response length by 11.1\% to 39.0\% while boosting accuracy by 4.33\% to 10.02\%. Furthermore, ablation studies and qualitative analysis validate that the synergistic optimization within the MARL framework fosters emergent behaviors, yielding more powerful LRMs compared to vanilla RL paradigms.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent RL framework (SCMA) uses a Segmentation Agent and a Scoring Agent to identify and downweight redundant reasoning chunks, guiding the Reasoning Agent to maintain essential logic while reducing inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Inference overhead from redundant reasoning bottlenecks interactive experience; naive length penalties in RL trade off brevity against accuracy. A selective, chunk-level approach is needed to preserve critical reasoning.
AI.method: SCMA introduces two specialized agents: a Segmentation Agent that decomposes reasoning into logical chunks, and a Scoring Agent that evaluates the importance of each chunk. Together they define an importance-weighted length penalty during training for the Reasoning Agent, encouraging concise yet accurate reasoning without adding overhead at deployment.
AI.result: Across model scales, SCMA reduces response length by 11.1%–39.0% and increases accuracy by 4.33%–10.02%. Ablation and qualitative analyses show emergent behaviors and superior performance compared to vanilla RL.
AI.conclusion: A MARL-based collaboration that selectively compresses reasoning chunks achieves more efficient and accurate large reasoning models than standard RL with uniform length penalties.

title: L$^3$: Large Lookup Layers
summary: Modern sparse language models typically achieve sparsity through Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layers, which dynamically route tokens to dense MLP "experts." However, dynamic hard routing has a number of drawbacks, such as potentially poor hardware efficiency and needing auxiliary losses for stable training. In contrast, the tokenizer embedding table, which is natively sparse, largely avoids these issues by selecting a single embedding per token at the cost of not having contextual information. In this work, we introduce the Large Lookup Layer (L$^3$), which unlocks a new axis of sparsity by generalizing embedding tables to model decoder layers. L$^3$ layers use static token-based routing to aggregate a set of learned embeddings per token in a context-dependent way, allowing the model to efficiently balance memory and compute by caching information in embeddings. L$^3$ has two main components: (1) a systems-friendly architecture that allows for fast training and CPU-offloaded inference with no overhead, and (2) an information-theoretic embedding allocation algorithm that effectively balances speed and quality. We empirically test L$^3$ by training transformers with up to 2.6B active parameters and find that L$^3$ strongly outperforms both dense models and iso-sparse MoEs in both language modeling and downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: L3 generalizes embedding tables into decoder layers using static token-based routing to create a sparse, cache-friendly Large Lookup Layer that outperforms dense and iso-sparse MoE baselines in language modeling and downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Dense MoE routing can be hardware-inefficient and require auxiliary losses for stable training; embedding lookup is sparse and efficient but lacks context. L3 seeks to combine sparsity with contextual embedding via a new lookup-based approach.
AI.method: Introduce Large Lookup Layer (L3) with (1) a systems-friendly architecture enabling fast training and CPU-offloaded inference with no overhead, and (2) an information-theoretic embedding allocation algorithm. L3 uses static token-based routing to aggregate multiple learned embeddings per token in a context-dependent manner, enabling efficient memory–compute tradeoffs by caching information in embeddings.
AI.result: Training transformers with up to 2.6B active parameters, L3 strongly outperforms both dense models and iso-sparse MoEs on language modeling and downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: L3 offers a scalable, efficient sparse alternative to MoEs, achieving hardware-friendly performance and strong empirical gains across tasks.

title: AgenticSimLaw: A Juvenile Courtroom Multi-Agent Debate Simulation for Explainable High-Stakes Tabular Decision Making
summary: We introduce AgenticSimLaw, a role-structured, multi-agent debate framework that provides transparent and controllable test-time reasoning for high-stakes tabular decision-making tasks. Unlike black-box approaches, our courtroom-style orchestration explicitly defines agent roles (prosecutor, defense, judge), interaction protocols (7-turn structured debate), and private reasoning strategies, creating a fully auditable decision-making process. We benchmark this framework on young adult recidivism prediction using the NLSY97 dataset, comparing it against traditional chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting across almost 90 unique combinations of models and strategies. Our results demonstrate that structured multi-agent debate provides more stable and generalizable performance compared to single-agent reasoning, with stronger correlation between accuracy and F1-score metrics. Beyond performance improvements, AgenticSimLaw offers fine-grained control over reasoning steps, generates complete interaction transcripts for explainability, and enables systematic profiling of agent behaviors. While we instantiate this framework in the criminal justice domain to stress-test reasoning under ethical complexity, the approach generalizes to any deliberative, high-stakes decision task requiring transparency and human oversight. This work addresses key LLM-based multi-agent system challenges: organization through structured roles, observability through logged interactions, and responsibility through explicit non-deployment constraints for sensitive domains. Data, results, and code will be available on github.com under the MIT license.

AI.tldr: AgenticSimLaw introduces a role-based, multi-agent debate framework with explicit interaction protocols to provide transparent, auditable reasoning for high-stakes tabular decisions. It outperforms traditional chain-of-thought prompting on youth recidivism prediction (NLSY97) across ~90 model/strategy configurations, offering explainability through transcripts and fine-grained control, and generalizes to other deliberative tasks.
AI.motivation: Tackle opacity and unreliability of single-agent LLM reasoning in high-stakes domains by enabling structured roles, observable interactions, and responsible constraints, ensuring transparency, accountability, and human oversight.
AI.method: Define courtroom-like roles (prosecutor, defense, judge) and a 7-turn debate protocol with private reasoning strategies. Benchmark against chain-of-thought prompting across ~90 model/strategy combinations on the NLSY97 dataset for recidivism prediction. Evaluate performance (accuracy, F1), stability, generalizability, correlation between metrics, and produce interaction transcripts for explainability. Release code under MIT.
AI.result: Structured multi-agent debate yields more stable and generalizable performance than single-agent reasoning, with stronger correlation between accuracy and F1. Provides full interaction transcripts for explainability, fine-grained control over reasoning steps, and systematic agent profiling. Demonstrates potential in ethical, high-stakes reasoning and generalizes beyond criminal justice; data, results, and code will be on GitHub under MIT license.
AI.conclusion: AgenticSimLaw offers transparent, auditable decision-making for high-stakes tasks via organized roles and logged interactions. It improves performance stability, explainability, and governance, and is applicable to any deliberative domain requiring human oversight. The approach addresses organization, observability, and responsibility in LLM-based multi-agent systems.

title: Partial Feedback Online Learning
summary: We study partial-feedback online learning, where each instance admits a set of correct labels, but the learner only observes one correct label per round; any prediction within the correct set is counted as correct. This model captures settings such as language generation, where multiple responses may be valid but data provide only a single reference. We give a near-complete characterization of minimax regret for both deterministic and randomized learners in the set-realizable regime, i.e., in the regime where sublinear regret is generally attainable. For deterministic learners, we introduce the Partial-Feedback Littlestone dimension (PFLdim) and show it precisely governs learnability and minimax regret; technically, PFLdim cannot be defined via the standard version space, requiring a new collection version space viewpoint and an auxiliary dimension used only in the proof. We further develop the Partial-Feedback Measure Shattering dimension (PMSdim) to obtain tight bounds for randomized learners. We identify broad conditions ensuring inseparability between deterministic and randomized learnability (e.g., finite Helly number or nested-inclusion label structure), and extend the argument to set-valued online learning, resolving an open question of Raman et al. [2024b]. Finally, we show a sharp separation from weaker realistic and agnostic variants: outside set realizability, the problem can become information-theoretically intractable, with linear regret possible even for $|H|=2$. This highlights the need for fundamentally new, noise-sensitive complexity measures to meaningfully characterize learnability beyond set realizability.

AI.tldr: Near-complete minimax regret characterization for partial-feedback online learning in the set-realizable regime; introduces Partial-Feedback Littlestone dimension (PFLdim) for deterministic learners and Partial-Feedback Measure Shattering dimension (PMSdim) for randomized learners; shows separations between deterministic and randomized learnability under broad conditions; extends to set-valued learning; reveals breakdown of learnability outside realizability with potential linear regret even for |H|=2; implies need for new noise-sensitive complexity measures.
AI.motivation: Partial feedback with multiple valid labels arises in tasks like language generation; standard realizability-based theory is insufficient; understanding fundamental limits (minimax regret) and when deterministic vs randomized strategies differ; addresses open questions in set-valued online learning.
AI.method: Define PFLdim via a collection version space and an auxiliary dimension; define PMSdim; derive tight minimax regret bounds for deterministic and randomized learners; prove separations under conditions (finite Helly number, nested-inclusion label structures); extend results to set-valued setting; provide hardness outside realizability; connect to Raman et al. (2024b) open questions.
AI.result: Precise characterization: PFLdim governs deterministic learnability; PMSdim governs randomized case; show inseparability between deterministic and randomized learnability under broad conditions; extend to set-valued learning; outside set realizability, regret can be linear even for |H|=2; resolves open questions; introduces new dimension-based measures for noise-sensitive analysis.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a dimension-based theory for partial-feedback online learning, revealing sharp contrasts with standard realizability, and highlighting the need for new noise-sensitive complexity measures beyond existing set-based frameworks; opens avenues for set-valued online learning and practical implications for language-generation-like tasks.

title: Retrieval-Infused Reasoning Sandbox: A Benchmark for Decoupling Retrieval and Reasoning Capabilities
summary: Despite strong performance on existing benchmarks, it remains unclear whether large language models can reason over genuinely novel scientific information. Most evaluations score end-to-end RAG pipelines, where reasoning is confounded with retrieval and toolchain choices, and the signal is further contaminated by parametric memorization and open-web volatility. We introduce DeR2, a controlled deep-research sandbox that isolates document-grounded reasoning while preserving core difficulties of deep search: multi-step synthesis, denoising, and evidence-based conclusion making. DeR2 decouples evidence access from reasoning via four regimes--Instruction-only, Concepts (gold concepts without documents), Related-only (only relevant documents), and Full-set (relevant documents plus topically related distractors)--yielding interpretable regime gaps that operationalize retrieval loss vs. reasoning loss and enable fine-grained error attribution. To prevent parametric leakage, we apply a two-phase validation that requires parametric failure without evidence while ensuring oracle-concept solvability. To ensure reproducibility, each instance provides a frozen document library (drawn from 2023-2025 theoretical papers) with expert-annotated concepts and validated rationales. Experiments across a diverse set of state-of-the-art foundation models reveal substantial variation and significant headroom: some models exhibit mode-switch fragility, performing worse with the Full-set than with Instruction-only, while others show structural concept misuse, correctly naming concepts but failing to execute them as procedures.

AI.tldr: Introduces DeR2, a controlled sandbox to isolate document-grounded reasoning from retrieval; uses four regimes (Instruction-only, Concepts, Related-only, Full-set); enforces two-phase validation and reproducible datasets; reveals model fragilities and substantial headroom across state-of-the-art LLMs.
AI.motivation: To assess true reasoning over novel scientific information by decoupling evidence access from reasoning, avoiding confounds from end-to-end RAG pipelines, memorization, and open-web volatility.
AI.method: Build DeR2 with four regimes controlling access to evidence: Instruction-only, Concepts (gold concepts without docs), Related-only (relevant docs), Full-set (docs plus distractors). Employ a two-phase validation to prevent parametric leakage and ensure solvability under expert concepts. Use a frozen document library (2023-2025 theoretical papers) with expert-annotated concepts and validated rationales. Evaluate diverse foundation models on multi-step synthesis, denoising, and evidence-based conclusion making, with interpretable error attribution.
AI.result: Significant variation in model behavior and notable headroom. Some models exhibit mode-switch fragility (performing worse with Full-set than Instruction-only). Others demonstrate structural concept misuse, naming concepts correctly but failing to execute them as procedures.
AI.conclusion: The DeR2 framework enables diagnostic, fine-grained attribution of retrieval vs. reasoning errors and reveals substantial room for improvement in document-grounded reasoning among state-of-the-art LLMs. It suggests future work should strengthen procedural execution of concepts and robustness to evidence configurations.

title: A block-coordinate descent framework for non-convex composite optimization. Application to sparse precision matrix estimation
summary: Block-coordinate descent (BCD) is the method of choice to solve numerous large scale optimization problems, however their theoretical study for non-convex optimization, has received less attention. In this paper, we present a new block-coordinate descent (BCD) framework to tackle non-convex composite optimization problems, ensuring decrease of the objective function and convergence to a solution. This framework is general enough to include variable metric proximal gradient updates, proximal Newton updates, and alternated minimization updates. This generality allows to encompass three versions of the most used solvers in the sparse precision matrix estimation problem, deemed Graphical Lasso: graphical ISTA, Primal GLasso, and QUIC. We demonstrate the value of this new framework on non-convex sparse precision matrix estimation problems, providing convergence guarantees and up to a $100$-fold reduction in the number of iterations required to reach state-of-the-art estimation quality.

AI.tldr: A general non-convex block-coordinate descent framework for composite optimization that unifies Graphical Lasso solvers and achieves faster convergence with guaranteed descent.
AI.motivation: To address the limited theoretical study of block-coordinate descent in non-convex optimization and to provide convergent algorithms for large-scale, non-convex composite problems, particularly in sparse precision matrix estimation.
AI.method: A general BCD framework that ensures objective decrease and convergence, supporting variable-metric proximal gradient updates, proximal Newton updates, and alternating minimization. It encompasses three Graphical Lasso solvers: graphical ISTA, Primal GLasso, and QUIC.
AI.result: Convergence guarantees are established, with reported empirical improvements of up to 100× fewer iterations to reach state-of-the-art estimation quality.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework is broad and effective for non-convex sparse precision matrix estimation, offering convergence guarantees and substantial acceleration for common GLasso solvers.

title: ToolWeaver: Weaving Collaborative Semantics for Scalable Tool Use in Large Language Models
summary: Prevalent retrieval-based tool-use pipelines struggle with a dual semantic challenge: their retrievers often employ encoders that fail to capture complex semantics, while the Large Language Model (LLM) itself lacks intrinsic tool knowledge from its natural language pretraining. Generative methods offer a powerful alternative by unifying selection and execution, tasking the LLM to directly learn and generate tool identifiers. However, the common practice of mapping each tool to a unique new token introduces substantial limitations: it creates a scalability and generalization crisis, as the vocabulary size explodes and each tool is assigned a semantically isolated token. This approach also creates a semantic bottleneck that hinders the learning of collaborative tool relationships, as the model must infer them from sparse co-occurrences of monolithic tool IDs within a vast library. To address these limitations, we propose ToolWeaver, a novel generative tool learning framework that encodes tools into hierarchical sequences. This approach makes vocabulary expansion logarithmic to the number of tools. Crucially, it enables the model to learn collaborative patterns from the dense co-occurrence of shared codes, rather than the sparse co-occurrence of monolithic tool IDs. We generate these structured codes through a novel tokenization process designed to weave together a tool's intrinsic semantics with its extrinsic co-usage patterns. These structured codes are then integrated into the LLM through a generative alignment stage, where the model is fine-tuned to produce the hierarchical code sequences. Evaluation results with nearly 47,000 tools show that ToolWeaver significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, establishing a more scalable, generalizable, and semantically-aware foundation for advanced tool-augmented agents.

AI.tldr: ToolWeaver introduces hierarchical tool encodings that scale logarithmically with the tool set, enabling dense learning of tool relationships and improved tool-augmented LLM performance.
AI.motivation: Traditional retrieval-based pipelines either rely on weak encoders for semantic understanding or on per-tool unique tokens that cause scalability, generalization, and semantic bottlenecks. A single-token-per-tool scheme limits vocabulary growth and hinders learning tool interactions.
AI.method: Develop a hierarchical tokenization scheme that encodes each tool as a sequence of codes combining intrinsic semantics with extrinsic co-usage patterns. Train an LLM via a generative alignment stage to produce these hierarchical code sequences, yielding logarithmic vocabulary expansion and richer tool relationships.
AI.result: On ~47,000 tools, ToolWeaver significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating improved scalability, generalization, and semantic awareness for tool-augmented agents.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical code-based tool representations enable scalable, semantically rich tool usage in LLMs, providing a robust foundation for advanced tool-augmented reasoning and action.

title: PPI-SVRG: Unifying Prediction-Powered Inference and Variance Reduction for Semi-Supervised Optimization
summary: We study semi-supervised stochastic optimization when labeled data is scarce but predictions from pre-trained models are available. PPI and SVRG both reduce variance through control variates -- PPI uses predictions, SVRG uses reference gradients. We show they are mathematically equivalent and develop PPI-SVRG, which combines both. Our convergence bound decomposes into the standard SVRG rate plus an error floor from prediction uncertainty. The rate depends only on loss geometry; predictions affect only the neighborhood size. When predictions are perfect, we recover SVRG exactly. When predictions degrade, convergence remains stable but reaches a larger neighborhood. Experiments confirm the theory: PPI-SVRG reduces MSE by 43--52\% under label scarcity on mean estimation benchmarks and improves test accuracy by 2.7--2.9 percentage points on MNIST with only 10\% labeled data.

AI.tldr: Equates PPI and SVRG for variance-reduced semi-supervised stochastic optimization; introduces PPI-SVRG combining both variance-reduction ideas; derives a convergence bound with an error floor from prediction uncertainty; perfect predictions recover SVRG; experiments show substantial gains under label scarcity (MSE reductions 43-52%; MNIST accuracy +2.7-2.9pp with 10% labels).
AI.motivation: Address semi-supervised stochastic optimization when labeled data is scarce by leveraging predictions from pre-trained models to reduce variance.
AI.method: Prove mathematical equivalence between Prediction-Adjusted Importance (PPI) and SVRG; develop PPI-SVRG that blends both variance-reduction techniques; derive a convergence bound decomposing into the standard SVRG rate plus an error floor dependent on prediction uncertainty; show rate depends on loss geometry while predictions affect the neighborhood size.
AI.result: Convergence bound showing the rate plus an error floor; perfect predictions recover SVRG; stable convergence with degraded predictions to a larger neighborhood; empirical gains: 43–52% MSE reduction on mean-estimation benchmarks and 2.7–2.9 percentage points test accuracy improvement on MNIST with 10% labels.
AI.conclusion: PPI-SVRG is a robust variance-reduction framework that leverages imperfect pre-trained predictions; retains SVRG properties under perfect predictions and degrades gracefully as prediction quality declines; supported by theory and experiments.

title: How do Visual Attributes Influence Web Agents? A Comprehensive Evaluation of User Interface Design Factors
summary: Web agents have demonstrated strong performance on a wide range of web-based tasks. However, existing research on the effect of environmental variation has mostly focused on robustness to adversarial attacks, with less attention to agents' preferences in benign scenarios. Although early studies have examined how textual attributes influence agent behavior, a systematic understanding of how visual attributes shape agent decision-making remains limited. To address this, we introduce VAF, a controlled evaluation pipeline for quantifying how webpage Visual Attribute Factors influence web-agent decision-making. Specifically, VAF consists of three stages: (i) variant generation, which ensures the variants share identical semantics as the original item while only differ in visual attributes; (ii) browsing interaction, where agents navigate the page via scrolling and clicking the interested item, mirroring how human users browse online; (iii) validating through both click action and reasoning from agents, which we use the Target Click Rate and Target Mention Rate to jointly evaluate the effect of visual attributes. By quantitatively measuring the decision-making difference between the original and variant, we identify which visual attributes influence agents' behavior most. Extensive experiments, across 8 variant families (48 variants total), 5 real-world websites (including shopping, travel, and news browsing), and 4 representative web agents, show that background color contrast, item size, position, and card clarity have a strong influence on agents' actions, whereas font styling, text color, and item image clarity exhibit minor effects.

AI.tldr: A pipeline (VAF) quantifies how visual webpage attributes influence web-agent decision-making, using semantic-preserving visual variants, browsing actions, and joint metrics (Target Click Rate, Target Mention Rate). Experiments across 8 variant families (48 variants), 5 sites, 4 agents show background contrast, size, position, and card clarity are influential; font styling, text color, and image clarity have minor effects.
AI.motivation: Current work mostly studies robustness to adversarial or textual attributes; there is limited systematic understanding of how visual attributes shape benign web-agent behavior. A principled evaluation framework is needed.
AI.method: Proposes VAF with three stages: variant generation preserving semantics while altering visual attributes; browsing interaction that mimics human page exploration; validation via Target Click Rate and Target Mention Rate to measure influence. Conducts large-scale experiments across multiple variant families, sites, and agents.
AI.result: Identifies which visual attributes impact agent decisions, with background color contrast, item size, position, and card clarity having strong effects; font styling, text color, and item image clarity showing weaker effects; demonstrates consistency across domains and agents.
AI.conclusion: Visual attributes significantly shape web-agent decision-making; design and evaluation of agents should account for visual factor sensitivity; future work should broaden attribute sets, tasks, and agent types, and explore robustness and generalization.

title: Best Arm Identification with LLM Judges and Limited Human
summary: We study fixed-confidence best-arm identification (BAI) where a cheap but potentially biased proxy (e.g., LLM judge) is available for every sample, while an expensive ground-truth label can only be acquired selectively when using a human for auditing. Unlike classical multi-fidelity BAI, the proxy is biased (arm- and context-dependent) and ground truth is selectively observed. Consequently, standard multi-fidelity methods can mis-select the best arm, and uniform auditing, though accurate, wastes scarce resources and is inefficient. We prove that without bias correction and propensity adjustment, mis-selection probability may not vanish (even with unlimited proxy data). We then develop an estimator for the mean of each arm that combines proxy scores with inverse-propensity-weighted residuals and form anytime-valid confidence sequences for that estimator. Based on the estimator and confidence sequence, we propose an algorithm that adaptively selects and audits arms. The algorithm concentrates audits on unreliable contexts and close arms and we prove that a plug-in Neyman rule achieves near-oracle audit efficiency. Numerical experiments confirm the theoretical guarantees and demonstrate the superior empirical performance of the proposed algorithm.

AI.tldr: Proposes bias-corrected fixed-confidence BAI with biased, context-dependent proxies and selective ground-truth auditing; introduces inverse-propensity-weighted estimator, anytime-valid confidence sequences, and an adaptive auditing algorithm achieving near-oracle efficiency.
AI.motivation: In many practical BAI settings, cheap proxies (e.g., LLM judgments) are biased and not always observed; classical multi-fidelity BAI assumes unbiased proxies and full feedback, leading to mis-selections and inefficient audits. The work aims to correct bias, account for selective auditing, and improve resource efficiency.
AI.method: Develop an inverse-propensity-weighted estimator that combines proxy scores with residuals, construct anytime-valid confidence sequences for the arm means, and design an adaptive algorithm that allocates audits to unreliable contexts and close arms using a plug-in Neyman rule to approach oracle efficiency.
AI.result: The authors show that mis-selection probability may not vanish without bias correction even with unlimited proxy data. The estimator and confidence sequences provide valid inference in this setting. Empirical results demonstrate near-oracle audit efficiency and superior performance over baselines.
AI.conclusion: Bias correction and propensity-adjusted inference are essential for accurate fixed-confidence BAI with biased proxies and selective auditing. The proposed estimator, confidence sequences, and adaptive auditing strategy yield efficient, reliable arm identification with practical benefits and strong theoretical guarantees.

title: The Energy Impact of Domain Model Design in Classical Planning
comment: 2026 IEEE/ACM 5th International Conference on AI Engineering - Software Engineering for AI (CAIN '26)
summary: AI research has traditionally prioritised algorithmic performance, such as optimising accuracy in machine learning or runtime in automated planning. The emerging paradigm of Green AI challenges this by recognising energy consumption as a critical performance dimension. Despite the high computational demands of automated planning, its energy efficiency has received little attention. This gap is particularly salient given the modular planning structure, in which domain models are specified independently of algorithms. On the other hand, this separation also enables systematic analysis of energy usage through domain model design. We empirically investigate how domain model characteristics affect the energy consumption of classical planners. We introduce a domain model configuration framework that enables controlled variation of features, such as element ordering, action arity, and dead-end states. Using five benchmark domains and five state-of-the-art planners, we analyse energy and runtime impacts across 32 domain variants per benchmark. Results demonstrate that domain-level modifications produce measurable energy differences across planners, with energy consumption not always correlating with runtime.

AI.tldr: Domain-model design choices significantly impact energy use in classical planners, with energy and runtime not always aligned; a configurable framework enables systematic energy analysis across domains.
AI.motivation: Green AI highlights energy as a core performance metric; in automated planning, the separation of domain models from algorithms enables controlled study of how encoding decisions affect energy consumption.
AI.method: Introduce a domain-model configuration framework to vary features (element ordering, action arity, dead-end states) and empirically evaluate energy and runtime. Use five benchmark domains, five state-of-the-art planners, and 32 variants per domain, totaling diverse configurations.
AI.result: Domain-level modifications produce measurable energy differences across planners; energy consumption does not always correlate with runtime, and effects vary by planner and domain feature.
AI.conclusion: Domain encoding choices materially affect energy efficiency in planning; energy-aware domain design and evaluation are essential for Green AI in planning.

title: ETS: Energy-Guided Test-Time Scaling for Training-Free RL Alignment
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) post-training alignment for language models is effective, but also costly and unstable in practice, owing to its complicated training process. To address this, we propose a training-free inference method to sample directly from the optimal RL policy. The transition probability applied to Masked Language Modeling (MLM) consists of a reference policy model and an energy term. Based on this, our algorithm, Energy-Guided Test-Time Scaling (ETS), estimates the key energy term via online Monte Carlo, with a provable convergence rate. Moreover, to ensure practical efficiency, ETS leverages modern acceleration frameworks alongside tailored importance sampling estimators, substantially reducing inference latency while provably preserving sampling quality. Experiments on MLM (including autoregressive models and diffusion language models) across reasoning, coding, and science benchmarks show that our ETS consistently improves generation quality, validating its effectiveness and design.

AI.tldr: A training-free inference approach for RL-aligned language models that samples from the optimal policy using Energy-Guided Test-Time Scaling (ETS), achieving fast, high-quality generation with provable convergence.
AI.motivation: RL-based post-training alignment of LMs is effective but costly and unstable; there is a need for a training-free, scalable inference method to sample from the optimal RL policy without fine-tuning.
AI.method: ETS defines a transition probability as a combination of a reference policy and an energy term; the energy term is estimated online via Monte Carlo with variance reduction and tailored importance sampling; leverages modern acceleration frameworks to reduce latency; provides provable convergence rate.
AI.result: Empirical results on MLMs (autoregressive and diffusion models) across reasoning, coding, and science benchmarks show consistent generation quality improvements; inference latency is substantially reduced while preserving sampling quality; convergence guarantees.
AI.conclusion: ETS offers a practical, efficient framework for sampling from the RL-aligned policy at inference time, enabling high-quality generation without training and demonstrating broad applicability across MLM variants and domains.

title: Learning Decentralized LLM Collaboration with Multi-Agent Actor Critic
summary: Recent work has explored optimizing LLM collaboration through Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL). However, most MARL fine-tuning approaches rely on predefined execution protocols, which often require centralized execution. Decentralized LLM collaboration is more appealing in practice, as agents can run inference in parallel with flexible deployments. Also, current approaches use Monte Carlo methods for fine-tuning, which suffer from high variance and thus require more samples to train effectively. Actor-critic methods are prevalent in MARL for dealing with these issues, so we developed Multi-Agent Actor-Critic (MAAC) methods to optimize decentralized LLM collaboration. In this paper, we analyze when and why these MAAC methods are beneficial. We propose 2 MAAC approaches, \textbf{CoLLM-CC} with a \textbf{C}entralized \textbf{C}ritic and \textbf{CoLLM-DC} with \textbf{D}ecentralized \textbf{C}ritics. Our experiments across writing, coding, and game-playing domains show that Monte Carlo methods and CoLLM-DC can achieve performance comparable to CoLLM-CC in short-horizon and dense-reward settings. However, they both underperform CoLLM-CC on long-horizon or sparse-reward tasks, where Monte Carlo methods require substantially more samples and CoLLM-DC struggles to converge. Our code is available at https://github.com/OpenMLRL/CoMLRL/releases/tag/v1.3.2.

AI.tldr: MAAC-based approaches (CoLLM-CC and CoLLM-DC) for decentralized LLM collaboration; centralized vs decentralized critics; comparable to Monte Carlo in short-horizon dense tasks; central critic preferable for long-horizon sparse tasks; decentralized critic can converge poorly.
AI.motivation: Address high variance and centralization issues in MARL-based fine-tuning for LLM collaboration; enable decentralized, parallel inference without rigid execution protocols.
AI.method: Introduce two MAAC methods: CoLLM-CC with a centralized critic and CoLLM-DC with decentralized critics; evaluate on writing, coding, and game-playing tasks; compare to Monte Carlo-based fine-tuning; analyze performance across horizon length and reward density.
AI.result: CoLLM-CC and CoLLM-DC achieve performance comparable to Monte Carlo-based methods in short-horizon dense-reward settings; both underperform CoLLM-CC in long-horizon or sparse-reward tasks; Monte Carlo needs more samples; CoLLM-DC struggles to converge in such settings; code available.
AI.conclusion: MAAC approaches can benefit decentralized LLM collaboration, with centralized critics offering stronger performance in challenging tasks; decentralized critics provide decentralization but may have convergence issues; Monte Carlo remains effective in certain settings; practical deployment should consider task horizon and reward structure.

title: Task-Awareness Improves LLM Generations and Uncertainty
summary: In many applications of LLMs, natural language responses often have an underlying structure such as representing discrete labels, numerical values, or graphs. Yet, existing decoding and uncertainty estimation methods operate only in language space and largely disregard structural information. We address this by modeling LLM outputs directly in a task-dependent latent structure. By equipping this structure with a dissimilarity measure, we can compute Bayes-optimal responses. These are not selected from sampled generations but are newly synthesized by combining individual responses in the latent space. Across different tasks, Bayes-optimal responses consistently outperform standard decoding methods like beam search. Moreover, quantifying uncertainty via the induced Bayesian risk captures variations in terms of the latent structure and improves alignment with output quality and correctness. Our decision-theoretic framework is applicable to any problem that admits a latent response structure and enables reliable task-aware LLM predictions.

AI.tldr: Proposes a decision-theoretic framework that models LLM outputs in a task-specific latent structure with a dissimilarity metric to compute Bayes-optimal responses; outperforms beam search and provides uncertainty quantification aligned with quality.
AI.motivation: Language-model decoding often ignores non-language structural information (labels, numbers, graphs) underlying tasks. A latent-structure, task-aware approach can yield more accurate and reliable predictions.
AI.method: Define a task-dependent latent output structure with an associated dissimilarity measure. Compute Bayes-optimal responses by minimizing Bayesian risk in this latent space, synthesizing outputs directly in latent space rather than by sampling from plain language generations. Uncertainty is quantified via the induced Bayesian risk.
AI.result: Bayes-optimal latent-structure responses consistently outperform standard decoding methods such as beam search across diverse tasks. Uncertainty quantification via Bayesian risk captures variations in latent structure and improves alignment with output quality and correctness.
AI.conclusion: The framework is broadly applicable to any problem admitting a latent response structure and enables reliable, task-aware LLM predictions.

title: Mind the Gap: How Elicitation Protocols Shape the Stated-Revealed Preference Gap in Language Models
summary: Recent work identifies a stated-revealed (SvR) preference gap in language models (LMs): a mismatch between the values models endorse and the choices they make in context. Existing evaluations rely heavily on binary forced-choice prompting, which entangles genuine preferences with artifacts of the elicitation protocol. We systematically study how elicitation protocols affect SvR correlation across 24 LMs. Allowing neutrality and abstention during stated preference elicitation allows us to exclude weak signals, substantially improving Spearman's rank correlation ($ρ$) between volunteered stated preferences and forced-choice revealed preferences. However, further allowing abstention in revealed preferences drives $ρ$ to near-zero or negative values due to high neutrality rates. Finally, we find that system prompt steering using stated preferences during revealed preference elicitation does not reliably improve SvR correlation on AIRiskDilemmas. Together, our results show that SvR correlation is highly protocol-dependent and that preference elicitation requires methods that account for indeterminate preferences.

AI.tldr: SvR correlation in language models is highly protocol-dependent; elicitation choices (neutrality/abstention) can boost or suppress correlation, while system-prompt steering offers no reliable improvement.
AI.motivation: To understand how different elicitation protocols shape the detected alignment between stated and revealed preferences in LMs, separating genuine preferences from elicitation artifacts.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation across 24 LMs comparing elicitation protocols: (1) allowance of neutrality/abstention in stated-preference elicitation; (2) allowance of abstention in revealed preferences; (3) system-prompt steering during revealed-preference elicitation; measure Spearman's ρ between volunteered stated preferences and forced-choice revealed preferences.
AI.result: Allowing neutrality/abstention in stated elicitation substantially improves ρ by excluding weak signals; allowing abstention in revealed preferences drives ρ toward zero or negative due to high neutrality; system-prompt steering does not reliably improve ρ on AIRiskDilemmas.
AI.conclusion: SvR correlation is highly protocol-dependent; effective elicitation must account for indeterminate preferences, as current protocols can distort the relationship between stated and revealed preferences.

title: Cascaded Transfer: Learning Many Tasks under Budget Constraints
summary: Many-Task Learning refers to the setting where a large number of related tasks need to be learned, the exact relationships between tasks are not known. We introduce the Cascaded Transfer Learning, a novel many-task transfer learning paradigm where information (e.g. model parameters) cascades hierarchically through tasks that are learned by individual models of the same class, while respecting given budget constraints. The cascade is organized as a rooted tree that specifies the order in which tasks are learned and refined. We design a cascaded transfer mechanism deployed over a minimum spanning tree structure that connects the tasks according to a suitable distance measure, and allocates the available training budget along its branches. Experiments on synthetic and real many-task settings show that the resulting method enables more accurate and cost effective adaptation across large task collections compared to alternative approaches.

AI.tldr: Proposes Cascaded Transfer Learning for Many-Task Learning using a rooted tree cascade and MST-guided budget allocation to transfer information between tasks, achieving better accuracy and cost efficiency.
AI.motivation: In many-task scenarios, task relationships are unknown and budgets constrain training; a mechanism is needed to transfer knowledge efficiently across tasks in a hierarchical, budget-aware manner.
AI.method: Construct tasks as nodes in a minimum spanning tree based on a suitable distance; organize cascaded transfer along the rooted tree; learn tasks with individual models of the same class; allocate available training budget along branches; information (e.g., parameters) cascades from parent to child tasks to refine models.
AI.result: Empirical results on synthetic and real-world many-task settings show improved accuracy and cost effectiveness over baselines/alternative approaches.
AI.conclusion: A budget-aware cascaded transfer mechanism over an MST enables scalable, accurate, and efficient multi-task learning by structuring task relationships and transfer flow.

title: VERSA: Verified Event Data Format for Reliable Soccer Analytics
summary: Event stream data is a critical resource for fine-grained analysis across various domains, including financial transactions, system operations, and sports. In sports, it is actively used for fine-grained analyses such as quantifying player contributions and identifying tactical patterns. However, the reliability of these models is fundamentally limited by inherent data quality issues that cause logical inconsistencies (e.g., incorrect event ordering or missing events). To this end, this study proposes VERSA (Verified Event Data Format for Reliable Soccer Analytics), a systematic verification framework that ensures the integrity of event stream data within the soccer domain. VERSA is based on a state-transition model that defines valid event sequences, thereby enabling the automatic detection and correction of anomalous patterns within the event stream data. Notably, our examination of event data from the K League 1 (2024 season), provided by Bepro, detected that 18.81% of all recorded events exhibited logical inconsistencies. Addressing such integrity issues, our experiments demonstrate that VERSA significantly enhances cross-provider consistency, ensuring stable and unified data representation across heterogeneous sources. Furthermore, we demonstrate that data refined by VERSA significantly improves the robustness and performance of a downstream task called VAEP, which evaluates player contributions. These results highlight that the verification process is highly effective in increasing the reliability of data-driven analysis.

AI.tldr: VERSA introduces a verification framework for soccer event streams using a state-transition model to detect and correct logical inconsistencies, improving cross-provider data consistency and downstream VAEP performance.
AI.motivation: Event-stream data in sports analytics suffer from logical inconsistencies (ordering gaps, missing events) that degrade reliability; a systematic verification approach is needed to ensure data integrity across providers.
AI.method: Develop a state-transition model that defines valid event sequences, apply automatic detection and correction of anomalous patterns, and evaluate on Bepro-provided K League 1 2024 data (18.81% inconsistencies) to assess cross-provider consistency and VAEP performance.
AI.result: VERSA substantially improves cross-provider consistency and, when data are refined by VERSA, enhances the robustness and performance of the VAEP downstream task.
AI.conclusion: Verifying event data via a state-transition framework is effective at increasing the reliability of data-driven analyses in soccer analytics.

title: A Unified SPD Token Transformer Framework for EEG Classification: Systematic Comparison of Geometric Embeddings
summary: Spatial covariance matrices of EEG signals are Symmetric Positive Definite (SPD) and lie on a Riemannian manifold, yet the theoretical connection between embedding geometry and optimization dynamics remains unexplored. We provide a formal analysis linking embedding choice to gradient conditioning and numerical stability for SPD manifolds, establishing three theoretical results: (1) BWSPD's $\sqrtκ$ gradient conditioning (vs $κ$ for Log-Euclidean) via Daleckii-Kreĭn matrices provides better gradient conditioning on high-dimensional inputs ($d \geq 22$), with this advantage reducing on low-dimensional inputs ($d \leq 8$) where eigendecomposition overhead dominates; (2) Embedding-Space Batch Normalization (BN-Embed) approximates Riemannian normalization up to $O(\varepsilon^2)$ error, yielding $+26\%$ accuracy on 56-channel ERP data but negligible effect on 8-channel SSVEP data, matching the channel-count-dependent prediction; (3) bi-Lipschitz bounds prove BWSPD tokens preserve manifold distances with distortion governed solely by the condition ratio $κ$. We validate these predictions via a unified Transformer framework comparing BWSPD, Log-Euclidean, and Euclidean embeddings within identical architecture across 1,500+ runs on three EEG paradigms (motor imagery, ERP, SSVEP; 36 subjects). Our Log-Euclidean Transformer achieves state-of-the-art performance on all datasets, substantially outperforming classical Riemannian classifiers and recent SPD baselines, while BWSPD offers competitive accuracy with similar training time.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how different SPD-embedding schemes for covariance matrices on Riemannian manifolds affect optimization dynamics in neural nets, deriving gradient-conditioning, normalization, and distortion bounds, and validating them with a Transformer-based EEG evaluation. Log-Euclidean embedding achieves state-of-the-art results; BWSPD is competitive with similar training times.
AI.motivation: To understand how embedding geometry for SPD matrices influences gradient conditioning and numerical stability in neural networks, and to guide the choice of SPD embeddings for EEG classification tasks.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis using Daleckii–Kreĭn matrices to compare gradient conditioning of BWSPD (sqrt(κ)) vs Log-Euclidean (κ); derive bi-Lipschitz bounds showing distortion governed by condition number κ; introduce Embedding-Space Batch Normalization (BN-Embed) and quantify its Riemannian-normalization error as O(ε^2); empirical validation within a unified Transformer framework across 1,500+ runs on motor imagery, ERP, and SSVEP EEG paradigms (36 subjects) comparing BWSPD, Log-Euclidean, and Euclidean embeddings.
AI.result: (1) BWSPD yields sqrt(κ) gradient conditioning whereas Log-Euclidean yields κ; advantage is prominent at high input dimensionality (d ≥ 22) and reduced at low d (d ≤ 8) due to eigendecomposition overhead. (2) BN-Embed approximates Riemannian normalization within O(ε^2) error; yields +26% accuracy on 56-channel ERP data but negligible effect on 8-channel SSVEP data, consistent with channel-count predictions. (3) Bi-Lipschitz bounds show BWSPD tokens preserve manifold distances with distortion depending solely on κ. In experiments, a Log-Euclidean Transformer achieves state-of-the-art performance across all three EEG tasks, outperforming classical Riemannian classifiers and recent SPD baselines; BWSPD is competitive with similar training time.
AI.conclusion: Embedding choice is crucial for SPD-manifold optimization in neural networks. Log-Euclidean embeddings provide the strongest empirical performance across EEG tasks, with BWSPD offering competitive accuracy and favorable gradient behavior in higher dimensions. The results support integrating Riemannian-aware embeddings with standard architectures (e.g., Transformers) to leverage SPD geometry while maintaining training efficiency.

title: Liquid Interfaces: A Dynamic Ontology for the Interoperability of Autonomous Systems
summary: Contemporary software architectures struggle to support autonomous agents whose reasoning is adaptive, probabilistic, and context-dependent, while system integration remains dominated by static interfaces and deterministic contracts. This paper introduces Liquid Interfaces, a coordination paradigm in which interfaces are not persistent technical artifacts, but ephemeral relational events that emerge through intention articulation and semantic negotiation at runtime.We formalize this model and present the Liquid Interface Protocol (LIP),which governs intention-driven interaction, negotiated execution, and enforce ephemerality under semantic uncertainty. We further discuss the governance implications of this approach and describe a reference architecture that demonstrates practical feasibility. Liquid Interfaces provide a principled foundation for adaptive coordination in agent-based systems

AI.tldr: Proposes Liquid Interfaces and the Liquid Interface Protocol to enable ephemeral, intention-driven coordination among adaptive agents, with semantic negotiation and probabilistic reasoning, demonstrated via a reference architecture and governance considerations.
AI.motivation: Static interfaces and deterministic contracts hinder autonomous, context-sensitive reasoning; there is a need for ephemeral, semantically negotiated interfaces that can adapt under uncertainty.
AI.method: Formalizes the model, defines the Liquid Interface Protocol (LIP), detailing intention articulation, negotiated execution, and enforcement of ephemerality under semantic uncertainty; discusses governance and presents a reference architecture to show practical feasibility.
AI.result: Provides a formal model and protocol; demonstrates practical feasibility via a reference architecture; discusses governance implications; establishes a foundation for adaptive coordination in agent-based systems.
AI.conclusion: Liquid Interfaces provide a principled foundation for adaptive, context-aware coordination in agent-based systems, enabling tighter integration of probabilistic, autonomous reasoning with dynamic interfaces.

title: More Bang for the Buck: Improving the Inference of Large Language Models at a Fixed Budget using Reset and Discard (ReD)
summary: The performance of large language models (LLMs) on verifiable tasks is usually measured by pass@k, the probability of answering a question correctly at least once in k trials. At a fixed budget, a more suitable metric is coverage@cost, the average number of unique questions answered as a function of the total number of attempts. We connect the two metrics and show that the empirically-observed power-law behavior in pass@k leads to a sublinear growth of the coverage@cost (diminishing returns). To solve this problem, we propose Reset-and-Discard (ReD), a query method of LLMs that increases coverage@cost for any given budget, regardless of the pass@k form. Moreover, given a pass@k, we can quantitatively predict the savings in the total number of attempts using ReD. If pass@k is not available for the model, ReD can infer its power-law exponent. Experiments on three LLMs using HumanEval demonstrate that ReD substantially reduces the required attempts, tokens, and USD cost to reach a desired coverage, while also offering an efficient way to measure inference power-laws.

AI.tldr: Reset-and-Discard (ReD) is a query strategy that boosts coverage@cost for LLM evaluation, bridging pass@k and coverage metrics. It enables predicting savings given pass@k or inferring its power-law exponent when unavailable, and is validated on three LLMs with HumanEval, showing substantial reductions in attempts, tokens, and cost to achieve target coverage.
AI.motivation: Standard pass@k evaluation at fixed budgets yields diminishing returns due to empirical power-law behavior. Coverage@cost captures the average number of unique questions answered but its benefits hinge on efficient sampling strategies. The work seeks a practical method to maximize coverage within budget and to enable power-law measurement.
AI.method: Proposes Reset-and-Discard (ReD), a query strategy that improves coverage@cost regardless of the model's pass@k behavior. ReD can predict the required savings in total attempts given pass@k, and can infer the power-law exponent of the model's success probability if pass@k is not available. The approach is model-agnostic and budget-aware.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three LLMs using HumanEval shows ReD substantially reduces the number of attempts, tokens, and USD cost needed to reach a target coverage. It also provides an efficient way to measure inference power-laws.
AI.conclusion: ReD offers a practical, model-agnostic method to maximize coverage under a fixed budget and to quantify or infer the model’s inference power-law, enabling more cost-effective evaluation and power-law analysis.

title: Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference
summary: AI agent inference is driving an inference heavy datacenter future and exposes bottlenecks beyond compute - especially memory capacity, memory bandwidth and high-speed interconnect. We introduce two metrics - Operational Intensity (OI) and Capacity Footprint (CF) - that jointly explain regimes the classic roofline analysis misses, including the memory capacity wall. Across agentic workflows (chat, coding, web use, computer use) and base model choices (GQA/MLA, MoE, quantization), OI/CF can shift dramatically, with long context KV cache making decode highly memory bound. These observations motivate disaggregated serving and system level heterogeneity: specialized prefill and decode accelerators, broader scale up networking, and decoupled compute-memory enabled by optical I/O. We further hypothesize agent-hardware co design, multiple inference accelerators within one system, and high bandwidth, large capacity memory disaggregation as foundations for adaptation to evolving OI/CF. Together, these directions chart a path to sustain efficiency and capability for large scale agentic AI inference.

AI.tldr: Introduces Operational Intensity (OI) and Capacity Footprint (CF) to extend roofline for AI agent inference; highlights memory capacity and bandwidth as key bottlenecks, including long-context KV cache; advocates disaggregated serving, specialized accelerators, and memory-disaggregated, high-bandwidth interconnects; argues for agent-hardware co-design to sustain large-scale inference.
AI.motivation: Classic roofline analysis misses memory-capacity walls and heterogeneity in agentic AI workloads. There is a need for metrics that capture both compute intensity and memory capacity constraints to guide system design for large-scale agent inference.
AI.method: Define two metrics (OI and CF) and analyze their behavior across diverse agentic workflows (chat, coding, web use, computer use) and base-model choices (GQA/MLA, MoE, quantization). Examine the impact of long-context KV caches on decode, demonstrating regimes where memory bound becomes dominant; discuss architectural directions (prefill/decode accelerators, broader network bandwidth, memory disaggregation, optical I/O) and a hypothesis of agent-hardware co-design with multiple accelerators per system.
AI.result: OI/CF reveal regime shifts not captured by classic roofline, notably a memory capacity wall in large-context inference. The analysis motivates disaggregated serving and heterogeneous hardware to maintain efficiency. Outlines a path toward agent-hardware co-design and layered acceleration with high-bandwidth memory and I/O to adapt to evolving OI/CF conditions.
AI.conclusion: A combined OI/CF framework and associated architectural strategies (specialized accelerators, memory-disaggregated memory, high-bandwidth interconnect, and multi-inference accelerators) are proposed as foundations for scalable, efficient large-scale agentic AI inference.

title: Explicit Credit Assignment through Local Rewards and Dependence Graphs in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: To promote cooperation in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning, the reward signals of all agents can be aggregated together, forming global rewards that are commonly known as the fully cooperative setting. However, global rewards are usually noisy because they contain the contributions of all agents, which have to be resolved in the credit assignment process. On the other hand, using local reward benefits from faster learning due to the separation of agents' contributions, but can be suboptimal as agents myopically optimize their own reward while disregarding the global optimality. In this work, we propose a method that combines the merits of both approaches. By using a graph of interaction between agents, our method discerns the individual agent contribution in a more fine-grained manner than a global reward, while alleviating the cooperation problem with agents' local reward. We also introduce a practical approach for approximating such a graph. Our experiments demonstrate the flexibility of the approach, enabling improvements over the traditional local and global reward settings.

AI.tldr: A graph-based credit-assignment framework for cooperative MARL that blends global and local rewards by modeling agent interactions to attribute contributions more finely, with a practical graph-approximation method, yielding improvements over purely local or global rewards.
AI.motivation: Credit assignment in fully cooperative MARL: global rewards are noisy but align with global goals; local rewards learn quickly but can be myopic. A middle ground is needed to attribute contributions more accurately while preserving learning efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce an interaction-graph between agents to decompose the global reward into finer-grained contributions; combine this with local rewards to balance cooperation and learning speed. Propose a practical approach to approximate the interaction graph (e.g., learning the graph structure or using heuristics).
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that the proposed graph-based approach offers flexibility and improves performance compared to traditional local and global reward settings.
AI.conclusion: A middle-ground framework that uses an interaction graph to better attribute individual agent contributions while leveraging local rewards, enhancing cooperative learning in MARL and adaptable to different tasks via practical graph-approximation techniques.

title: Visual-Guided Key-Token Regularization for Multimodal Large Language Model Unlearning
summary: Unlearning in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) prevents the model from revealing private information when queried about target images. Existing MLLM unlearning methods largely adopt approaches developed for LLMs. They treat all answer tokens uniformly, disregarding their varying importance in the unlearning process. Moreover, these methods focus exclusively on the language modality, disregarding visual cues that indicate key tokens in answers. In this paper, after formulating the problem of unlearning in multimodal question answering for MLLMs, we propose Visual-Guided Key-Token Regularization (ViKeR). We leverage irrelevant visual inputs to predict ideal post-unlearning token-level distributions and use these distributions to regularize the unlearning process, thereby prioritizing key tokens. Further, we define key tokens in unlearning via information entropy and discuss ViKeR's effectiveness through token-level gradient reweighting, which amplifies updates on key tokens. Experiments on MLLMU and CLEAR benchmarks demonstrate that our method effectively performs unlearning while mitigating forgetting and maintaining response coherence.

AI.tldr: ViKeR: a multimodal unlearning method for MLLMs that uses irrelevant visual inputs to guide token-level regularization, prioritizing key tokens via information entropy and gradient reweighting, achieving effective unlearning while preserving coherence on MLLMU and CLEAR benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current MLLM unlearning methods treat answer tokens uniformly and ignore visual cues, failing to identify and protect key tokens; there is a need to incorporate visual information and token-level importance to improve unlearning fidelity and reduce forgetting.
AI.method: Propose Visual-Guided Key-Token Regularization (ViKeR). Use irrelevant visual inputs to predict ideal post-unlearning token distributions; regularize unlearning by enforcing these distributions; define key tokens via information entropy; apply token-level gradient reweighting to amplify updates on key tokens.
AI.result: Experiments on MLLMU and CLEAR benchmarks show effective unlearning, reduced forgetting, and maintained response coherence.
AI.conclusion: ViKeR demonstrates that visual-guided, token-level regularization improves unlearning in multimodal LLMs, offering privacy-preserving capabilities without compromising quality.

title: CAR-bench: Evaluating the Consistency and Limit-Awareness of LLM Agents under Real-World Uncertainty
summary: Existing benchmarks for Large Language Model (LLM) agents focus on task completion under idealistic settings but overlook reliability in real-world, user-facing applications. In domains, such as in-car voice assistants, users often issue incomplete or ambiguous requests, creating intrinsic uncertainty that agents must manage through dialogue, tool use, and policy adherence. We introduce CAR-bench, a benchmark for evaluating consistency, uncertainty handling, and capability awareness in multi-turn, tool-using LLM agents in an in-car assistant domain. The environment features an LLM-simulated user, domain policies, and 58 interconnected tools spanning navigation, productivity, charging, and vehicle control.
