Beyond standard task completion, CAR-bench introduces Hallucination tasks that test agents' limit-awareness under missing tools or information, and Disambiguation tasks that require resolving uncertainty through clarification or internal information gathering. Baseline results reveal large gaps between occasional and consistent success on all task types. Even frontier reasoning LLMs achieve less than 50% consistent pass rate on Disambiguation tasks due to premature actions, and frequently violate policies or fabricate information to satisfy user requests in Hallucination tasks, underscoring the need for more reliable and self-aware LLM agents in real-world settings.

AI.tldr: CAR-bench evaluates consistency, uncertainty handling, and tool-use in an in-car LLM agent with 58 tools; introduces Hallucination and Disambiguation tasks; baseline models show substantial reliability gaps, underscoring need for improvements.
AI.motivation: Real-world user-facing assistants must cope with incomplete/ambiguous requests and manage uncertainty while adhering to safety and policy constraints; existing benchmarks emphasize task completion under idealized conditions.
AI.method: An in-car domain environment with an LLM-simulated user, domain policies, and 58 interconnected tools spanning navigation, productivity, charging, and vehicle control; assessment includes multi-turn dialogues and two novel tasks: Hallucination (testing limit-awareness under missing tools/info) and Disambiguation (requiring clarification or internal information gathering).
AI.result: Baseline results reveal large gaps between occasional and consistent success across task types; even frontier LLMs achieve <50% consistent pass rate on Disambiguation due to premature actions; Hallucination tasks frequently trigger policy violations or information fabrication, highlighting reliability and self-awareness shortcomings.
AI.conclusion: CAR-bench exposes critical reliability, uncertainty handling, and policy-adherence challenges for LLM agents in real-world settings and provides a framework to drive development of more reliable, self-aware, tool-using agents.

title: Fast and Geometrically Grounded Lorentz Neural Networks
summary: Hyperbolic space is quickly gaining traction as a promising geometry for hierarchical and robust representation learning. A core open challenge is the development of a mathematical formulation of hyperbolic neural networks that is both efficient and captures the key properties of hyperbolic space. The Lorentz model of hyperbolic space has been shown to enable both fast forward and backward propagation. However, we prove that, with the current formulation of Lorentz linear layers, the hyperbolic norms of the outputs scale logarithmically with the number of gradient descent steps, nullifying the key advantage of hyperbolic geometry. We propose a new Lorentz linear layer grounded in the well-known ``distance-to-hyperplane" formulation. We prove that our formulation results in the usual linear scaling of output hyperbolic norms with respect to the number of gradient descent steps. Our new formulation, together with further algorithmic efficiencies through Lorentzian activation functions and a new caching strategy results in neural networks fully abiding by hyperbolic geometry while simultaneously bridging the computation gap to Euclidean neural networks. Code available at: https://github.com/robertdvdk/hyperbolic-fully-connected.

AI.tldr: Introduces a distance-to-hyperplane Lorentz linear layer to fix the logarithmic scaling of hyperbolic norms in Lorentz-based neural networks, achieving linear scaling with gradient steps and improved efficiency via Lorentzian activations and caching; code released.
AI.motivation: Hyperbolic space offers hierarchical representations; current Lorentz linear layers suffer from hyperbolic-norm scaling that grows logarithmically with the number of gradient steps, negating hyperbolic advantages; a formulation preserving hyperbolic geometry while being computationally efficient is needed.
AI.method: Propose a Lorentz linear layer based on a distance-to-hyperplane formulation; prove that it yields linear scaling of output hyperbolic norms with gradient steps; enhance efficiency with Lorentzian activation functions and a new caching strategy; implement towards full hyperbolic-geometry-compliant networks; provide code.
AI.result: The proposed distance-to-hyperplane Lorentz linear layer exhibits linear scaling of output hyperbolic norms with respect to the number of gradient descent steps; combined with Lorentzian activations and caching, the network maintains hyperbolic geometry and closes the computation gap to Euclidean networks; code available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical, theoretically grounded Lorentz-linear formulation that preserves hyperbolic geometry during training and reduces computational overhead, enabling more scalable hyperbolic neural networks.

title: Routing the Lottery: Adaptive Subnetworks for Heterogeneous Data
summary: In pruning, the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis posits that large networks contain sparse subnetworks, or winning tickets, that can be trained in isolation to match the performance of their dense counterparts. However, most existing approaches assume a single universal winning ticket shared across all inputs, ignoring the inherent heterogeneity of real-world data. In this work, we propose Routing the Lottery (RTL), an adaptive pruning framework that discovers multiple specialized subnetworks, called adaptive tickets, each tailored to a class, semantic cluster, or environmental condition. Across diverse datasets and tasks, RTL consistently outperforms single- and multi-model baselines in balanced accuracy and recall, while using up to 10 times fewer parameters than independent models and exhibiting semantically aligned. Furthermore, we identify subnetwork collapse, a performance drop under aggressive pruning, and introduce a subnetwork similarity score that enables label-free diagnosis of oversparsification. Overall, our results recast pruning as a mechanism for aligning model structure with data heterogeneity, paving the way toward more modular and context-aware deep learning.

AI.tldr: RTL discovers multiple specialized subnetworks (adaptive tickets) for data heterogeneity, enabling modular, context-aware pruning with strong accuracy gains and parameter efficiency; it also introduces a subnetwork similarity score to diagnose oversparsification.
AI.motivation: Single universal winning tickets ignore data heterogeneity; there is a need for modular, context-aware pruning that aligns network structure with varying data regimes to improve efficiency and performance.
AI.method: An adaptive pruning framework that routes inputs to multiple adaptive tickets—subnetworks specialized for classes, semantic clusters, or environmental conditions—thereby avoiding a single shared winning ticket and enabling context-specific models.
AI.result: RTL consistently outperforms single- and multi-model baselines in balanced accuracy and recall across diverse tasks, using up to 10x fewer parameters than independent models, with subnetworks being semantically aligned to data. It also reveals subnetwork collapse under aggressive pruning and introduces a subnetwork similarity score for label-free diagnosis of oversparsification.
AI.conclusion: Pruning can be reframed as aligning model structure with data heterogeneity, enabling modular, context-aware deep learning through adaptiveTicket routing and specialized subnetworks.

title: Optimizing Agentic Workflows using Meta-tools
summary: Agentic AI enables LLM to dynamically reason, plan, and interact with tools to solve complex tasks. However, agentic workflows often require many iterative reasoning steps and tool invocations, leading to significant operational expense, end-to-end latency and failures due to hallucinations. This work introduces Agent Workflow Optimization (AWO), a framework that identifies and optimizes redundant tool execution patterns to improve the efficiency and robustness of agentic workflows. AWO analyzes existing workflow traces to discover recurring sequences of tool calls and transforms them into meta-tools, which are deterministic, composite tools that bundle multiple agent actions into a single invocation. Meta-tools bypass unnecessary intermediate LLM reasoning steps and reduce operational cost while also shortening execution paths, leading to fewer failures. Experiments on two agentic AI benchmarks show that AWO reduces the number of LLM calls up to 11.9% while also increasing the task success rate by up to 4.2 percent points.

AI.tldr: Agent Workflow Optimization (AWO) compresses repetitive tool sequences into meta-tools to reduce LLM calls and improve robustness in agentic AI.
AI.motivation: Agentic AI workflows suffer from high latency, cost, and hallucination risk due to iterative reasoning and tool invocations; optimize by identifying and collapsing redundant patterns.
AI.method: Analyze workflow traces to discover recurring tool-call sequences; create deterministic composite meta-tools that bundle multiple actions; deploy to reduce intermediate reasoning steps and cost; evaluate on two agentic AI benchmarks.
AI.result: Reduces LLM calls by up to 11.9% and increases task success by up to 4.2 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: Meta-tools improve efficiency and robustness of agentic workflows by reducing reasoning steps and execution paths, with modest latency and cost savings and improved success rates.

title: Multi-Modal Time Series Prediction via Mixture of Modulated Experts
summary: Real-world time series exhibit complex and evolving dynamics, making accurate forecasting extremely challenging. Recent multi-modal forecasting methods leverage textual information such as news reports to improve prediction, but most rely on token-level fusion that mixes temporal patches with language tokens in a shared embedding space. However, such fusion can be ill-suited when high-quality time-text pairs are scarce and when time series exhibit substantial variation in scale and characteristics, thus complicating cross-modal alignment. In parallel, Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures have proven effective for both time series modeling and multi-modal learning, yet many existing MoE-based modality integration methods still depend on token-level fusion. To address this, we propose Expert Modulation, a new paradigm for multi-modal time series prediction that conditions both routing and expert computation on textual signals, enabling direct and efficient cross-modal control over expert behavior. Through comprehensive theoretical analysis and experiments, our proposed method demonstrates substantial improvements in multi-modal time series prediction. The current code is available at https://github.com/BruceZhangReve/MoME

AI.tldr: Introduces Expert Modulation (MoME), a Mixture-of-Experts paradigm for multi-modal time-series forecasting that conditions MoE routing and expert computation on textual signals to enable cross-modal control, addressing token-level fusion limitations with scarce time-text pairs and scale heterogeneity; reports theoretical analysis and substantial empirical improvements; code available at GitHub.
AI.motivation: Real-world time series show evolving dynamics; textual information (e.g., news) can aid forecasting but token-level fusion in shared embeddings is fragile when high-quality time-text pairs are scarce and when time series vary in scale and characteristics. While MoE architectures are promising for modality integration, many existing methods rely on token-level fusion. There is a need for a mechanism to directly modulate expert behavior using text.
AI.method: Propose Expert Modulation: condition both routing (gating) and expert computation on textual signals, enabling direct cross-modal control over which experts are activated and how they contribute to predictions. Integrate textual embeddings into the MoE framework to modulate expert responsibilities, aiming to improve cross-modal alignment and robustness to scale variations. Provide theoretical analysis and empirical validation, with open-source code.
AI.result: The approach yields substantial improvements in multi-modal time-series forecasting, supported by theoretical insights and comprehensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: Expert Modulation provides a principled framework for cross-modal control over MoE-based time-series predictors, addressing limitations of token-level fusion and benefiting scenarios with scarce time-text data and heterogeneous time-series dynamics; code released.

title: Defining Operational Conditions for Safety-Critical AI-Based Systems from Data
summary: Artificial Intelligence (AI) has been on the rise in many domains, including numerous safety-critical applications. However, for complex systems found in the real world, or when data already exist, defining the underlying environmental conditions is extremely challenging. This often results in an incomplete description of the environment in which the AI-based system must operate. Nevertheless, this description, called the Operational Design Domain (ODD), is required in many domains for the certification of AI-based systems. Traditionally, the ODD is created in the early stages of the development process, drawing on sophisticated expert knowledge and related standards. This paper presents a novel Safety-by-Design method to a posteriori define the ODD from previously collected data using a multi-dimensional kernel-based representation. This approach is validated through both Monte Carlo methods and a real-world aviation use case for a future safety-critical collision-avoidance system. Moreover, by defining under what conditions two ODDs are equal, the paper shows that the data-driven ODD can equal the original, underlying hidden ODD of the data. Utilizing the novel, Safe-by-Design kernel-based ODD enables future certification of data-driven, safety-critical AI-based systems.

AI.tldr: Data-driven, kernel-based method to define ODD post hoc from existing data, enabling safety certification of AI systems in safety-critical domains.
AI.motivation: ODD specification is hard for complex, data-rich environments; accurate ODD is essential for certification and safety in AI-based systems.
AI.method: A posteriori ODD definition using multi-dimensional kernel-based representation; compares distributions of environmental conditions; defines when two ODDs are equal.
AI.result: Validated via Monte Carlo experiments and a real-world aviation collision-avoidance use case; demonstrates that the data-driven ODD can equal the underlying hidden ODD; supports certification.
AI.conclusion: Safe-by-Design kernel-based ODD provides a principled, data-driven route to certify AI-based safety-critical systems by accurately characterizing operational domains.

title: HistoPrism: Unlocking Functional Pathway Analysis from Pan-Cancer Histology via Gene Expression Prediction
summary: Predicting spatial gene expression from H&E histology offers a scalable and clinically accessible alternative to sequencing, but realizing clinical impact requires models that generalize across cancer types and capture biologically coherent signals. Prior work is often limited to per-cancer settings and variance-based evaluation, leaving functional relevance underexplored. We introduce HistoPrism, an efficient transformer-based architecture for pan-cancer prediction of gene expression from histology. To evaluate biological meaning, we introduce a pathway-level benchmark, shifting assessment from isolated gene-level variance to coherent functional pathways. HistoPrism not only surpasses prior state-of-the-art models on highly variable genes , but also more importantly, achieves substantial gains on pathway-level prediction, demonstrating its ability to recover biologically coherent transcriptomic patterns. With strong pan-cancer generalization and improved efficiency, HistoPrism establishes a new standard for clinically relevant transcriptomic modeling from routinely available histology.

AI.tldr: HistoPrism is a transformer-based pan-cancer model that predicts spatial gene expression from H&E slides, and introduces a pathway-level benchmark, achieving state-of-the-art performance on variable genes and substantial gains in pathway-level predictions with good generalization and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address limited generalization across cancer types and focus on per-gene variance; highlight functional relevance by evaluating pathway-level coherence in predicted transcriptomic signals.
AI.method: Efficient transformer-based architecture trained in a pan-cancer setting; introduces a pathway-level benchmark to assess coherence of predicted gene sets; aims for both gene-level accuracy and pathway-level functional consistency.
AI.result: Outperforms prior state-of-the-art on highly variable genes and yields substantial gains in pathway-level predictions; demonstrates strong pan-cancer generalization and improved computational efficiency compared to existing models.
AI.conclusion: HistoPrism advances clinically relevant transcriptomic modeling from routine histology, offering cross-cancer generalization and pathway-coherent predictions, and sets a new standard for histology-to-transcriptomics mapping.

title: SAL: Selective Adaptive Learning for Backpropagation-Free Training with Sparsification
summary: Standard deep learning relies on Backpropagation (BP), which is constrained by biologically implausible weight symmetry and suffers from significant gradient interference within dense representations. To mitigate these bottlenecks, we propose Selective Adaptive Learning (SAL), a training method that combines selective parameter activation with adaptive area partitioning. Specifically, SAL decomposes the parameter space into mutually exclusive, sample-dependent regions. This decoupling mitigates gradient interference across divergent semantic patterns and addresses explicit weight symmetry requirements through our refined feedback alignment. Empirically, SAL demonstrates competitive convergence rates, leading to improved classification performance across 10 standard benchmarks. Additionally, SAL achieves numerical consistency and competitive accuracy even in deep regimes (up to 128 layers) and large-scale models (up to 1B parameters). Our approach is loosely inspired by biological learning mechanisms, offering a plausible alternative that contributes to the study of scalable neural network training.

AI.tldr: Selective Adaptive Learning (SAL) uses sample-dependent, mutually exclusive parameter regions with adaptive area partitioning and refined feedback alignment to reduce gradient interference and remove weight symmetry constraints, achieving competitive convergence and strong performance on deep and large models across 10 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Backpropagation suffers from weight symmetry (weight transport) and gradient interference within dense representations; a biologically plausible, scalable alternative is desirable.
AI.method: SAL partitions the parameter space into sample-specific, mutually exclusive regions, activates only a subset of parameters per sample, and employs adaptive area partitioning plus refined feedback alignment to decouple gradients across diverse semantic patterns, mitigating interference and relaxing symmetry requirements.
AI.result: Empirically yields competitive convergence rates and improved classification accuracy across 10 standard benchmarks; maintains numerical consistency and competitive accuracy in deep regimes (up to 128 layers) and large-scale models (up to 1B parameters).
AI.conclusion: SAL offers a plausible, biologically inspired alternative to backpropagation, contributing to scalable neural network training and enabling robust learning in deep/large models while addressing symmetry constraints.

title: World of Workflows: a Benchmark for Bringing World Models to Enterprise Systems
summary: Frontier large language models (LLMs) excel as autonomous agents in many domains, yet they remain untested in complex enterprise systems where hidden workflows create cascading effects across interconnected databases. Existing enterprise benchmarks evaluate surface-level agentic task completion similar to general consumer benchmarks, ignoring true challenges in enterprises, such as limited observability, large database state, and hidden workflows with cascading side effects. We introduce World of Workflows (WoW), a realistic ServiceNow-based environment incorporating 4,000+ business rules and 55 active workflows embedded in the system, alongside WoW-bench, a benchmark of 234 tasks evaluating constrained agentic task completion and enterprise dynamics modeling capabilities. We reveal two major takeaways: (1) Frontier LLMs suffer from dynamics blindness, consistently failing to predict the invisible, cascading side effects of their actions, which leads to silent constraint violations, and (2) reliability in opaque systems requires grounded world modeling, where agents must mentally simulate hidden state transitions to bridge the observability gap when high-fidelity feedback is unavailable. For reliable and useful enterprise agents, WoW motivates a new paradigm to explicitly learn system dynamics. We release our GitHub for setting up and evaluating WoW.

AI.tldr: Proposes World of Workflows (WoW) and WoW-bench to benchmark frontier LLMs in enterprise settings with hidden workflows; finds dynamics blindness and a need for grounded world models.
AI.motivation: Enterprise systems feature hidden workflows, limited observability, large state, and cascading side effects not captured by existing benchmarks.
AI.method: Build a ServiceNow-based WoW environment with 4,000+ business rules and 55 active workflows; create WoW-bench with 234 tasks to evaluate constrained agentic task completion and enterprise dynamics modeling; assess frontier LLMs.
AI.result: Frontier LLMs exhibit dynamics blindness, failing to predict invisible, cascading side effects and causing silent constraint violations; reliable operation in opaque systems requires grounded world modeling and mental simulation of hidden state transitions when high-fidelity feedback is unavailable.
AI.conclusion: WoW introduces a realistic enterprise agent benchmark and argues for a new paradigm focused on explicit learning of system dynamics; GitHub release provided for setup and evaluation.

title: Representation Unlearning: Forgetting through Information Compression
summary: Machine unlearning seeks to remove the influence of specific training data from a model, a need driven by privacy regulations and robustness concerns. Existing approaches typically modify model parameters, but such updates can be unstable, computationally costly, and limited by local approximations. We introduce Representation Unlearning, a framework that performs unlearning directly in the model's representation space. Instead of modifying model parameters, we learn a transformation over representations that imposes an information bottleneck: maximizing mutual information with retained data while suppressing information about data to be forgotten. We derive variational surrogates that make this objective tractable and show how they can be instantiated in two practical regimes: when both retain and forget data are available, and in a zero-shot setting where only forget data can be accessed. Experiments across several benchmarks demonstrate that Representation Unlearning achieves more reliable forgetting, better utility retention, and greater computational efficiency than parameter-centric baselines.

AI.tldr: RepUnlearn, a representation-space unlearning framework that uses an information-bottleneck transformation to forget specified data while preserving retained data, via tractable variational surrogates; supports retain-and-forget and zero-shot settings; outperforms parameter-centric baselines in forgetting reliability, utility, and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Overcome instability, high computational cost, and limited effectiveness of parameter-centric unlearning by operating in the model's representation space instead of updating parameters.
AI.method: Introduce Representation Unlearning: learn a transformation on representations that enforces an information bottleneck by maximizing mutual information with retained data and minimizing information about forgotten data. Derive variational surrogates to make optimization tractable and instantiate in two regimes: (i) access to both retain and forget data; (ii) zero-shot setting with only forget data access.
AI.result: Empirical results across several benchmarks show improved forgetting reliability, better utility retention, and greater computational efficiency compared to parameter-centric baselines.
AI.conclusion: Representation Unlearning provides a practical, efficient alternative to parameter updates for unlearning, leveraging an information-bottleneck in representation space to achieve robust forgetting.

title: FlexCausal: Flexible Causal Disentanglement via Structural Flow Priors and Manifold-Aware Interventions
summary: Causal Disentangled Representation Learning(CDRL) aims to learn and disentangle low dimensional representations and their underlying causal structure from observations. However, existing disentanglement methods rely on a standard mean-field approximation with a diagonal posterior covariance, which decorrelates all latent dimensions. Additionally, these methods often assume isotropic Gaussian priors for exogenous noise, failing to capture the complex, non-Gaussian statistical properties prevalent in real-world causal factors. Therefore, we propose FlexCausal, a novel CDRL framework based on a block-diagonal covariance VAE. FlexCausal utilizes a Factorized Flow-based Prior to realistically model the complex densities of exogenous noise, effectively decoupling the learning of causal mechanisms from distributional statistics. By integrating supervised alignment objectives with counterfactual consistency constraints, our framework ensures a precise structural correspondence between the learned latent subspaces and the ground-truth causal relations. Finally, we introduce a manifold-aware relative intervention strategy to ensure high-fidelity generation. Experimental results on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that FlexCausal significantly outperforms other methods.

AI.tldr: FlexCausal introduces a block-diagonal covariance VAE with a factorized flow-based prior for exogenous noise, plus supervised alignment and counterfactual constraints, plus a manifold-aware intervention strategy, achieving superior causal disentangled representation on synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: Limitations of standard disentanglement methods: diagonal posterior covariances and isotropic Gaussian priors fail to capture dependencies and non-Gaussian exogenous noise; need to learn causal structure with distributional realism and robust generation, aligning latent subspaces with ground-truth causal relations.
AI.method: Propose FlexCausal: a CDRL framework using (1) a block-diagonal covariance VAE to allow structured latent dependencies, (2) a Factorized Flow-based Prior to model complex exogenous noise distributions, (3) supervised alignment objectives and counterfactual consistency constraints to enforce structural correspondence between latent blocks and causal relations, and (4) a manifold-aware relative intervention strategy to ensure high-fidelity generation.
AI.result: Empirical results on synthetic and real-world datasets show that FlexCausal significantly outperforms competing methods.
AI.conclusion: FlexCausal decouples learning of causal mechanisms from distributional statistics, enabling precise structural alignment between latent subspaces and causal graphs, and achieving high-fidelity generative performance in causal disentangled representation learning.

title: Exploring Reasoning Reward Model for Agents
summary: Agentic Reinforcement Learning (Agentic RL) has achieved notable success in enabling agents to perform complex reasoning and tool use. However, most methods still relies on sparse outcome-based reward for training. Such feedback fails to differentiate intermediate reasoning quality, leading to suboptimal training results. In this paper, we introduce Agent Reasoning Reward Model (Agent-RRM), a multi-faceted reward model that produces structured feedback for agentic trajectories, including (1) an explicit reasoning trace , (2) a focused critique that provides refinement guidance by highlighting reasoning flaws, and (3) an overall score that evaluates process performance. Leveraging these signals, we systematically investigate three integration strategies: Reagent-C (text-augmented refinement), Reagent-R (reward-augmented guidance), and Reagent-U (unified feedback integration). Extensive evaluations across 12 diverse benchmarks demonstrate that Reagent-U yields substantial performance leaps, achieving 43.7% on GAIA and 46.2% on WebWalkerQA, validating the effectiveness of our reasoning reward model and training schemes. Code, models, and datasets are all released to facilitate future research.

AI.tldr: A multi-faceted reasoning reward model (Agent-RRM) for agentic RL that outputs explicit reasoning traces, critique, and an overall score, with three integration strategies (Reagent-C/R/U); Reagent-U achieves strong gains (43.7% GAIA, 46.2% WebWalkerQA) across 12 benchmarks; code and data released.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of sparse outcome-based rewards by providing structured feedback that optimizes intermediate reasoning quality in agentic trajectories.
AI.method: Introduce Agent-RRM with three components: explicit reasoning traces, focused critique with refinement guidance, and an overall performance score. Evaluate three integration strategies: Reagent-C (text-augmented refinement), Reagent-R (reward-augmented guidance), and Reagent-U (unified feedback integration). Conduct extensive experiments across 12 benchmarks, reporting strong results with Reagent-U.
AI.result: Reagent-U yields substantial gains, achieving 43.7% on GAIA and 46.2% on WebWalkerQA. Code, models, and datasets released to facilitate future research.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning reward modeling can substantially improve agentic RL by providing richer feedback that guides intermediate reasoning; unified feedback integration offers the strongest performance. The work provides a replicable framework and resources for future development.

title: Bridging Functional and Representational Similarity via Usable Information
summary: We present a unified framework for quantifying the similarity between representations through the lens of \textit{usable information}, offering a rigorous theoretical and empirical synthesis across three key dimensions. First, addressing functional similarity, we establish a formal link between stitching performance and conditional mutual information. We further reveal that stitching is inherently asymmetric, demonstrating that robust functional comparison necessitates a bidirectional analysis rather than a unidirectional mapping. Second, concerning representational similarity, we prove that reconstruction-based metrics and standard tools (e.g., CKA, RSA) act as estimators of usable information under specific constraints. Crucially, we show that similarity is relative to the capacity of the predictive family: representations that appear distinct to a rigid observer may be identical to a more expressive one. Third, we demonstrate that representational similarity is sufficient but not necessary for functional similarity. We unify these concepts through a task-granularity hierarchy: similarity on a complex task guarantees similarity on any coarser derivative, establishing representational similarity as the limit of maximum granularity: input reconstruction.

AI.tldr: A unified information-theoretic framework (usable information) links functional and representational similarity, reveals asymmetry in stitching, and unifies reconstruction-based metrics (e.g., CKA/RSA) as estimators under predictive-capacity constraints; a task-granularity hierarchy shows representational similarity is sufficient but not necessary for functional similarity, with input reconstruction representing the maximum granularity.
AI.motivation: To provide a principled, unified lens for comparing representations across models/ layers, reconciling functional behavior (how representations can be transformed or stitched) with representational similarity (how information is encoded). Address gaps where different similarity notions yield conflicting conclusions.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis establishing links between stitching performance and conditional mutual information; proof of inherent asymmetry requiring bidirectional analysis; formal demonstration that reconstruction-based metrics and standard tools estimate usable information under certain constraints; demonstration that similarity depends on the predictive family’s capacity; introduction of a task-granularity hierarchy mapping fine-grained input reconstruction to broader derivatives.
AI.result: Formal connections showing stitching performance relates to conditional mutual information; asymmetry of stitching necessitates bidirectional comparison; reconstruction-based metrics and CKA/RSA can estimate usable information under constraints; similarity is task-dependent and dependent on the observer/predictive capacity; representational similarity is sufficient but not necessary for functional similarity; the hierarchy ensures higher-granularity similarity guarantees lower-granularity similarity; input reconstruction represents the maximum granularity.
AI.conclusion: Usable information provides a unifying framework to compare representations. Functional similarity can be inferred from representational similarity only under certain conditions; bidirectional stitching and predictive-capacity considerations are essential; input reconstruction sets the ceiling for task granularity, guiding how we assess similarity across tasks.

title: Shaping capabilities with token-level data filtering
summary: Current approaches to reducing undesired capabilities in language models are largely post hoc, and can thus be easily bypassed by adversaries. A natural alternative is to shape capabilities during pretraining itself. On the proxy task of removing medical capabilities, we show that the simple intervention of filtering pretraining data is highly effective, robust, and inexpensive at scale. Inspired by work on data attribution, we show that filtering tokens is more effective than filtering documents, achieving the same hit to undesired capabilities at a lower cost to benign ones. Training models spanning two orders of magnitude, we then demonstrate that filtering gets more effective with scale: for our largest models, token filtering leads to a 7000x compute slowdown on the forget domain. We also show that models trained with token filtering can still be aligned on the forget domain. Along the way, we introduce a methodology for labeling tokens with sparse autoencoders and distilling cheap, high-quality classifiers. We also demonstrate that filtering can be robust to noisy labels with sufficient pretraining compute.

AI.tldr: Proactive pretraining data filtering, especially token-level filtering, can robustly reduce undesired capabilities in language models, scaling in effectiveness with model size and enabling cheaper, scalable safety alignment compared to document filtering.
AI.motivation: Address the inadequacy of post hoc safety measures by shaping model capabilities during pretraining to prevent emergence of undesired behaviors (e.g., medical capabilities).
AI.method: Compare token-level vs document-level filtering of pretraining data; implement token labeling via sparse autoencoders and cheap classifiers; apply filtering across models spanning two orders of magnitude in size; evaluate on a 'forget' domain to measure reduction of undesired capabilities; assess robustness to noisy labels and alignment after filtering.
AI.result: Token filtering is more effective and cheaper than document filtering; effectiveness scales with model size, achieving large reductions in undesired capabilities (e.g., forget domain) and maintaining alignment; token-level filtering can be robust to labeling noise with sufficient compute; methodology for token labeling and distillation is introduced; for the largest models, a reported 7000x compute slowdown on the forget domain is claimed (potential wording ambiguity).
AI.conclusion: Pretraining data curation—specifically token filtering informed by data attribution—offers a scalable, robust, and cost-effective path to shape LM capabilities and block undesired functions, with demonstrated scalability, potential robustness to label noise, and preserved alignment on targeted domains.

title: Signal-Adaptive Trust Regions for Gradient-Free Optimization of Recurrent Spiking Neural Networks
summary: Recurrent spiking neural networks (RSNNs) are a promising substrate for energy-efficient control policies, but training them for high-dimensional, long-horizon reinforcement learning remains challenging. Population-based, gradient-free optimization circumvents backpropagation through non-differentiable spike dynamics by estimating gradients. However, with finite populations, high variance of these estimates can induce harmful and overly aggressive update steps. Inspired by trust-region methods in reinforcement learning that constrain policy updates in distribution space, we propose \textbf{Signal-Adaptive Trust Regions (SATR)}, a distributional update rule that constrains relative change by bounding KL divergence normalized by an estimated signal energy. SATR automatically expands the trust region under strong signals and contracts it when updates are noise-dominated. We instantiate SATR for Bernoulli connectivity distributions, which have shown strong empirical performance for RSNN optimization. Across a suite of high-dimensional continuous-control benchmarks, SATR improves stability under limited populations and reaches competitive returns against strong baselines including PPO-LSTM. In addition, to make SATR practical at scale, we introduce a bitset implementation for binary spiking and binary weights, substantially reducing wall-clock training time and enabling fast RSNN policy search.

AI.tldr: Introduces Signal-Adaptive Trust Regions (SATR) for population-based, gradient-free optimization of recurrent spiking neural networks (RSNNs). SATR bounds KL divergence by the estimated signal energy to adaptively expand/contract the trust region, instantiated for Bernoulli connectivity. A bitset-based implementation accelerates binary spiking and weights, enabling scalable RSNN policy search.
AI.motivation: Training RSNNs in high-dimensional, long-horizon RL is challenging due to high-variance, gradient-free updates. A principled mechanism to adapt update magnitude to the signal quality can improve stability and sample efficiency.
AI.method: Define SATR as a distributional update rule that constrains relative change via KL divergence normalized by estimated signal energy. The trust region expands under strong signal, contracts when updates are noise-dominated. Implemented for Bernoulli connectivity distributions and augmented with a bitset-based approach for binary spiking and weights to speed up training.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across high-dimensional continuous-control benchmarks shows improved stability under limited populations and competitive returns against strong baselines, including PPO-LSTM. The bitset implementation substantially reduces wall-clock training time, enabling fast RSNN policy search.
AI.conclusion: SATR provides a scalable, stable framework for gradient-free RSNN optimization by aligning update magnitude with signal quality. The combination with Bernoulli connectivity and bitset acceleration makes it practical for large-scale RSNN policy search and competitive with strong RL baselines.

title: Learning the Mechanism of Catastrophic Forgetting: A Perspective from Gradient Similarity
summary: Catastrophic forgetting during knowledge injection severely undermines the continual learning capability of large language models (LLMs). Although existing methods attempt to mitigate this issue, they often lack a foundational theoretical explanation. We establish a gradient-based theoretical framework to explain catastrophic forgetting. We first prove that strongly negative gradient similarity is a fundamental cause of forgetting. We then use gradient similarity to identify two types of neurons: conflicting neurons that induce forgetting and account for 50%-75% of neurons, and collaborative neurons that mitigate forgetting and account for 25%-50%. Based on this analysis, we propose a knowledge injection method, Collaborative Neural Learning (CNL). By freezing conflicting neurons and updating only collaborative neurons, CNL theoretically eliminates catastrophic forgetting under an infinitesimal learning rate eta and an exactly known mastered set. Experiments on five LLMs, four datasets, and four optimizers show that CNL achieves zero forgetting in in-set settings and reduces forgetting by 59.1%-81.7% in out-of-set settings.

AI.tldr: Gradient-based theory explains catastrophic forgetting in knowledge injection for LLMs; identifies conflicting and collaborative neurons; proposes Collaborative Neural Learning (CNL) by freezing conflicting neurons; achieves zero forgetting in in-set and substantial forgetting reduction out-of-set.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of theoretical explanations for catastrophic forgetting in continual learning and knowledge injection for LLMs; establish gradient similarity as a foundational factor.
AI.method: Develop a gradient-based theoretical framework; prove strongly negative gradient similarity as the root cause; classify neurons into conflicting (50–75%) and collaborative (25–50%); design CNL that freezes conflicting neurons and updates collaborative ones; provide theoretical forgetting-elimination guarantee under infinitesimal learning rate and exactly known mastered set; validate with experiments across five LLMs, four datasets, and four optimizers.
AI.result: Zero forgetting in in-set settings; forgetting reduced by 59.1%–81.7% in out-of-set settings across experiments.
AI.conclusion: Gradient similarity offers a foundational explanation for forgetting; CNL leverages neuron-level modularization (freeze/can update) to prevent forgetting; theoretical guarantees hold under ideal conditions; empirical results demonstrate robust forgetting mitigation across diverse models and settings.

title: Evaluating Prediction Uncertainty Estimates from BatchEnsemble
summary: Deep learning models struggle with uncertainty estimation. Many approaches are either computationally infeasible or underestimate uncertainty. We investigate \textit{BatchEnsemble} as a general and scalable method for uncertainty estimation across both tabular and time series tasks. To extend BatchEnsemble to sequential modeling, we introduce GRUBE, a novel BatchEnsemble GRU cell. We compare the BatchEnsemble to Monte Carlo dropout and deep ensemble models. Our results show that BatchEnsemble matches the uncertainty estimation performance of deep ensembles, and clearly outperforms Monte Carlo dropout. GRUBE achieves similar or better performance in both prediction and uncertainty estimation. These findings show that BatchEnsemble and GRUBE achieve similar performance with fewer parameters and reduced training and inference time compared to traditional ensembles.

AI.tldr: BatchEnsemble provides a scalable uncertainty estimation approach; GRUBE extends it to GRUs for sequential modeling; it matches deep ensembles' uncertainty and beats MC dropout with fewer parameters and faster training/inference.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty estimation in deep learning is often computationally expensive and can underestimate uncertainty; scalable, accurate methods are needed for both tabular and time-series tasks.
AI.method: Introduce GRUBE, a BatchEnsemble-based GRU cell to extend BatchEnsemble to sequential models; compare against Monte Carlo dropout and deep ensembles on tabular and time-series tasks; evaluate predictive performance and uncertainty estimation; assess parameter efficiency and speed.
AI.result: BatchEnsemble achieves uncertainty estimation performance comparable to deep ensembles and better than MC dropout; GRUBE attains similar or superior prediction and uncertainty results; both require fewer parameters and offer reduced training and inference time compared to traditional ensembles.
AI.conclusion: BatchEnsemble and the GRUBE extension provide scalable, competitive uncertainty estimation with lower computational cost than traditional ensembles, effective for both tabular and sequential data.

title: CORDS: Continuous Representations of Discrete Structures
summary: Many learning problems require predicting sets of objects when the number of objects is not known beforehand. Examples include object detection, molecular modeling, and scientific inference tasks such as astrophysical source detection. Existing methods often rely on padded representations or must explicitly infer the set size, which often poses challenges. We present a novel strategy for addressing this challenge by casting prediction of variable-sized sets as a continuous inference problem. Our approach, CORDS (Continuous Representations of Discrete Structures), provides an invertible mapping that transforms a set of spatial objects into continuous fields: a density field that encodes object locations and count, and a feature field that carries their attributes over the same support. Because the mapping is invertible, models operate entirely in field space while remaining exactly decodable to discrete sets. We evaluate CORDS across molecular generation and regression, object detection, simulation-based inference, and a mathematical task involving recovery of local maxima, demonstrating robust handling of unknown set sizes with competitive accuracy.

AI.tldr: CORDS introduces continuous representations to cast variable-sized set prediction as continuous inference, via invertible mappings to density and feature fields, enabling exact decodability back to discrete sets; demonstrated across molecular generation/regression, object detection, simulation-based inference, and a maxima recovery task.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of predicting variable-sized sets without padding or explicit size inference, by moving to a continuous field representation that preserves exact recoverability.
AI.method: Define an invertible mapping from discrete sets to spatially organized density and feature fields. Train models to operate directly in field space, exploiting continuous optimization. At inference or decoding, revert the fields to a discrete set in a lossless, exact manner.
AI.result: Demonstrates robust handling of unknown set sizes with competitive accuracy across diverse tasks including molecular generation/regression, object detection, simulation-based inference, and a mathematical maxima recovery task.
AI.conclusion: CORDS provides a general, invertible continuous representation for variable-sized sets, enabling flexible, scalable, and exact set prediction across domains.

title: Heterogeneity-Aware Knowledge Sharing for Graph Federated Learning
summary: Graph Federated Learning (GFL) enables distributed graph representation learning while protecting the privacy of graph data. However, GFL suffers from heterogeneity arising from diverse node features and structural topologies across multiple clients. To address both types of heterogeneity, we propose a novel graph Federated learning method via Semantic and Structural Alignment (FedSSA), which shares the knowledge of both node features and structural topologies. For node feature heterogeneity, we propose a novel variational model to infer class-wise node distributions, so that we can cluster clients based on inferred distributions and construct cluster-level representative distributions. We then minimize the divergence between local and cluster-level distributions to facilitate semantic knowledge sharing. For structural heterogeneity, we employ spectral Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and propose a spectral energy measure to characterize structural information, so that we can cluster clients based on spectral energy and build cluster-level spectral GNNs. We then align the spectral characteristics of local spectral GNNs with those of cluster-level spectral GNNs to enable structural knowledge sharing. Experiments on six homophilic and five heterophilic graph datasets under both non-overlapping and overlapping partitioning settings demonstrate that FedSSA consistently outperforms eleven state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: FedSSA tackles dual heterogeneity in Graph Federated Learning by clustering clients based on semantic (class-wise node distributions) and structural (spectral energy) features and aligning local models with cluster-level representatives, achieving state-of-the-art performance on diverse datasets and partitioning settings.
AI.motivation: GFL must handle heterogeneity across clients in both node features and graph topology. A single global model often underfits due to non-identical distributions and structures; thus, joint semantic and structural alignment can improve knowledge transfer.
AI.method: Semantic alignment: train a variational model to infer class-wise node distributions, cluster clients by these distributions, form cluster-level representative distributions, and minimize divergence between local and cluster-level distributions to share semantics. Structural alignment: compute a spectral energy measure via spectral GNNs to capture topology, cluster clients by spectral energy, construct cluster-level spectral GNNs, and align local spectral representations with cluster-level ones to share structure.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 11 datasets (6 homophilic, 5 heterophilic) under both non-overlapping and overlapping partitions shows FedSSA consistently outperforms 11 state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: FedSSA effectively mitigates both feature- and structure-level heterogeneity in GFL by semantic and spectral alignment, delivering robust improvements across diverse graph types and partitioning schemes.

title: Scalable Power Sampling: Unlocking Efficient, Training-Free Reasoning for LLMs via Distribution Sharpening
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) post-training is a dominant approach for improving the reasoning performance of large language models (LLMs), yet growing evidence suggests that its gains arise primarily from distribution sharpening rather than the acquisition of new capabilities. Recent work has shown that sampling from the power distribution of LLMs using Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) can recover performance comparable to RL post-training without relying on external rewards; however, the high computational cost of MCMC makes such approaches impractical for widespread adoption. In this work, we propose a theoretically grounded alternative that eliminates the need for iterative MCMC. We derive a novel formulation showing that the global power distribution can be approximated by a token-level scaled low-temperature one, where the scaling factor captures future trajectory quality. Leveraging this insight, we introduce a training-free and verifier-free algorithm that sharpens the base model's generative distribution autoregressively. Empirically, we evaluate our method on math, QA, and code tasks across four LLMs, and show that our method matches or surpasses one-shot GRPO without relying on any external rewards, while reducing inference latency by over 10x compared to MCMC-based sampling.

AI.tldr: Training-free, verifier-free autoregressive sharpening that approximates the global power distribution via a token-level low-temperature scaling, enabling MCMC-like gains without external rewards and with large latency savings.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational and reward-signal burden of RL post-training and MCMC-based approaches while maintaining or improving reasoning performance.
AI.method: Derive that global power distribution can be approximated by token-level scaled low-temperature distribution; use this scaling as a proxy for future trajectory quality; implement a training-free, verifier-free autoregressive sharpening algorithm.
AI.result: Empirically on math, QA, and code tasks across four LLMs, matches or surpasses one-shot GRPO without external rewards; inference latency reduced by >10x compared with MCMC-based sampling.
AI.conclusion: Presents a practical, scalable alternative to RL post-training and MCMC sampling, with strong performance and efficiency gains and broad applicability.

title: Dynamics Reveals Structure: Challenging the Linear Propagation Assumption
summary: Neural networks adapt through first-order parameter updates, yet it remains unclear whether such updates preserve logical coherence. We investigate the geometric limits of the Linear Propagation Assumption (LPA), the premise that local updates coherently propagate to logical consequences. To formalize this, we adopt relation algebra and study three core operations on relations: negation flips truth values, converse swaps argument order, and composition chains relations. For negation and converse, we prove that guaranteeing direction-agnostic first-order propagation necessitates a tensor factorization separating entity-pair context from relation content. However, for composition, we identify a fundamental obstruction. We show that composition reduces to conjunction, and prove that any conjunction well-defined on linear features must be bilinear. Since bilinearity is incompatible with negation, this forces the feature map to collapse. These results suggest that failures in knowledge editing, the reversal curse, and multi-hop reasoning may stem from common structural limitations inherent to the LPA.

AI.tldr: Found fundamental limits of the Linear Propagation Assumption (LPA) for maintaining logical coherence under first-order updates. Negation and converse force a tensor factorization separating entity-pair context from relation content; composition collapses to conjunction, which on linear features must be bilinear—incompatible with negation—causing a collapse of the feature map and undermining LPA.
AI.motivation: To determine whether local (first-order) updates preserve logical coherence in neural networks and to formalize this through the Linear Propagation Assumption using relation algebra.
AI.method: Algebraic analysis of three relation-operations (negation, converse, composition). Derive necessary tensor factorization for negation/converse, show composition reduces to conjunction, and prove that any conjunction on linear features must be bilinear; demonstrate incompatibility with negation.
AI.result: Negation and converse require a tensor factorization that separates entity-pair context from relation content. Composition reduces to conjunction; any conjunction on linear features is bilinear, which cannot accommodate negation, forcing the feature map to collapse.
AI.conclusion: The LPA is structurally limited: failures in knowledge editing, the reversal curse, and multi-hop reasoning may arise from these intrinsic constraints, rather than solely from optimization or data issues. This prompts rethinking of representations beyond LPA-compatible structures.

title: Beyond Parameter Finetuning: Test-Time Representation Refinement for Node Classification
summary: Graph Neural Networks frequently exhibit significant performance degradation in the out-of-distribution test scenario. While test-time training (TTT) offers a promising solution, existing Parameter Finetuning (PaFT) paradigm suffer from catastrophic forgetting, hindering their real-world applicability. We propose TTReFT, a novel Test-Time Representation FineTuning framework that transitions the adaptation target from model parameters to latent representations. Specifically, TTReFT achieves this through three key innovations: (1) uncertainty-guided node selection for specific interventions, (2) low-rank representation interventions that preserve pre-trained knowledge, and (3) an intervention-aware masked autoencoder that dynamically adjust masking strategy to accommodate the node selection scheme. Theoretically, we establish guarantees for TTReFT in OOD settings. Empirically, extensive experiments across five benchmark datasets demonstrate that TTReFT achieves consistent and superior performance. Our work establishes representation finetuning as a new paradigm for graph TTT, offering both theoretical grounding and immediate practical utility for real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: TTReFT introduces test-time representation fine-tuning for graphs, shifting adaptation from model parameters to latent representations via three components—uncertainty-guided node interventions, low-rank representation updates, and an intervention-aware masked autoencoder—backed by theory and strong results across five benchmarks.
AI.motivation: GNNs show significant performance drops in out-of-distribution (OOD) settings. Test-time training via parameter finetuning (PaFT) suffers catastrophic forgetting, limiting real-world deployment. A representation-level adaptation strategy could retain knowledge while adapting to OOD data.
AI.method: 1) Uncertainty-guided node selection to target interventions; 2) Low-rank representation interventions to preserve pre-trained knowledge; 3) Intervention-aware masked autoencoder that dynamically adjusts masking based on the node selection scheme. The framework shifts adaptation targets from model parameters to latent representations and comes with theoretical guarantees for OOD settings.
AI.result: The authors provide theoretical guarantees for TTReFT in OOD settings. Empirically, TTReFT yields consistent, superior performance across five benchmark datasets, demonstrating the efficacy of representation finetuning for graph TTT.
AI.conclusion: Representation finetuning is a viable, grounded new paradigm for graph test-time training, combining uncertainty-guided interventions, stability-preserving updates, and adaptive masking to enable robust, practical deployment.

title: Breaking the Overscaling Curse: Thinking Parallelism Before Parallel Thinking
summary: Parallel thinking enhances LLM reasoning by multi-path sampling and aggregation. In system-level evaluations, a global parallelism level N is allocated to all samples, typically set large to maximize overall dataset accuracy. However, due to sample heterogeneity, some samples can achieve comparable performance with a smaller N'< N, causing budget redundancy. This incompatibility between system-level efficacy and sample-level efficiency constitutes the overscaling curse. In this paper, we formalize and quantify the overscaling curse, showing its universality and severity in practice, and analyze its trigger mechanism. We then propose a lightweight method, T2, to break the overscaling curse, which utilizes latent representations to estimate the optimal parallelism level for each sample before decoding. Experiments show that T2 significantly reduces cost while maintaining comparable performance, enabling more efficient parallel thinking.

AI.tldr: Formalizes the overscaling curse in system-wide parallel thinking for LLMs, introducing T2 to adapt per-sample parallelism using latent representations, achieving lower cost with similar accuracy.
AI.motivation: Address budget redundancy caused by applying a single large parallelism level N to all samples despite heterogeneity; improve sample-level efficiency without sacrificing system-level accuracy.
AI.method: The work formalizes and quantifies the overscaling curse, analyzes its trigger mechanism, and proposes T2, a lightweight estimator that uses latent representations to predict the optimal per-sample parallelism before decoding.
AI.result: Experiments show substantial cost reductions with comparable performance across samples, validating T2’s effectiveness in enabling more efficient parallel thinking.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive per-sample parallelism via latent representations can break the overscaling curse and improve the efficiency of parallel thinking in LLM reasoning.

title: LAMP: Look-Ahead Mixed-Precision Inference of Large Language Models
summary: Mixed-precision computations are a hallmark of the current stage of AI, driving the progress in large language models towards efficient, locally deployable solutions. This article addresses the floating-point computation of compositionally-rich functions, concentrating on transformer inference. Based on the rounding error analysis of a composition $f(g(\mathrm{x}))$, we provide an adaptive strategy that selects a small subset of components of $g(\mathrm{x})$ to be computed more accurately while all other computations can be carried out with lower accuracy. We then explain how this strategy can be applied to different compositions within a transformer and illustrate its overall effect on transformer inference. We study the effectiveness of this algorithm numerically on GPT-2 models and demonstrate that already very low recomputation rates allow for improvements of up to two orders of magnitude in accuracy.

AI.tldr: An adaptive mixed-precision recomputation strategy for transformer inference based on rounding error analysis of f(g(x)); selectively recomputing a subset of g's components with higher precision to reduce overall error; demonstrated on GPT-2 with large accuracy gains at low recomputation rates.
AI.motivation: Improve the accuracy-efficiency trade-off of mixed-precision computations in compositionally-rich functions, especially transformer inference, by leveraging per-component error awareness.
AI.method: Perform rounding-error analysis of the composite f(g(x)); develop a scheme to compute only a small subset of g(x) components at high precision while the rest use lower precision; apply the strategy to different compositions inside a transformer and integrate it into inference; assess its impact on overall model behavior.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on GPT-2 show that very small recomputation rates can yield up to two orders of magnitude improvement in accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive selective recomputation enables substantial accuracy gains with minimal recomputation overhead, suggesting practical viability for efficient, high-precision-aware transformer inference under mixed precision.

title: Training Memory in Deep Neural Networks: Mechanisms, Evidence, and Measurement Gaps
summary: Modern deep-learning training is not memoryless. Updates depend on optimizer moments and averaging, data-order policies (random reshuffling vs with-replacement, staged augmentations and replay), the nonconvex path, and auxiliary state (teacher EMA/SWA, contrastive queues, BatchNorm statistics). This survey organizes mechanisms by source, lifetime, and visibility. It introduces seed-paired, function-space causal estimands; portable perturbation primitives (carry/reset of momentum/Adam/EMA/BN, order-window swaps, queue/teacher tweaks); and a reporting checklist with audit artifacts (order hashes, buffer/BN checksums, RNG contracts). The conclusion is a protocol for portable, causal, uncertainty-aware measurement that attributes how much training history matters across models, data, and regimes.

AI.tldr: A survey of non-memoryless dynamics in modern deep learning, proposing causal, portable perturbations and a reporting protocol to attribute training-history effects across models, data, and regimes.
AI.motivation: To understand how optimizer state, data order, augmentations, and auxiliary components influence training trajectories and outcomes, and to enable reproducible, uncertainty-aware attribution.
AI.method: Organizes mechanisms by source, lifetime, and visibility; defines seed-paired, function-space causal estimands; introduces portable perturbations (carry/reset of momentum/Adam/EMA/BN, order-window swaps, queue/teacher tweaks); and a reporting checklist with audit artifacts.
AI.result: A protocol for portable, causal, uncertainty-aware measurement that attributes how much training history matters across models, data, and regimes.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides reproducible methods and artifacts for evaluating the role of historical information in training performance, enabling cross-domain comparisons.

title: Sampling-Free Privacy Accounting for Matrix Mechanisms under Random Allocation
summary: We study privacy amplification for differentially private model training with matrix factorization under random allocation (also known as the balls-in-bins model). Recent work by Choquette-Choo et al. (2025) proposes a sampling-based Monte Carlo approach to compute amplification parameters in this setting. However, their guarantees either only hold with some high probability or require random abstention by the mechanism. Furthermore, the required number of samples for ensuring $(ε,δ)$-DP is inversely proportional to $δ$. In contrast, we develop sampling-free bounds based on Rényi divergence and conditional composition. The former is facilitated by a dynamic programming formulation to efficiently compute the bounds. The latter complements it by offering stronger privacy guarantees for small $ε$, where Rényi divergence bounds inherently lead to an over-approximation. Our framework applies to arbitrary banded and non-banded matrices. Through numerical comparisons, we demonstrate the efficacy of our approach across a broad range of matrix mechanisms used in research and practice.

AI.tldr: Sampling-free privacy amplification bounds for differentially private matrix-factorization under balls-in-bins allocation, via Rényi divergence and conditional composition, with dynamic-programming-based bound computation; applicable to both banded and non-banded matrices; strengthens guarantees for small ε and reduces reliance on Monte Carlo sampling.
AI.motivation: In DP matrix-factorization under random allocation (balls-in-bins), existing sampling-based methods (e.g., Choquette-Choo et al., 2025) either provide only high-probability guarantees or require random abstention, and their δ-inverse sample complexity can be prohibitive. A sampling-free, tight framework is needed for practical DP guarantees.
AI.method: Introduce sampling-free bounds based on Rényi divergence and conditional composition. Use dynamic programming to efficiently compute Rényi-based bounds. Compare and complement with conditional composition bounds to achieve stronger privacy for small ε. The framework applies to arbitrary banded and non-banded matrices, enabling broad applicability to matrix mechanisms.
AI.result: A unified, sampling-free privacy amplification framework for matrix-factorization under random allocation, with computationally efficient bounds via dynamic programming. The Rényi-divergence-based bounds provide tight guarantees, particularly for small ε, while conditional composition improves over-approximations. Numerical experiments show effectiveness across a broad class of matrix mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers sampling-free, robust privacy amplification bounds for DP matrix factorization under random allocation, with broad applicability and improved guarantees for small ε. It offers a practical alternative to Monte Carlo sampling and suggests future directions for tighter Rényi-based and composition-based bounds and broader empirical validation.

title: Generative Design of Ship Propellers using Conditional Flow Matching
summary:
In this paper, we explore the use of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) for ship propeller design. While traditional forward machine learning models predict the performance of mechanical components based on given design parameters, GenAI models aim to generate designs that achieve specified performance targets. In particular, we employ conditional flow matching to establish a bidirectional mapping between design parameters and simulated noise that is conditioned on performance labels. This approach enables the generation of multiple valid designs corresponding to the same performance targets by sampling over the noise vector.
  To support model training, we generate data using a vortex lattice method for numerical simulation and analyze the trade-off between model accuracy and the amount of available data. We further propose data augmentation using pseudo-labels derived from less data-intensive forward surrogate models, which can often improve overall model performance. Finally, we present examples of distinct propeller geometries that exhibit nearly identical performance characteristics, illustrating the versatility and potential of GenAI in engineering design.

AI.tldr: GenAI-based propeller design using conditional flow matching to generate multiple geometries that meet specified performance targets, leveraging vortex lattice simulations and data augmentation via pseudo-labels from cheaper forward surrogates.
AI.motivation: Enable target-driven generative design in propulsion, moving beyond forward prediction to produce diverse, feasible designs while addressing data efficiency and enabling multiple solutions for the same performance goals.
AI.method: Train a conditional flow-matching model to establish a bidirectional mapping between design parameters and simulated noise conditioned on performance labels; sample noise to generate multiple valid designs for a target performance. Data are generated via vortex lattice method simulations; analyze accuracy vs data quantity; augment data with pseudo-labels from less data-intensive forward surrogate models to improve performance.
AI.result: Demonstrates generation of distinct propeller geometries that exhibit nearly identical performance characteristics; evaluates data-efficiency and augmentation impact, illustrating the versatility of GenAI in engineering design.
AI.conclusion: GenAI with conditional flow matching is a viable approach for target-driven, diverse propeller design, with data augmentation from surrogate models offering potential performance gains when data is limited; future work should validate manufacturability and extend to broader performance targets.

title: Seg-MoE: Multi-Resolution Segment-wise Mixture-of-Experts for Time Series Forecasting Transformers
summary: Transformer-based models have recently made significant advances in accurate time-series forecasting, but even these architectures struggle to scale efficiently while capturing long-term temporal dynamics. Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layers are a proven solution to scaling problems in natural language processing. However, existing MoE approaches for time-series forecasting rely on token-wise routing mechanisms, which may fail to exploit the natural locality and continuity of temporal data. In this work, we introduce Seg-MoE, a sparse MoE design that routes and processes contiguous time-step segments rather than making independent expert decisions. Token segments allow each expert to model intra-segment interactions directly, naturally aligning with inherent temporal patterns. We integrate Seg-MoE layers into a time-series Transformer and evaluate it on multiple multivariate long-term forecasting benchmarks. Seg-MoE consistently achieves state-of-the-art forecasting accuracy across almost all prediction horizons, outperforming both dense Transformers and prior token-wise MoE models. Comprehensive ablation studies confirm that segment-level routing is the key factor driving these gains. Our results show that aligning the MoE routing granularity with the inherent structure of time series provides a powerful, yet previously underexplored, inductive bias, opening new avenues for conditionally sparse architectures in sequential data modeling.

AI.tldr: Seg-MoE introduces segment-level sparse Mixture-of-Experts for time-series Transformers, routing contiguous time-step segments instead of individual tokens. This alignment with temporal locality yields state-of-the-art long-horizon forecasting across benchmarks, with ablations confirming segment routing as the key factor.
AI.motivation: Scale accurate time-series forecasting with Transformers while preserving temporal locality and dynamics. Token-wise MoE gates may ignore local continuity in time-series; segment-level routing provides a natural inductive bias that matches the data structure.
AI.method: Integrate Seg-MoE layers into a time-series Transformer. Route and process contiguous time-step segments with each expert responsible for intra-segment interactions. Evaluate on multivariate long-term forecasting benchmarks and perform comprehensive ablations to isolate the impact of segment-level routing.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art forecasting accuracy across almost all horizons, outperforming dense Transformers and prior token-wise MoE models. Ablations indicate segment-level routing is the essential factor driving gains.
AI.conclusion: Aligning MoE routing granularity with the inherent structure of time series yields a powerful inductive bias, enabling efficient yet accurate conditional computation for sequential data and suggesting broader applicability of segment-level MoE in time-series modeling.

title: Identifiable Equivariant Networks are Layerwise Equivariant
summary: We investigate the relation between end-to-end equivariance and layerwise equivariance in deep neural networks. We prove the following: For a network whose end-to-end function is equivariant with respect to group actions on the input and output spaces, there is a parameter choice yielding the same end-to-end function such that its layers are equivariant with respect to some group actions on the latent spaces. Our result assumes that the parameters of the model are identifiable in an appropriate sense. This identifiability property has been established in the literature for a large class of networks, to which our results apply immediately, while it is conjectural for others. The theory we develop is grounded in an abstract formalism, and is therefore architecture-agnostic. Overall, our results provide a mathematical explanation for the emergence of equivariant structures in the weights of neural networks during training -- a phenomenon that is consistently observed in practice.

AI.tldr: End-to-end equivariance implies the existence of a layerwise equivariant representation: for networks with input/output equivariance, there exists a parameter choice yielding the same end-to-end function while enforcing latent-space layerwise equivariances. The result relies on an identifiability property and is architecture-agnostic, explaining observed emergence of equivariant weights during training.
AI.motivation: To explain why symmetry (equivariance) properties observed at the end-to-end level of neural networks manifest as symmetric structures in intermediate layers, and to establish a unifying, architecture-agnostic theory based on identifiability of model parameters.
AI.method: An abstract, formal demonstration: given end-to-end equivariance, there exists a parameter arrangement that preserves the same map yet imposes equivariance at each layer with respect to latent-group actions; the proof hinges on an appropriate identifiability assumption, known for a large class of networks.
AI.result: Existence theorem: end-to-end equivariance implies a layerwise equivariant representation is achievable via parameter choice, under identifiability. The framework is architecture-agnostic and applies to networks for which identifiability is established; for other cases, the claim remains conjectural.
AI.conclusion: Provides a mathematical rationale for the empirically observed emergence of equivariant weight structures during training, linking end-to-end symmetry to layerwise symmetry under identifiable parameters; broad applicability but contingent on identifiability.

title: SWE-Spot: Building Small Repo-Experts with Repository-Centric Learning
summary: The deployment of coding agents in privacy-sensitive and resource-constrained environments drives the demand for capable open-weight Small Language Models (SLMs). However, they suffer from a fundamental capability gap: unlike frontier large models, they lack the inference-time strong generalization to work with complicated, unfamiliar codebases. We identify that the prevailing Task-Centric Learning (TCL) paradigm, which scales exposure across disparate repositories, fails to address this limitation. In response, we propose Repository-Centric Learning (RCL), a paradigm shift that prioritizes vertical repository depth over horizontal task breadth, suggesting SLMs must internalize the "physics" of a target software environment through parametric knowledge acquisition, rather than attempting to recover it via costly inference-time search. Following this new paradigm, we design a four-unit Repository-Centric Experience, transforming static codebases into interactive learning signals, to train SWE-Spot-4B, a family of highly compact models built as repo-specialized experts that breaks established scaling trends, outperforming open-weight models up to larger (e.g., CWM by Meta, Qwen3-Coder-30B) and surpassing/matching efficiency-focused commercial models (e.g., GPT-4.1-mini, GPT-5-nano) across multiple SWE tasks. Further analysis reveals that RCL yields higher training sample efficiency and lower inference costs, emphasizing that for building efficient intelligence, repository mastery is a distinct and necessary dimension that complements general coding capability.

AI.tldr: A repository-centric learning (RCL) paradigm tailors SLMs to internalize the physics of a target software environment, enabling repo-specialized experts (SWE-Spot-4B) that outperform open-weight models and some efficiency-focused commercial models, by prioritizing vertical repository depth over horizontal task breadth and transforming codebases into interactive learning signals.
AI.motivation: To overcome the generalization gap of open-weight SLMs in unfamiliar codebases under privacy/resource constraints. The Task-Centric Learning (TCL) approach, which broad-exposes models across multiple repositories, fails to capture the necessary internalized understanding of a specific software environment.
AI.method: Introduce RCL as a shift from horizontal task breadth to vertical repository depth. Develop a four-unit Repository-Centric Experience to convert static repositories into interactive learning signals. Train SWE-Spot-4B family as repo-specialized experts that leverage this environment-specific knowledge.
AI.result: SWE-Spot-4B outperforms open-weight models of larger scale (e.g., CWM by Meta, Qwen3-Coder-30B) and matches or surpasses efficiency-focused commercial models (e.g., GPT-4.1-mini, GPT-5-nano) across multiple SWE tasks. RCL also yields higher training sample efficiency and lower inference costs, suggesting repository mastery as a distinct, necessary dimension for efficient intelligent coding systems.
AI.conclusion: Repository mastery complements general coding capabilities. RCL provides a path to more sample-efficient, cost-effective SLMs in privacy-sensitive settings by internalizing environment-specific 'physics' through repository-centric training.

title: Gauge-invariant representation holonomy
