comment: 6 pages, 1 table. Accepted at European Symposium on Artificial Neural Networks (ESANN 2026) as oral presentation
summary: We introduce a scalable method to approximate the kernel of the Linearized Laplace Approximation (LLA). For this, we use a surrogate deep neural network (DNN) that learns a compact feature representation whose inner product replicates the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK). This avoids the need to compute large Jacobians. Training relies solely on efficient Jacobian-vector products, allowing to compute predictive uncertainty on large-scale pre-trained DNNs. Experimental results show similar or improved uncertainty estimation and calibration compared to existing LLA approximations. Notwithstanding, biasing the learned kernel significantly enhances out-of-distribution detection. This remarks the benefits of the proposed method for finding better kernels than the NTK in the context of LLA to compute prediction uncertainty given a pre-trained DNN.

AI.tldr: A scalable method to learn a kernel surrogate for LLA by training a DNN to mimic NTK, enabling efficient uncertainty estimation on large pre-trained models.
AI.motivation: LLA kernel estimation requires large Jacobians, making scalability challenging; a learned kernel could offer similar or better uncertainty estimates with reduced computation, and possibly improve OOD detection.
AI.method: Train a surrogate DNN to learn a compact feature representation whose inner product matches the NTK, using Jacobian-vector products instead of full Jacobians; apply to predictive uncertainty on large pre-trained DNNs.
AI.result: Empirical evidence shows comparable or improved uncertainty estimation and calibration versus existing LLA approximations; deliberately biasing the learned kernel further improves out-of-distribution detection.
AI.conclusion: A scalable, learnable kernel approach can outperform NTK in the LLA framework for uncertainty quantification on large DNNs, with kernel design impacting OOD performance.

title: Constrained Meta Reinforcement Learning with Provable Test-Time Safety
summary: Meta reinforcement learning (RL) allows agents to leverage experience across a distribution of tasks on which the agent can train at will, enabling faster learning of optimal policies on new test tasks. Despite its success in improving sample complexity on test tasks, many real-world applications, such as robotics and healthcare, impose safety constraints during testing. Constrained meta RL provides a promising framework for integrating safety into meta RL. An open question in constrained meta RL is how to ensure the safety of the policy on the real-world test task, while reducing the sample complexity and thus, enabling faster learning of optimal policies. To address this gap, we propose an algorithm that refines policies learned during training, with provable safety and sample complexity guarantees for learning a near optimal policy on the test tasks. We further derive a matching lower bound, showing that this sample complexity is tight.

AI.tldr: A constrained meta-RL algorithm with provable safety and tight sample-complexity guarantees for test tasks, achieved via policy refinement during training to obtain a near-optimal safe policy.
AI.motivation: In real-world domains, safety constraints during testing hinder rapid learning; constrained meta-RL aims to learn across task distributions while guaranteeing safety and improving sample efficiency.
AI.method: The paper proposes a policy-refinement algorithm that operates on policies learned during training to ensure safety on test tasks; it provides formal safety and sample-complexity guarantees and derives a matching lower bound, establishing the tightness of the sample complexity.
AI.result: Provable safety guarantees, finite-sample performance guarantees on test tasks, and a matching lower bound demonstrating optimality of the derived sample complexity.
AI.conclusion: This work advances constrained meta-RL by delivering safe, sample-efficient adaptation to new tasks and proving the optimality of the approach in a worst-case sense.

title: READY: Reward Discovery for Meta-Black-Box Optimization
summary: Meta-Black-Box Optimization (MetaBBO) is an emerging avenue within Optimization community, where algorithm design policy could be meta-learned by reinforcement learning to enhance optimization performance. So far, the reward functions in existing MetaBBO works are designed by human experts, introducing certain design bias and risks of reward hacking. In this paper, we use Large Language Model~(LLM) as an automated reward discovery tool for MetaBBO. Specifically, we consider both effectiveness and efficiency sides. On effectiveness side, we borrow the idea of evolution of heuristics, introducing tailored evolution paradigm in the iterative LLM-based program search process, which ensures continuous improvement. On efficiency side, we additionally introduce multi-task evolution architecture to support parallel reward discovery for diverse MetaBBO approaches. Such parallel process also benefits from knowledge sharing across tasks to accelerate convergence. Empirical results demonstrate that the reward functions discovered by our approach could be helpful for boosting existing MetaBBO works, underscoring the importance of reward design in MetaBBO. We provide READY's project at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ICML_READY-747F.

AI.tldr: Automated reward discovery for MetaBBO using LLMs with an evolutionary, multi-task framework to improve effectiveness and efficiency; demonstrates boosted performance and knowledge sharing across MetaBBO methods.
AI.motivation: Address design bias and reward hacking risk in handcrafted MetaBBO reward signals by leveraging LLMs to automatically discover robust rewards, while ensuring scalability and cross-task knowledge transfer.
AI.method: 1) Use a large language model as the reward-discovery engine for MetaBBO. 2) Effectiveness: implement a tailored evolution paradigm within iterative LLM-based program search to continuously improve discovered rewards. 3) Efficiency: introduce a multi-task evolution architecture enabling parallel reward discovery across diverse MetaBBO approaches and sharing knowledge to accelerate convergence.
AI.result: Empirical results show that the automatically discovered reward functions can boost existing MetaBBO methods, highlighting the value of reward design and automated discovery.
AI.conclusion: Automated, LLM-assisted reward discovery with an evolutionary, multi-task framework is effective for MetaBBO, reducing human bias in reward design while enhancing optimization performance and scalability.

title: Visual Disentangled Diffusion Autoencoders: Scalable Counterfactual Generation for Foundation Models
summary: Foundation models, despite their robust zero-shot capabilities, remain vulnerable to spurious correlations and 'Clever Hans' strategies. Existing mitigation methods often rely on unavailable group labels or computationally expensive gradient-based adversarial optimization. To address these limitations, we propose Visual Disentangled Diffusion Autoencoders (DiDAE), a novel framework integrating frozen foundation models with disentangled dictionary learning for efficient, gradient-free counterfactual generation directly for the foundation model. DiDAE first edits foundation model embeddings in interpretable disentangled directions of the disentangled dictionary and then decodes them via a diffusion autoencoder. This allows the generation of multiple diverse, disentangled counterfactuals for each factual, much faster than existing baselines, which generate single entangled counterfactuals. When paired with Counterfactual Knowledge Distillation, DiDAE-CFKD achieves state-of-the-art performance in mitigating shortcut learning, improving downstream performance on unbalanced datasets.

AI.tldr: Introduces Visual Disentangled Diffusion Autoencoders (DiDAE) to generate gradient-free, diverse counterfactuals for frozen foundation models using disentangled dictionary learning, enabling robust mitigation of spurious correlations; when combined with Counterfactual Knowledge Distillation (DiDAE-CFKD), achieves state-of-the-art shortcut learning mitigation.
AI.motivation: Foundation models are vulnerable to spurious correlations and Clever Hans shortcuts. Existing fixes often require unavailable group labels or expensive gradient-based optimization. A gradient-free, efficient method for generating counterfactuals is needed.
AI.method: Freeze foundation models and apply disentangled dictionary learning to edit embeddings along interpretable, disentangled directions. Decode edits with a diffusion autoencoder to produce multiple diverse, disentangled counterfactuals per factual input. Combine with Counterfactual Knowledge Distillation (CFKD) to form DiDAE-CFKD.
AI.result: DiDAE enables rapid generation of multiple, disentangled counterfactuals per factual instance, outperforming baselines that generate single entangled counterfactuals. DiDAE-CFKD achieves state-of-the-art mitigation of shortcut learning and improves downstream performance on unbalanced datasets.
AI.conclusion: DiDAE provides a scalable, gradient-free framework for generating disentangled counterfactuals to robustify foundation models against spurious correlations. When paired with CFKD (DiDAE-CFKD), it yields leading performance in mitigating shortcut learning.

title: MoHETS: Long-term Time Series Forecasting with Mixture-of-Heterogeneous-Experts
summary: Real-world multivariate time series can exhibit intricate multi-scale structures, including global trends, local periodicities, and non-stationary regimes, which makes long-horizon forecasting challenging. Although sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) approaches improve scalability and specialization, they typically rely on homogeneous MLP experts that poorly capture the diverse temporal dynamics of time series data. We address these limitations with MoHETS, an encoder-only Transformer that integrates sparse Mixture-of-Heterogeneous-Experts (MoHE) layers. MoHE routes temporal patches to a small subset of expert networks, combining a shared depthwise-convolution expert for sequence-level continuity with routed Fourier-based experts for patch-level periodic structures. MoHETS further improves robustness to non-stationary dynamics by incorporating exogenous information via cross-attention over covariate patch embeddings. Finally, we replace parameter-heavy linear projection heads with a lightweight convolutional patch decoder, improving parameter efficiency, reducing training instability, and allowing a single model to generalize across arbitrary forecast horizons. We validate across seven multivariate benchmarks and multiple horizons, with MoHETS consistently achieving state-of-the-art performance, reducing the average MSE by $12\%$ compared to strong recent baselines, demonstrating effective heterogeneous specialization for long-term forecasting.

AI.tldr: MoHETS is an encoder-only Transformer with sparse Mixture-of-Heterogeneous-Experts (MoHE) layers for long-horizon forecasting of real-world multivariate time series. It combines a shared depthwise-convolution expert with routed Fourier-based experts, uses cross-attention over covariates for exogenous inputs, and employs a convolutional patch decoder. It achieves state-of-the-art across seven benchmarks, reducing MSE by ~12% vs strong baselines.
AI.motivation: Real-world multivariate time series exhibit multi-scale structures (global trends, local periodicities) and non-stationary regimes, making long-horizon forecasting challenging. Sparse MoE with homogeneous MLPs struggles to capture diverse temporal dynamics and remains computationally burdensome.
AI.method: Introduce MoHE layers within an encoder-only Transformer. Route temporal patches to a small subset of heterogeneous experts: a shared depthwise-convolution expert for sequence-level continuity and routed Fourier-based experts for patch-level periodic structures. Incorporate exogenous information via cross-attention over covariate patch embeddings. Replace parameter-heavy linear heads with a lightweight convolutional patch decoder to improve efficiency and horizon generalization.
AI.result: Empirical validation on seven multivariate benchmarks across multiple horizons shows MoHETS achieves state-of-the-art performance, reducing average MSE by 12% compared with strong recent baselines.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective heterogeneous specialization for long-term forecasting, with robustness to non-stationary dynamics, improved parameter efficiency, and the ability to generalize across arbitrary forecast horizons.

title: Low-Rank Plus Sparse Matrix Transfer Learning under Growing Representations and Ambient Dimensions
summary:
Learning systems often expand their ambient features or latent representations over time, embedding earlier representations into larger spaces with limited new latent structure. We study transfer learning for structured matrix estimation under simultaneous growth of the ambient dimension and the intrinsic representation, where a well-estimated source task is embedded as a subspace of a higher-dimensional target task.
  We propose a general transfer framework in which the target parameter decomposes into an embedded source component, low-dimensional low-rank innovations, and sparse edits, and develop an anchored alternating projection estimator that preserves transferred subspaces while estimating only low-dimensional innovations and sparse modifications. We establish deterministic error bounds that separate target noise, representation growth, and source estimation error, yielding strictly improved rates when rank and sparsity increments are small.
  We demonstrate the generality of the framework by applying it to two canonical problems. For Markov transition matrix estimation from a single trajectory, we derive end-to-end theoretical guarantees under dependent noise. For structured covariance estimation under enlarged dimensions, we provide complementary theoretical analysis in the appendix and empirically validate consistent transfer gains.

AI.tldr: Transfers subspace knowledge from a source task to a growing target task in structured matrix estimation by embedding the source into the target, estimating low-rank innovations and sparse edits via an anchored alternating projection method, with deterministic error bounds.
AI.motivation: As ambient dimension and intrinsic representation expand, leverage a well-estimated source task to improve estimation in the target task.
AI.method: Decompose the target parameter into an embedded source component, low-dimensional low-rank innovations, and sparse edits; use an anchored alternating projection estimator that preserves transferred subspaces while estimating only the innovations and edits.
AI.result: Deterministic error bounds that separate target noise, representation growth, and source estimation error; strictly improved rates when rank and sparsity increments are small; end-to-end guarantees for Markov transition matrix estimation with dependent noise; supplementary analysis for enlarged-parameter covariance estimation and empirical validation of transfer gains.
AI.conclusion: The framework is general and applicable to canonical problems like Markov transition estimation and structured covariance estimation, showing consistent transfer gains under controlled representation growth.

title: Managing Solution Stability in Decision-Focused Learning with Cost Regularization
summary: Decision-focused learning integrates predictive modeling and combinatorial optimization by training models to directly improve decision quality rather than prediction accuracy alone. Differentiating through combinatorial optimization problems represents a central challenge, and recent approaches tackle this difficulty by introducing perturbation-based approximations. In this work, we focus on estimating the objective function coefficients of a combinatorial optimization problem. Our study demonstrates that fluctuations in perturbation intensity occurring during the learning phase can lead to ineffective training, by establishing a theoretical link to the notion of solution stability in combinatorial optimization. We propose addressing this issue by introducing a regularization of the estimated cost vectors which improves the robustness and reliability of the learning process, as demonstrated by extensive numerical experiments.

AI.tldr: Regularizing estimated cost vectors in perturbation-based decision-focused learning improves robustness to perturbation intensity fluctuations in estimating objective coefficients of combinatorial problems.
AI.motivation: Decision-focused learning aligns predictive modeling with decision quality, but differentiating through combinatorial optimization is challenging. Perturbation-based approximations are used to enable gradients, yet fluctuations in perturbation intensity during training can destabilize learning. A theoretical link to solution stability motivates a robustness approach.
AI.method: Introduce a regularization term on the estimated cost vectors; establish a theoretical connection to solution stability to explain robustness gains; validate the approach with extensive numerical experiments.
AI.result: Regularization reduces sensitivity to perturbation fluctuations, improving robustness and reliability of the learning process; demonstrated by extensive numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: Regularizing objective coefficients in perturbation-based decision-focused learning yields more robust and reliable coefficient estimation for combinatorial optimization, mitigating the adverse effects of perturbation intensity variability.

title: Not All Code Is Equal: A Data-Centric Study of Code Complexity and LLM Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly exhibit strong reasoning abilities, often attributed to their capacity to generate chain-of-thought-style intermediate reasoning. Recent work suggests that exposure to code can further enhance these skills, but existing studies largely treat code as a generic training signal, leaving open the question of which properties of code actually contribute to improved reasoning. To address this gap, we study the structural complexity of code, which captures control flow and compositional structure that may shape how models internalise multi-step reasoning during fine-tuning. We examine two complementary settings: solution-driven complexity, where complexity varies across multiple solutions to the same problem, and problem-driven complexity, where complexity reflects variation in the underlying tasks. Using cyclomatic complexity and logical lines of code to construct controlled fine-tuning datasets, we evaluate a range of open-weight LLMs on diverse reasoning benchmarks. Our findings show that although code can improve reasoning, structural properties strongly determine its usefulness. In 83% of experiments, restricting fine-tuning data to a specific structural complexity range outperforms training on structurally diverse code, pointing to a data-centric path for improving reasoning beyond scaling.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning LLMs with code of controlled structural complexity reveals that structure, not quantity, governs reasoning gains; focusing on a narrow complexity range improves reasoning in most experiments.
AI.motivation: Demonstrate which properties of code contribute to reasoning improvements in LLMs to guide data-efficient fine-tuning beyond scaling.
AI.method: Construct datasets using cyclomatic complexity and logical lines of code; study two scenarios: solution-driven and problem-driven complexity; evaluate open-weight LLMs on reasoning benchmarks.
AI.result: Structural complexity strongly influences usefulness of code; data with restricted complexity outperforms structurally diverse code in 83% of experiments.
AI.conclusion: A data-centric approach, selecting code by structural properties, can enhance reasoning capabilities without increasing model size.

title: A Low-Complexity Plug-and-Play Deep Learning Model for Generalizable Massive MIMO Precoding
summary: Massive multiple-input multiple-output (mMIMO) downlink precoding offers high spectral efficiency but remains challenging to deploy in practice because near-optimal algorithms such as the weighted minimum mean squared error (WMMSE) are computationally expensive, and sensitive to SNR and channel-estimation quality, while existing deep learning (DL)-based solutions often lack robustness and require retraining for each deployment site. This paper proposes a plug-and-play precoder (PaPP), a DL framework with a backbone that can be trained for either fully digital (FDP) or hybrid beamforming (HBF) precoding and reused across sites, transmit-power levels, and with varying amounts of channel estimation error, avoiding the need to train a new model from scratch at each deployment. PaPP combines a high-capacity teacher and a compact student with a self-supervised loss that balances teacher imitation and normalized sum-rate, trained using meta-learning domain-generalization and transmit-power-aware input normalization. Numerical results on ray-tracing data from three unseen sites show that the PaPP FDP and HBF models both outperform conventional and deep learning baselines, after fine-tuning with a small set of local unlabeled samples. Across both architectures, PaPP achieves more than 21$\times$ reduction in modeled computation energy and maintains good performance under channel-estimation errors, making it a practical solution for energy-efficient mMIMO precoding.

AI.tldr: Plug-and-play precoder PaPP uses a teacher-student DL framework with meta-learning to enable robust, transferable FDP/HBF precoding for mMIMO, reducing computation energy and adapting with little unlabeled data across sites and power levels.
AI.motivation: WMMSE delivers near-optimal performance but is computationally expensive and sensitive to SNR and channel estimation; existing DL approaches lack robustness and require retraining per deployment site; a transferable, energy-efficient solution is needed.
AI.method: PaPP combines a high-capacity teacher with a compact student using a self-supervised loss that balances teacher imitation and normalized sum-rate; employs meta-learning for domain-generalization and transmit-power-aware input normalization; supports fully digital and hybrid beamforming, and can be reused across sites with minimal local unlabeled data for fine-tuning.
AI.result: On ray-tracing data from three unseen sites, PaPP's FDP and HBF models outperform conventional methods and DL baselines after fine-tuning with a small amount of local unlabeled samples; achieves >21× reduction in modeled computation energy and remains robust to channel-estimation errors.
AI.conclusion: PaPP offers a practical, energy-efficient mMIMO precoding framework with strong cross-site generalization and minimal retraining needs, suitable for deployment across varying transmit powers and channel conditions.

title: Breaking the Regional Barrier: Inductive Semantic Topology Learning for Worldwide Air Quality Forecasting
summary: Global air quality forecasting grapples with extreme spatial heterogeneity and the poor generalization of existing transductive models to unseen regions. To tackle this, we propose OmniAir, a semantic topology learning framework tailored for global station-level prediction. By encoding invariant physical environmental attributes into generalizable station identities and dynamically constructing adaptive sparse topologies, our approach effectively captures long-range non-Euclidean correlations and physical diffusion patterns across unevenly distributed global networks. We further curate WorldAir, a massive dataset covering over 7,800 stations worldwide. Extensive experiments show that OmniAir achieves state-of-the-art performance against 18 baselines, maintaining high efficiency and scalability with speeds nearly 10 times faster than existing models, while effectively bridging the monitoring gap in data-sparse regions.

AI.tldr: OmniAir proposes semantic topology learning for global station-level air quality prediction, achieving state-of-the-art performance with adaptive graphs and a large WorldAir dataset, while being efficient and scalable.
AI.motivation: Address extreme spatial heterogeneity and poor generalization of transductive models to unseen regions in global air quality forecasting.
AI.method: Encode invariant physical environmental attributes into station identities; dynamically construct adaptive sparse topologies to capture long-range non-Euclidean correlations and diffusion patterns; curate WorldAir dataset with 7,800+ stations.
AI.result: Outperforms 18 baselines; state-of-the-art accuracy; nearly 10x faster than existing models; scalable to large networks and improves monitoring in data-sparse regions.
AI.conclusion: Semantic topology learning with invariant attributes and adaptive graphs provides effective, scalable global station-level forecasting, and the WorldAir dataset supports broad benchmarking.

title: Hardware-Triggered Backdoors
summary: Machine learning models are routinely deployed on a wide range of computing hardware. Although such hardware is typically expected to produce identical results, differences in its design can lead to small numerical variations during inference. In this work, we show that these variations can be exploited to create backdoors in machine learning models. The core idea is to shape the model's decision function such that it yields different predictions for the same input when executed on different hardware. This effect is achieved by locally moving the decision boundary close to a target input and then refining numerical deviations to flip the prediction on selected hardware. We empirically demonstrate that these hardware-triggered backdoors can be created reliably across common GPU accelerators. Our findings reveal a novel attack vector affecting the use of third-party models, and we investigate different defenses to counter this threat.

AI.tldr: Hardware-induced numerical variations enable backdoors in ML models by flipping predictions on different hardware; demonstrated on GPUs; defenses discussed.
AI.motivation: Diversity of hardware causes subtle numeric differences; this can be exploited to embed stealthy, hardware-triggered backdoors, especially in models from third parties.
AI.method: Relocate the decision boundary near a target input; induce hardware-specific numerical deviations that flip the output; validate across common GPU accelerators; assess defenses.
AI.result: Feasibility of hardware-triggered backdoors demonstrated across GPUs; reliable across accelerators; introduces a new threat vector; evaluated defensive options.
AI.conclusion: Raises security concerns for ML deployment on heterogeneous hardware; underscores need for hardware-agnostic validation and defense mechanisms; suggests directions for mitigation.

title: Optimistic Transfer under Task Shift via Bellman Alignment
summary:
We study online transfer reinforcement learning (RL) in episodic Markov decision processes, where experience from related source tasks is available during learning on a target task. A fundamental difficulty is that task similarity is typically defined in terms of rewards or transitions, whereas online RL algorithms operate on Bellman regression targets. As a result, naively reusing source Bellman updates introduces systematic bias and invalidates regret guarantees.
  We identify one-step Bellman alignment as the correct abstraction for transfer in online RL and propose re-weighted targeting (RWT), an operator-level correction that retargets continuation values and compensates for transition mismatch via a change of measure. RWT reduces task mismatch to a fixed one-step correction and enables statistically sound reuse of source data.
  This alignment yields a two-stage RWT $Q$-learning framework that separates variance reduction from bias correction. Under RKHS function approximation, we establish regret bounds that scale with the complexity of the task shift rather than the target MDP. Empirical results in both tabular and neural network settings demonstrate consistent improvements over single-task learning and naïve pooling, highlighting Bellman alignment as a model-agnostic transfer principle for online RL.

AI.tldr: Proposes one-step Bellman alignment and Re-weighted Targeting (RWT) to enable safe transfer in online RL, yielding a two-stage Q-learning framework with regret guarantees under RKHS and empirical gains.
AI.motivation: Task similarity is often defined via rewards or transitions, but online RL operates on Bellman regression targets; naive reuse of source Bellman updates induces bias and undermines regret guarantees.
AI.method: Define one-step Bellman alignment as the transfer abstraction; introduce RWT operator-level correction that reweights continuation values through a change of measure to compensate for transition mismatch; present a two-stage RWT Q-learning framework separating variance reduction from bias correction; analyze under RKHS function approximation.
AI.result: Regret bounds scale with the complexity of the task shift rather than the target MDP; empirical results in both tabular and neural network settings show consistent improvements over single-task learning and naive pooling.
AI.conclusion: Bellman alignment serves as a model-agnostic transfer principle for online RL, enabling sound reuse of source data and improving learning performance.

title: LoRIF: Low-Rank Influence Functions for Scalable Training Data Attribution
summary: Training data attribution (TDA) identifies which training examples most influenced a model's prediction. The best-performing TDA methods exploits gradients to define an influence function. To overcome the scalability challenge arising from gradient computation, the most popular strategy is random projection (e.g., TRAK, LoGRA). However, this still faces two bottlenecks when scaling to large training sets and high-quality attribution: \emph{(i)} storing and loading projected per-example gradients for all $N$ training examples, where query latency is dominated by I/O; and \emph{(ii)} forming the $D \times D$ inverse Hessian approximation, which costs $O(D^2)$ memory. Both bottlenecks scale with the projection dimension $D$, yet increasing $D$ is necessary for attribution quality -- creating a quality-scalability tradeoff. We introduce \textbf{LoRIF (Low-Rank Influence Functions)}, which exploits low-rank structures of gradient to address both bottlenecks. First, we store rank-$c$ factors of the projected per-example gradients rather than full matrices, reducing storage and query-time I/O from $O(D)$ to $O(c\sqrt{D})$ per layer per sample. Second, we use truncated SVD with the Woodbury identity to approximate the Hessian term in an $r$-dimensional subspace, reducing memory from $O(D^2)$ to $O(Dr)$. On models from 0.1B to 70B parameters trained on datasets with millions of examples, LoRIF achieves up to 20$\times$ storage reduction and query-time speedup compared to LoGRA, while matching or exceeding its attribution quality. LoRIF makes gradient-based TDA practical at frontier scale.

AI.tldr: LoRIF introduces a low-rank approach to training data attribution (TDA) that reduces storage and I/O and Hessian memory by exploiting gradient low-rank structure. It stores rank-c factors of projected per-example gradients and uses truncated SVD with Woodbury to approximate the Hessian in an r-dimensional subspace, achieving up to 20x storage reduction and faster query times relative to LoGRA while maintaining or improving attribution quality.
AI.motivation: Gradient-based influence-function TDA scales poorly due to large per-example gradients and the O(D^2) Hessian inverse computation. Existing random-projection methods (TRAK, LoGRA) ease some computation but incur I/O and memory bottlenecks that worsen with projection dimension D, creating a quality-scalability tradeoff.
AI.method: LoRIF stores low-rank (rank-c) factors of projected per-example gradients, reducing per-layer per-sample storage from O(D) to O(c√D). It also applies a truncated SVD to approximate the Hessian term within an r-dimensional subspace using the Woodbury identity, reducing Hessian memory from O(D^2) to O(Dr). These steps enable scalable gradient-based TDA at large model scales.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on models from 0.1B to 70B parameters trained on large datasets shows LoRIF achieves up to 20× storage reduction and query-time speedup compared to LoGRA, while matching or surpassing LoGRA's attribution quality.
AI.conclusion: LoRIF makes gradient-based TDA practical at frontier-scale models by addressing both I/O/storage and memory bottlenecks without sacrificing attribution quality.

title: Robust Multimodal Representation Learning in Healthcare
summary: Medical multimodal representation learning aims to integrate heterogeneous data into unified patient representations to support clinical outcome prediction. However, real-world medical datasets commonly contain systematic biases from multiple sources, which poses significant challenges for medical multimodal representation learning. Existing approaches typically focus on effective multimodal fusion, neglecting inherent biased features that affect the generalization ability. To address these challenges, we propose a Dual-Stream Feature Decorrelation Framework that identifies and handles the biases through structural causal analysis introduced by latent confounders. Our method employs a causal-biased decorrelation framework with dual-stream neural networks to disentangle causal features from spurious correlations, utilizing generalized cross-entropy loss and mutual information minimization for effective decorrelation. The framework is model-agnostic and can be integrated into existing medical multimodal learning methods. Comprehensive experiments on MIMIC-IV, eICU, and ADNI datasets demonstrate consistent performance improvements.

AI.tldr: A Dual-Stream Feature Decorrelation Framework is proposed to disentangle causal features from spurious biases in medical multimodal data using latent confounders and causal analysis; it is model-agnostic and shows improved generalization on MIMIC-IV, eICU, and ADNI.
AI.motivation: Real-world medical multimodal datasets contain systematic biases from multiple sources that impair generalization. Existing fusion-centric methods overlook bias features and spurious correlations; there is a need to decorrelate causal information from biases.
AI.method: A causal-biased decorrelation framework with dual-stream neural networks that separate causal features from biased ones via latent confounders and structural causal analysis. It employs generalized cross-entropy loss and mutual information minimization to achieve decorrelation and is model-agnostic, enabling integration into existing multimodal learning pipelines.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on MIMIC-IV, eICU, and ADNI demonstrate consistent performance improvements over baselines.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling causal features from biases improves generalization in medical multimodal learning; the proposed framework is adaptable and can augment existing methods.

title: Entropy-Based Dimension-Free Convergence and Loss-Adaptive Schedules for Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion generative models synthesize samples by discretizing reverse-time dynamics driven by a learned score (or denoiser). Existing convergence analyses of diffusion models typically scale at least linearly with the ambient dimension, and sharper rates often depend on intrinsic-dimension assumptions or other geometric restrictions on the target distribution. We develop an alternative, information-theoretic approach to dimension-free convergence that avoids any geometric assumptions. Under mild assumptions on the target distribution, we bound KL divergence between the target and generated distributions by $O(H^2/K)$ (up to endpoint factors), where $H$ is the Shannon entropy and $K$ is the number of sampling steps. Moreover, using a reformulation of the KL divergence, we propose a Loss-Adaptive Schedule (LAS) for efficient discretization of reverse SDE which is lightweight and relies only on the training loss, requiring no post-training heavy computation. Empirically, LAS improves sampling quality over common heuristic schedules.

AI.tldr: Dimension-free convergence for diffusion models: KL bound O(H^2/K) and a Loss-Adaptive Schedule (LAS) for reverse SDE discretization; empirical gains.
AI.motivation: Overcome dimension-dependent convergence bounds and costly post-training schemes; provide a simple, information-theoretic, dimension-free framework.
AI.method: Information-theoretic derivation bounding KL between target and generated distributions; reformulate KL; design LAS based on training loss requiring no heavy post-training computation.
AI.result: KL divergence is bounded by O(H^2/K) (up to endpoint factors) with H as Shannon entropy and K as sampling steps; LAS improves sampling quality over common heuristic schedules in experiments.
AI.conclusion: Provides a dimension-free theoretical guarantee and a practical, low-overhead discretization strategy for diffusion models, with empirical performance benefits.

title: Clarity: The Flexibility-Interpretability Trade-Off in Sparsity-aware Concept Bottleneck Models
summary: The widespread adoption of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) across fields has amplified concerns about model interpretability. Distressingly, these models are often treated as black-boxes, with limited or non-existent investigation of their decision making process. Despite numerous post- and ante-hoc interepretability methods, systematic and objective evaluation of the learned representations remains limited, particularly for sparsity-aware methods that are increasingly considered to "induce interpretability". In this work, we focus on Concept Bottleneck Models and investigate how different modeling decisions affect the emerging representations. We introduce the notion of clarity, a measure, capturing the interplay between the downstream performance and the sparsity and precision of the concept representation, while proposing an interpretability assessment framework using datasets with ground truth concept annotations. We consider both VLM- and attribute predictor-based CBMs, and three different sparsity-inducing strategies: per example $\ell_1, \ell_0$ and Bernoulli-based formulations. Our experiments reveal a critical trade-off between flexibility and interpretability, under which a given method can exhibit markedly different behaviors even at comparable performance levels. The code will be made publicly available upon publication.

AI.tldr: Introduces a clarity measure for Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) in Vision-Language Models, analyzes how design choices and sparsity strategies affect interpretability, and proposes an evaluation framework using ground-truth concept annotations; finds a trade-off between flexibility and interpretability with different sparsity methods; code to be released.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of systematic, objective evaluation of learned representations in sparsity-aware CBMs for vision-language models and to understand how modeling decisions influence interpretability.
AI.method: Proposes a novel interpretability metric, 'clarity,' that balances downstream performance with the sparsity and precision of concept representations. Evaluates both VLM-based CBMs and attribute-predictor-based CBMs under three sparsity-inducing strategies (per-example L1, L0, and Bernoulli-based). Uses datasets with ground-truth concept annotations to enable quantitative interpretability assessment.
AI.result: Reveals a critical trade-off between flexibility and interpretability: a given method can behave very differently at similar performance levels. No single approach universally superior; interpretability depends on the sparsity strategy and model type. Code will be publicly released.
AI.conclusion: Interpretability in CBMs is shaped not just by accuracy but by how sparsity, precision, and model architecture interact. The paper offers an evaluation framework and a new clarity metric to guide design choices and future work toward balancing interpretability with predictive performance.

title: Dependence of Equilibrium Propagation Training Success on Network Architecture
summary: The rapid rise of artificial intelligence has led to an unsustainable growth in energy consumption. This has motivated progress in neuromorphic computing and physics-based training of learning machines as alternatives to digital neural networks. Many theoretical studies focus on simple architectures like all-to-all or densely connected layered networks. However, these may be challenging to realize experimentally, e.g. due to connectivity constraints. In this work, we investigate the performance of the widespread physics-based training method of equilibrium propagation for more realistic architectural choices, specifically, locally connected lattices. We train an XY model and explore the influence of architecture on various benchmark tasks, tracking the evolution of spatially distributed responses and couplings during training. Our results show that sparse networks with only local connections can achieve performance comparable to dense networks. Our findings provide guidelines for further scaling up architectures based on equilibrium propagation in realistic settings.

AI.tldr: Equilibrium propagation on locally connected lattices can match dense networks in XY-model tasks, suggesting energy-efficient, realistic neuromorphic architectures.
AI.motivation: Address rising energy consumption in AI and the mismatch between idealized dense models and hardware-constrained, sparse connectivity.
AI.method: Apply equilibrium propagation to train an XY model on locally connected lattices; monitor spatial responses and couplings during training; compare to dense networks on benchmark tasks.
AI.result: Sparse, locally connected networks achieve performance comparable to dense networks; training dynamics remain interpretable through distributed spatial activity and evolving couplings.
AI.conclusion: Local connectivity suffices for competitive performance under equilibrium propagation, enabling scalable, energy-efficient hardware-oriented designs; guides future architecture scaling under realistic constraints.

title: Embracing Aleatoric Uncertainty in Medical Multimodal Learning with Missing Modalities
summary: Medical multimodal learning faces significant challenges with missing modalities prevalent in clinical practice. Existing approaches assume equal contribution of modality and random missing patterns, neglecting inherent uncertainty in medical data acquisition. In this regard, we propose the Aleatoric Uncertainty Modeling (AUM) that explicitly quantifies unimodal aleatoric uncertainty to address missing modalities. Specifically, AUM models each unimodal representation as a multivariate Gaussian distribution to capture aleatoric uncertainty and enable principled modality reliability quantification. To adaptively aggregate captured information, we develop a dynamic message-passing mechanism within a bipartite patient-modality graph using uncertainty-aware aggregation mechanism. Through this process, missing modalities are naturally accommodated, while more reliable information from available modalities is dynamically emphasized to guide representation generation. Our AUM framework achieves an improvement of 2.26% AUC-ROC on MIMIC-IV mortality prediction and 2.17% gain on eICU, outperforming existing state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: Proposes Aleatoric Uncertainty Modeling (AUM) to handle missing medical modalities by representing unimodal features as multivariate Gaussians and using a dynamic message-passing mechanism on a bipartite patient-modality graph to quantify reliability and adaptively aggregate information, achieving improved AUC-ROC on MIMIC-IV and eICU.
AI.motivation: Address the prevalence of missing modalities and the uncertainty inherent in medical data acquisition, which is not adequately captured by methods assuming equal modality contribution or random missingness.
AI.method: Model each unimodal representation as a multivariate Gaussian to capture aleatoric uncertainty. Use a dynamic message-passing mechanism on a bipartite patient-modality graph with uncertainty-aware aggregation to adaptively weight available modalities and accommodate missing ones.
AI.result: Achieves 2.26% AUC-ROC improvement on MIMIC-IV mortality prediction and 2.17% on eICU compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: AUM enables principled quantification of modality reliability and adaptive information aggregation under missing modalities, leading to improved multimodal medical predictions.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Data-Based Method for Fast and Reliable Shape Optimization
summary: Data-based optimization (DBO) offers a promising approach for efficiently optimizing shape for better aerodynamic performance by leveraging a pretrained surrogate model for offline evaluations during iterations. However, DBO heavily relies on the quality of the training database. Samples outside the training distribution encountered during optimization can lead to significant prediction errors, potentially misleading the optimization process. Therefore, incorporating uncertainty quantification into optimization is critical for detecting outliers and enhancing robustness. This study proposes an uncertainty-aware data-based optimization (UA-DBO) framework to monitor and minimize surrogate model uncertainty during DBO. A probabilistic encoder-decoder surrogate model is developed to predict uncertainties associated with its outputs, and these uncertainties are integrated into a model-confidence-aware objective function to penalize samples with large prediction errors during data-based optimization process. The UA-DBO framework is evaluated on two multipoint optimization problems aimed at improving airfoil drag divergence and buffet performance. Results demonstrate that UA-DBO consistently reduces prediction errors in optimized samples and achieves superior performance gains compared to original DBO. Moreover, compared to multipoint optimization based on full computational simulations, UA-DBO offers comparable optimization effectiveness while significantly accelerating optimization speed.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty-aware DBO (UA-DBO) uses a probabilistic encoder–decoder surrogate to quantify and penalize predictive uncertainty during optimization, improving robustness and speed versus standard DBO and approaching full-sim multipoint optimization in effectiveness.
AI.motivation: DBO depends on training data; out-of-distribution samples yield large surrogate errors and mislead optimization; need to detect and mitigate uncertainty for robust, data-efficient aerodynamic optimization.
AI.method: Train a probabilistic encoder–decoder surrogate that outputs predictive uncertainty; integrate uncertainty into a model-confidence-aware objective for data-based optimization; test on two airfoil problems (drag divergence, buffet).
AI.result: UA-DBO consistently reduces surrogate prediction errors on optimized samples; achieves better performance than standard DBO; near-parity with full-CFD multipoint optimization in efficacy but with significant speedup.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware surrogates enhance robustness and efficiency of DBO; applicable to other domains; potential considerations include calibration of uncertainty, computational overhead, and dependence on database quality.

title: Bridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning
summary: Temporal knowledge graph reasoning (TKGR) aims to predict future events by inferring missing entities with dynamic knowledge structures. Existing LLM-based reasoning methods prioritize contextual over structural relations, struggling to extract relevant subgraphs from dynamic graphs. This limits structural information understanding, leading to unstructured, hallucination-prone inferences especially with temporal inconsistencies. To address this problem, we propose IGETR (Integration of Graph and Editing-enhanced Temporal Reasoning), a hybrid reasoning framework that combines the structured temporal modeling capabilities of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with the contextual understanding of LLMs. IGETR operates through a three-stage pipeline. The first stage aims to ground the reasoning process in the actual data by identifying structurally and temporally coherent candidate paths through a temporal GNN, ensuring that inference starts from reliable graph-based evidence. The second stage introduces LLM-guided path editing to address logical and semantic inconsistencies, leveraging external knowledge to refine and enhance the initial paths. The final stage focuses on integrating the refined reasoning paths to produce predictions that are both accurate and interpretable. Experiments on standard TKG benchmarks show that IGETR achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming strong baselines with relative improvements of up to 5.6% on Hits@1 and 8.1% on Hits@3 on the challenging ICEWS datasets. Additionally, we execute ablation studies and additional analyses confirm the effectiveness of each component.

AI.tldr: IGETR combines Graph Neural Network-based temporal reasoning with LLM-guided path editing to ground and refine candidate reasoning paths, achieving state-of-the-art results on ICEWS TKGR benchmarks.
AI.motivation: TKGR methods often rely on unstructured LLM reasoning, leading to hallucinations and poor use of dynamic graph structure; a hybrid approach should ground reasoning in reliable temporal-structural evidence before leveraging external knowledge.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) temporal GNN grounding to identify structurally and temporally coherent candidate paths; (2) LLM-guided path editing to fix logical/semantic inconsistencies and augment with external knowledge; (3) integration of refined paths to produce accurate, interpretable predictions; extensive ablations validate each component.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on ICEWS benchmarks with relative improvements up to 5.6% in Hits@1 and 8.1% in Hits@3; ablation studies confirm the contribution of each stage.
AI.conclusion: A principled hybrid framework that leverages structured temporal graph modeling with contextual LLM reasoning yields accurate and interpretable TKGR predictions, addressing hallucination risks and temporal inconsistencies.

title: Investigation into using stochastic embedding representations for evaluating the trustworthiness of the Fréchet Inception Distance
summary: Feature embeddings acquired from pretrained models are widely used in medical applications of deep learning to assess the characteristics of datasets; e.g. to determine the quality of synthetic, generated medical images. The Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) is one popular synthetic image quality metric that relies on the assumption that the characteristic features of the data can be detected and encoded by an InceptionV3 model pretrained on ImageNet1K (natural images). While it is widely known that this makes it less effective for applications involving medical images, the extent to which the metric fails to capture meaningful differences in image characteristics is not obviously known. Here, we use Monte Carlo dropout to compute the predictive variance in the FID as well as a supplemental estimate of the predictive variance in the feature embedding model's latent representations. We show that the magnitudes of the predictive variances considered exhibit varying degrees of correlation with the extent to which test inputs (ImageNet1K validation set augmented at various strengths, and other external datasets) are out-of-distribution relative to its training data, providing some insight into the effectiveness of their use as indicators of the trustworthiness of the FID.

AI.tldr: Assesses the reliability of Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) for medical images by estimating predictive uncertainty with Monte Carlo dropout in both the FID computation and the embedding model’s latent features, finding that predictive variances correlate variably with out-of-distribution extent and can inform the trustworthiness of FID.
AI.motivation: FID assumes that a pretrained ImageNet-based feature extractor captures data characteristics; this is suspect for medical images. The work investigates whether predictive uncertainty can reveal when FID is unreliable for medical-image quality assessment.
AI.method: Apply Monte Carlo dropout to estimate predictive variance in two places: (i) the FID computation itself and (ii) the latent feature embeddings used for FID. Evaluate on ImageNet1K validation set with varying augmentations and on other external datasets to probe out-of-distribution behavior.
AI.result: Predictive variances show varying degrees of correlation with the extent to which inputs are out-of-distribution relative to training data, providing insight into when FID may be trustworthy as a quality metric for medical images and when it may fail.
AI.conclusion: Estimating predictive uncertainty in FID and in feature embeddings can serve as a diagnostic for the reliability of FID in medical-image applications, guiding its use or prompting alternative domain-specific metrics.

title: PowerGenie: Analytically-Guided Evolutionary Discovery of Superior Reconfigurable Power Converters
summary: Discovering superior circuit topologies requires navigating an exponentially large design space-a challenge traditionally reserved for human experts. Existing AI methods either select from predefined templates or generate novel topologies at a limited scale without rigorous verification, leaving large-scale performance-driven discovery underexplored. We present PowerGenie, a framework for automated discovery of higher-performance reconfigurable power converters at scale. PowerGenie introduces: (1) an automated analytical framework that determines converter functionality and theoretical performance limits without component sizing or SPICE simulation, and (2) an evolutionary finetuning method that co-evolves a generative model with its training distribution through fitness selection and uniqueness verification. Unlike existing methods that suffer from mode collapse and overfitting, our approach achieves higher syntax validity, function validity, novelty rate, and figure-of-merit (FoM). PowerGenie discovers a novel 8-mode reconfigurable converter with 23% higher FoM than the best training topology. SPICE simulations confirm average absolute efficiency gains of 10% across 8 modes and up to 17% at a single mode. Code will be released upon publication.

AI.tldr: PowerGenie enables scalable automated discovery of high-performance reconfigurable power converters via analytical evaluation and evolutionary generative fine-tuning, yielding a novel 8-mode converter with ~23% FoM gain and SPICE-verified efficiency improvements.
AI.motivation: Overcome the exponential design space and limitations of template-based or small-scale AI methods in discovering high-performance converter topologies, enabling large-scale, performance-driven exploration with verification.
AI.method: Two-part framework: (1) an automated analytical framework to determine converter functionality and theoretical performance limits without component sizing or SPICE; (2) evolutionary finetuning that co-evolves a generative model with its training distribution using fitness selection and explicit uniqueness verification to mitigate mode collapse and overfitting.
AI.result: Discovery of an 8-mode reconfigurable converter with 23% higher FoM than the best training topology; SPICE simulations show average absolute efficiency gains of 10% across 8 modes and up to 17% for a single mode; improved syntax validity, function validity, and novelty over baselines.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates scalable, verified topology discovery for power converters and yields substantial performance gains; code release promised to support reproducibility.

title: Elign: Equivariant Diffusion Model Alignment from Foundational Machine Learning Force Fields
summary: Generative models for 3D molecular conformations must respect Euclidean symmetries and concentrate probability mass on thermodynamically favorable, mechanically stable structures. However, E(3)-equivariant diffusion models often reproduce biases from semi-empirical training data rather than capturing the equilibrium distribution of a high-fidelity Hamiltonian. While physics-based guidance can correct this, it faces two computational bottlenecks: expensive quantum-chemical evaluations (e.g., DFT) and the need to repeat such queries at every sampling step. We present Elign, a post-training framework that amortizes both costs. First, we replace expensive DFT evaluations with a faster, pretrained foundational machine-learning force field (MLFF) to provide physical signals. Second, we eliminate repeated run-time queries by shifting physical steering to the training phase. To achieve the second amortization, we formulate reverse diffusion as a reinforcement learning problem and introduce Force--Energy Disentangled Group Relative Policy Optimization (FED-GRPO) to fine-tune the denoising policy. FED-GRPO includes a potential-based energy reward and a force-based stability reward, which are optimized and group-normalized independently. Experiments show that Elign generates conformations with lower gold-standard DFT energies and forces, while improving stability. Crucially, inference remains as fast as unguided sampling, since no energy evaluations are required during generation.

AI.tldr: Post-training framework Elign amortizes expensive DFT evaluations and runtime energy queries for 3D molecular conformer diffusion by using a pretrained ML force field for physical signals and a reinforcement-learning-based fine-tuning (FED-GRPO) of the denoising policy; achieves faster inference with lower DFT energies/forces and improved stability.
AI.motivation: 3D molecular conformer generation must respect Euclidean symmetries and sample thermodynamically favorable, mechanically stable structures. E(3)-equivariant diffusion can inherit biases from semi-empirical training data and requires expensive quantum-chemical evaluations for guidance; the goal is to make physics-guided sampling practical by removing runtime energy evaluations and expensive computations.
AI.method: Two-tier amortization: (1) Replace expensive DFT with a pretrained foundational ML force field (MLFF) to provide physical signals during diffusion; (2) Move steering into training by reframing reverse diffusion as reinforcement learning; introduce FED-GRPO (Force–Energy Disentangled Group Relative Policy Optimization) with a potential-based energy reward and a force-based stability reward, both independently optimized and group-normalized; denoising policy is fine-tuned accordingly.
AI.result: Experiments show generated conformations have lower gold-standard DFT energies and forces and improved stability; inference remains as fast as unguided sampling because no energy evaluations are required during generation.
AI.conclusion: Elign successfully amortizes both DFT evaluations and runtime energy queries, enabling physics-guided diffusion with fast inference and improved conformational quality; future work should examine generalization to new systems, sensitivity to MLFF quality, and refinement of reward designs.

title: Geometry of Drifting MDPs with Path-Integral Stability Certificates
summary: Real-world reinforcement learning is often \emph{nonstationary}: rewards and dynamics drift, accelerate, oscillate, and trigger abrupt switches in the optimal action. Existing theory often represents nonstationarity with coarse-scale models that measure \emph{how much} the environment changes, not \emph{how} it changes locally -- even though acceleration and near-ties drive tracking error and policy chattering. We take a geometric view of nonstationary discounted Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) by modeling the environment as a differentiable homotopy path and tracking the induced motion of the optimal Bellman fixed point. This yields a length-curvature-kink signature of intrinsic complexity: cumulative drift, acceleration/oscillation, and action-gap-induced nonsmoothness. We prove a solver-agnostic path-integral stability bound and derive gap-safe feasible regions that certify local stability away from switch regimes. Building on these results, we introduce \textit{Homotopy-Tracking RL (HT-RL)} and \textit{HT-MCTS}, lightweight wrappers that estimate replay-based proxies of length, curvature, and near-tie proximity online and adapt learning or planning intensity accordingly. Experiments show improved tracking and dynamic regret over matched static baselines, with the largest gains in oscillatory and switch-prone regimes.

AI.tldr: A geometric, solver-agnostic approach to nonstationary RL using a differentiable homotopy of the environment to track the Bellman optimal fixed point; introduces HT-RL and HT-MCTS that adapt learning/planning based on online proxies of path length, curvature, and near-tie proximity; shows improved dynamic regret, especially in oscillatory and switch-prone regimes.
AI.motivation: Real-world RL is nonstationary; existing theory uses coarse-scale environment-change measures and misses local geometry such as acceleration and near-ties that cause tracking error and policy chattering. A geometric, homotopy-based view aims to quantify intrinsic complexity and enable proactive adaptation.
AI.method: Model the environment as a differentiable homotopy path and track the induced motion of the optimal Bellman fixed point. Derive a length-curvature-kink signature of nonstationary complexity (drift, acceleration/oscillation, and nonsmoothness from near ties). Prove a solver-agnostic path-integral stability bound and construct gap-safe feasible regions away from switch regimes. Develop HT-RL and HT-MCTS wrappers that online estimate proxies for length, curvature, and near-tie proximity to adapt learning or planning intensity.
AI.result: Experiments show improved tracking and dynamic regret over matched static baselines, with the largest gains in oscillatory and switch-prone regimes.
AI.conclusion: Offers a geometry-driven, solver-agnostic framework for nonstationary RL. The HT wrappers enable adaptive control of learning/planning based on online geometric proxies, improving robustness to nonstationarity and abrupt regime shifts.

title: Negatives-Dominant Contrastive Learning for Generalization in Imbalanced Domains
summary: Imbalanced Domain Generalization (IDG) focuses on mitigating both domain and label shifts, both of which fundamentally shape the model's decision boundaries, particularly under heterogeneous long-tailed distributions across domains. Despite its practical significance, it remains underexplored, primarily due to the technical complexity of handling their entanglement and the paucity of theoretical foundations. In this paper, we begin by theoretically establishing the generalization bound for IDG, highlighting the role of posterior discrepancy and decision margin. This bound motivates us to focus on directly steering decision boundaries, marking a clear departure from existing methods. Subsequently, we technically propose a novel Negative-Dominant Contrastive Learning (NDCL) for IDG to enhance discriminability while enforce posterior consistency across domains. Specifically, inter-class decision-boundary separation is enhanced by placing greater emphasis on negatives as the primary signal in our contrastive learning, naturally amplifying gradient signals for minority classes to avoid the decision boundary being biased toward majority classes. Meanwhile, intra-class compactness is encouraged through a re-weighted cross-entropy strategy, and posterior consistency across domains is enforced through a prediction-central alignment strategy. Finally, rigorous yet challenging experiments on benchmarks validate the effectiveness of our NDCL. The code is available at https://github.com/Alrash/NDCL.

AI.tldr: A theoretical and algorithmic study of Imbalanced Domain Generalization (IDG) introducing Negative-Dominant Contrastive Learning (NDCL) to steer decision boundaries under long-tailed, multi-domain shifts, supported by a generalization bound and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of theoretical foundations and practical methods in IDG, where domain and label shifts entangle to shape decision boundaries; aim to directly steer these boundaries for better minority-class generalization.
AI.method: Derive a generalization bound for IDG highlighting posterior discrepancy and decision margin; propose NDCL that (1) emphasizes negatives in contrastive learning to enhance inter-class boundary separation and minority-class gradient signals, (2) uses a re-weighted cross-entropy for intra-class compactness, and (3) enforces posterior consistency across domains via a prediction-centered alignment strategy.
AI.result: The approach yields improved discriminability and posterior alignment across domains; rigorous experiments on benchmarks validate NDCL's effectiveness, with code released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: NDCL offers a principled way to bias decision boundaries away from majority classes while maintaining cross-domain posterior consistency, addressing both domain and label shifts in IDG; strong empirical results support its potential and invite further theoretical and empirical exploration.

title: Rate-Distortion Optimization for Transformer Inference
summary: Transformers achieve superior performance on many tasks, but impose heavy compute and memory requirements during inference. This inference can be made more efficient by partitioning the process across multiple devices, which, in turn, requires compressing its intermediate representations. In this work, we introduce a principled rate-distortion-based framework for lossy compression that learns compact encodings that explicitly trade off bitrate against accuracy. Experiments on language benchmarks show that the proposed codec achieves substantial savings with improved accuracy in some cases, outperforming more complex baseline methods. We characterize and analyze the rate-distortion performance of transformers, offering a unified lens for understanding performance in representation coding. This formulation extends information-theoretic concepts to define the gap between rate and entropy, and derive some of its bounds. We further develop probably approximately correct (PAC)-style bounds for estimating this gap. For different architectures and tasks, we empirically demonstrate that their rates are driven by these bounds, adding to the explainability of the formulation.

AI.tldr: Introduces a rate-distortion-based lossy compression framework for transformer intermediate representations to enable cross-device inference with controlled bitrate; shows bitrate savings and occasional accuracy gains on language tasks, and provides theoretical bounds and PAC-style estimates to explain rate-distortion behavior.
AI.motivation: Transformers are powerful but expensive at inference; partitioning across devices and compressing intermediate representations can reduce compute/memory; a principled trade-off framework with theoretical guarantees is needed to explain rate-distortion behavior in representation coding for transformer models.
AI.method: Proposes a principled rate-distortion-based codec that learns compact encodings by optimizing bitrate vs distortion (accuracy) trade-off; conducts experiments on language benchmarks; derives PAC-style bounds and a unified rate-entropy metric; analyzes rate-distortion performance across architectures/tasks.
AI.result: Achieves substantial bitrate savings with improved accuracy in some cases; outperforms more complex baselines; empirical results support the proposed rate-distortion framework and align with theoretical bounds, providing explainability across architectures/tasks.
AI.conclusion: Extends information-theoretic concepts to transformer representation coding by defining a rate–entropy gap, deriving bounds, and establishing PAC-style estimates; offers a unified framework to understand and predict rate-distortion behavior and to enable more efficient inference across architectures and tasks.

title: Exploring Diverse Generation Paths via Inference-time Stiefel Activation Steering
comment: 34 pages, 2 figures. Accepted for publication at ICLR 2026
summary: Language models often default to a narrow set of high-probability outputs, leaving their generation paths homogeneous and prone to mode collapse. Sampling-based strategies inject randomness but still struggle to guarantee diversity across multiple concurrent generation runs. We address this limitation by introducing STARS ($\textbf{St}$iefel-based $\textbf{A}$ctivation Steering for Diverse $\textbf{R}$ea$\textbf{S}$oning), a training-free, inference-time intervention method that transforms activation steering into an exploration engine. At each token, STARS collects the hidden activations of concurrent generation runs and optimizes multiple additive steering directions jointly on the Stiefel manifold. STARS maximizes the geometric volume of the steered activations, while the Stiefel manifold induces orthogonality of the steering interventions. This formulation explicitly promotes divergent activation vectors of concurrent generation runs, and implicitly promotes divergent generation trajectories. This manifold optimization formulation can be solved using a Riemannian gradient descent algorithm with convergence guarantees, but this algorithm is too time-consuming for real-time inference. To guarantee low latency, we further design a lightweight one-step update with an aggressive, closed-form stepsize. For test case generation and scientific discovery benchmarks, STARS consistently outperforms standard sampling methods, achieving greater diversity without sacrificing qualitative performance.

AI.tldr: STARS is a training-free, inference-time activation steering method on the Stiefel manifold to promote diverse, concurrent generation runs, achieving higher diversity with low latency.
AI.motivation: Language models exhibit mode collapse and limited diversity; existing sampling methods do not guarantee diversity across concurrent generations at real-time latency. A principled, geometry-aware intervention can induce diverse trajectories.
AI.method: Collect activations from concurrent generations at each token, learn multiple additive steering directions on the Stiefel manifold to maximize the volume of steered activations while enforcing orthogonality; implement via a Riemannian gradient descent with convergence guarantees, and a lightweight one-step closed-form update for real-time latency.
AI.result: STARS achieves greater diversity than standard sampling on test-case generation and scientific discovery benchmarks, with no evident loss in qualitative performance.
AI.conclusion: A training-free, inference-time manifold-based activation steering method offers a practical and theoretically grounded path to diverse generation, balancing exploration and quality with low latency.

title: Putting a Face to Forgetting: Continual Learning meets Mechanistic Interpretability
summary: Catastrophic forgetting in continual learning is often measured at the performance or last-layer representation level, overlooking the underlying mechanisms. We introduce a mechanistic framework that offers a geometric interpretation of catastrophic forgetting as the result of transformations to the encoding of individual features. These transformations can lead to forgetting by reducing the allocated capacity of features (worse representation) and disrupting their readout by downstream computations. Analysis of a tractable model formalizes this view, allowing us to identify best- and worst-case scenarios. Through experiments on this model, we empirically test our formal analysis and highlight the detrimental effect of depth. Finally, we demonstrate how our framework can be used in the analysis of practical models through the use of Crosscoders. We present a case study of a Vision Transformer trained on sequential CIFAR-10. Our work provides a new, feature-centric vocabulary for continual learning.

AI.tldr: A feature-centric mechanistic framework models catastrophic forgetting as geometric transformations of feature encodings, linking capacity allocation and readout disruption; tractable-model analysis reveals best- and worst-case transformations; depth is detrimental; Crosscoders show practical applicability; case study with a Vision Transformer on sequential CIFAR-10 yields a new feature-centric vocabulary for continual learning.
AI.motivation: Address the gap that forgetting is typically measured at end-task performance or last-layer representations, neglecting underlying mechanisms; a mechanistic, feature-level interpretation can unify understanding and guide design.
AI.method: Introduce a geometric/mechanistic framework for feature encodings; analyze a tractable model to derive theoretical best- and worst-case forgetting scenarios; conduct experiments on the model to test predictions; apply the framework to Crosscoders and validate with a Vision Transformer trained on sequential CIFAR-10.
AI.result: Identifies how transformations reduce feature capacity and disrupt readout, explains depth-related deterioration, and demonstrates practical applicability via Crosscoders; case study confirms framework usefulness and provides a new feature-centric vocabulary.
AI.conclusion: A feature-centric, mechanistic perspective provides deeper insight into continual learning forgetting, linking representation dynamics to practical performance; informs design choices and analysis across models.

title: TBDFiltering: Sample-Efficient Tree-Based Data Filtering
summary: The quality of machine learning models depends heavily on their training data. Selecting high-quality, diverse training sets for large language models (LLMs) is a difficult task, due to the lack of cheap and reliable quality metrics. While querying existing LLMs for document quality is common, this is not scalable to the large number (billions) of documents used in training. Instead, practitioners often use classifiers trained on sparse quality signals. In this paper, we propose a text-embedding-based hierarchical clustering approach that adaptively selects the documents to be evaluated by the LLM to estimate cluster quality. We prove that our method is query efficient: under the assumption that the hierarchical clustering contains a subtree such that each leaf cluster in the tree is pure enough (i.e., it mostly contains either only good or only bad documents), with high probability, the method can correctly predict the quality of each document after querying a small number of documents. The number of such documents is proportional to the size of the smallest subtree with (almost) pure leaves, without the algorithm knowing this subtree in advance. Furthermore, in a comprehensive experimental study, we demonstrate the benefits of our algorithm compared to other classifier-based filtering methods.

AI.tldr: An embedding-based hierarchical clustering method for efficiently estimating LLM training data quality via selective LLM queries, with a theoretical purity-based guarantee and empirical gains over classifier-based filtering.
AI.motivation: Curating high-quality, diverse training data for LLMs is hard because cheap, reliable quality metrics are scarce. Querying billions of documents with LLMs is impractical, and classifier-based proxies on sparse signals may be insufficient.
AI.method: Construct text embeddings and build a hierarchical clustering of candidate documents. Adaptively select a small subset of documents to query the LLM to assess cluster quality, then propagate quality estimates to leaves. The key theoretical result shows that if there exists a subtree whose leaves are nearly pure (mostly good or bad), the method will correctly predict the quality of most documents with high probability, querying only documents proportional to the size of the smallest such pure subtree, without knowing it in advance.
AI.result: The authors provide a theoretical guarantee of query efficiency under a purity-subtree assumption and report empirical improvements over other classifier-based filtering methods in extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers a scalable, data-efficient mechanism for training-data curation for LLMs, balancing fewer LLM queries with accurate quality estimation, and outperforms traditional classifier-based filters in practice.

title: From Logits to Latents: Contrastive Representation Shaping for LLM Unlearning
summary: Most LLM unlearning methods aim to approximate retrain-from-scratch behaviors with minimal distribution shift, often via alignment-style objectives defined in the prediction space. While effective at reducing forgotten content generation, such approaches may act as suppression: forgotten concepts can persist in representations and remain entangled with retained knowledge. We introduce CLReg, a contrastive representation regularizer that identifies forget features while pushing them away from retain features, explicitly reducing forget-retain interference with minimal shifts on retain features. We provide first theoretical insights that relate representation shaping to entanglement reduction. Across unlearning benchmarks and LLMs of different sizes, CLReg decreases forget-retain representation entanglement that facilitates mainstream unlearning methods without positing extra privacy risks, inspiring future work that reshapes the representation space to remove forget concepts.

AI.tldr: CLReg is a contrastive representation regularizer that separates forget concepts from retain concepts in LLMs, reducing forget–retain entanglement in the representation space to improve unlearning with minimal impact on retained knowledge.
AI.motivation: Current unlearning approaches suppress forgotten content in outputs but often leave forget-related information entangled in representations, risking persistent leakage and suboptimal unlearning. Reducing representation entanglement could yield more robust forgetting with smaller shifts to retained knowledge.
AI.method: Introduce CLReg, a contrastive objective in representation space that identifies forget features and pushes them away from retain features, explicitly minimizing forget–retain interference while preserving retain representations. The authors provide theoretical links between representation shaping and entanglement reduction and validate the approach across unlearning benchmarks and LLMs of varying sizes.
AI.result: CLReg decreases forget–retain representation entanglement and complements mainstream unlearning methods, reducing the persistence of forget concepts without increasing privacy risks. Empirical results show consistent improvements across benchmarks and model scales.
